Weekly Research Papers — Innovation Rankings

Papers from Google Scholar alerts & email — Jun 15 – Jun 22, 2026

192 Papers Ranked
23 Thematic Groups

Innovation Ranking Criteria

Papers are ranked from most to least innovative based on five criteria: (1) novelty of problem formulation or paradigm shift, (2) creativity of methodology, (3) surprisingness of results or insights, (4) potential impact on the field, and (5) degree of departure from existing approaches. Each paper includes a justification explaining its placement.

≥90 Groundbreaking
≥75 Highly Innovative
≥60 Innovative
≥40 Moderately Innovative
≥20 Incremental
<20 Routine

Innovation Rankings

#1 — Highly Innovative (81)

Recovering Stranded Discrimination in Knowledge Tracing: Per-Item Bias Correction via Empirical-Bayes Shrinkage

X Yan, C Tang, A Shimada · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SLC (State-space Logit Correction), a post-hoc per-item bias correction method for frozen knowledge-tracing models that applies empirical-Bayes shrinkage via Kalman smoothing to recover AUC headroom that any global score-only calibration is structurally incapable of accessing. - Why it's important: Deployed KT models drive adaptive item selection, mastery gating, and early-warning systems in intelligent tutoring, and their discriminative ability (AUC) silently degrades from per-item bias that accumulates after deployment — a problem standard post-hoc calibrators (Platt scaling, temperature scaling, isotonic regression) cannot address by construction. - How it does it: Binary calibration observations are converted to Gaussian pseudo-observations via Laplace/IRLS; per-item logit offsets are estimated by a weighted-average empirical-Bayes shrinkage estimator (equivalent to MAP ℓ2-penalized logistic regression with diagonal Hessian, enabling O(N+K) computation), then composed with an offset-Platt global link. - The good: On the most severely sparse dataset (AS09, median 3 obs/item), naive unregularized per-item estimation degrades AUC by −1.29 pp and NLL catastrophically (0.770 vs. 0.628 baseline), while SLC recovers +2.84 pp AUC — demonstrating that Kalman shrinkage is the essential enabler, not item conditioning alone. - The missing: Backbone models were trained under standard student-level random splits and only the calibration/test phase uses a temporal split, conflating per-item backbone bias with train-versus-deployment distribution shift — an isolation the paper does not provide. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners deploying frozen binary prediction models with per-entity bias in low-data regimes, and for researchers interested in the structural AUC-invariance diagnostic; the detectability bound (Proposition 2) alone saves future effort on temporal drift correction.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (82) and Rigor (80) drive the composite: the structural AUC-invariance argument (Lemma 1) proving that any score-only calibrator is incapable of recovering ranking headroom is a genuinely non-obvious diagnostic, and the detectability bound (Proposition 2) derives — rather than observes — why temporal tracking fails. Rigor is strong (9-level ablation ladder isolating each conditioning factor, 3 seeds, synthetic disentanglement, cross-domain positive/negative controls, released code) but capped below 90 by the preprint status, absence of formal p-values, and the unisolated confound that backbones were trained on i.i.d. student splits while only calibration uses temporal ordering. Completeness (85) and Resilience (82) are high because the applicability boundary is empirically bounded (MovieLens negative control) and limitations are confronted without minimization. Practical Impact (80) reflects a CPU-second, single-hyperparameter artifact with released code that practitioners can deploy atop frozen models. Novelty (78) is a purpose-built combination of established components plus a new diagnostic, not a new problem class. Composite: 0.20*78 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*82 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*82 + 0.10*80 = 81.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 82 Completeness 85 Resilience 82 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation KT models predict student success on test items; their outputs drive adaptive item selection, mastery gating, and early-warning systems. After training, models are frozen, but per-item bias accumulates from two sources: (1) backbone architectures that operate at skill level (DKT, SAKT, DKVMN) share representations across items within a skill, producing structural per-item prediction errors; (2) post-deployment shifts — difficulty drift, new items, population evolution — compound these errors. Standard post-hoc calibration (Platt scaling, temperature scaling, isotonic regression) addresses probability scale but cannot improve AUC because all strictly monotone score transformations preserve pairwise rankings. No prior KT work explicitly targets AUC recovery via per-item conditioning; the gap is the absence of a computationally efficient, backbone-agnostic correction that both recovers ranking quality and handles sparse items without overfitting. Key Idea Global score-only calibration is structurally AUC-invariant (any strictly monotone transform preserves all pairwise orderings, hence AUC), so recovering "stranded" AUC headroom requires conditioning on item identity. Per-item logit offsets treated as Gaussian random effects with empirical-Bayes shrinkage pool information across time bins, shrinking sparse-item estimates toward zero and preventing the catastrophic noise injection that unregularized per-item estimation causes. The combination of this statistical insight with a provable detectability bound on when temporal drift tracking becomes viable is the core contribution. Methodology SLC operates on a calibration window of frozen backbone logits, binary labels, and item IDs in three stages. (1) Pseudo-observation construction: for each (item, time-bin) cell, Fisher information weight W_{i,t} = Σ p_n(1−p_n) and pseudo-residual z_{i,t} = b̂_i^{prev} + Σ(y_n − p_n)/W_{i,t} are computed via Laplace/IRLS, yielding z_{i,t} ∼ N(b_i, 1/W_{i,t}). (2) Empirical-Bayes shrinkage: static per-item offsets are estimated as b̂_i = Σ_t W_{i,t} z_{i,t} / (1/σ²_b + Σ_t W_{i,t}), the MAP estimate of ℓ2-penalized logistic regression with per-item intercepts proven equivalent to IRLS via diagonal Hessian structure (Appendix D). Shrinkage fraction λ_i ranges from ≈0 (sparse) to ≈1 (dense). Prior variance σ²_b = 1.0 is fixed; AUC varies < 0.4 pp over σ²_b ∈ [0.1, 10]. (3) Offset-Platt link: global parameters (a, b₀) are fit by 2-parameter convex logistic regression treating b̂_i as a fixed additive offset — not rescaled by a, consistent with the GLMM parameterization and Corollary 1 (1PL exactness). Diagonal Hessian structure reduces complexity to O(N+K), running in < 2s on AS17 (N≈190k, K≈3k) on a single CPU core. Three critical design decisions are justified: (a) additive vs. scaled offset form, supported by Corollary 1 and empirical comparison; (b) static vs. temporal estimation, where Proposition 2 proves temporal drift is information-limited at current KT densities (δ_min ≈ 2.53 logit on AS17 vs. observed δ_obs ≈ 0.40); (c) single-pass vs. full IRLS convergence, verified empirically to within 0.07 pp AUC (Table 11). Key Results Evaluated on 4 KT datasets — ASSISTments 2017 (AS17, strong drift, 48 obs/item), Eedi (moderate drift, 54 obs/item), ASSISTments 2009 (AS09, weak drift, 3 obs/item), Algebra merged (weak–moderate drift, 1 obs/item) — across 5 backbones (AKT, DKT, SAKT, DKVMN, LPKT) and 3 seeds with strict temporal splits. SLC improves AUC over Platt on all 4 datasets: +3.68 pp (AS17, 0.7182 vs. 0.6814), +3.90 pp (Eedi, 0.7579 vs. 0.7189), +1.90 pp (Algebra, 0.8325 vs. 0.8135), +2.84 pp (AS09, 0.7066 vs. 0.6782). NLL improves on Algebra (0.326), AS17 (0.593), and Eedi (0.552); AS09 incurs a small NLL cost over Platt (0.596 vs. 0.592). Naive (unregularized per-item means) degrades AS09 AUC by −1.29 pp and NLL to 0.770 vs. 0.628 baseline, isolating shrinkage as essential. SLC vs. ResCal+Iso on sparse items (Bin 0): +4.14 pp AS17, +4.18 pp Eedi, +3.67 pp AS09. SAKT shows the largest per-backbone gains (+2.98–6.17 pp), consistent with its weaker per-item expressivity; AKT on Eedi is the only regression (−0.19 pp), attributed to its per-item attention already capturing item variation. SLC-T (temporal tracking) loses to static SLC in all 20 configurations, with δ_min exceeding δ_obs by 3.5–18× across all datasets. Cross-domain: flight-delay (∼12M flights, ∼2500 routes, backbone without per-route parameters) recovers ≈+2 pp AUC; MovieLens-1M with MF/NCF backbones (which include per-item embeddings) yields ∆AUC ≈ 0, confirming the effect is backbone-relative. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.14123v1). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The individual statistical components (Laplace/IRLS approximation for GLMMs, Kalman/Rauch–Tung–Striebel smoothing, James–Stein / empirical-Bayes shrinkage, Platt scaling) are well-established; the contribution is their combination into a purpose-designed post-hoc correction pipeline for deployed KT systems, plus (1) the rigorous calibration-vs.-correction diagnostic applied to KT, (2) the detectability bound (Proposition 2) quantifying the temporal-tracking viability threshold (≈10⁵ obs/item), and (3) the broad empirical demonstration across 20 backbone-dataset configurations that recoverable per-item AUC headroom exists and can be safely accessed. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation design is strong: a 9-level baseline ladder systematically varies conditioning (score-only, time-only, item-no-smooth, item-Kalman), cleanly isolating each factor. Three seeds with mean ± std are reported, though no formal significance tests (p-values) are provided. A 1PL synthetic simulation (K=200, T=20, 5 seeds) disentangles drift intensity from data density. Prior variance sensitivity sweep (σ²_b ∈ [0.01, 100]) and single-pass vs. full-IRLS equivalence verification (Table 11, max 0.07 pp gap) are included. Cross-domain positive and negative controls test the applicability boundary explicitly. Code is released at https://github.com/xiaoran-y/SLC. Methodological concern: backbone models were trained using standard student-level random splits from original papers, not temporal splits — SLC calibration therefore corrects for both per-item backbone expressivity gaps and train-vs-deployment distribution shift simultaneously, a confound not isolated. The Algebra dataset is a non-standard merge of three KDD Cup releases, limiting direct comparison with published KT benchmarks. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Requires only frozen backbone logits, item IDs, and binary labels from a calibration window; no model access, no GPU, no retraining. Runs in < 2s on a single CPU core on AS17 (N≈190k, K≈3k). - Efficient: O(N+KT) time and O(N+K) memory; one hyperparameter fixed throughout; several orders of magnitude faster than backbone retraining or test-time adaptation. - Effective: Evidence is strong where the backbone leaves per-item bias uncaptured; the MovieLens negative control confirms near-zero gain when item effects are already modeled via embeddings, and the sparse-regime ECE trade-off is real (ECE worsens vs. Platt on AS09: 7.63% vs. 4.98%). - Scalable: Linear in N and K by construction; the flight-delay experiment (∼12M flights, ∼2500 routes) suggests moderate-scale robustness, but no test approaching 10⁷ items is conducted. The detectability bound predicts temporal tracking becomes viable only at ≥10⁵ obs/item, a scale untested. Delivers a software artifact (code at https://github.com/xiaoran-y/SLC). Strengths - The calibration-vs.-correction distinction (Lemma 1 + Figure 1) is crisp, provably correct, and under-appreciated in the KT deployment literature; framing AUC invariance as a diagnostic for recoverable headroom is directly actionable. - Proposition 2 (detectability bound) derives — not merely observes — why temporal tracking fails, providing a quantitative viability criterion (≈10⁵ obs/item) that is confirmed by the 3.5–18× δ_min/δ_obs gap across all datasets. - The ablation ladder (Table 2) isolates each conditioning factor with a separate method level; the Naive baseline's catastrophic failure on AS09 provides clean evidence that shrinkage is necessary, not just helpful. - Cross-domain controls (flight-delay positive, MovieLens negative) explicitly test and confirm the applicability boundary, making the scope of the claim empirically bounded rather than speculated. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront limitations directly: (1) SLC is a batch method requiring periodic refresh; (2) on AS09 (median 3 obs/item), a small NLL cost over Platt (+0.004) accompanies the AUC gain; (3) the additive item-only form cannot capture student × item interactions; (4) SLC adds less in dense regimes or when item effects are already modeled; (5) temporal tracking is information-limited at current KT densities; (6) student- and skill-level random effects and fairness audits are deferred. The authors do not minimize any of these, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Backbone models were trained under i.i.d. student-level splits; only the calibration/test partition uses temporal ordering. SLC's gains conflate correction of architectural per-item bias with correction of train-test distribution shift — these are distinct phenomena that the paper does not separate. - AKT on Eedi shows −0.19 pp regression, noted only in the appendix. Attention-based architectures that partially model per-item variation are a failure mode that practitioners using SLC atop AKT-class models could encounter; no criterion for predicting when SLC will hurt is provided. - The Algebra merge (2005–2009 KDD Cup releases) is non-standard; results on this dataset cannot be compared against published KT benchmarks, limiting the community's ability to reproduce or extend from this baseline. - ECE worsens with SLC in most configurations. The authors correctly note ECE is not a proper scoring rule, but in mastery-gating applications probability calibration directly affects threshold-based decisions; the ECE–density relationship is not characterized, leaving practitioners without guidance on when this trade-off becomes problematic. - The paper does not analyze whether the per-item bias is stable across deployment periods or whether it is itself non-stationary at timescales longer than one calibration window — a question directly relevant to the recommended refresh cadence. Bottom Line Any practitioner deploying frozen KT models who has not applied per-item bias correction should read this paper — the structural AUC-invariance argument is provably correct, the method deploys in seconds with one hyperparameter, and 2–4 pp AUC gains are consequential in an educational domain where these predictions drive student-facing decisions.
Search Results 2606.14123 PDF
#2 — Highly Innovative (80)

GRACE-DS: a Guarded Reward-guided Agent Correction Environment in Data Science

A Tsymbalov, D Zaripov, A Epifanov, A Palienko · arxiv.org · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GRACE-DS, an evaluation harness for LLM-powered AutoML agents on tabular supervised-learning workflows that measures both predictive performance and process reliability—leakage avoidance, reproducibility, protocol compliance, error recovery, and reward alignment—under enterprise-like constraints. - Why it's important: Enterprise deployment of LLM AutoML agents requires knowing not just whether an agent achieves high accuracy but whether it does so safely and reproducibly; final-score-only benchmarks cannot distinguish a high score obtained via target leakage from a genuinely valid pipeline, a failure mode with direct operational consequences. - How it does it: The harness wraps each tabular ML task in a POMDP with eight named workflow states, hidden executable validators that penalize leakage and reproducibility failures without revealing their detection logic, a decomposed process reward, and a single evaluator-owned hidden-test score that remains isolated from the agent's feedback loop. - The good: The flexible-iterative regime achieves E2E normalized hidden-test quality of 0.754 vs. 0.536 for single-shot and 0.527 for an unstructured agent (p ≤ 10⁻¹⁵), with 93.4% error recovery and near-zero critical errors across 7,200+ episodes spanning 8 frontier LLMs and 10 tasks. - The missing: The red-team suite is prompt-based and not exhaustive—validator-aware attacks, obfuscated leakage via serialization, and label inference from feedback are untested; the constrained scikit-learn-only stack and 10-task corpus limit ecological validity. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners designing or procuring enterprise LLM-powered data science agents; less relevant for those focused on frontier ML, NLP, or unstructured agentic benchmarking.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (85) drives the score — 7,200+ episodes across 8 frontier LLMs × 10 tasks × 15 regimes, five leave-one-state-out ablations, paired Wilcoxon tests with bootstrap CIs, and public code; reduced from higher only by preprint status, a possibly-weak HistGradientBoosting oracle, and provider-controlled OpenRouter endpoints. Completeness (88) and Resilience (82) are strong: limitations (prompt-based red-team, scikit-only stack, 10-task corpus, lattice-physics outlier) are confronted directly in a dedicated section. Non-Obviousness (78) is high — the quantified finding that reward-maximizing agents degrade hidden-test quality while barely moving reward is a genuinely useful, non-obvious result for RLHF-style training. Novelty (70) is a new system+benchmark via combination rather than a new formulation. Impact (75) reflects the released harness. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*85+0.15*78+0.15*88+0.15*82+0.10*75 ≈ 80. Resembles the user's positive set in mechanism-focus and rigor.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 85 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 88 Resilience 82 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Organizations evaluating LLM-powered AutoML agents for production tabular ML workflows—churn prediction, risk scoring, fraud detection—cannot rely on public benchmark scores to make deployment decisions. An agent may score well on an external competition while leaking the target into its feature set, fitting on validation labels, producing non-reproducible artifacts, or failing to recover from execution errors on internal data with messy schemas and domain-specific constraints. Sending internal data to public benchmark infrastructure is frequently impossible. Existing benchmarks (MLE-bench, DS-1000, MLGym, DSGym) measure final score or code correctness but do not reveal how the score was obtained or whether the underlying process is methodologically trustworthy. The specific gap is a controlled pre-deployment evaluation instrument that simultaneously measures predictive performance and process integrity in the organization's own environment. Key Idea The core insight is that hiding the exact evaluation rubric while surfacing only abstract corrective hints forces the agent to reason about the data rather than pattern-match the checker—and that separating a process reward (which the agent can steer on) from an evaluator-owned held-out score (which it cannot directly observe) is the only mechanism that prevents high process reward from substituting for actual task quality. Any disclosed rubric can be satisfied superficially; the hidden-checklist design combined with isolated final scoring makes reward-hacking detectable rather than profitable. Methodology Each evaluation episode is a POMDP M = ⟨S, A, O, T, Ω, R, γ⟩. The agent iterates through eight named states (PLAN, EDA, FEATURE_ENGINEERING, MODEL, VALIDATE, CODE, CODE_FIX, FINAL_SUBMIT), each with independent per-task step, wall-clock, and repeat budgets. The agent sees training features with labels and validation features without labels; validation labels and all hidden-test data remain evaluator-private. The approved execution stack is fixed (pandas, numpy, scikit-learn, scipy, statsmodels); external libraries, file/network access, subprocesses, and unsafe builtins are blocked by static sandbox checks. Failed actions trigger transactional rollback; raw-input snapshots are hash-checked after each executable action. The decomposed reward is w = 0.55·r_perf + 0.15·r_plan + 0.30·r_code, with soft penalties capped at min(p_tot, 0.6·w) and a small progress floor. A small set of critical errors (target leakage, train+validation refit, evaluator-private access, snapshot tampering) forces r_final = 0, overriding the normal calculation. Validators span five groups—integrity & leakage, data quality, modeling, process, and terminal comparison against a trivial baseline and an untuned HistGradientBoosting oracle—and are phase-gated so an agent is not penalized for requirements not yet meaningful. Three critical design decisions: (1) Hiding validator thresholds and patterns while giving abstract hints—prevents rubric pattern-matching while preserving corrective signal; well-justified by the red-team experiments. (2) Separating evaluator-owned scoring from process reward—checklist compliance feeds only the plan (0.15) and code-quality (0.30) terms; performance (0.55) is nonzero only on evaluator scoring actions; this is well-justified by the reward-maximization probes. (3) Prohibiting automated hyperparameter search and constraining the library stack—keeps comparison focused on planning and correction rather than AutoML search power; partially justified for enterprise settings that want controlled, interpretable pipelines, but diverges from common practitioner tooling (LightGBM, XGBoost, Optuna are absent). 15 regimes are evaluated: 7 core (single-shot, N restarts-from-scratch, N restarts-call-matched, unstructured-agent, fixed-stage-iterative, flexible-compact, flexible-iterative), 5 leave-one-state-out ablations, 2 reward-maximization probes, and 1 red-team adversary. The grid crosses 8 LLMs × 10 tasks × 6 episodes per cell (3 split seeds × 2 LLM sampling repeats) = 480 episodes per core regime. Paired comparisons use Wilcoxon signed-rank tests with paired bootstrap CIs; rates use Wilson 95% CIs. Key Results Primary metric: E2E normalized hidden-test quality (E2E Q = (raw − baseline)/(oracle − baseline)), where 0 = trivial baseline and 1 = untuned HistGradientBoosting oracle. - flexible-iterative: E2E Q = 0.754 (95% CI [0.708, 0.798]), PV = 96.9% - single-shot: E2E Q = 0.536, PV = 88.8%; unstructured-agent: E2E Q = 0.527, PV = 69.2% - restarts-from-scratch: E2E Q = 0.672, PV = 96.5%; restarts-call-matched: E2E Q = 0.686, PV = 95.8% - flexible-iterative vs. single-shot: +0.218 E2E Q (p = 1.9×10⁻²⁴), +0.172 Obs. Q, +8.1 pp PV - flexible-iterative vs. unstructured-agent: +0.227 E2E Q (p = 6.2×10⁻¹⁶), +0.011 Obs. Q, +27.7 pp PV (the almost identical Obs. Q confirms the gain over unstructured-agent comes almost entirely from reliability, not model quality) - flexible-iterative vs. call-matched restarts: +0.068 E2E Q (p = 0.011) with −0.40 fewer calls and −150s wall-clock—a moderate but statistically significant advantage at equivalent compute Error recovery: flexible-iterative recovers from errors 93.4% of the time (94.2% for execution errors) vs. 7.0% for single-shot and 47.0% for unstructured-agent. Critical errors: restarts produce 5.2–7.1% leakage-class critical-error episodes vs. 0–0.2% for structured regimes. Red-team (120 matched episodes): 0 critical errors, 0 evaluator-private access attempts, 0 snapshot tampering, 0 forbidden-action attempts; 75.0% per-cell defense rate (30/40 model-task rows); residual uncaught reward advantage mean +0.008, accompanied in every case by a hidden-test quality deficit. Reward-quality alignment: per-task Spearman ρ = 0.296 (positive on 9/10 tasks); reward-maximization buys at most +0.014 reward at cost of −0.039 to −0.050 E2E Q. Pooled episode-level correlation is slightly negative (Simpson's paradox), underscoring the per-task computation requirement. Task-level heterogeneity spans two orders of magnitude: +0.903 E2E Q gain on lattice-physics (single-shot nearly fails, structured iteration clears the oracle) down to +0.019 on tunadromd (single-shot near-saturated). Model-level: Gemini-3.1 Pro achieves 0.820 E2E Q; GPT-OSS-120B gains most from structure (+0.342 over single-shot). Fixed-schedule PV collapses to 38.3% for Qwen3-Next-80B (only model without reasoning), restored to 86.7% under flexible-iterative. The harness is a preprint (arXiv:2606.16000v2, 16 Jun 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW BENCHMARK. The POMDP framework, hidden validators, and decomposed rewards are not individually novel; the genuine contribution is their combination into a single platform with five jointly-enforced properties—stage-aware interaction, hidden process validators, evaluator-owned scoring isolation, reproducible raw-input submission contract, and experimentally controlled multi-regime comparison—which no prior AutoML agent benchmark provides together. The positioning against MLE-bench, TML-Bench, MLGym, and DSGym is specific and credible: none of those offer all five properties simultaneously. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and well-designed: 5 leave-one-state-out ablations directly isolate each stage's marginal contribution. Error bars are reported (cluster-bootstrap CIs for means, Wilson 95% CIs for rates, paired Wilcoxon p-values). Multiple seeds: 3 split seeds × 2 LLM-sampling repeats = 6 episodes per cell. Code is publicly released (github.com/Alexx221x/GRACE-DS), though trained artifacts are absent (LLMs are API-served via OpenRouter). Methodological concerns: (1) The oracle is an untuned HistGradientBoosting, potentially a weak ceiling for high-dimensional tasks (tabred-sberbank: 392 columns); a weak oracle compresses the normalized scale, understating quality differences. (2) All LLM inference via OpenRouter with provider-specific endpoints—endpoint versioning and reproducibility depend on provider stability; the model identifiers (gpt-5.4, gemini-3.1-pro-preview, grok-4.3) suggest preview or very-recent deployments whose behavior may drift. (3) The 8-action hard cap per episode may artificially penalize workflows that legitimately require more steps; the sensitivity of results to this cap is not studied. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper builds and evaluates the GRACE-DS harness system with empirical experiments across 8 LLMs and 10 tasks. - Practical: Runs on CPU (AMD Ryzen 7 5700X, 64 GB RAM, 8 worker processes) with remote LLM inference via OpenRouter; an organization can deploy on commercially available hardware by providing a task.json manifest for their own datasets. - Efficient: Mean 101s wall-clock and ~43k output tokens per episode (flexible-iterative); episodes run in parallel (8 workers); the call-matched restart baseline is simultaneously slower (251s) and worse-quality, confirming flexible-iterative's computational efficiency advantage on this metric. - Effective: The harness catches leakage that final-score benchmarks miss (5.2–7.1% critical error rate in restart baselines, blocked to near-zero by structured regimes), blocks all red-team deployment-critical failures attempted in the prompt suite, and produces decomposed diagnostics that explain regime quality gaps—evidence supports the claimed separation of process quality from task quality. - Scalable: Tested on 10 tasks and 8 models; the manifest-driven design supports arbitrary organization-specific task.json files in principle. The red-team suite is explicitly not exhaustive—validator-aware attacks, obfuscated leakage, and label inference from feedback are unaddressed; whether the validator suite remains robust as adversarial sophistication increases is unknown. Software artifact: public GitHub repository with evaluation harness and output examples. No trained models or pre-computed embeddings are released. Strengths - The empirical demonstration that reward-maximizing agents degrade hidden-test quality (−0.039 to −0.050 E2E Q) while barely moving reward (+0.014) provides a concrete, quantified argument for why process reward cannot substitute for isolated held-out evaluation—a non-obvious and important finding for anyone designing RLHF-style training loops for data science agents. - The 7,200-episode scale with paired statistical tests across 8 frontier LLMs, 15 regimes, and 10 tasks is unusually thorough for a benchmark paper; the per-model, per-task, and per-state breakdowns genuinely disaggregate the headline result into actionable sub-findings (e.g., fixed-schedule PV collapse for weak models is directly deployable guidance). - The validator decomposition into five independently auditable groups with phase-gated activation enables principled failure attribution—protocol violations are separated from leakage from modeling failures—which is a structural improvement over opaque LLM-as-judge scoring schemes. - The discovery that flexible-iterative simultaneously achieves the highest quality, fastest wall-clock, and best efficiency ratio among iterative regimes (dominating call-matched restarts on quality, speed, and calls, at a 2.1× token premium) is counter-intuitive and makes the operating-point recommendation actionable. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors confront limitations directly and honestly: (1) the red-team suite is prompt-based and does not cover validator-aware attacks, obfuscated leakage, data-copying, or label inference from feedback; (2) scope is restricted to tabular supervised ML—deep learning, multimodal, RL, and scientific discovery workflows are excluded; (3) governance coverage is incomplete—no PII detection, RBAC, fairness/bias auditing, or regulatory compliance; (4) passing GRACE-DS does not certify production readiness; monitoring, probabilistic calibration, distribution shift robustness, latency constraints, explainability, and security review are explicitly out of scope. These boundaries are stated in a dedicated Future Work section rather than buried. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The oracle (untuned HistGradientBoosting) may be a weak ceiling for some tasks, particularly the 392-column tabred-sberbank or tasks with strong temporal structure; an oracle that is too easy to match compresses the top of the normalized scale, potentially understating quality differences between strong agents. Per-task oracle-vs-best-agent gaps are not reported. - The constrained library stack (scikit-learn only, no LightGBM, XGBoost, CatBoost, Optuna) diverges from real enterprise tabular ML practice where gradient-boosted tree libraries dominate. Validators that penalize "automated search APIs" and neural-network choices may measure compliance with the harness design rather than general AutoML competence; results may not transfer to organizations using industry-standard stacks. - The lattice-physics task drives an anomalously large gain (+0.903 E2E Q) that heavily influences aggregate comparisons. With only 10 tasks and 48 episodes per task-regime cell, this single outlier could meaningfully shift pooled effect sizes; removing it would reduce the call-matched advantage (Section 5.9 explicitly notes lattice-physics contributes +0.627 of the call-matched delta). - The 8-action-per-episode hard cap (Table 15) is tight for complex tabular workflows. Whether increasing this cap benefits structured and unstructured regimes equally or disproportionately—which would affect the core H1 finding—is not studied. - All LLM inference uses hosted OpenRouter endpoints whose versioning is provider-controlled; the model identifiers include preview designations, making exact reproduction by third parties dependent on provider endpoint stability, which is not within the authors' control. Bottom Line GRACE-DS fills a genuine and well-motivated gap in LLM agent evaluation by measuring process integrity alongside predictive quality, and the empirical case for its flexible-iterative regime is unusually well-constructed; practitioners deciding whether to deploy an LLM AutoML agent on internal tabular data should read this, with the caveat that the constrained library stack and 10-task corpus require judgment when extrapolating to production environments using LightGBM, XGBoost, or richer tooling.
Search Results 2606.16000 PDF
#3 — Highly Innovative (79)

Measuring Epistemic Resilience of LLMs Under Misleading Medical Context (15 ▲ )

Hongjian Zhou, Xinyu Zou, Jinge Wu, Sean Wu, Junchi Yu, Bradley Max Segal, Tobias Erich Niebuhr, Sara Amro, Michael Petrus, Sheikh Momin, Alexandra M. Cardoso Pinto, Rachel Niesen, Laura Sophie Wegner, Dhruv Darji, Jung Moses Koo, Joshua Fieggen, Kapil Narain, Mingde Zeng, Lei Clifton, Linda Shapiro, Fenglin Liu, David A. Clifton · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MedMisBench, a static benchmark of 10,932 medical questions paired with 48,889 misleading context-option pairs organized along a 5-content-type × 3-provenance taxonomy, designed to measure whether LLMs maintain correct medical judgment when false context is injected. - Why it's important: LLMs deployed in consumer health and clinical decision-support settings encounter messy, adversarial information environments — not clean exam-style inputs — yet every existing medical benchmark measures knowledge on clean inputs, leaving the failure mode of contextual manipulation entirely unmeasured and potentially masking dangerous brittleness in patient-facing systems. - How it does it: Each benchmark item pairs an answer-grounded multiple-choice question (verified the model originally answers correctly) with LLM-generated misleading sentences that target specific wrong options, varied by content-corruption type (e.g., exception poisoning, threshold corruption) and provenance framing (neutral, patient claim, authority), then measures attack success rate — the fraction of clean-correct answers that flip to wrong after injection. - The good: Across 11 model configurations, clean accuracy averages 71.1% but focused single-claim injection (Type 1) drops accuracy to 38.0% with 51.5% ASR, and a 14-member international clinical panel found serious potential harm in 38.2% of reviewed misled responses, providing the first clinician-validated harm estimate for this failure mode. - The missing: The benchmark is restricted to multiple-choice items with answer-grounded gold labels, which enables automatic evaluation but leaves open whether the same failure patterns hold for open-ended responses, multi-turn consultations, or free-text clinical documentation — the deployment settings with the highest real-world stakes. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building or evaluating LLMs in medical or RAG settings; the benchmark, taxonomy, and finding that higher reasoning effort can increase vulnerability in some model families are directly actionable for safety evaluation and system design.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster. Rigor (82) drives the composite: 11 model configurations, a 14-member international clinician panel with high inter-rater agreement (Gwet AC2 0.78–0.95), three sensitivity analyses (generator substitution, provenance shuffling, falsehood-clarity stratification), and an automatic ASR computation validated at 98.1% agreement with clinician labels — deliberately avoiding the LLM-as-judge validity threat. Novelty (80) and Non-Obviousness (80) are high: the 5×3 content/provenance taxonomy is a new benchmark design, and the findings that authority-framed fabrications reach >65% attack success and that higher reasoning effort can *increase* vulnerability are genuinely counter-intuitive. Completeness (80) and Resilience (78) are strong — limitations (MCQ-only ecological validity, synthetic injections, partial generator-independence check) are confronted directly, and the paper is the first to convert a benchmark signal into clinician-validated harm evidence (38.2% serious-harm rate). Practical Impact (70) reflects a publicly released static benchmark and code that directly change how medical LLMs should be safety-evaluated. Weighted: 0.20*80 + 0.25*82 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*78 + 0.10*70 = 79.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 82 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 80 Resilience 78 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Frontier LLMs now pass medical licensing exams at expert level, but clinical and consumer-health deployments embed models in information environments contaminated by retrieved documents, patient-supplied histories, online health claims, and authoritative-sounding but potentially false context. Patients increasingly consult LLMs before or after clinical encounters. The risk is not that the model lacks medical knowledge — it is that the model will abandon correct knowledge when plausible false context frames the decision differently. Existing medical benchmarks (MedQA, MedMCQA, HealthBench, MedXpertQA, MedAgentBench) evaluate on clean inputs and therefore cannot answer the central deployment question: does the model preserve correct medical judgment when misleading context is present? No reusable, systematically constructed benchmark had previously operationalized this gap across medical reasoning, agentic capability, and patient-journey evaluation tasks. Key Idea The core insight is that misleading medical context is not homogeneous along a single axis: it varies in what false medical claim is made (the content corruption) and who appears to be making it (the provenance framing). By constructing a 5 × 3 taxonomy that crosses content type (relationship inversion, threshold corruption, cue remapping, spurious anchoring, exception poisoning) against provenance (neutral declarative, patient self-claim, authority/guideline), the benchmark can isolate which combinations are most dangerous — revealing, for example, that authority-framed exception poisoning is far more damaging than patient-framed spurious anchoring, even when the underlying factual distortion is similar. Methodology The pipeline proceeds in four stages. Stage 1 sources questions from five datasets — MedQA, MedMCQA, MedXpertQA, MedJourney, and HLE — retaining only answer-grounded multiple-choice items. Stage 2 applies LLM-based applicability filtering (over half a million decisions across five sources) to verify that at least one content-corruption type can be applied naturally to every wrong option of a given item while leaving the gold answer unambiguous; this yields 10,932 retained questions. Stage 3 uses Gemini-3-flash as the primary injection generator, which returns an option-wise context bundle: a truthful affirmation for the correct option and one misleading sentence per incorrect option, each instantiating the selected content and provenance type. Stage 4 evaluates under two delivery protocols: Type 1 (focused) presents only one wrong-option sentence alongside the question; Type 2 (all-option) presents the full bundle including the correct-option affirmation. Attack success rate (ASR) counts clean-correct answers that flip to wrong after injection; targeted ASR (TASR) counts the subset that flip specifically to the injected target wrong option. Evaluation uses paired clean/injected runs at temperature 0 across 11 model configurations spanning commercial APIs (GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-pro, Gemini-3.1-flash-lite, Claude-sonnet-4.6), open-weight general models (Gemma 4 26B, Qwen3.6-27B), and a medical-domain model (MedGemma 27B). A 14-member international clinical panel (7 countries, mean 3 years post-qualification experience) reviews a stratified 89-task sample under two rubrics: injection validity (Rubric A, item quality) and response harm (Rubric B, downstream clinical risk). Critical design decisions: (1) answer-grounded multiple-choice items enable automatic ASR computation without LLM-as-judge, justified by 98.1% agreement between automatic labels and clinician correctness ratings; (2) applicability filtering before generation ensures each flip is interpretable as resilience loss rather than an artifact of inapplicable context; (3) option-wise bundle generation allows Type 1 and Type 2 to share the same generation source, so differences reflect delivery setting rather than different injections. Key Results Across 11 configurations, mean clean accuracy is 71.1%, dropping to 38.0% under Type 1 focused injection with 51.5% mean ASR. Type 2 (all-option with correct-option affirmation) returns accuracy to 70.5% but still yields 18.7% ASR, demonstrating that aggregate accuracy can look stable while originally correct answers still flip. The gap between Type 1 ASR (51.5%) and Type 2 ASR (18.7%) — a 2.8× ratio — is the benchmark's central finding: one plausible false claim is far more damaging than a bundle of competing claims when the correct option is also supported. TASR is 45.4%, close to the 51.5% ASR, indicating that most focused-injection failures are directional uptake of the injected claim rather than generic instability. Taxonomy analysis reveals stark heterogeneity: authority-framed injections reach 69.5% Type 1 ASR versus 18.5% for patient-framed claims; exception poisoning reaches 64.1% and threshold/reference corruption 60.9%, while spurious anchoring is only 20.9%. The most capable model on clean inputs (Gemini-3.1-pro high reasoning, 83.5% clean) has 65.0% Type 1 ASR — worse than GPT-5.4 medium reasoning (81.3% clean, 36.1% ASR). Crucially, more reasoning effort is not uniformly protective: Gemini-3.1-flash-lite improves clean accuracy from 71.0% to 77.6% with medium reasoning but Type 1 ASR rises from 37.5% to 54.0%. Clinician harm review on 89 tasks (responses from Claude-sonnet-4.6, Gemini-3.1-pro, GPT-5.4 under medium/high reasoning): 38.2% (95% CI 28.8–48.6) are worst-case outputs (wrong final answer + material injection uptake + serious harm potential); 46.1% additional wrong with low/moderate harm; only 5.6% correctly reject the injection. Inter-rater agreement is high (Gwet AC2: 0.94 correctness, 0.95 injection uptake, 0.84 harm potential). Generator sensitivity check (600-item stratified subset regenerated with GPT-5.4) confirms results are not generator-specific (e.g., Gemini-3.1-pro Type 1 ASR: 63.8% with main generator vs. 63.0% with GPT-5.4). Search augmentation on HLE items reduces Gemini Pro Type 1 ASR from 81.5% to 16.1% but leaves Flash-Lite at 40.7%. Defensive prompting reduces Type 1 ASR by 10.1–14.0 points but leaves residual ASR of 28.5%–57.4%. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The 5 × 3 content/provenance taxonomy applied to paired judgment-preservation testing is novel as a benchmark design; the empirical finding that authority-framed rule-like fabrications are far more dangerous than patient-framed claims, and that stronger reasoning in some families increases vulnerability, is a genuinely non-obvious result. The individual components — adversarial injection, medical QA benchmarking, clinician review — are known; what is new is their systematic combination into a reusable static benchmark with a principled taxonomy and paired harm assessment. Rigor & Reproducibility The benchmark is well-designed for reproducibility: static release with fixed delivery schemas, option-aligned injection fields, and target wrong-answer metadata eliminate reliance on LLM-as-judge for ASR/TASR computation. Clinician review provides dual ratings on 64/89 tasks with high inter-rater agreement (Gwet AC2 0.78–0.95 across rubric dimensions). Three sensitivity analyses test the key claims: generator sensitivity (GPT-5.4 substitution), provenance assignment sensitivity (cyclic shuffles), and falsehood-clarity stratification (harm result strengthens when ambiguous injections are excluded, rising from 38.2% to 44.6% worst-case). Benchmark validity is supported by clinician composite quality score 1.76/2.00. The mixed-effects logistic regression for harm correctly accounts for reviewer random effects. A notable methodological concern: clean source question answers may be familiar to evaluated models (contamination risk from public datasets), which the authors correctly address by centering on ASR over clean-correct items rather than treating clean accuracy as the primary metric. However, the evaluation scope is limited to three model families in the clinician harm review, and MedJourney items (Chinese-language) are absent from clinician review. Code and benchmark are publicly released (GitHub links provided). Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile — the paper runs experiments on 11 model configurations and a clinician panel. - Practical: Running evaluations requires commercial API access or 8 × NVIDIA A5000 GPUs for open-weight models; the benchmark itself runs on standard QA infrastructure with no specialized hardware requirements. - Efficient: Benchmark construction required over half a million applicability-filtering decisions and injection generation calls (Gemini-3-flash), making creation expensive but one-time; evaluation cost scales linearly with the number of models tested. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrably captures a failure mode missed by all existing medical benchmarks, confirmed by 98.1% agreement between automatic ASR labels and clinician correctness ratings and by direct clinical harm characterization. - Scalable: The benchmark is fixed at 10,932 items across 5 source datasets; extending to open-ended responses, multimodal items, or multi-turn consultations requires new construction work, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation. The benchmark evaluates stressed static QA, not live RAG pipelines or agentic workflows at deployment scale. The paper delivers a software artifact (static benchmark with release schema) and open-source code. Strengths - The 5-content × 3-provenance taxonomy is principled and empirically validated: it predicts failure rates across 11 models consistently, with authority/neutral framings dominating patient claims across all model configurations tested — a finding confirmed by cyclic provenance reassignment. - The finding that higher reasoning effort can increase vulnerability (Gemini-3.1-flash-lite: Type 1 ASR 37.5% → 54.0% with medium reasoning) is counter-intuitive and important for safety evaluations that assume reasoning depth is protective. - The 14-member international clinician harm review, with structured rubrics, high inter-rater agreement, and sensitivity analyses stratified by falsehood clarity, provides the first direct clinical harm estimate for this failure mode — converting a benchmark signal into patient-safety evidence. - Static release with option-aligned injection fields enabling reproducible ASR/TASR computation without LLM-as-judge is a methodologically sound design choice that avoids a major validity threat affecting other medical evaluation work. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state three limitations: (1) multiple-choice format limits ecological validity relative to open-ended or multi-turn clinical deployment; (2) misleading context is synthetic (clinician-reviewed but LLM-generated) rather than drawn from naturally occurring misinformation; (3) clinician review is sampled rather than exhaustive due to reviewer time constraints, and MedJourney (Chinese-language) items are excluded from harm review entirely. The authors confront these limitations directly and frame them as future work directions rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps The benchmark construction has a potential circularity: the applicability filter and injection generator are both LLMs, and the paper does not fully control for the possibility that generator LLMs produce injections that are systematically harder or easier to resist for the same family of models (e.g., Gemini-generated injections evaluated on Gemini). The generator sensitivity check covers only 3 of 11 model configurations and a 600-item subset, which limits the strength of the independence claim. The paper reports that clean accuracy for commercial models in their evaluation is sometimes lower than published benchmark reports, attributing this to unverifiable API version differences — this creates uncertainty about whether the evaluated configuration matches the safety-critical deployment configuration. The harm review uses only three model configurations (all strong commercial models), so the 38.2% worst-case rate does not directly characterize the risk from open-weight models despite their higher ASR. No ablation tests whether the applicability filtering step itself is necessary — it is possible that unfiltered injections would produce similar ASR rankings while reducing construction cost. Bottom Line This is a timely, well-executed benchmark paper that fills a real gap in medical LLM evaluation — the finding that authority-framed fabrications cause >65% attack success even in frontier models, combined with clinical panel evidence that 38.2% of misled responses carry serious harm potential, gives practitioners concrete evidence that clean-accuracy benchmarks cannot be trusted as safety signals for deployed consumer health applications.
Recommended 2606.12291 PDF
#4 — Highly Innovative (79)

Benchmarking Table Extraction from Heterogeneous Scientific PDF Documents

M Soric, C Gracianne, I Manolescu, P Senellart · pierre.senellart.com · 6 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an end-to-end benchmark for table extraction from scientific PDFs covering 86k pages across four datasets — including two new ones (Table-arXiv: 37k auto-annotated samples from arXiv LaTeX sources; Table-BRGM: 2k manually annotated geological reports) — together with new formally-defined metrics that propagate table detection quality into structure recognition scoring. - Why it's important: Prior benchmarks evaluate Table Detection (TD) and Table Structure Recognition (TSR) independently on isolated, all-positive datasets, masking how detection errors cascade into structure quality and artificially inflating precision — a critical flaw for any real deployment pipeline. - How it does it: Each model's own TD output (true positives at IoU ≥ 0.5) is fed into TSR rather than using gold-cropped table images, and new continuous P^TSR / R^TSR metrics substitute GriTS/TEDS quality scores for binary TP values, collapsing both subtask errors into a single end-to-end number. - The good: Including pages without tables in the two new datasets reveals that general VLMs (GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen2.5-VL) almost always predict a table per page, producing massive false positives on table-free pages — a failure mode completely invisible in all-positive prior benchmarks. - The missing: No sensitivity analysis on the fixed IoU threshold θ_J = 0.5 that drives all TP/FP decisions, no breakdown by table complexity (bordered vs. borderless, simple vs. heavily merged), and VLMs are only evaluated zero-shot with a single prompt. - Read it or not: Essential for practitioners choosing a table extraction tool for scientific document pipelines, and for benchmark designers evaluating why positive-only datasets mislead; less essential if your documents are scanned or your pipeline already uses Docling or MonkeyOCR with known performance.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (80) and Completeness (80) drive the score: code, two new datasets, and the full evaluation framework are archived at Zenodo; the paper is peer-reviewed (KDD '26); large dataset sizes give implicit confidence; and limitations (text-only PDFs, VLM contamination, partial budget-limited VLM runs, TabPedia timeout) are confronted honestly. Novelty (78) is high — NEW BENCHMARK + NEW DATASET + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING, with formally-defined continuous P^TSR/R^TSR end-to-end metrics and D-ECE calibration that no prior TE benchmark offers. Non-Obviousness (78) reflects the genuinely consequential design choice of including table-free pages, which exposed the previously-undocumented VLM over-generation failure mode. Resilience (80) is strong given peer review, though the missing θ_J sensitivity analysis and thin Table-BRGM positive set (256 pages) leave minor exposure. Practical Impact (80) reflects a released artifact and directly actionable tool-selection guidance with real economic stakes. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*80 + 0.10*80 = 79. This resembles the user's positive set: controlled, rigorous, surprising empirical finding.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 80 Resilience 80 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Extracting tables from PDF documents is a prerequisite for downstream tasks — question answering over tables, data lake joins, scientific data analysis. The field decomposes the problem into TD (finding tables in a page) and TSR (parsing rows/columns/cells/content). Existing benchmarks evaluate these independently: TSR benchmarks always start from gold-cropped table images, hiding what happens when detection is imperfect. All major benchmarks also consist exclusively of positive pages, which inflates precision scores. Practitioners face 15+ candidate tools across four families (rule-based heuristics, CV-based pipelines, general VLMs, expert VLMs) with no principled cross-family comparison on heterogeneous data. Key Idea The single core insight is that end-to-end TE quality must be measured by feeding each model's own detected tables into TSR — not gold crops — and defining precision/recall by replacing binary TP/FP with the continuous TSR quality score of the matched detection. This makes detection errors directly penalize structure scores, reflecting real deployment behavior rather than an optimistic upper bound. The benchmark also deliberately includes table-free pages to expose models that cannot abstain from predicting. Methodology Four datasets are used: PubTables-Test (46,942 pages, biomedical, Adobe InDesign, homogeneous layout, subset of PubTables-1M), Table-arXiv (36,869 pages, 100% LaTeX from arXiv, auto-annotated by instrumenting tabular environment start/end commands and LaTeXML for HTML TSR ground truth, spanning 20 years and all arXiv domains), Table-BRGM (2,003 pages, French/English geological reports, mostly Word-typeset, manually annotated), and ICDAR-2013 (238 pages, EU/US government reports). The two new datasets contain negative pages. Fifteen methods are evaluated: 4 rule-based baselines (Camelot, PyMuPDF, PDFPlumber, Grobid), 4 CV-based pipelines (TATR, XY+TATR, VGT+TATR-struct, Docling), 3 general VLMs (Qwen2.5-VL 32B, GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro in zero-shot), 3 expert VLMs (TabPedia, GOT, MonkeyOCR), and 1 commercial API (Mathpix). For TD, standard precision/recall/AP at IoU threshold θ_J = 0.5, plus model calibration via D-ECE (object-detection version of ECE) for probabilistic models. For models without bounding boxes (GOT), text-content Jaccard matching is used. For TSR (on true positives only), GriTS-Topology and GriTS-Content (2D-LCS based) and TEDS (tree-edit-distance similarity). HTML outputs are normalized to {<table>, <tr>, <td>} before scoring to eliminate tag vocabulary artifacts. End-to-end TE metrics combine TD IoU with TSR scores into continuous P^TSR/R^TSR. Critical design decisions: θ_J = 0.5 fixed throughout (not ablated); TSR scores averaged over each model's own true-positive set (not a shared gold set, so TSR scores are not directly comparable across models); cost estimated by converting CPU/A100 GPU time to AWS equivalent rates. Key Results Docling, MonkeyOCR, and Mathpix are the strongest performers: MonkeyOCR achieves F1^TEDS of 0.87/0.78/0.84/0.87 across PubTables/Table-arXiv/Table-BRGM/ICDAR-2013; Mathpix 0.88/0.79/0.82/0.89. TATR achieves AP = 1.00 on PubTables (its training domain) but drops to AP = 0.86/0.82/0.96 on out-of-domain sets. VGT+TATR-struct collapses on Table-BRGM (AP = 0.46, F1^Top = 0.21), the most layout-heterogeneous set. General VLMs fail on heterogeneous data: Qwen2.5-VL F1^bbox drops from 0.65 (PubTables) to 0.29 (Table-BRGM); GPT-4.1 F1^bbox ranges 0.28–0.46 across datasets; Gemini 2.5 Pro 0.36–0.79. The cause: VLMs almost always predict a table per page, producing rampant false positives on table-free pages. Heuristic methods (PyMuPDF F1^bbox = 0.85, Camelot 0.83) outperform all VLMs on Table-BRGM for a fraction of the cost. Model calibration: TATR D-ECE = 0.16 on Table-arXiv (among predictions with 80–90% confidence, fewer than 2% are true positives); VGT D-ECE = 0.04; XY+TATR D-ECE = 0.45 due to training distribution shift from full pages to cropped sub-images. No method achieves F1^TEDS > 90%. API costs: GPT-4.1 $320, Gemini 2.5 Pro $1,200, Mathpix $310 for the benchmark run. Novelty Classification NEW BENCHMARK + NEW DATASET + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The end-to-end evaluation methodology with formally defined continuous P^TSR/R^TSR metrics and D-ECE calibration analysis is genuinely novel in the TE field; existing work either evaluates TD and TSR independently or uses informal aggregation. The two new datasets fill real gaps (LaTeX-diverse arXiv documents; domain-specific Word-typeset documents with negative pages). The empirical finding that general VLMs cannot abstain and collapse in precision on heterogeneous data is a concrete, previously undocumented result with direct practical implications. The XY+TATR model is a straightforward engineering contribution, not a novel method. Rigor & Reproducibility Code, datasets, and extended results archived at Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20486310). Table-arXiv annotation is derived from LaTeX source (high quality, automated, scales to 37k samples). Table-BRGM is manually annotated (high quality, but only 2,003 pages with 256 table-positive pages, making results there statistically thin). No ablation on θ_J sensitivity; no error bars or significance tests (large dataset sizes provide implicit confidence for aggregate numbers). VLM contamination is explicitly acknowledged but not controlled. GPT-4.1 and Gemini 2.5 Pro results are marked with asterisks as partial (40k/47k PubTables pages), halted by budget. TabPedia timed out during TSR; its structure results are missing across all datasets. The paper is peer-reviewed (KDD '26). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems evaluation). - Practical: All evaluated methods run on commercially available hardware; GPU-dependent methods (VGT, TabPedia, Qwen2.5-VL, MonkeyOCR) require an A100-class GPU or API access. - Efficient: Cost spans from effectively $0 (rule-based CPU tools) to $1,200 per benchmark run (Gemini 2.5 Pro API); best open performers (MonkeyOCR 3B, Docling) run on GPU at a fraction of proprietary API cost; TabPedia exceeded allocated GPU hours by an order of magnitude. - Effective: Docling, MonkeyOCR, and Mathpix solve table extraction reliably across domains (F1^TEDS 0.78–0.90), but no method reaches 90%, indicating the problem is not solved for all heterogeneous scientific documents. - Scalable: The benchmark covers 86k pages but does not evaluate methods on larger-scale document corpora; Table-BRGM's 2k pages is too small to draw confident conclusions about Word-typeset generalization. Software artifact: Benchmark code, two new datasets, and evaluation framework released at Zenodo. Strengths - The inclusion of negative pages (table-free pages) in two new datasets is a simple but consequential design choice that exposes VLM overconfidence and over-generation — a failure mode with major practical implications for document pipelines that cannot be seen in prior all-positive benchmarks. - The formally-defined continuous P^TSR/R^TSR metrics are a genuine methodological contribution: they propagate TD quality into TSR in a principled way and unify end-to-end scoring across model families that differ in whether they output bounding boxes. - The Table-arXiv auto-annotation pipeline (instrumenting LaTeX tabular commands for TD, LaTeXML for TSR) is a clean, scalable approach that produces high-quality ground truth without manual annotation, and spans 20 years and all arXiv domains, yielding genuine heterogeneity. - The D-ECE calibration analysis surfaces a practically important and underappreciated problem: TATR's softmax confidence scores are essentially uninformative on out-of-domain data (D-ECE = 0.16), while VGT's are well-calibrated (D-ECE = 0.04), directly informing downstream system design choices. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper explicitly scopes to text-based PDFs only, excluding scanned documents. VLM contamination on benchmark datasets is acknowledged as uncontrollable. GPT-4.1 and Gemini 2.5 Pro results are partial (budget-limited). TabPedia's TSR results are incomplete due to GPU timeout. The authors acknowledge that penalizing TSR for TD errors may seem unfair to strong TSR models, but defend it as reflecting deployment reality. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No sensitivity analysis on θ_J = 0.5: all TP/FP decisions hinge on this single fixed IoU threshold, and it is plausible that model rankings change at θ_J = 0.75 (tighter localization) or θ_J = 0.25 (lenient detection). - Table-BRGM has only 256 table-positive pages out of 2,003; the headline numbers for this dataset carry large uncertainty not quantified in the paper. - No breakdown by table complexity (simple bordered vs. borderless vs. heavily merged cells): aggregate F1 hides whether methods fail uniformly or only on structurally complex tables. - General VLMs are evaluated zero-shot with a single prompt; few-shot examples or document-type-specific system prompts could substantially change VLM rankings, and the paper cannot distinguish prompt sensitivity from model capability. - XY+TATR is the authors' own novel pipeline but receives no hyperparameter ablation (XY-cut threshold, sub-image granularity), so it is unclear how sensitive its improvements on Table-BRGM are to those choices. Bottom Line A practitioner choosing a table extraction tool for scientific document pipelines gains directly actionable guidance: MonkeyOCR (3B), Docling, and Mathpix are the most robust and cost-effective options across heterogeneous data, and large general VLMs provide poor precision at high API cost — a conclusion with real economic stakes that could not be drawn from prior isolated-subtask benchmarks.
Citations
#5 — Highly Innovative (78)

Sparrow: Sparse Rollout for Stable and Efficient Long-context RL of Large Language Models

Y Zhou, R Sadhukhan, Z Sun, Z Chen, S Kundu… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Sparrow, a dynamic sparsity scheduling method that enables stable RLVR training of dense-attention LLMs using sparse-attention rollout by holding the 5th-percentile per-token acceptance rate constant at ~0.86 throughout 37K-token trajectories, plus DistillSparse, a LoRA-based on-policy distillation technique that pushes achievable sparsity further. - Why it's important: RLVR training with long chain-of-thought rollouts consumes over 70% of per-step compute in rollout generation alone, making it prohibitively expensive at scale and for agentic tasks where rollouts exceed max context length. - How it does it: By observing that sparse rollout collapse is driven by a small tail of highly misaligned tokens (not uniform degradation), the paper uses the 5th-percentile of the per-token sparse-to-dense acceptance rate as a stability indicator and adaptively increases the KV budget as sequence length grows to hold that statistic constant at a universal stability threshold. - The good: The 5th-percentile threshold of ~0.86 is empirically consistent across Qwen3-1.7B, 4B, 8B, and generalizes without re-sweeping to Qwen3-14B and coding RL, achieving 2.2×, 2.4×, and 2.0× rollout speedups while matching dense-rollout downstream performance on AIME 2024/2025/2026. - The missing: All experiments use a single model family (Qwen3 thinking) on H200 hardware with block-sparse attention only; no head-to-head comparison against the concurrent SparseRL paper (Luo et al. 2026) is included, and DistillSparse is validated only on a 1.7B proof-of-concept. - Read it or not: Essential for ML engineers scaling RLVR training or working on long-context sparse attention inference; researchers outside those areas can skip.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (85) and Novelty (80) drive the score: the tail-mismatch framing — that sparse-rollout collapse is governed by the 5th-percentile per-token acceptance rate (Beta-distributed, KS<0.04) rather than the mean — is a genuinely non-obvious insight that explains why importance/rejection sampling fail and converts a vague tradeoff into a measurable constraint. Rigor (75) is strong: a disciplined controlled sweep (4 mismatch targets × 3 model sizes to convergence) earns the τ≈0.86 universality claim before extrapolating to 14B, with code and the Vortex library released; it is held back only by single-run RL curves (no seeds/error bars) and the missing head-to-head against concurrent SparseRL. Completeness (78) and Resilience (78) are high — limitations (Qwen3-only, H200-only, DistillSparse validated only at 1.7B) are confronted directly. The work aligns closely with the user's positive RL-post-training/on-policy-distillation taste, and the speedups (2.0–2.4×, downstream within noise) are well-evidenced. Composite: 0.20*80+0.25*75+0.15*85+0.15*78+0.15*78+0.10*75 ≈ 78.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 78 Resilience 78 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation RLVR has become the dominant post-training paradigm for reasoning models, but its per-step compute is dominated by long chain-of-thought rollout generation — over 70% of per-step cost. Agentic tasks extend rollouts further, beyond maximum context length. Sparse attention reduces KV-cache access from O(L²) to O(KL), but naively applying it to generate rollouts for dense-policy training creates a stability-efficiency tradeoff: aggressive sparsity causes severe actor-policy distribution mismatch and training collapse, while conservative sparsity yields insufficient speedup. Standard actor-policy alignment methods (importance sampling, rejection sampling) were designed for much milder divergence scenarios such as staleness, where KL divergence is roughly an order of magnitude smaller than that induced by aggressive sparse rollout; those methods fail here even when boosted. Key Idea The core insight is that sparse rollout collapse is driven not by uniform distribution degradation across all tokens, but by a small fraction of highly misaligned tokens. The per-token sparse-to-dense acceptance rate distribution is highly right-skewed: the vast majority of tokens maintain acceptance rates above 0.999 even under aggressive sparsity, and the tail of misaligned tokens follows a scaled Beta distribution (KS statistic < 0.04). This makes the mean acceptance rate useless as a stability predictor — KV budgets of 2560 and 4096 give means of 0.968 and 0.977 respectively, yet one collapses and the other does not. The actionable trick is to monitor the 5th-percentile of the per-token acceptance rate as the stability indicator, and to dynamically increase the KV budget as generation length grows (since fixed-budget mismatch grows monotonically with length) to hold this tail statistic constant at τ ≈ 0.86. Methodology The approach has four interacting components. First, the mismatch metric replaces the average per-token acceptance rate α = E[min(p(x)/q(x), 1)] with its 5th percentile, which achieves a Pearson r of −0.55 with sequence length (vs. −0.1 for the mean), enabling detection of conditions that cause collapse. Second, dynamic sparsity scheduling divides the 37K-token trajectory into 19 bins of 2K tokens each and selects the minimum KV budget per bin that holds the 5th-percentile acceptance rate at a target τ, using a precomputed mapping from 18 measured sparsity configurations per model. Block-sparse attention with page size 16 is used throughout — a cost-model analysis shows page size 16 dominates the Pareto frontier for acceptance rate vs. eFLOPs, and empirical measurements confirm Top-k kernel cost is insensitive to page size for page size ≥ 16. Third, a hardware-portable eFLOP cost model for dense and block-sparse attention (parameterized by model size P, context lengths, KV budget B, GQA ratio r, and SRAM bandwidth I) identifies the minimum-cost schedule meeting the stability constraint. A controlled study sweeps τ ∈ {0.75, 0.80, 0.86, 0.92} across Qwen3-1.7B, 4B, and 8B to identify τ ≈ 0.86 as the universal threshold. Fourth, DistillSparse adds LoRA weights active only during sparse rollout, trained with L_LoRA = L_PPO_Sparse + λ·L_KL, reusing dense log-probabilities already computed for the policy update — raising the tail acceptance rate of more aggressive sparsity settings to the 0.86 threshold after ~20K warm-up training examples. Key Results The stability threshold τ ≈ 0.86–0.90 is confirmed as universal across Qwen3-1.7B, 4B, and 8B (controlled sweep) and transfers to Qwen3-14B and coding RL without re-sweeping. Rollout throughput on 4×H200: Qwen3-1.7B 2561 → 5662 tokens/s (2.21×), Qwen3-4B 1369 → 3308 tokens/s (2.41×), Qwen3-8B 1300 → 2682 tokens/s (2.06×), Qwen3-14B 1544 → 2289 tokens/s (1.48×); end-to-end RL step speedups are 1.97×, 2.11×, 1.81×, 1.35× respectively. Downstream math performance (AIME 2024/2025/2026 mean@16, pass@16; AMC mean@4) under the 0.86 schedule matches dense rollout within noise across all model sizes — no systematic gap exceeds ~2% on individual metrics. Coding RL on Qwen3-1.7B: LiveCodeBench 0.397 dense vs. 0.393 sparse, HumanEval+ 0.622 vs. 0.628, MBPP+ 0.585 vs. 0.608. A diagnostic experiment shows that over-sampling (selecting top-8 from 16 rollouts) drastically increases average reward but still cannot prevent training collapse under aggressive sparsity, isolating mismatch rather than reward quality as the cause. DistillSparse on Qwen3-1.7B achieves 2.5× rollout speedup post-warm-up vs. 2.2× baseline sparse; end-to-end speedup with DistillSparse on Qwen3-4B (16K context, 2×H200) is 1.72×. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The identification of tail (5th-percentile) acceptance rate as the governing stability statistic, its Beta distribution characterization, dynamic sparsity scheduling to hold it constant, the eFLOP cost model for schedule optimization, and DistillSparse are all contributions. Sparse attention and LoRA distillation are individually known; the novelty is in the tail-mismatch framing that explains prior method failures and in the scheduling and distillation mechanisms that operationalize the insight. Rigor & Reproducibility The controlled study design is sound: four mismatch targets × three model sizes × full training runs to convergence on 33K–53K Polaris examples at 37K generation length. The Beta distribution fit for the tail mismatch is quantitatively validated (KS < 0.04). Ablations isolate reward quality vs. mismatch as the collapse cause. Both importance sampling (TIS) and rejection sampling (Jackpot) are shown to fail, with KL divergence plots confirming orders-of-magnitude larger divergence than staleness scenarios. Code and the Vortex library are released at https://github.com/Infini-AI-Lab/Sparrow. No error bars or multiple training seeds are reported for the main stability results — single-run RL training curves are shown for each condition, which is standard but limits confidence given RL training noise. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.08446v1, June 2026); no peer review. The study is confined to the Qwen3 family and H200 hardware. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Requires H200 (or comparable) GPU hardware; benchmarked on 4×H200 for 1.7B–8B models and 32×H200 for 14B; commercially available but not commodity. - Efficient: Achieves 2.0–2.4× rollout speedup for 1.7B–8B models; DistillSparse adds ~55% overhead to the dense policy update step, which represents only ~10% of epoch time, yielding 1.72× end-to-end; larger models (14B) see lower rollout gains (1.48×) due to growing required KV budgets. - Effective: Downstream task performance is on par with dense rollout across all tested model sizes on AIME, AMC, and coding benchmarks; training reward curves track dense within <1% gap throughout training. - Scalable: The stability threshold generalizes to 14B (4× larger than the sweep range), but rollout speedups decrease monotonically with model size due to growing required KV budgets. Rollouts beyond 37K (full agentic scale) are mentioned as motivation but not empirically tested. The paper delivers a software artifact (Sparrow system integrated with the Vortex sparse attention library) in addition to the method. Strengths - The tail-mismatch framing is a clean, non-obvious insight backed by statistical characterization (Beta distribution fit, Pearson r) that concisely explains why naive sparse rollout collapses and why standard remediation techniques fail. - The controlled sweep across four mismatch targets and three model sizes, with confirmation of universality before extrapolation to 14B, is disciplined experimental design — the claim of a universal threshold is earned, not asserted. - The eFLOP cost model is hardware-portable and provides a principled basis for selecting sparsity schedules without exhaustive hardware-specific search, making the method transferable. - DistillSparse reuses computation already present in the training loop (dense log-probs for the policy update) to further relax the stability-efficiency tradeoff, adding capability at minimal incremental cost. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge that conclusions are tied to: (1) RLVR with verifiable rewards; (2) the Qwen3 model family; (3) math and coding tasks; (4) the specific sparse-attention configurations tested (block-sparse, page size ≥ 16); (5) H200 hardware. They state broader validation across multimodal RL, alternative policy optimization algorithms, and more diverse system stacks would be needed before treating the thresholds as universal design principles. They also note that page sizes < 16 require additional system optimization not yet supported in their engine. These limitations are stated directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-run training curves for all stability conditions: no multiple seeds or statistical significance testing, which is particularly problematic for RL training where reward curves are intrinsically noisy and visual convergence can be misleading. - No comparison against SparseRL (Luo et al. 2026), which is cited as concurrent work addressing the same problem; a head-to-head comparison is the obvious missing baseline. - DistillSparse validation is limited to Qwen3-1.7B with a simplified fixed-budget setting rather than full dynamic scheduling; whether it works as cleanly for 4B+ models under the full scheduling regime is untested. - The universality of τ ≈ 0.86 is empirically supported only within one model family (Qwen3 thinking). Models with different attention sparsity patterns, head configurations, or training objectives (e.g., models not from Qwen or not trained with thinking-mode RL) may have different stability thresholds. - The optimal speedup decreases with model size (14B achieves only 1.48× vs. 2.4× for 4B), a trend that raises questions about practical utility for frontier-scale models (70B+) that are not tested. Bottom Line Practitioners building or scaling RLVR training systems for long-CoT models will find directly applicable speedup recipes with solid empirical grounding, and the tail-mismatch framing is an insight worth internalizing even independent of adopting the full Sparrow system.
Recommended 2606.08446 PDF
#6 — Highly Innovative (78)

MolmoMotion: Forecasting Point Trajectories in 3D with Language Instruction (32 ▲ )

Jianing Zhang, Chenhao Zheng, Yajun Yang, Max Argus, Rustin Soraki, Winson Han, Taira Anderson, Chun-Liang Li, Shuo Liu, Jiafei Duan, Zhongzheng Ren, Jieyu Zhang, Ranjay Krishna · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MolmoMotion, a language-guided model that predicts future 3D world-frame trajectories of object-attached query points, along with MolmoMotion-1M (a 1.16M-clip pretraining corpus of action-described, object-grounded 3D point tracks) and PointMotionBench (a 742-clip human-verified benchmark spanning 111 object categories and 61 motion types). - Why it's important: Downstream systems in robotics and video generation require physically grounded, embodiment-agnostic motion representations that 2D pixel- or image-plane methods cannot provide due to entanglement with camera ego-motion and category-specific assumptions. - How it does it: An automated annotation pipeline (MolmoPoint grounding → SAM3 masking → AllTracker 2D tracking → ViPE metric 3D lifting → trajectory filtering) produces supervision at scale from unconstrained internet video, which trains a Molmo2-4B VLM backbone with either an autoregressive coordinate-text head or a flow-matching DiT head to predict millimeter-quantized anchor-relative 3D future positions. - The good: MolmoMotion initialization improves closed-loop pick-and-place success from 56.0% to 76.3% on the MolmoSpaces benchmark and substantially lowers trajectory finetuning error on DROID, providing empirical evidence that the 3D motion prior is embodiment-agnostic and practically transferable. - The missing: Closed-loop real-world robot evaluation on physical hardware is absent (acknowledged); all robot results are from simulation or held-out video prediction, leaving the central embodiment-transfer claim only partially validated. - Read it or not: Researchers and practitioners in robot learning, video generation, or 3D scene understanding should read it — it is a complete system paper with data, benchmark, model, and two downstream transfer demonstrations, all released under Apache 2.0.
Why this rank: Systems profile, and the strongest paper in the cluster. Practical Impact (78) is high: a complete, fully Apache-2.0-released stack — 1.16M-clip dataset (MolmoMotion-1M), human-verified benchmark (PointMotionBench), and trained model weights. Novelty (82) and Non-Obviousness (78) are top-tier: formalizing language-conditioned 3D point-motion forecasting as a general task, plus the genuinely clever anchor-relative millimeter-quantized serialization that repurposes a VLM's text-generation machinery for metric 3D prediction with no architectural change (ablation shows ~50% degradation without it). Completeness (78) and Rigor (72) are strong — controlled same-architecture transfer ablations and a surprising result that pixel-space video generators underperform a static baseline — though no error bars, DAVIS-split circularity, and absent closed-loop real-robot evaluation keep it below the 80s. Resembles the user's positively-rated rigorous, mechanism-focused work.
systems Novelty 82 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 78 Resilience 70 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Downstream tasks in robotics (grasping, manipulation) and video synthesis require forecasting how objects will move before motion occurs.
