Dialogue quality is assessed via LLM-as-a-judge (Gemma 4 31B-IT) on naturalness and coherence (1–3 scales), validated against human annotation of 360 dialogues. Key Results: The schema-guided agent achieves the highest average resolve rate across all models at 46.9%, versus 44.1% for OH Interactive and 32.9% for OpenHands, at the lowest average cost ($0.38 per problem). Per-model gains of the schema-guided agent over OpenHands range from 3.6 to 9.1 percentage points (3–14% relative improvement as stated in the abstract). Notably, GPT-5-mini (58.8% resolved) nearly matches GPT-5 (58.0%) at roughly one-fifth the cost ($0.24 vs. $0.86 per problem); GPT-5 mini outperforms GPT-5 on easy problems (<15 min, 70.6% vs. 67.0%) while falling behind on hard ones (1h+, 20.0% vs. 24.4%). The user simulator ablation on 50 stratified problems shows removing follow-up simulation drops resolve rates by 10–28 points depending on model, confirming multi-turn utility. Human evaluation of the user simulator finds 99.7%+ turn-level adherence on faithfulness, goal adherence, and environment adherence; self-revision raises defect-free dialogue rate from 82.5% to 97.5%. LLM-as-a-judge achieves Cohen's κ = 0.70 (naturalness) and 0.51 (coherence) against human ratings, with 100% and 84.3% system-ranking accuracy, respectively. GPT-5 shows low naturalness due to leaking system-prompt content and failing to close dialogues; Devstral fails to engage with dialogue entirely in 23–34% of sessions. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK, NEW DATASET. The primary contribution is a task formulation and evaluation dataset that closes a recognized measurement gap (interactive vs. autonomous SWE evaluation), supported by a novel user simulator design and a dialogue-theory-informed agent. The schema-guided agent is a prompt-engineering contribution (NEW METHOD at low novelty level) rather than a training-time intervention. The combination of persona-grounded simulation with self-revision for behavioral constraint enforcement is a modest but concrete methodological contribution to LLM-based user simulation. Rigor & Reproducibility: The ablation removing the user simulator (Table 3, n=50 stratified) directly validates the benchmark's multi-turn requirement. The user simulator is validated through human annotation of 360 dialogues, with two-level (turn and dialogue) reporting and an ablation of the self-revision step. LLM-as-a-judge validation follows established methodology (Kocmi et al., 2021) with both κ and rank-accuracy reporting. Code and data are publicly released at https://jlabnlp.github.io/dialogue-swe-bench/. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported for the main resolve-rate results (Table 1); stochastic variation across runs is not quantified. The user simulator is open-source; the underlying problem set is SWE-Bench Verified (MIT license). The LLM-as-a-judge uses Gemma 4 31B-IT, which is open-weights. A methodological concern: the benchmark is derived from SWE-Bench Verified, which the authors acknowledge is skewed toward Python and particular issue types, but they do not quantify what fraction of issues are bug reports vs. feature requests and whether this distribution matches real-world usage. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (runs coding agents on a live benchmark with execution-based evaluation). - Practical: The benchmark infrastructure runs on standard API-accessible LLMs; the user simulator uses a quantized open-weight 70B model that requires a GPU server but is publicly available. - Efficient: Cost per problem ranges from $0.12 (Qwen3-Coder + OH Interactive) to $1.13 (GPT-5 + OpenHands); the user simulator cost is excluded from all reported figures, which understates total evaluation cost. - Effective: The 10–28 point drop when the user simulator is ablated confirms the benchmark genuinely exercises multi-turn capabilities; resolve rates are measured by execution tests from SWE-Bench Verified, a well-established evaluation standard. - Scalable: The benchmark is fixed at 500 problems from SWE-Bench Verified; scaling to other repositories, languages, or issue types requires new data collection and is acknowledged as future work. The benchmark delivers a dataset and simulator as software artifacts. Strengths: - The ablation study directly falsifies the null hypothesis that the benchmark is solvable without dialogue: removing the user simulator after the first turn drops resolve rates by 10–28 points across all four models, a clean and decisive validation. - The user simulator's self-revision mechanism is empirically justified: it raises defect-free dialogue rates from 82.5% to 97.5% (human-annotated), making the simulator reliable enough to substitute for live human annotation in evaluation. - The finding that GPT-5 underperforms GPT-5-mini on easy SWE tasks due to dialogue failure modes (excessive questioning, failure to follow up) is a concrete, counter-intuitive result with practical implications for model selection in interactive settings. - The LLM-as-a-judge validation against human ratings on 360 dialogues is appropriately rigorous, with rank-accuracy as a secondary metric beyond κ, and the separation of naturalness (κ=0.70) from coherence (κ=0.51) is honest about where automatic evaluation is less reliable. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The benchmark inherits SWE-Bench Verified's distribution bias toward Python repositories and particular issue types, which may not cover the full range of real-world coding agent use. The authors explicitly state future work is needed to assess dialogue capability across a greater variety of repository-level task domains. These limitations are stated directly and proportionally, which is creditable. Analyst-Identified Gaps: No statistical significance testing is reported for the main resolve-rate comparisons in Table 1 (only dialogue quality has significance tests); given the variability visible in stratified results (Table 7), some of the smaller performance gaps (e.g., OH Interactive 44.1% vs. schema-guided 46.9%) may not be statistically significant. The user simulator's faithfulness is evaluated on 120 sampled dialogues, but sampling strategy is not fully described — it's unclear whether difficult or edge-case dialogues are represented. The schema-guided agent is prompt-only; no training-time intervention is explored, leaving open whether the gains are robust to prompt reformulation or model updates. The paper evaluates only two closed models (GPT-5, GPT-5-mini) and two open-weight models (Devstral, Qwen3); the open-weight models perform substantially worse (23–43% vs. 54–59% resolved), but this gap is not analyzed in depth. Finally, excluding user simulator inference cost from all run costs (Table 8) understates total evaluation cost in a potentially significant way given LLaMa 3.3 70B at 3–8 turns per dialogue across 500 problems. Bottom Line: Dialogue-SWEBench fills a real and well-motivated measurement gap — the mismatch between how coding agents are evaluated and how they are actually used — and delivers a clean, reproducible benchmark with a validated user simulator; it is the most credible existing tool for measuring interactive coding agent capability, and any researcher building or deploying conversational coding assistants should engage with it.
Search Results 2606.13995 PDF
#28 — Innovative (72)

Prefilling-dLLM: Predictive Prefilling for Long-Context Inference in Diffusion Language Models

J Xiong, Q Han, S Gong, Y Hsieh, C Wu, C Tao, C Zhao… · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PREFILLING-DLLM, a training-free prefill-decode disaggregation framework for diffusion language models (dLLMs) that partitions the input prefix into chunks, caches their KV representations once, and selects only the top-K most relevant chunks (with intra-chunk token sparsity) for each denoising step. - Why it's important: dLLMs recompute the entire prefix at every one of T denoising steps, causing O((Lp+Ld)²·T) compute that becomes prohibitive beyond 8K tokens, blocking practical long-context deployment of a model class that otherwise offers attractive parallel decoding. - How it does it: Prefix chunks are independently prefilled with intra-chunk bidirectional attention, scored for relevance via a self-information NLL and a pseudo-label logit signal, and only the top-K chunks' cached KVs (pruned to top-B query-relevant tokens) are loaded during iterative denoising; a Split-S FlexAttention kernel parallelizes the resulting non-contiguous KV access. - The good: On InfiniteBench (128K contexts), the method achieves 43.62 average accuracy on Dream-7B versus 30.32 for the strongest prior baseline (Fast-dLLM v2), while simultaneously delivering 9.1–28.0× throughput speedup at 8K–32K—meaning quality and speed both improve over all competing dLLM acceleration methods. - The missing: All InfiniteBench KV-retrieval scores are 0.00 for every method including the proposed one, exposing a complete failure on key-value association that the paper does not investigate; and Dream-7B's native 2K context requires up to 64× YaRN extrapolation for the 128K experiments, making generalization to purpose-built long-context dLLMs uncertain. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or accelerating dLLM inference systems—the combination of a training-free approach, clear complexity analysis, and a counter-intuitive result (sparse prefilling outperforms dense attention) makes this a reference contribution for the dLLM efficiency subfield; practitioners interested only in autoregressive LLMs can skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical (systems-oriented) profile. Non-Obviousness (78) and Impact (75) drive it: the counter-intuitive result that sparse prefilling improves dLLM accuracy is a real surprise, and the work ships a training-free, open-sourced framework with rigorously derived complexity reduction matching measured speedups. Rigor (70) is solid (extensive ablations, public code) but capped by single-run point estimates and a heavy YaRN ×64 extrapolation confound. Resilience (65) is pulled down by the unexplained 0.00 KV-retrieval failure and the YaRN-vs-method attribution ambiguity. Composite ≈ 0.20*72+0.25*70+0.15*78+0.15*72+0.15*65+0.10*75 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 72 Resilience 65 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Masked diffusion language models generate text by jointly denoising all token positions over T iterative steps. At each step, every token in the combined prefix+response sequence attends to every other token via bidirectional attention, so the per-step cost is O((Lp+Ld)²) and the total inference cost is O((Lp+Ld)²·T). For long-context inputs (8K–128K tokens), this recomputation of a largely static prefix becomes the dominant bottleneck, eliminating the parallel-decoding speed advantage dLLMs nominally hold over autoregressive models. Existing dLLM acceleration work (Fast-dLLM, Sparse-dLLM, dKV-Cache) reduces cost within the standard inference loop but does not separate prefix computation from iterative decoding. The prefill-decode disaggregation strategy common in AR serving (DistServe, Mooncake) has no direct analogue for dLLMs because dLLMs have no causal KV cache built during left-to-right generation. Key Idea: The key insight is empirical: response tokens in dLLMs exhibit strong locality bias when attending to the prefix—attention decays rapidly with token distance and intensifies as denoising progresses—so a small fraction of prefix chunks captures the majority of useful attention signal. This means the prefix KV cache can be computed once (not per denoising step) and only the most relevant K chunks need participate in each decode step, making the per-step cost O(Ld²+K·C) rather than O((Lp+Ld)²). Methodology: The framework operates in three stages. (1) Prefill: the prefix is split into N=⌈Lp/C⌉ non-overlapping chunks of size C. A BOS token is prepended to each chunk as a delimiter. Each chunk is independently processed with intra-chunk bidirectional self-attention concatenated with the query, yielding per-chunk KV caches in O(N·C²) total. Chunk relevance is scored by two signals: a Self-Information Score (negative average NLL of the query conditioned on the chunk) and a Pseudo-Label Score (NLL of a preliminary response estimate—pseudo-labels obtained by a partial denoising run over each chunk). The combined score s(ci)=sI(ci)+sP(ci) ranks chunks; the top-K are retained. (2) Intra-chunk sparsity: for each retained chunk, token eviction scores are computed as cumulative bidirectional attention weights between chunk tokens and query tokens; only the top-B tokens per chunk survive in the KV cache. (3) Decoding: T iterative denoising steps each compute attention only over query+response tokens concatenated with the cached top-K×top-B prefix KVs; the prefix KV is static throughout all T steps. A Split-S FlexAttention kernel decomposes the asymmetric decode attention (short query against long KV) by partitioning the KV dimension into S splits processed in parallel via log-sum-exp merging, avoiding gather of non-contiguous chunk KVs into contiguous memory. Critical design decisions: (a) full-mask KV construction (chunks cached under the same masking pattern as final decoding) substantially outperforms chunk-only or chunk-query variants in ablations; (b) pseudo-label rounds should be short (1–2 partial denoising rounds), with additional rounds degrading chunk-ranking signal; (c) the optimal chunk size and K are model-dependent and require validation-set tuning. Key Results: Evaluated on LongBench (2K–32K contexts, 16 subtasks) and InfiniteBench (>128K, 6 tasks) using Dream-7B (native 2K, extended via YaRN) and UltraLLaDA (native 128K). Baselines: Vanilla dLLM, Fast-dLLM, Fast-dLLM v2, Sparse-dLLM, dKV-Cache. On LongBench, Ours (inter+intra) scores 34.59 average on Dream-7B (vs. 28.60 Vanilla, 31.66 Fast-dLLM v2) and 37.02 on UltraLLaDA (vs. 36.00 Vanilla, 36.68 Sparse-dLLM). On InfiniteBench, Dream-7B Ours scores 43.62 vs. Fast-dLLM v2's 30.32, with Passkey at 95.42 (all other methods below 40) and Number retrieval at 70.00. Throughput on a single A800 GPU (Dream-7B, bf16, 32 generated tokens, LongBench MF-en): 9.1× at 8K, 16.1× at 16K, 28.0× at 32K, surpassing Sparse-dLLM at 16K and 32K despite Sparse-dLLM being faster at 8K. The Split-S kernel achieves 5.8–10.2× latency reduction over vanilla FlexAttention for the decode phase. A counter-intuitive finding: intra-chunk sparsity improves accuracy on Dream-7B from 22.01 (inter-only) to 34.59 (inter+intra) while reducing compute—the opposite of what sparse attention does to AR LLMs. On the lost-in-the-middle benchmark, Vanilla Dream-7B collapses at 16K and 32K; PREFILLING-DLLM maintains near-perfect exact-match across all positions up to 32K. Chunk size 256 (top-16) maintains >90% EM at 128K contexts. All methods, including the proposed one, score 0.00 on InfiniteBench KV-retrieval. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The disaggregation of prefill from iterative denoising in dLLMs is genuinely novel—no prior work applied this system-level separation to bidirectional diffusion models. The finding that intra-chunk sparsity improves rather than hurts dLLM accuracy (unlike AR sparse attention) is a substantive empirical surprise. The BOS-as-periodic-anchor finding for the lost-in-the-middle phenomenon is a novel mechanistic observation. The chunk scoring via pseudo-labels, the Split-S FlexAttention kernel, and the intra-chunk token eviction are individually combinations of known ideas (RAG retrieval, FlexAttention, attention-score token selection) adapted non-trivially to the dLLM setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are extensive and systematic: top-K, chunk size C, chunk BOS, scoring function (self-info vs. pseudo-label), number of pseudo-labels, denoising rounds, KV construction strategy, retained-token budget B, and token-retention score function are all ablated separately on MF-en for both Dream-7B and UltraLLaDA (Tables 3–6). Error bars and significance tests are absent; all reported numbers appear to be single-run point estimates. Efficiency numbers use 5 measured samples (stated in Figure 4 caption), which is thin for variance estimation. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/menik1126/Prefilling-dLLM. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.10537v1, June 2026), not yet peer-reviewed. The evaluation compares against dLLM acceleration baselines only; no comparison with AR LLMs at equivalent scale is provided. The Dream-7B experiments use YaRN with RoPE scale factor 64 for 128K contexts, which is a heavy extrapolation regime; it is unclear how much of the performance improvement over other dLLM methods is attributable to the proposed method versus the YaRN configuration used. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems-oriented, with concrete throughput measurements on real hardware). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A800 GPU in bf16; no training required; uses standard FlashInfer and FlexAttention kernels available in open-source libraries. - Efficient: Decoding latency is constant at ~0.86s regardless of context length (8K–32K), while prefilling scales linearly from 1.66s (8K) to 4.14s (32K); the one-time prefill cost is amortized across all T denoising steps, and the end-to-end 28× speedup at 32K demonstrates that the amortization is effective. - Effective: The evidence shows genuine improvement on long-context retrieval and QA tasks, with the surprising intra-chunk sparsity result confirmed across two model families; however, effectiveness on open-ended generation tasks (summarization, code completion) is modest and not clearly better than vanilla on all subtasks. - Scalable: Complexity analysis shows decoding cost is independent of prefix length; throughput scales supralinearly with context (speedup grows from 9.1× to 28×); the 128K InfiniteBench results provide some evidence at larger scale, but experiments beyond 32K for efficiency measurement and beyond two 7B-scale models are absent. The work delivers a software artifact (open-source code) and a trained-model-free inference framework applicable to any masked dLLM. Strengths: - The complexity reduction from O((Lp+Ld)²·T) to O(N·C²+(Ld²+K·C)·T) is rigorously derived, and the empirical speedups (9.1–28×) match the theoretical scaling prediction—the decoding latency stays constant while only the one-time prefill scales, exactly as the analysis predicts. - The counter-intuitive finding that sparse prefilling improves over dense attention in dLLMs (due to removal of misleading distant-token noise in bidirectional attention) is backed by results across two model architectures and both the LongBench and InfiniteBench suites. - The BOS-as-periodic-anchor analysis is carefully controlled: it distinguishes chunk BOS tokens (which absorb only 2.72% total attention and form low-magnitude ridges) from AR attention sinks (which absorb 20–60%), providing mechanistic grounding for why the approach eliminates lost-in-the-middle without introducing a different artifact. - The Split-S FlexAttention kernel addresses a concrete system bottleneck (highly asymmetric decode attention over non-contiguous cached KVs) with a principled log-sum-exp merging approach, yielding 5.8–10.2× latency reduction independently validated. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) Chunk selection is static post-prefill with no dynamic re-selection, so inaccurate pseudo-labels can cause relevant context to be permanently excluded. (2) Hyperparameters C and K require task-specific tuning, with smaller chunks improving accuracy but underutilizing GPU compute. (3) FlexAttention lacks paged memory management, forcing the prefix KV to be reloaded at every decoding step. (4) Evaluation is limited to Dream-7B and UltraLLaDA on English benchmarks; generalization to other dLLM architectures, larger scales, or multilingual settings is unverified. The authors engage with these directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - All methods, including PREFILLING-DLLM, score 0.00 on InfiniteBench KV-retrieval, which is a structured key-value lookup task. This systematic failure is neither explained nor discussed; it is unclear whether the task format, the model's bidirectional masking paradigm, or the chunk retrieval mechanism is the cause. - Dream-7B has a 2K native training length; all experiments beyond 4K use YaRN extrapolation (up to ×64 for 128K). The comparison against other dLLM acceleration methods on Dream-7B is therefore a comparison of different extrapolation strategies as much as acceleration strategies. It would be more informative to test primarily on UltraLLaDA (native 128K) for the long-context results. - Pseudo-label scoring requires a preliminary partial denoising run over each chunk independently before the main decoding, adding a non-trivial prefill overhead that is included in the latency breakdown (Figure 10) but not broken out per-component; the net benefit of pseudo-label scoring over pure self-information scoring on end-to-end latency is not reported. - No ablation evaluates what fraction of the speedup comes from avoiding prefix recomputation across T steps versus the Split-S kernel improvement; the two contributions are conflated in all end-to-end numbers. - The chunk granularity ablation (Figure 9) is conducted only on a lost-in-the-middle retrieval task; it is unclear whether the same optimal C generalizes to summarization or code completion tasks where the relevant context may be distributed rather than localized. Bottom Line: A practitioner building or optimizing dLLM inference pipelines gains something concrete here—a training-free, code-released system that simultaneously improves quality and throughput over all prior dLLM acceleration baselines, with a mechanistically grounded explanation and extensive ablations—but should be cautious about the heavy reliance on YaRN extrapolation for the most dramatic results and the unresolved 0.00 performance on KV-retrieval tasks.
Recommended 2606.10537 PDF
#29 — Innovative (72)

Show the Signal, Hide the Noise: Spectral Forcing for Pixel-Space Diffusion (15 ▲ )

Weichen Fan, Haiwen Diao, Penghao Wu, Ziwei Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Spectral Forcing (SF), a parameter-free time-conditional 2D-DCT radial low-pass operator applied to rectified-flow diffusion inputs before the patch embedder, reducing FID by 14.5% on ImageNet-256 at the JiT-700M/32 coarse-tokenization operating point. - Why it's important: Pixel-space diffusion models waste denoiser capacity on frequency-time regions where the optimal prediction is a deterministic closed-form map rather than data-distribution modeling — recovering this capacity improves generation quality at zero parameter cost and without touching the forward process, loss, or sampler. - How it does it: By analytically deriving the per-band data-to-noise contour k*(t) = (1−t)^(−2/α) from natural-image power-law spectra and rectified-flow dynamics, then imposing this boundary explicitly as a monotonically-expanding radial DCT cutoff c(t) before patchification, saturating to the identity at t=1 so the trajectory still integrates full-bandwidth velocity. - The good: The 14.5% FID and 13% IS improvement at JiT-700M/32 (60 epochs) is consistent across 5 seeds, holds across training budgets (8.0% at 120 epochs), matches a published ~145-epoch reference in 120 epochs, and transfers without modification to a text-to-image native VLM (SenseNova-U1). - The missing: Evaluation is confined to ImageNet-256 at one resolution and the JiT architecture family; no comparison against latent diffusion at equivalent parameter budget, leaving the core motivation (pixel-space competitiveness) without a direct head-to-head. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on pixel-space or native-VLM diffusion with coarse tokenization — it offers a theoretically grounded, drop-in training improvement; skip if your pipeline uses latent diffusion.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (75) is the anchor — 5-seed averaging on all headline numbers, ablations over schedules/patch-sizes/spectra/resolutions, a direct per-band MSE wedge diagnostic on a real 700M checkpoint, plus released code AND weights. Novelty (70) and the honest characterization of where the operator HURTS (rectangle data, fine tokenization) lift Completeness (72). Resilience (68) is limited by single-dataset/single-architecture scope and the unresolved theory-practice gap (linear schedule beats the analytically-derived one with only post-hoc explanations). Composite ≈ 0.20*70+0.25*75+0.15*72+0.15*72+0.15*68+0.10*70 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Pixel-space diffusion models process full-bandwidth noisy images at every timestep, but the information available to the denoiser is strongly frequency-dependent. Under rectified-flow, the noisy observation z_t = t·x + (1−t)·ε mixes signal and noise per frequency band k according to DNR(k,t) = k^(−α)/(1−t)^2 for natural images whose spectrum follows P(k) ∝ k^(−α). This defines a moving bandwidth front k*(t) = (1−t)^(−2/α): above it, noise dominates and the optimal velocity prediction collapses to a deterministic function (either −z_t/(1−t) for low t / high k, or zero for high t / high k) with no data-distribution information. A standard denoiser must discover this front internally while allocating capacity across all (t,k) — wasting computation on bands where no learning occurs. This capacity-allocation inefficiency is acutest in the coarse-patch tokenization regime (64 tokens at 256²) used by recent pixel-space transformers and native vision-language models. Key Idea: The implicit coarse-to-fine structure of diffusion training partitions (t,k) space into a signal-recovery wedge and two deterministic-baseline regions; making this boundary explicit at the input side — via a parameter-free time-conditional 2D-DCT radial low-pass that grows from an aggressive cutoff at t=0 to the identity at t=1 — frees the denoiser from discovering the front internally and redirects capacity to the signal-bearing wedge, without altering the forward process, loss, EMA, sampler, or guidance. Methodology: The method is derived in three stages. First, a 1D rectified-flow transformer (~178k params) is trained on synthetic power-law signals and a per-band MSE diagnostic log10(MSE_net/MSE_zero) is measured at convergence on a dense (k,t) grid, confirming three capacity regions: signal recovery, closed-form denoising, and predict-zero. The same diagnostic is re-run on a real JiT-700M/32 ImageNet checkpoint, recovering identical structure. Second, 2D DiT toy experiments (~3M params, h=64) sweep patch size p∈{2,4,8,16} and data spectrum (power-law, structured, rectangles) to characterize the regime: the operator is beneficial when p is large enough that patchification already aggressively bandlimits, and when high-frequency content is noise-dominated (hurts on rectangle data where edges are essential signal). Third, the Spectral Forcing operator is defined as SFt(z) = IDCT(DCT(z) ⊙ M(t)), with soft radial mask M(t)[u,v] = σ(κ·(c(t)−r(u,v))), normalized DCT radius r(u,v) = √(u²+v²)/√(2(W−1)²), and cutoff schedule c(t) = c_min + (c_max−c_min)·f(t). Fixed hyperparameters: c_min=0.05, c_max=1.0, κ=30. Five schedule shapes are ablated (linear, t², √t, cosine, analytical ∝(1−t)^(−2/α)); linear f(t)=t is the empirical default for ImageNet-256 at 64 tokens, with analytical recovering at higher resolution in toys. Critical design decisions: (i) time-dependence is analytically forced by the data endpoint — a constant c<1 cannot generate full-bandwidth images, well-justified; (ii) DCT mask rather than spatial Gaussian blur — empirically validated (Gaussian blur degrades FID from 44.68 to 67.24); (iii) linear beats analytically-derived schedule at the headline ImageNet-256 64-token setting, for which the paper provides three plausible but individually unverified explanations (finite-α spectral tail deviation, patchify double-bandlimiting, training dynamics at small c(t)). Key Results: All ImageNet FID-50k numbers are averaged over 5 random seeds against same-recipe JiT baselines. At JiT-700M/32, 64 tokens: baseline 24.19 → SF 20.68 FID at 60ep (+14.5%); 19.90 → 17.53 at 90ep (+11.9%); 16.46 → 15.15 at 120ep (+8.0%); IS improves 83.28 → 93.96 at 60ep (+13%). The 120-ep SF FID 15.15 matches a published ~145-epoch reference (FID 15.24), representing a ~17% wall-clock reduction. At JiT-130M/32 (64 tokens): +11.6% at 15ep, +3.9% at 60ep, compressing to +0.4% at 100ep and holding +1.5% at 200ep — mostly data-efficiency at this scale. At JiT-130M/16 (256 tokens): +2.2% at 60ep, within evaluator noise. Operator comparison at JiT-130M/32 (60ep): constant LP (c=0.5, no time-dep) FID 45.45 (−1.7% vs. baseline 44.68), spatial Gaussian blur 67.24 (−50.5%), Focal Frequency Loss 71.45 (−59.9%), Blurring Diffusion 60.75 (−36.0%), DCTDiff 50.12 (−12.2%) — SF is the only operator that helps. Transfer to SenseNova-U1 (native VLM, 64 tokens, stage-1 100k steps): DPG-Bench 64.35→67.85 overall, wins 9/13 subcategories; GenEval 3.87%→4.56% overall (+17.9%), single-object +2.81pp (+19.1%), colors +1.33pp (+15.6%). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The Spectral Forcing operator is a genuinely new architectural adapter: the specific combination of (a) a closed-form per-band DNR boundary derived from natural-image power-law spectra and rectified-flow dynamics, (b) a parameter-free time-conditional 2D-DCT radial mask enforcing that boundary at the input, and (c) a principled regime-characterization via per-band MSE diagnostics has not appeared in prior work. Individual components (DCT, low-pass filters, spectral analysis of diffusion, coarse-to-fine analyses) are prior art; the integration, derivation, and the explicit identification of the capacity-allocation problem as the target are the original contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: All headline ImageNet results are averaged over 5 seeds; toy experiments report mean ± std over n=3–5 seeds per resolution. Ablations are thorough: five schedule shapes, four patch sizes, three data spectra, four image resolutions, and a c_min sweep. The per-band MSE wedge diagnostic provides an independent mechanistic check on the theoretical motivation. Code is released (GitHub: WeichenFan/Spectral_Forcing) and model weights on HuggingFace. No confidence intervals are reported on per-row FID deltas in Table 3 beyond the stated 5-seed averaging, so statistical significance of the smaller gains (+0.4% at JiT-130M/32 100ep, +2.2% at fine tokenization) is ambiguous. The analytical schedule under-performs linear on the headline task despite being derived from first principles — three explanations are offered but none individually tested, leaving a gap between theory and best-performing variant. The wedge-motivation analysis uses a checkpoint trained on the same ImageNet-256 benchmark used for the headline evaluation; this is not benchmark gaming, but the theoretical framework was developed with awareness of the empirical target. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL — methods, experiments, and training runs on ImageNet-256 and SenseNova-U1. - Practical: Runs on 8 standard GPUs (A100 class, batch 128/GPU); the DCT operator is parallelizable and adds no parameters or memory. - Efficient: ~0.5% per-step compute overhead (one forward + inverse 2D-DCT at 256²); inference overhead is identical; the operator can be removed in a single line if overhead is a constraint. - Effective: Evidence is strong that SF solves the stated problem at coarse tokenization (64 tokens, 256²): consistent FID/IS improvement across training budgets, model scales, and seeds; the mechanism is verified via the wedge diagnostic on a real checkpoint. - Scalable: Tested at 256² primarily; a single 512² run (JiT-130M/32, 30ep) shows +3.4% FID, and toy resolution scaling is favorable at h≥128, but no production-scale pixel-space model beyond SenseNova-U1 early-stage training is evaluated; the 512² result is one model, one epoch budget, and not averaged over seeds. Software artifact: Code and trained model weights publicly released. Strengths: - The per-band log10(MSE_net/MSE_zero) diagnostic is an unusually direct empirical test of the wasted-capacity claim, applied both to a toy model and to a real 700M-parameter ImageNet checkpoint, providing mechanistic evidence rather than indirect ablation. - The operator is genuinely parameter-free and recipe-agnostic — it inserts before the patch embedder with no changes to loss, EMA, sampler, or CFG, making adoption a three-line code change. - The paper is honest about regime boundaries: it explicitly characterizes where the operator hurts (rectangle data, very fine tokenization) and provides controlled toy experiments that let practitioners predict whether their setting is favorable before running full training. - The transfer to SenseNova-U1 without modification demonstrates that the input-side spectral prior generalizes beyond class-conditional ImageNet to text-conditional native-VLM generation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge: (i) 0.5% per-step compute overhead (trivially removable); (ii) hyperparameters c_min, c_max, κ held fixed across all ImageNet configurations without tuning — per-configuration tuning would only widen the reported gap; (iii) evaluation restricted to ImageNet-256 and JiT architecture, with SF's recipe-agnostic nature offered as justification for not testing other backbones. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The paper positions pixel-space diffusion as a latent-space alternative, yet no comparison against latent diffusion (e.g., Stable Diffusion, FLUX) at equivalent parameter count and training budget is provided — making it unclear whether pixel-space + SF is competitive with the latent-space baseline the method is meant to challenge. - The SenseNova-U1 experiment is at stage-1 100k steps with non-EMA weights, and all four compositional GenEval categories (two-object, counting, position, color attribution) score 0% for both methods — the transfer claim is real but early-stage; whether SF retains gains at final convergence in a billion-parameter model is untested. - Three post-hoc explanations are offered for why linear beats the analytically-derived schedule on ImageNet-256 at 64 tokens, but none is individually verified with a targeted ablation; the mismatch between the principled derivation and the best-performing heuristic remains an open theoretical gap. - The 512² scaling experiment is a single run at 30 epochs with one model size, insufficient to claim the behavior holds at higher resolution across budgets. - No ablation isolates the contribution of the analytical bandwidth front derivation versus simpler linear ramp intuition — the theory predicts the qualitative schedule shape, but the linear ramp chosen empirically has no direct grounding in the derivation. Bottom Line: Any practitioner building pixel-space or native-VLM diffusion models in the coarse-tokenization regime should integrate Spectral Forcing — it is a theoretically motivated, empirically validated, zero-parameter improvement with negligible overhead and publicly released code.
Recommended 2606.15236 PDF
#30 — Innovative (72)

Memory is Reconstructed, Not Retrieved: Graph Memory for LLM Agents (54 ▲ )

Shuo Ji, Yibo Li, Bryan Hooi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MRAgent, a framework that replaces static top-k or fixed-neighborhood memory retrieval with an iterative, LLM-driven active reconstruction process over a Cue–Tag–Content heterogeneous graph, achieving substantially better accuracy and lower token cost on long-horizon conversational QA benchmarks. - Why it's important: LLM agents deployed in long-horizon interactive settings (personal assistants, decision-support systems) fail on multi-hop and temporal memory queries because existing memory systems commit all retrieval targets upfront from the query alone, with no ability to infer intermediate cues or adapt based on discovered evidence. - How it does it: Memory is organized as a three-layer heterogeneous graph (episodic events, semantic facts, topic abstractions) connected via lightweight associative tag nodes; at each reconstruction step, the LLM selects graph traversal actions, executes them, prunes irrelevant branches, and updates its evidence state — iterating until sufficient context is accumulated. - The good: Achieves a 23.3% relative improvement in LLM-Judge score over the strongest baseline (Mem0 with Gemini) on LOCOMO while reducing per-query token consumption to 118k vs. 632k for A-Mem — simultaneously more accurate and more efficient. - The missing: No full-context baseline (stuffing all dialogue history into a 1M-token context window) is included, which would clarify when structured memory compression actually beats brute-force context for the most capable frontier models. - Read it or not: Researchers building conversational agents with long interaction histories should read this; the theoretical grounding, ablation quality, and dual accuracy+efficiency gains are compelling, though practitioners should note unresolved questions about memory construction robustness and scalability beyond tested history lengths.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. This is the strongest paper in the cluster. Rigor (78) is the dominant contributor — peer-reviewed at ICML 2026 (rare in this cluster of preprints), structurally isolated ablations (CE vs. CTE vs. CTC; reasoning on/off), three runs with mean±std on the LLM-Judge metric, and public code; deductions for GPT-4o-mini judge bias with no human-eval validation and a large unexplained F1↔J divergence. Novelty (75) is high — modeling memory access as a stateful sequential decision process with the tag node as a genuine architectural primitive is a NEW METHOD, not an application. Non-Obviousness (75) is strong: tag-before-content pruning and deferring relational reasoning to query time are non-trivial design insights, and the formal expressivity-separation theorem grounds the motivation. Completeness (70) and Resilience (68) are good but capped by the missing full-context (1M-token) baseline and untested memory-construction robustness. The simultaneous accuracy (+23–33% relative) and 5.4× token-efficiency gains, plus alignment with the user's positive set (rigorous mechanism-level analysis), justify the top score. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*60 = 72.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 68 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents operating over extended interaction histories — personal assistants, tutoring systems, long-horizon decision-support — cannot retain dialogue history in context due to finite context windows, so they rely on external memory. Existing memory systems use "passive" retrieval: a static function of the query that selects top-k similar units (RAG, Mem0, LangMem) or expands fixed N-hop neighborhoods from seed nodes (A-Mem, Zep). Both paradigms fail on queries requiring intermediate inference — e.g., determining the month event X occurred before asking what person Y did that month — because the retrieval strategy is frozen at query time and cannot pivot based on what was discovered. This gap leaves multi-hop and temporally grounded queries systematically underserved. Key Idea: The core insight is to model memory access as a stateful sequential decision process rather than a one-shot function. At each step, the LLM acts as an adaptive policy that reasons over its currently accumulated evidence to select which traversal action to take next in the memory graph. The enabling structural trick is the "tag" node: a lightweight associative intermediate (a short semantic phrase like "Tournament Victory") that connects fine-grained cue keywords to episodic content. By exposing tags before full content, the LLM can evaluate and prune retrieval branches cheaply, avoiding the combinatorial explosion that would result from unconstrained N-hop graph expansion while retaining the flexibility to follow previously unplanned retrieval paths. Methodology: The system has two phases. Memory Construction: an LLM-based distillation pipeline segments raw dialogue into episodic units, extracts Cue–Tag–Episode triples (cues are named entities or attributes; tags are two-word relational phrases; episodes are event text), semantic units (stable facts about entities with aspect-level tags, e.g., cue=Nate, tag=preference, content="Nate enjoys movies and games"), and topic nodes (LLM-summarized clusters of related episodes). This yields a heterogeneous graph M = (C, V, R) with typed relation triples (cue, tag, content). Active Reconstruction: given query x, the agent initializes a reconstruction state S^(t) = (Z^(t), H^(t)) — an active candidate set and accumulated evidence — then iterates: (1) f_select (LLM) picks traversal actions from {Cue→Tag, (Cue,Tag)→Content, Content→(Cue,Tag)}; (2) the graph executes the operators to produce candidates; (3) f_route (LLM) prunes irrelevant candidates; (4) accumulated context is updated; (5) the LLM evaluates whether evidence is sufficient. Maximum 8 turns and 10 tool invocations per turn. Critical design decisions: (a) Tags as intermediates — well-justified as they let the LLM prune before expanding to expensive full content; (b) Deferring relational reasoning to retrieval rather than encoding it at construction time — justified by the resulting construction simplicity and query-specificity of evidence gathered; (c) Separating episodic and semantic memory layers — supported by ablation showing complementarity. A formal theoretical contribution (Theorem 4.1, proven via a Binary-Tree Needle-in-a-Haystack construction) shows that the active hypothesis class strictly contains the passive class for any retrieval budget T ≥ 2: active can achieve zero error on a task distribution where passive error is at least ε_Y(1 - T/2^(T-1)). Key Results: On LOCOMO (50 conversations, ~200 QA pairs each, 4 question types), MRAgent improves overall LLM-Judge score from 68.31 to 84.21 with Gemini-2.5-Flash (23.3% relative over Mem0) and from 78.61 to 88.32 with Claude-Sonnet-4.5 (12.4% relative over LangMem). Gains are largest on temporal queries (J: 80.37 vs. 61.68 for best baseline with Gemini) and open-domain queries (J: 68.75 vs. 41.67). On LongMemEval-S (500 questions, ~115K token histories), MRAgent reaches 72.95 overall J vs. 54.92 for MemoryOS (best baseline) — a ~33% relative improvement; MRAgent* (Claude retrieval + Gemini construction) reaches 86.76. Token consumption is 118k per sample vs. 632k (A-Mem), 3,268k (LangMem), 273k (MemoryOS), 245k (Mem0). Runtime is 586s vs. 533s (Mem0, best). Ablation on LOCOMO multi-hop shows: no-reasoning CTE outperforms no-reasoning CE (tags help structurally), reasoning consistently outperforms no-reasoning across all graph variants (multi-turn access is the primary driver), and removing the semantic memory layer causes measurable degradation in all conditions. Budget sensitivity shows increasing reconstruction depth T improves accuracy monotonically while increasing parallel calls K per turn saturates quickly — depth is not substitutable by breadth. Baselines are current and representative (2024–2025 systems); LLM backbones are frontier models as of 2025–2026. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The active reconstruction paradigm over a structured associative graph is genuinely novel: no prior memory system integrates LLM reasoning into the retrieval loop to adaptively select and prune traversal paths. The Cue–Tag–Content graph structure synthesizes existing ideas (knowledge graphs, cognitive science memory models, episodic/semantic distinction) into a new design tailored for active traversal, with the tag as a novel architectural primitive enabling cheap branch evaluation before expensive content access. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations isolate structural (CE vs. CTE vs. CTC) and reasoning (with/without multi-turn navigation) contributions independently — well-designed. Three independent evaluation runs with mean ± std for LLM-Judge scores are reported; F1 scores lack standard deviations. Adversarial questions excluded from LOCOMO evaluation — disclosed, but limits comparability with some prior work. GPT-4o-mini as LLM-judge introduces evaluation bias; F1 and J scores diverge substantially in some conditions (e.g., MRAgent on Gemini multi-hop: F1=43.69 but J=75.17), suggesting the judge may be lenient on partial answers — no human evaluation subset is provided to validate judge calibration. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/Ji-shuo/MRAgent. Published at ICML 2026 (peer-reviewed). Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on standard commercial LLM APIs (Gemini-2.5-Flash, Claude-Sonnet-4.5); no specialized hardware required beyond standard inference infrastructure. - Efficient: 118k tokens per LongMemEval query (5.4× fewer than A-Mem, comparable to Mem0); runtime of 586s is roughly comparable to Mem0 (533s) and far better than MemoryOS (3,135s), but still far from real-time interactive use — multi-hop queries requiring many traversal turns incur proportionally higher latency. - Effective: Ablations and multi-benchmark improvements confirm that both the structural (CTC graph) and algorithmic (active reasoning) components independently contribute; the method genuinely solves multi-hop memory queries that passive methods cannot, not merely a proxy task. - Scalable: Memory graph grows monotonically — no forgetting, consolidation, or pruning mechanism exists, which the authors acknowledge raises storage overhead in long-lived deployments. Evaluated on histories up to ~115K tokens (LongMemEval-S); behavior at year-scale agent histories is not demonstrated or estimated. The work delivers a software framework (code released), not a trained model or dataset. Strengths: - The formal expressivity separation (Theorem C.5) via a constructive Binary-Tree Needle-in-a-Haystack task is a genuine theoretical contribution that grounds the empirical motivation rather than just hand-waving at cognitive science analogies. - Simultaneous improvement in accuracy (up to 33% relative) and token efficiency (5.4× reduction) is unusual — typically more expressive retrieval costs more; the deferred relational reasoning design achieves the opposite by deferring complex joint reasoning to query time rather than encoding it eagerly at construction. - The budget sensitivity experiment (Figure 9) cleanly shows that sequential reconstruction depth is irreplaceable by parallel breadth — a practically important finding for system design. - Evidence coverage analysis per retrieval operator (Table 6) demonstrates that the system specializes different operators for different query types (temporal → `query_conversation_time`; multi-hop → `query_tag_events`, `query_topic_events`), giving mechanistic insight into why the approach works rather than treating the LLM as a black box. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: (1) Reconstruction latency grows with depth: multi-hop queries requiring many traversal steps incur substantially higher latency than single-shot retrieval. (2) Static construction with no forgetting or consolidation mechanism causes the memory graph to grow monotonically, raising storage overhead in long-lived deployments. The authors present both directly and propose adaptive construction and lightweight memory maintenance as future directions — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) No full-context baseline is included: with 1M+ token context windows now commercially available in Gemini and Claude, a comparison that simply stuffs the full 115K-token history into context would clarify how much headroom structured memory provides over brute-force context. (2) Memory construction quality is entirely untested: the entire system depends on the LLM correctly extracting cues, tags, and episodic segments, but no robustness analysis, error injection study, or quality metric for the constructed graph is presented — noisy extraction would propagate silently to retrieval failures. (3) The LLM-judge evaluation gap (F1 vs. J diverge by 30+ points in some rows) is unexplained and unsupported by human evaluation; the judge calibration deserves validation especially given the large margin claims. (4) Evaluation is confined to conversational QA benchmarks; there is no demonstration on agentic tasks where memory informs downstream tool-use or planning decisions, limiting generalizability beyond the QA setting. (5) The theoretical separation holds formally but uses a synthetic distribution (a perfect binary tree with encoded routing bits); the proof does not address whether the gap is practically large under realistic conversational memory distributions. Bottom Line: This is a solid, well-executed paper with a principled contribution — practitioners building long-horizon conversational agents gain a concrete, public-code memory architecture that is simultaneously more accurate and more token-efficient than current alternatives, with the unusual bonus of formal theoretical support; the missing full-context baseline and untested construction robustness are real gaps but do not undermine the core claim.
Recommended 2606.06036 PDF
#31 — Innovative (72)

A neurosymbolic approach to natural language formalization and verification

C An, S Bayless, S Buliani, D Cassel, B Cook… · amazon.science · ai agents regulated industries - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ARc (Automated Reasoning checks), a deployed neurosymbolic system that formalizes natural language policy documents into SMT-LIB logic offline and verifies LLM-generated answers against those formalized policies at inference time using redundant translation and SMT-based semantic equivalence checking, achieving >99% soundness. - Why it's important: LLM-based chatbots deployed in regulated industries (healthcare, finance, legal) produce legally and financially consequential incorrect answers with no available formal correctness guarantees, and existing guardrails either share the same failure modes as the LLMs they check or cannot parse natural language. - How it does it: Multiple LLMs independently translate each query-answer pair into premise-conclusion pairs expressed in the pre-built policy model's schema; an SMT solver checks whether those translations semantically agree before using any one of them for verification, flagging disagreements as low-confidence rather than risking a false approval. - The good: On ConditionalQA-logic, ARc achieves 99.4% soundness with 1.8% FPR and only 5 untranslatable errors out of 1689 decisions — the only competing approach meeting the 99% soundness threshold is LINC, which has 2× lower recall and 139× more errors. - The missing: The evaluation dataset is mechanically mutated from ConditionalQA by the authors to match ARc's own output taxonomy, which makes the benchmark structurally favorable to ARc and leaves the core claim unvalidated against naturally occurring LLM failure modes. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building policy compliance guardrails for regulated industry deployments; researchers in neurosymbolic AI will find the separation of policy formalization from query verification and the SMT-based confidence mechanism to be the substantive technical contributions.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (deployed commercial service in Amazon Bedrock Guardrails). Practical Impact (80) and Novelty (75) lead: the architectural separation of offline, human-vettable policy formalization from online per-query verification is a genuine advance over all prior single-pass neurosymbolic work, SMT-based semantic-equivalence as a cross-model confidence metric is strictly more informative than LINC's majority voting, and the system is actually deployed with real-world adoption evidence. It uniquely demonstrates >99% soundness (99.4%, 5 errors vs. LINC's 696). Rigor (65) and Resilience (60) are bounded by a circular benchmark: ConditionalQA-logic was author-mutated to match ARc's own output taxonomy, no competitor was designed for it, the dataset and code are unreleased, and 14.9% recall means 85% of valid answers are deferred with no architectural path to improvement. Completeness (70) is helped by candid acknowledgment of the dataset-representativeness limitation. Composite: 0.15*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*80 = 72.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Organizations deploying LLM-based chatbots in regulated industries face a specific problem: the chatbot answers questions about institutional policy documents (refund eligibility, employee benefits, loan qualification) and occasionally produces plausible but logically incorrect answers. The legal and financial consequences of incorrect approvals at scale — where a chatbot wrongly tells thousands of users they qualify for a benefit — are severe. Existing guardrail approaches fall into two failure modes: neural LLM-as-judge approaches share the same hallucination-prone architecture as the systems being checked and cannot provide formal guarantees; pure symbolic systems can provide guarantees but cannot interpret natural language without hand-authoring formal representations. Prior neurosymbolic work formalized both background knowledge and query together in a single pass per instance, preventing offline human vetting of the policy formalization and making each verification instance fully dependent on autoformalization quality. Key Idea The central trick is architectural: separate the one-time offline formalization of the policy document (which can be vetted, tested, and refined by domain experts using linting and symbolic test-case generation) from the per-query inference-time formalization of each answer (where redundant independent translations across k LLMs are cross-checked for semantic equivalence via SMT before any verdict is issued). Translation disagreement across LLMs triggers a conservative abstention rather than a verdict, trading recall for soundness. The insight is that policy documents, being written for human consumption, lack complex quantification and are expressible in quantifier-free SMT-LIB with non-linear arithmetic (QF_NIRA), making SMT solving both tractable and exact. Methodology The system consists of two components. The Policy Model Creator (PMC) splits an NL policy document into text spans, processes each span with an LLM that produces SMT-LIB datatypes, variables, and logical constraints, and runs a self-refinement loop to repair malformed syntax. Span-level policy units are composed into a unified model by embedding all variables, clustering them by cosine similarity, unifying intra-cluster variables, and aggregating rules while dropping syntactic duplicates. The resulting policy model is expressed in QF_NIRA (integers, reals, booleans, enumerated datatypes), restricted to this fragment because human-facing policies lack complex quantification. Three critical design decisions: (1) divide-and-conquer spanning to avoid LLM context-length and distractor degradation on large documents — well-justified by cited prior work; (2) embedding-based variable unification across spans to resolve synonymous cross-section references — justified by necessity for cross-rule reasoning; (3) restriction to QF_NIRA for tractability — appropriate for the stated domain but potentially too restrictive for policies with defeasible reasoning or temporal ordering. An offline vetting phase provides: a linter detecting unused variables, contradictory rules, and disjoint rule sets; structured-English rule views for non-expert inspection; NL-feedback-triggered LLM repair; manual test cases run through the AV; and symbolic test-case generation where an SMT solver explores the policy model's state space to produce provably-correct labeled test cases. The Answer Verifier (AV) takes a question-answer conversation and the policy model, calls k LLMs (k=3 in experiments) to independently translate the NL content into lists of premise-conclusion pairs (P, C) expressed in the policy model's schema. The confidence score of a unique (P, C) pair is the fraction of k translations that non-vacuously entail P ⇒ C (entail without rendering P contradictory). If confidence falls below the configured threshold (3/3 or 2/3), the result is TranslationAmbiguous. For high-confidence pairs, an SMT solver determines the finding: Valid (M ∧ P ⊨ C), Invalid (M ∧ P ⊨ ¬C), Satisfiable (consistent but not entailed), Impossible (M ⊨ ¬P), TooComplex, or NoTranslations. For Valid/Invalid/Impossible findings, the SMT solver extracts the relevant policy rules. For Satisfiable findings, it generates witness assignments showing scenarios where the conclusion holds and where it does not. Key Results Evaluated on ConditionalQA-logic, an author-augmented version of the ConditionalQA dev set (563 instances: 377 Valid, 112 Invalid, 56 Satisfiable, 4 Impossible, 14 NoTranslations), each evaluated 3× for 1689 total decisions. ARc (#3-ensemble, threshold=3/3) achieves 99.4% soundness, 1.8% FPR, 94.4% precision, 14.9% recall, and 5 total errors. Of 14 competitors evaluated, only LINC also meets the 99% soundness threshold (99.9%), but LINC achieves only 6.6% recall and produces 696 errors vs. ARc's 5. The LLMaJ (#3-ensemble, threshold=3/3) baseline achieves 98.2% soundness with 30 false positives vs. ARc's 10, failing the 99% threshold. LLMaJ with extended thinking (Claude Sonnet 4.5) achieves 97.1% soundness with 55.0% recall — highest recall of LLMaJ variants, lowest soundness. Redundant translation ablation (Table 1, rows 1–3): removing redundancy drops soundness from 99.4% to 98.4% and raises FPR from 1.8% to 4.8%, while raising recall from 14.9% to 28.0%. The RyanAir airline refund policy case study (Table 2): human vetting raises soundness from 96.8% to 100% and recall from 25.0% to 45.5% on a held-out test suite. The feedback loop experiment (Fig. 4): 3 rounds of ARc-grounded LLM answer revision raises the Valid answer rate from 9% to 46% across 6 real-world business policies. By finding type (Table 5, up to 5 revision rounds): Invalid → Valid success rate 83.3% (avg 2.40 iterations), Satisfiable → Valid 68.8% (avg 1.45 iterations), Impossible → 0% (requires policy model revision). PMC scales to a 274-page document producing 600 datatypes, 1968 variables, and 1467 rules with approximately linear growth. Per-query verification latency is 5–15 seconds with 3 concurrent LLM calls. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. Genuinely novel contributions: (1) the architectural separation of offline policy formalization from online query verification, enabling human vetting as a first-class workflow — no prior neurosymbolic system does this; (2) SMT-based semantic equivalence as a cross-model translation confidence metric, which is strictly more informative than LINC's majority voting over solver outcomes because it detects logical disagreement even when two translations produce the same solver outcome via different logical paths; (3) symbolic test-case generation from the policy model using an SMT solver to produce provably-correct labeled test cases for vetting. The underlying components — LLM autoformalization, SMT-LIB, Z3-style solvers — are well-established; the contribution is their integration and the separation architecture. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present: redundant translation is ablated in Table 1 (rows 1–3), human vetting is ablated in Table 2, and PMC rules fed as in-context information to LLMaJ are ablated in Table 4. The 3-repetition evaluation accounts for LLM nondeterminism but no statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported. The evaluation dataset (ConditionalQA-logic) is non-public — derived by the authors through systematic manipulation of the public ConditionalQA dev set, but the augmented version is not released. No code or model weights are released; ARc is accessible only as a commercial cloud service (Amazon Bedrock Guardrails). A notable Goodhart concern: the evaluation categories (Invalid, Satisfiable, Impossible) were defined to match ARc's output taxonomy, making the benchmark structurally aligned with ARc's capabilities by construction. The RyanAir case study partially conflates vetting data with evaluation data — some test cases were run during human vetting, with approximately one-third held out, but the exact split and potential selection bias are not analyzed. Deployability Profile: SYSTEMS (ARc is a deployed commercial service in Amazon Bedrock Guardrails, evaluated against live business policies). - Practical: Runs via LLM API calls and a cloud-hosted SMT solver with no specialized hardware; deployed as a managed AWS service accessible to any Bedrock customer. - Efficient: Three parallel LLM calls add 5–15 seconds per Q/A validation and multiply API cost by 3×; policy formalization is a one-time offline cost amortized across all future verifications against that policy; at high query volumes, the latency may be prohibitive for real-time interactive applications. - Effective: 99.4% soundness on ConditionalQA-logic and 100% soundness on the vetted RyanAir policy confirm correctness on tested domains, but the 14.9% recall means 85% of valid answers are deferred rather than confirmed, limiting net throughput of approved responses. - Scalable: PMC demonstrates approximately linear scaling with document size up to 274 pages (~137,000 tokens); per-query latency behavior at 10×–100× production query volume is not reported; the paper does not analyze how SMT solving time scales with policy model complexity (number of rules, nonlinearity). ARc delivers a deployed commercial software service, not an open artifact, reusable proof technique, or released dataset. Strengths - The separation of policy formalization (amortized, offline, human-vettable) from query verification (online, inference-time) is a genuine architectural innovation relative to all prior neurosymbolic work; it transforms policy formalization quality from a per-query risk into a one-time, improvable asset. - SMT-based semantic equivalence checking as a confidence metric is substantively stronger than LINC's outcome-level majority voting: it detects cases where two translations disagree at the formula level even when they happen to produce the same solver outcome, providing finer-grained uncertainty quantification. - The 7-category finding taxonomy with logically grounded explanations (extracted rules, witness assignments) is both auditable for human review and actionable for automated revision loops — demonstrated concretely by the 9% → 46% Valid rate improvement in 3 iterations and the 83.3% success rate for Invalid-type revisions. - The paper reports actual commercial deployment and real-world adoption evidence (education, financial AI, logistics) — rare for a systems paper, and meaningful evidence that the engineering quality and operational tradeoffs are production-grade. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly and candidly acknowledge: policies with numerical tables, cross-references, or implicit assumptions are challenging to formalize without human vetting; ambiguous pronouns and implicit temporal scoping cause incorrect formalizations; 3 LLM calls produce 5–15 second latency and increased API cost; human vetting is a significant upfront cost for large documents; and the mechanically mutated evaluation examples may not capture naturally occurring LLM failure modes. The direct acknowledgment of the dataset representativeness limitation is a credibility positive, though it also underscores that the primary evaluation may be optimistic. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Circular benchmark design: ConditionalQA-logic was designed by the authors with categories (Invalid, Satisfiable, Impossible, NoTranslations) matching ARc's output taxonomy. No competing method was designed with this taxonomy in mind, creating an asymmetric advantage. Evaluation on naturally occurring LLM failures — which the authors themselves flag as future work — would be a far more credible soundness test. - Recall deficiency has no architectural path to improvement: 14.9% recall is critically low; the iterative revision loop recovers some valid answers but does not address the underlying cause (conservative abstention on translation disagreement). The paper offers no analysis of what fraction of the 85% deferred cases are deferred due to genuine policy ambiguity vs. autoformalization failure — a distinction critical for prioritizing improvements. - QF_NIRA expressiveness may be systematically insufficient for real policies: Defeasible reasoning (broad rule with enumerated exceptions), temporal ordering of policy provisions, and cardinality constraints are common in legal and HR documents and fall outside QF_NIRA. The RyanAir case study already surfaces this failure mode (autoformalization missing the exception clause to a broad prohibition); the paper does not analyze what fraction of real-world policy complexity is inexpressible in the chosen fragment. - Human vetting cost is unquantified for planning purposes: "Several person-hours" for RyanAir's refund policy gives no generalizable estimate; practitioners adopting ARc have no basis for scoping vetting effort across policy types or lengths. - No cross-domain generalization study: The six real-world business policies are internal and unreleased; no breakdown by policy domain, document length, or formalization complexity is reported, making it impossible to assess whether the RyanAir results generalize across domains. Bottom Line A practitioner building policy compliance guardrails for regulated industry LLM deployments gains concrete value from this paper: it is the only published work demonstrating >99% soundness on a multi-category logical verification task with a fully deployed system, and the vetting toolkit and architectural separation of policy formalization from query verification represent a meaningfully better engineering approach than prior neurosymbolic work — though the recall deficit and circular benchmark limit confidence in the headline claims.
Search Results
#32 — Innovative (72)

Rethinking RAG in Long Videos: What to Retrieve and How to Use It? (32 ▲ )

Yuho Lee, Jisu Shin, Nicole Hee-Yeon Kim, Jihwan Bang, Juntae Lee, Kyuwoong Hwang, Fatih Porikli, Hwanjun Song · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces V-RAGBench, a 2,100-query benchmark of ⟨query, evidence chunk, answer⟩ triplets over hour-scale egocentric videos designed for decoupled retrieval/generation evaluation, and CARVE, a VideoRAG method that selects the optimal modality-granularity representation per retrieved chunk via parallel retrieval and multimodal cross-encoder reranking. - Why it's important: Existing VideoRAG benchmarks allow most queries to be answered without video (prior work shows >50% of common video QA samples solvable from language priors alone), masking retrieval failures and making it impossible to identify which representation choices actually matter. - How it does it: CARVE precomputes four representations per 2-minute chunk (visual/textual × frame/clip), runs four parallel nearest-neighbor retrievals into a shared candidate pool, rescores each candidate under the configuration that retrieved it using a multimodal cross-encoder, and propagates each chunk's winning configuration into the generator, yielding a context that mixes modalities chunk by chunk. - The good: CARVE achieves Recall@5 = 0.603 and nDCG@5 = 0.433 on V-RAGBench, outperforming all eight baselines, and surpasses trained query-level routers (LoRA fine-tuned on the training split) at generation without any training. - The missing: The 300-query test set is small with no confidence intervals reported; absolute generation pass rates remain low across all methods (max 0.367), and what makes retrieved evidence difficult to use in generation is not explained beyond the configuration choice. - Read it or not: Worth reading for VideoRAG and multimodal retrieval researchers — the benchmark design is rigorous and the chunk-level insight is actionable and empirically well-supported, though the egocentric-only scope limits immediate generalization.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) leads: the insight that optimal (modality, granularity) is a property of the chunk rather than the query, and the repurposing of cross-encoder reranking as a per-chunk configuration selector, is conceptually clean and empirically validated. Novelty (75) is strong — a new VideoRAG benchmark jointly enforcing three properties no existing dataset satisfies, plus a training-free method that beats LoRA-trained query-level routers. Rigor (72) is good — comprehensive ablations (Concatenation, random assignment, top-k grid, reranker-backbone robustness), human-validated benchmark quality (κ=0.63), LLM-judge validated against humans (κ=0.61) — but it is a preprint, code/weights are not announced as released, and the 300-query test set carries no confidence intervals at ~0.04 pass-rate margins. Completeness (72) and Resilience (72) are solid with directly-confronted scope limits, though the unexplained retrieval-hit/generation-fail gap (108/300) is a real open question. Practical Impact (58) is held down by the 8×H200 preprocessing stack, quadrupled storage, and no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*58 = 72.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 72 Resilience 72 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Systems that retrieve query-relevant evidence from hour-long egocentric videos — the VideoRAG setting — must decide not only which chunks to retrieve but which representation to use: visual embeddings vs. textual MLLM summaries, and at frame-level vs. clip-level temporal granularity. These two axes interact in ways that existing work leaves unexamined, because prior methods apply a single (modality, granularity) configuration uniformly across all chunks given a query. Separately, the evaluation infrastructure is broken: existing video QA benchmarks drawn from Ego4D and EgoLife were not designed for VideoRAG, lack the property that each query is uniquely tied to one evidence chunk, and allow correct answers without any retrieval, so end-to-end accuracy cannot distinguish good retrieval from good language priors. The problem belongs to wearable-device and personal-agent domains where first-person video streams are the primary knowledge source. Key Idea: The optimal (modality, granularity) configuration for a video chunk is a property of that chunk's content, not the query: a transient visual detail is best discriminated by frame-level visual embeddings, while a semantically structured activity is better captured by a clip-level textual summary. The core technical move is to re-purpose cross-encoder reranking — standard in text RAG for improving precision but unused in VideoRAG — as a mechanism for per-chunk configuration selection: each candidate is rescored only under the configuration that retrieved it, the highest-scoring configuration wins per chunk, and that winning configuration is propagated into the generator, producing a modality-interleaved context. Methodology: The pipeline has offline indexing and online inference stages. Offline, each 2-minute chunk v receives four representations: (vis, frame) = mean-pooled embeddings of 5 k-means++ keyframes; (vis, clip) = single chunk embedding from Qwen3-VL-Embedding-2B; (text, frame) = Qwen3-VL-8B summaries of each keyframe concatenated and embedded; (text, clip) = Qwen3-VL-8B summary of the full chunk embedded (capped at 512 tokens). Online Stage 1 (Parallel Candidate Pooling): a single query embedding is dot-product matched against each of four precomputed index shards; top-k (default k=5) chunks per configuration are merged into a configuration-tagged candidate pool. No additional chunk-encoding cost is incurred at inference since embeddings are precomputed. Online Stage 2 (Chunk-Adaptive Reranking): Qwen3-VL-Reranker-2B rescores each tagged candidate (v, (m,g)) under its retrieving configuration only, assigns per-chunk winning configurations via argmax over scores, and produces a final top-k list that interleaves chunks from all four configurations. Generation feeds each chunk to the generator (Qwen3-VL-8B/32B or Gemma-4-26B) under its winning representation. Three critical design decisions: (1) scoring under the retrieving configuration rather than all four — justified by the Concatenation ablation which fails to match CARVE despite using all four; (2) 2-minute chunks — empirically motivated by 2-3 minute semantic content spans in long-form video; (3) k-means++ keyframe selection to cover distinct visual states rather than uniform temporal sampling. V-RAGBench construction draws 216 hour-scale egocentric videos from Ego4D (174 videos, avg. 86 min) and EgoLife (42 videos, avg. 379 min). Event extraction uses CLIP-based temporal segmentation followed by k-means++ semantic deduplication to suppress recurring routines. Gemini-3-flash-preview generates up to 3 candidate QA triples per event chunk (67,370 initial candidates). Five post-hoc filters retain 5,907 queries (8.77%), enforcing semantic non-redundancy, visual grounding, shortcut-bias removal, empirical answerability (Gemini-2.5-flash), and evidence uniqueness (answers must not be recoverable from top-10 non-evidence chunks). Category-balanced subsampling yields 2,100 final queries (1,800 train, 300 test). Key Results: On the 300-query V-RAGBench test: CARVE retrieval achieves Recall@5 = 0.603 and nDCG@5 = 0.433, vs. strongest retrieval baselines VideoRAG-A (0.510 Recall@5) and GQR (0.340 nDCG@5). Generation pass rates: 0.357 (Qwen3-VL-8B), 0.367 (Qwen3-VL-32B), 0.320 (Gemma-4-26B), consistently leading all nine baselines across generator families. CARVE outperforms trained query-level routers (non-LLM MLP: 0.329, LLM LoRA: 0.310) without any training. Per-source: on EgoLife (up to 9-hour videos), CARVE nDCG@5 = 0.394 vs. best baseline 0.302 — larger absolute margin than on Ego4D (0.484 vs. 0.408). Modality ablation shows no single configuration dominates: best single config (vis, clip) achieves 0.507 Recall@5 vs. 0.603 for all four; cross-modality expansion is a more reliable synergy source than within-modality granularity expansion. Winning configuration distribution (Table 3) is genuinely spread: {text, frame} ~25-37%, {vis, clip} ~24-36%, {vis, frame} ~24-28%, {text, clip} ~11-15% across ranks 1-5. Latency is 4.6s per query end-to-end, competitive with visual-only configurations (5.2-7.3s) and faster than concatenation (8.2s), because visual representations are passed only for chunks that win under visual configurations. Experiments run on 8× NVIDIA H200 GPUs. This is a preprint; no peer-review is stated. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The benchmark jointly enforces three VideoRAG-specific properties (non-recurring evidence, visual grounding, evidence localization) on hour-scale egocentric video — no existing dataset satisfies all three together. The chunk-level configuration selection via cross-encoder reranking is a genuine methodological novelty within VideoRAG; reranking is established in text RAG but has not previously been applied as a configuration-selection mechanism for multimodal video chunks. The specific insight that modality-granularity should be decided per chunk rather than per query is both conceptually clean and empirically validated. The individual components (MLLM embeddings, cross-encoders, parallel retrieval) are existing building blocks assembled in a novel way. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are comprehensive: modality-granularity grid (Table 2), reranking strategy variants including random assignment and concatenation (Table 4), trained router comparison (Table 5), effect of top-k across k=1,2,4,8,10 (Figure 4 heatmaps), per-source performance breakdown (Table 16), and reranker backbone robustness with LamRA-Rank-7B (Table 18). Retrieval metrics carry no run-to-run variance (deterministic). Generation evaluation uses LLM-as-a-judge (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B) validated by human annotators: Cohen's κ = 0.61 with 2.2% identified error rate across 600 sampled instances. Benchmark quality is validated with human annotation: 96.9% answerability rate on 577 sampled pairs, κ = 0.63. Annotators required first-author publications at top-tier AI venues and CEFR C2 English proficiency. Code, data, and trained model weights are not announced as publicly released. The 300-query test set is small: at pass rate differences of ~0.04 between CARVE and top baselines, no significance tests are reported. The benchmark construction relies on GPT-5.2-chat and Gemini-2.5-flash for filtering, introducing model-dependent artifacts in which queries survive. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Requires 8× NVIDIA H200 (141GB) GPUs for offline indexing with Qwen3-VL-8B as captioner; online inference at 4.6s/query is feasible, but the preprocessing stack is not accessible on commodity hardware. - Efficient: Offline preprocessing requires generating MLLM summaries for all chunks at 4 representations each; this cost scales linearly with corpus size and is not quantified in wall-clock time or storage requirements; online query cost is modest since chunk embeddings are precomputed. - Effective: Evidence of effectiveness is solid on V-RAGBench across three generator families; Recall@5 improves 18% over the strongest baseline and generation pass rate improves ~13% relative, but maximum pass rate of 0.367 indicates that successful retrieval frequently fails to produce correct answers (108 retrieval-hit but generation-fail cases out of ~300 queries at 8B scale). - Scalable: Tested on videos up to ~9 hours and corpora of 216 videos; performance gap widens on EgoLife (longer videos), suggesting CARVE scales well within this range, but no evaluation at 10×–100× corpus scale is provided and offline storage for 4 representations quadruples relative to single-configuration baselines. The paper delivers a benchmark dataset and an inference-time method but no released software artifact, library, or pretrained model is announced.
