Whether CRUMB + fine-tuning outperforms MICP + fine-tuning is unknown. - No public code release is stated; the JPMorganChase legal disclaimer raises uncertainty about artifact availability, which limits reproducibility for practitioners. - K=20 is used as a fixed default regardless of dataset size or T; the paper ablates K but does not provide principled guidance for setting K relative to T or N. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying PFNs on tabular datasets with N > 10,000 will find CRUMB directly useful: it delivers kNN-quality accuracy at K/T of the forward-pass count, requires no retraining, integrates with any PFN checkpoint, and provides a mechanistically grounded covariate-drift bonus — backed by rigorous ablations that genuinely establish what drives the gain.
Citations 2606.11473 PDF
#44 — Innovative (70)

AgentCyberRange: Benchmarking Frontier AI Systems in Realistic Cyber Ranges

F Liu, J Dai, Y Fan, W Mai, Z Li, B Chen, J Zhang, Z Lou… · arxiv.org · generating environments for agent testing in enterprises - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AGENTCYBERRANGE, an open benchmark comprising 110 web vulnerabilities across 15 real applications and 8 enterprise-like cyber ranges with 156 internal hosts, together with CAGE, a scalable evaluation toolchain for measuring the autonomous end-to-end cyberattack capability of frontier AI systems. - Why it's important: Existing AI cybersecurity benchmarks evaluate isolated skills — CTF flag capture, single-vulnerability reproduction, exploit generation — and cannot reveal when AI systems cross the threshold from toy capability into realistic multi-stage intrusion, which is the threat model that actually matters for security planning. - How it does it: The benchmark preserves the operational chain of real intrusions (external discovery → web exploitation → post-exploitation across segmented networks) and verifies success via runtime evidence (canary database reads with URL matching, marker files under /tmp or /root) rather than self-reported claims or flag strings. - The good: GPT-5.5 discovers a genuine zero-day arbitrary file write in ComfyUI (115K GitHub stars) and mutates payloads to bypass Windows Defender mid-run, demonstrating that frontier agents already produce operational risk that extends beyond the benchmark's own scope. - The missing: Only 8 post-exploitation ranges yield a per-range success rate computed over a very small denominator, and GPT-5.5 runs at "xhigh" reasoning effort while other models use default or "max" settings — the effort mismatch conflates model capability with inference-time compute, making cross-system comparisons ambiguous. - Read it or not: Essential for AI safety researchers tracking offensive AI capability emergence and for practitioners designing cyber-range evaluation components for model release pipelines; the benchmark and toolchain are open-sourced.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (open benchmark infrastructure). Practical Impact (85) drives the composite — the first open, reproducible, multi-host dual-track (web + post-exploitation) cyber-range with zero-day coverage and evidence-based verification, fully open-sourced (code + HuggingFace dataset), is a genuine community contribution prior closed benchmarks (TLO) lacked. Novelty (75) is strong: NEW DATASET + NEW SYSTEM at 266 instances vs. 13–104 for predecessors, plus a real ComfyUI zero-day discovered mid-evaluation. Rigor (55) is the limiting dimension: self-constructed and self-evaluated by one team, post-exploitation rates over only 8 ranges, no error bars, and the uncontrolled reasoning-effort mismatch (GPT-5.5 'xhigh' vs. others 'max'/default) conflates model capability with inference compute. Completeness (70) and Resilience (60) are helped by candid limitation acknowledgment but hurt by dropping 12 Claude refusal trials rather than scoring them. Composite: 0.15*75 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*55 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.30*85 = 70.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 70 Resilience 60 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Frontier AI systems — general-purpose coding agents such as Codex, Claude Code, and Qwen — have demonstrated nontrivial ability to detect and exploit single vulnerabilities in real software. The open question is whether these isolated skills combine into the operational chain of a real intrusion: discovering hidden attack surfaces, gaining an initial foothold, performing internal reconnaissance, chaining credential reuse, and moving laterally across segmented networks. This matters because AI safety and cybersecurity risk assessments need a measurable, reproducible signal for when and how quickly that threshold is crossed. Prior public benchmarks (CTF benchmarks, CVE-Bench, ExploitGym, CyberGym, PentestGPT, XBOW, AISI TLO) either evaluate isolated single-vulnerability tasks, lack post-exploitation coverage, use small or closed-range infrastructure, exclude zero-day content, or are measurement studies rather than open reproducible benchmarks. The result is a systematic blind spot: frontier systems are not routinely evaluated under conditions that reflect the end-to-end structure of real attacks. Key Idea The core insight is that realistic cyber attack evaluation requires preserving the full operational chain — external web attack surface discovery, exploitation to obtain a foothold, and internal post-exploitation across multi-host enterprise-like networks with defensive pressure — and that verification must be evidence-based (observable runtime effects, not self-report) to prevent false positives. The secondary technical contribution is the CAGE toolchain's agent adapter abstraction, which decouples heterogeneous CLI-driven agent harnesses (Codex, Claude Code, Qwen Code, Kimi Code) from benchmark logic so they run under matched prompts and step budgets through a single interface. Methodology AGENTCYBERRANGE consists of two tracks. WebExploitBench deploys 15 real web applications as isolated Docker containers covering 110 vulnerabilities (18 zero-day, 56 one-day, 36 synthetic) across 17 OWASP-aligned categories (SQL injection 19, XSS 14, broken access control 13, SSRF 12, etc.) in PHP, Java, and Python applications. The synthetic vulnerabilities are placed behind realistic application state (login, order placement) to require genuine exploration rather than payload-to-visible-endpoint matching. PostExploitBench provides 8 enterprise-like cyber ranges with 156 Docker-networked hosts spanning 3–7 isolated /24 subnets; 43 hosts participate in intended attack chains while 113 are decoys. Ranges cover 12 post-exploitation technique categories: lateral movement, privilege escalation, credential discovery/reuse, file/config discovery, internal reconnaissance, service pivoting, database abuse, CI/repository access, SMB pivoting, persistence, and defense evasion. Some ranges include a simulated defender agent and AV/EDR-like software. Three difficulty levels provide increasing hint disclosure: Level-0 (target URL or entry IP only), Level-1 (+ vulnerable URL paths or network topology), Level-2 (+ vulnerability type or CVE identifiers and credentials). This scaffolding separates exploration capability from exploitation capability. Agents are evaluated with a fixed step budget (150 steps for web, 500 for post-exploitation) and a two-hour timeout, measured under Pass@1, Pass@3(Avg), and Pass@3(Max) across three independent runs. The CAGE verifier checks exploitation effects at runtime: for web tasks it validates the PoC triggers the required observable effect (canary read, XSS alert, SSRF callback) and matches the URL to the reference endpoint to distinguish co-located vulnerabilities of the same type — an improvement over prior benchmarks that only check effect type. For post-exploitation, agents must write marker files to /tmp (user compromise) or /root (root compromise) on each host, which CAGE polls periodically. Six AI systems were evaluated, each paired with its native harness where available: Codex+GPT-5.5 (xhigh effort), Claude Code+Opus-4.7 (max), Qwen Code+Qwen-3.7-Max, Kimi Code+Kimi-2.6, and Claude Code as scaffold for DeepSeek-V4-Pro and GLM-5.1. Key Results On web exploitation at Level-0, GPT-5.5 leads with 19.09% Pass@1, 16.06% Pass@3(Avg), 28.18% Pass@3(Max) across all 110 vulnerabilities — discovering 31 unique vulnerabilities across 13 classes and 12 applications in its best run. Claude-Opus-4.7 reaches 14.55% Pass@3(Avg); Qwen-3.7-Max 12.42%; GLM-5.1 and DeepSeek-V4-Pro both 8.18%; Kimi-2.6 3.03%. Vulnerability depth is the primary failure mode: detection rate drops from 35% at depth-2 (2 application interactions needed to reach the endpoint) to 11% at depth-6. Adding vulnerable URL hints (Level-1) increases GPT-5.5's Pass@3(Avg) from 16.06% to 32.12% (+16 pp), confirming exploration — not exploitation — is the dominant bottleneck. On post-exploitation at Level-0, GPT-5.5 achieves 31.71% Pass@1, 31.71% Pass@3(Avg), 43.90% Pass@3(Max) across 8 ranges. Claude-Opus-4.7 reaches 15.04% Pass@3(Avg) but had 12 trials excluded due to safety refusals. At Level-2 with full CVE and credential hints, GPT-5.5 reaches 46.34% Pass@3(Avg) and 68.29% Pass@3(Max). Run-to-run variance is high across all agents: even GPT-5.5 finds only 17/30 discovered vulnerabilities consistently across runs. The detailed attack trajectory for GPT-5.5 in Range-1 shows successful chaining of AJ-Report unauth RCE, H2 database credential extraction, reverse tunnel establishment, DedeCMS file upload exploit, PHP disable_functions bypass via FFI, SUID privilege escalation, and Spring CVE-2024-38816 path traversal across three segmented networks — though the range is not fully compromised due to failure on the final ActiveMQ pivot. Out-of-benchmark, GPT-5.5 discovers a novel arbitrary file write zero-day in ComfyUI (attacker-controlled workflow writes outside intended output directory). Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the first open, multi-host, dual-track (web + post-exploitation) cyber-range benchmark with zero-day coverage and evidence-based verification at this scale (266 total benchmark instances vs. 13–104 for prior open benchmarks). The CAGE toolchain's adapter-based design for heterogeneous CLI agent evaluation is a practical engineering contribution. The evaluation methodology (three difficulty levels as diagnostic decomposition, behavioral trajectory analysis mapped to MITRE ATT&CK) is a competent application of established evaluation practice rather than a conceptual advance. Rigor & Reproducibility Pass@3 provides some variance measurement, and the run-to-run breakdown (Figure 7) honestly quantifies instability. Behavioral trajectory logging via in-container model proxy is a credibility-positive design. However, several concerns apply. The benchmark is designed and evaluated by the same team, with no independent verification of vulnerability correctness or benchmark integrity. Post-exploitation performance statistics are computed over only 8 ranges, making per-range success rates statistically fragile. GPT-5.5 runs at "xhigh" reasoning effort while other systems run at "max" or default; this is not controlled, so performance differences conflate model quality with inference-time compute budget. The 12 excluded Claude-Opus-4.7 refusal trials are dropped rather than treated as failures, which inflates Claude's reported success rate. No error bars or significance tests are reported for the aggregate rates. Code and benchmark setups are open-sourced at github.com/AgentCyberRange; dataset on HuggingFace. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.14295, June 2026); not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — the paper builds and evaluates a deployed benchmark infrastructure. - Practical: CAGE runs on a single Ubuntu 22.04 server (Intel Xeon 6982P-C, 247 GiB RAM, 2 TB NVMe) with Docker Engine; web application containers and cyber-range networks are managed by Docker Compose, and the benchmark is open-sourced, making it accessible to organizations with comparable hardware. - Efficient: Per-trial compute is dominated by LLM API costs ($7–40M tokens per full evaluation run at up to $37 M tokens for GPT-5.5 post-exploitation) and container orchestration overhead; the cyber ranges with 20+ containers per range require non-trivial storage and RAM per concurrent trial, which constrains parallelism. - Effective: The benchmark successfully differentiates six frontier systems on both tracks, correlates hint level with performance in the expected direction, and produces behavioral trajectories that explain failure modes — it measures what it claims. - Scalable: The benchmark is currently limited to 8 post-exploitation ranges and 15 web applications; the paper does not evaluate whether CAGE's container orchestration scales to dozens of concurrent ranges or how range construction effort scales, and these are not claimed. The work delivers a software artifact (benchmark setups, CAGE pipeline, dataset). Strengths - The operational chain realism is the benchmark's defining strength: requiring exploration → exploitation → foothold → lateral movement in a single evaluation prevents the isolated-skill inflation that afflicts CTF and single-CVE benchmarks, and the difficulty-level decomposition provides actionable diagnostic granularity about where each agent fails. - The two-layer web verification (effect check + URL matching against reference PoC) is a concrete methodological improvement over prior work that credits any same-type exploit regardless of endpoint, preventing false positives on co-located vulnerabilities. - Discovery of a genuine ComfyUI zero-day and AV evasion via payload mutation as a byproduct of benchmark evaluation is a compelling, concrete safety signal that substantially strengthens the paper's risk narrative beyond stated benchmark metrics. - Open-sourcing the full infrastructure (benchmark setups, CAGE pipeline, dataset) at this scale is a genuine community contribution; prior multi-host pentest benchmarks (TLO) were closed. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that AGENTCYBERRANGE covers only web exploitation and post-exploitation, not phishing, Windows domain attacks, cloud IAM abuse, supply-chain compromise, or social engineering. They acknowledge that longer budgets, stronger tools, or system-specific prompting could increase measured success rates, while API instability, safety refusals, and execution failures can reduce them. They note that runtime-evidence verifiers reduce false positives but may undercount partial progress or alternative valid attack paths, requiring manual validation for out-of-benchmark findings. The ethical limitations section notes responsible disclosure was completed before including zero-day content. The authors confront these limits directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The reasoning-effort mismatch is the most significant unacknowledged confound: GPT-5.5 runs at "xhigh" effort while Claude-Opus-4.7 runs at "max" and other models at default. Since the paper's central claim is a comparison of frontier AI systems' offensive capability, this conflation of inference budget with model capability undermines the ranking's validity. A controlled ablation over reasoning effort levels for at least the top two systems is absent. - The post-exploitation benchmark has only 8 ranges, so Pass@3(Avg) rates are computed over 8 data points. A 1-range difference in success rate corresponds to ~12.5 percentage points — the granularity is too coarse for reliable conclusions about relative performance among mid-tier agents. - Safety refusals from Claude-Opus-4.7 (12 trials excluded) are treated as missing data rather than as a meaningful behavior: refusal is itself a relevant property of a system being evaluated for offensive risk, and its omission from the reported rates mischaracterizes what Claude actually does when asked to conduct realistic cyberattacks. - The benchmark is self-constructed and self-evaluated by the same team; no independent security experts validated which synthetic vulnerabilities are realistic or whether the intended attack paths are the only valid paths. This is a potential Goodhart's-Law concern for the post-exploitation verifier, which credits only marker-file writing rather than generalized host control. - The paper does not analyze API cost versus performance trade-offs systematically — GPT-5.5 costs ~$37M tokens per post-exploitation run, roughly 1.7–2× the cost of Claude or DeepSeek, which matters for any organization considering whether to use these systems for authorized red-teaming. Bottom Line A practitioner in AI security or red-teaming gains genuinely useful capability baselines from this paper — the benchmark infrastructure is the first open, reproducible, multi-host system at this scope, the results honestly quantify how far current agents are from reliable end-to-end compromise, and the zero-day discovery case is a credible operational risk signal — but the reasoning-effort confound and 8-range post-exploitation denominator mean the system-level ranking should be treated as indicative rather than definitive.
Search Results 2606.14295 PDF
#45 — Innovative (69)

Coordinated Scheduling for MoE LLM Serving

Y Sun, Z Zhang, J Jiang, G Haffari, M Xu, F Liu… · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Gimbal, a coordinated cross-level scheduling system for MoE LLM serving that couples fine-grained DP-engine pressure signals (KV-cache usage, remaining prefill tokens, queue backlog, backend MoE expert pressure) with an online source-DP-to-expert routing matrix to drive both request dispatching and dynamic expert placement. - Why it's important: Serving MoE models under real, heterogeneous workloads causes silent imbalance that request-count-based dispatch cannot detect—a 2K-token request consumes 10× the KV cache and generates different all-to-all communication than a 200-token request even when both count as one request. - How it does it: A layer-wise source-DP-to-expert activation matrix (A_{l,s,e}), piggybacked on the normal MoE dispatch path with a fused Triton kernel, is combined with aggregate expert load and migration cost in a greedy heuristic calibrated against an offline MINLP solution to place experts dynamically; backend expert pressure is fed back to the frontend dispatcher to close the loop. - The good: The ablation shows that the coordination mechanism alone—feeding MoE pressure back to the DP scheduler—adds 16.5% TTFT and 6.5% TPOT reduction beyond simply enabling both DP and EP optimizations independently, confirming the cross-level design is the core contribution. - The missing: The entire evaluation uses a single 4-GPU node and one model (Qwen3-30B-A3B); multi-node performance is claimed but not tested, and profiling-window sensitivity is unanalyzed. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers and engineers deploying MoE models (DeepSeek, Qwen) on multi-GPU DP+EP setups; the coordinated scheduling framework and source-aware expert placement are immediately actionable on standard vLLM stacks.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Strongest on Rigor (68) for this cluster — five ablation configs cleanly isolate the cross-level coordination gain (16.5% TTFT / 6.5% TPOT beyond co-enabling DP+EP), three seeds across five distributions and five rates, and reported error bars. Novelty (72) credits the source-DP-to-expert matrix as an online statistic and the dispatch↔placement feedback loop. The composite is held to Innovative rather than higher by single-model/single-node scope (Qwen3-30B-A3B, one 4-GPU node), an oracle-strengthened Sem-MoE baseline, no profiling-window sensitivity, and no code release. Composite ≈ 0.15*72+0.15*68+0.10*72+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.30*70 = 69.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Serving MoE LLMs in production combines data parallelism (DP, replicating engines), expert parallelism (EP, distributing experts across GPUs), and often tensor parallelism (TP). Two failure modes arise simultaneously: (1) DP-engine imbalance, because real workloads exhibit heavy-tailed input-length distributions (both BurstGPT and LMSYS-Chat-1M GPT-4 traces show long-tail requests up to 6K tokens), and request count treats all requests as equivalent even though a 2K-token request incurs 187.5 MiB KV cache and 2× higher TTFT vs. a 200-token request; and (2) expert hotspots, because sparse routing concentrates token traffic on a small subset of experts per layer. Current systems address these independently: frontend routers use Round-Robin or request-count dispatch, while backend expert balancers (EPLB-style) use aggregate activation counts. Neither layer knows about the other. The coupling is real but ignored: the DP engine that receives a request determines the source of all-to-all communication in MoE layers, so expert placement relative to that source directly affects cross-DP communication cost. Meanwhile, high expert load on EP ranks co-located with a DP engine reduces that engine's effective capacity, a signal the frontend scheduler never sees. Key Idea The core insight is that the DP-source assignment and expert placement are entangled in two directions: the DP source determines which tokens enter the all-to-all path, so optimal expert placement is source-dependent rather than load-balanced in aggregate; and backend MoE pressure is a meaningful signal for frontend dispatch, not just an EP-internal concern. The enabling trick is the layer-wise source-DP-to-expert matrix A_{l,s,e}, a lightweight online statistic (collected by fusing source counting into the existing MoE dispatch path) that disambiguates which DP source generates traffic to which expert—information not present in the aggregate B_{l,e} matrix used by prior EPLB-style systems. This matrix allows expert placement to simultaneously balance load, reduce source-dependent communication, and limit migration overhead, while the pressure feedback closes the loop between placement and dispatch. Methodology Gimbal has two cooperating components. The fine-grained DP-engine scheduler (Section 4) operates in two levels. At the inter-engine level (Algorithm 1), each backend engine asynchronously reports a compact scalar trace: remaining prefill tokens (not original prompt length, since chunked prefill may split work), waiting prefill tokens, KV-cache usage, and backend MoE expert pressure (normalized token-equivalent load from EP ranks co-located with that DP engine). The Engine Select Module reads the latest available trace and applies a two-tier decision: if any engine's KV usage exceeds 90% and the cross-engine gap exceeds 10%, route to the lowest-KV engine (hard memory protection); otherwise compute a composite pressure score (prefill_rem + wait + comp_estimate + P_kv(kv) + P_moe(moe)) and select the minimum, falling back to round-robin if scores are within a noise threshold. A compensation term prevents the scheduler from repeatedly assigning to the same engine before traces refresh. At the intra-engine level (Algorithm 2), a lightweight SJF ordering with aging schedules shorter prefill requests first, promoting requests waiting longer than 5 seconds (derived from P99 TTFT measurements) to prevent starvation. The source-aware expert load balancer (Section 5) collects two statistics piggybacked on the normal MoE dispatch path: aggregate activation count B_{l,e} (reused from EPLB's existing path) and the source-aware matrix A_{l,s,e}. The placement objective is formally a MINLP: minimize C_load (sum of squared deviations of rank load from layer average) + C_comm (sum over all layers, sources, experts, ranks of A_{l,s,e} × D_{s,g} × x_{l,e,g}, where D_{s,g} is the communication cost between source s and rank g) + C_mig (migration cost for moves from current placement). Solving this MINLP takes ~15s for a 48-layer model, so it is used offline as a calibration reference only. The online heuristic ranks experts by a hotness signal derived from both B and A, then greedily assigns each expert to the feasible rank minimizing S_{l,e,g} = α·C_comm_{l,e,g} + β·C_load_{l,e,g} + γ·C_mig_{l,e,g}. Weights are calibrated against the MINLP: (α, β, γ) = (1.0, 0.0025, 1.0) achieves >80% placement-decision match and keeps communication within 0.6% of the offline reference. Three design decisions are well-justified: (1) KV-cache priority is correct because KV exhaustion triggers preemption cascades; (2) the compensation term is necessary because asynchronous traces can be stale; (3) the MINLP calibration is well-motivated by showing that uncalibrated greedy overreacts to transient load, degrading TTFT by 10.8% relative. Key Results All experiments run on a single node with 4 NVIDIA H100 80GB SXM5 GPUs (NVLink), DP=2, TP=2, EP=4, evaluating Qwen3-30B-A3B on BurstGPT traces reshaped into 5 request-length distributions (Random, Central, Descending, Two-end, Average) at 2–4 RPS, 1000 requests each, 3 random seeds. Baselines are vLLM (request-count DP + aggregate EPLB), MoETuner (offline affinity-based static placement), and Sem-MoE (static ILP placement + oracle routing, since the actual prediction model was unavailable). Versus vLLM: average TTFT −42.9%, average TPOT −33.3%, P99 TTFT −44.3%, end-to-end latency −32.0%, throughput at RPS=4 +3.0% (1.26%–4.20% across distributions). Improvements grow with load: TTFT reduction goes from 33.1% at RPS=2 to 48.3% at RPS=4. Versus MoETuner: TTFT −47.0%, TPOT −36.2%. Versus Sem-MoE (oracle): TTFT −34.7%, TPOT −29.5%. Ablation establishes component contributions: Gimbal-DP alone yields TTFT −25.1%/TPOT −13.4%; Gimbal-EP alone yields TTFT −26.2%/TPOT −22.7%; their non-collaborative union yields TTFT −29.8%/TPOT −27.3%; the full coordinated design achieves TTFT −41.4%/TPOT −32.0%—a 16.5%/6.5% additional gain from the feedback loop alone. Runtime traces confirm that vLLM keeps ~91 and ~90 in-flight requests per engine at RPS=4 with ~26–27% KV usage; Gimbal reduces these to ~58 and ~57 with ~17% KV usage and cuts the cross-engine prompt-throughput gap from 1,485 to 768 tokens/s. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The layer-wise source-DP-to-expert matrix as an online serving statistic and the coordinated feedback loop feeding backend MoE pressure to the frontend DP scheduler are genuinely novel contributions; fine-grained pressure-based scheduling and expert load balancing are individually known ideas applied to the MoE serving stack. The specific combination—source-aware dynamic placement jointly optimized with migration stability and coordinated with dispatch—has not appeared in prior work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: five ablation configurations cleanly isolate DP, EP, their non-collaborative union, and the full coordinated system. Three random seeds are reported across five workload distributions and five request rates—adequate for a systems paper. Error bars appear in Figure 10 (throughput) and Figure 11 (normalized latency). No code or model weights are publicly released (not mentioned). One methodological concern: the Sem-MoE comparison uses an oracle variant with perfect foreknowledge of request-to-engine assignments, because the actual prediction model was unavailable. This makes Sem-MoE artificially strong on the routing side, so Gimbal's 34.7% TTFT gain over oracle Sem-MoE is conservative but the real-world gap vs. imperfect Sem-MoE is unknown. MoeTuner's placement logic is reimplemented by the authors rather than using the original codebase, introducing potential reimplementation error. Evaluation covers only one model and one hardware node. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on standard H100 hardware with NVLink; requires vLLM as base; the Triton kernel and ~1.7K-line Python/Triton addition are the only implementation prerequisites. The fused collection path achieves sub-vLLM latency overhead (Figure 13 shows the optimized path is below the baseline no-collection bar). - Efficient: The online heuristic runs at end of each profiling window, not on the critical path; the MINLP (15s) is offline only. Runtime trace reporting adds a compact scalar payload to vLLM's existing asynchronous statistics channel. Three successive kernel optimizations reduce source-aware matrix collection to below-baseline cost. - Effective: Yes—end-to-end TTFT and TPOT reductions are consistent across five workload distributions, three seeds, and five request rates, with the ablation confirming the mechanism (not just scheduling randomness) drives the gains. Throughput improvement is small (3%) but does not decrease, showing no throughput-latency tradeoff. - Scalable: Tested on a single 4-GPU node at 2–4 RPS; multi-node behavior is explicitly uncharacterized. Expert migration cost (1.08s first rearrangement, 0.72s subsequent) is measured at this scale but is not analyzed under larger EP groups, more layers, or larger models. The paper claims benefits should grow at scale but provides no evidence. Delivers a prototype system (vLLM fork); no open release stated. Strengths - The coordinated feedback loop is cleanly motivated by three independent empirical observations (length heterogeneity, expert skew, engine-MoE coupling) before any system is proposed, making the design rationale tight. - The ablation quantifies the specific gain from cross-level coordination (16.5% TTFT, 6.5% TPOT) over simply co-enabling two independent optimizations—this is the strongest evidence that the architectural insight is load-bearing, not just the sum of its parts. - The MINLP-calibration approach converts an intractable formal objective into a calibrated practical heuristic without losing fidelity: >80% placement match and <0.6% communication gap vs. offline optimum. - The profiling overhead engineering (fast path + A2A-assisted + fused Triton kernel) is non-trivial and demonstrates production-level attention to inference-critical-path costs. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) Gimbal was evaluated on a single-node 4-GPU deployment; multi-node extension is left to future work, with the note that cross-level coordination should provide greater benefit at larger scale. (2) Only Qwen3-30B-A3B was evaluated; the authors claim the approach is not Qwen-specific since it relies on signals (KV usage, prefill tokens, expert activation counts) that are commonly exposed, but provide no cross-model evidence. (3) Future work includes more adaptive placement policies. The authors confront these limitations directly at the end of Section 9. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Sem-MoE oracle comparison is a known limitation acknowledged implicitly by the authors, but its implications are understated: Sem-MoE with real (imperfect) predictions would likely perform worse than the oracle, making Gimbal's 34.7% TTFT advantage a lower bound on the real gap—the paper should state this explicitly. - Only a single MoE model is tested. MoE routing patterns vary substantially between architectures (e.g., DeepSeek-V3's fine-grained MoE with 256 routed experts vs. Qwen3's 128-expert coarser routing); the source-expert traffic skew magnitude and structure may differ, and the claim that Gimbal generalizes is unsubstantiated. - The profiling window length is a critical parameter (it controls adaptation speed vs. stability) but receives no sensitivity analysis; only the weight calibration (α, β, γ) is analyzed. - The 2–4 RPS regime represents light-to-moderate load for production deployments; it is unclear whether the DP-source traffic skew is persistent enough at higher multiplexing rates (dozens of concurrent requests per engine) to provide the source-aware placement advantage. - No failure-mode analysis: if expert hotspots shift abruptly (burst of long-context requests), the profiling window may capture stale traffic and the heuristic may place experts sub-optimally for the next window; this transient behavior is not characterized. Bottom Line This is a credible and well-executed systems paper that addresses a real gap—MoE serving systems that treat dispatch and expert placement as independent problems—and demonstrates via a clean ablation that the coordination mechanism itself (not just its components) drives a meaningful fraction of the gains; practitioners deploying MoE models on multi-GPU DP+EP stacks should read it, though the single-model, single-node, modest-scale evaluation means multi-node production validation is needed before adoption at scale.
Search Results 2606.15177 PDF
#46 — Innovative (69)

TileFuse: A Fused Mixed-Precision Kernel Library for Efficient Quantized LLM Inference on AMD NPUs

W Pang, GH Jun, F Liu, D Chen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TileFuse, a close-to-metal mixed-precision kernel library for AMD XDNA2 NPUs that fuses INT4/INT8 unpacking, dequantization, and GEMM/GEMV execution into a single kernel flow to enable direct deployment of off-the-shelf AWQ-quantized LLMs on laptop NPUs. - Why it's important: Edge NPUs are increasingly present in consumer SoCs but remain practically unusable for LLM inference because their proprietary stacks do not support standard quantization formats like AWQ, forcing users to either retool models or fall back to the integrated GPU. - How it does it: TileFuse co-designs an offline interleaved column-major pre-tiling weight layout (packing scale/zero-point metadata contiguously with weight tiles) with per-core fused microkernels that dequantize each weight block once into a local buffer and reuse it, plus a 2-stage memory-core weight distribution path that maps GEMV across all 32 AIE compute cores instead of just 8. - The good: On Ryzen AI 7 350, TileFuse achieves up to 2.0× lower prefilling latency and 64.6% lower energy than tuned llama.cpp iGPU kernels for Llama3-8B, and outperforms FastFlowLM prefilling throughput by 1.45× and ScalingLLM (Qualcomm) by 1.61× at 1024-token context. - The missing: Token generation (GEMV) consistently underperforms the iGPU baseline because NPU dispatch and reconfiguration overhead (milliseconds) dominates GEMV execution time (hundreds of microseconds); this structural bottleneck is acknowledged but not solved. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on NPU kernel design, edge LLM deployment, or hardware-software co-design for quantized inference; it provides rare close-to-metal insight into XDNA2 dataflow that is unavailable elsewhere in open literature.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (70) and Rigor (68) lead: an open-source kernel library (MLIR-AIE/IRON) with thorough component-isolating ablations across two platforms and a current llama.cpp baseline, delivering 2.0× prefill speedup and 64.6% energy reduction on real Ryzen AI hardware. Novelty (72) credits the co-designed interleaved pre-tiling that solves XDNA2's >8K GEMM limit plus the 32-core GEMV distribution. Resilience (62) is the limiter — token-generation GEMV consistently loses to the iGPU (unsolved dispatch overhead), W8A16 regresses on the higher-end HX 370, no accuracy/perplexity validation, and a non-apples-to-apples cross-vendor Table 3. Composite ≈ 0.15*72+0.15*68+0.10*70+0.15*68+0.15*62+0.30*70 = 69.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Edge SoCs increasingly ship with dedicated NPUs (AMD XDNA2, Apple ANE, Intel NPU, Qualcomm HTP) that promise better performance-per-watt than integrated GPUs for AI workloads. However, in practice these NPUs are exposed only through high-level, compiler-driven stacks (ONNX Runtime, Core ML, OpenVINO, QNN) that support narrow quantization subsets — typically per-tensor or symmetric per-channel schemes — and not the asymmetric per-group formats (group size 128 AWQ W4A16) that dominate open-source edge LLM deployment. As a result, developers who want to run pre-quantized Llama or Gemma models on a laptop NPU must either adapt the model to a proprietary format or accept iGPU fallback. The specific gaps are: (1) no existing AMD NPU work supports GEMM weight-matrix dimensions above 8K, which excludes large MLP layers; (2) prior BFP16-format XDNA2 kernels achieve high TOPS but cannot serve as practical LLM kernels due to insufficient accuracy; (3) fusing dequantization into GEMM introduces a weight-reuse problem that naive implementations fail to solve efficiently. Key Idea: The core insight is that weight layout and kernel execution must be co-designed offline rather than at runtime: by reorganizing quantized weights into an interleaved column-major tile layout that packs per-group metadata (scales, zero-points) immediately adjacent to the weights they govern, each compute core can receive a single contiguous DMA stream that contains everything needed for fused unpacking, dequantization, and matrix multiply — eliminating both the global-memory BF16 materialization step and the large-stride DMA access that blocked large GEMM support. Reusing the dequantized BF16 tile in per-core local memory then amortizes the dequantization cost across multiple activation rows. Methodology: TileFuse targets transformer linear layers (attention projections, MLP projections) and has three components. (1) Offline weight pre-tiling: applied once at deployment time with no runtime overhead. For W4A16, each k×n tile stores INT4 weights contiguously followed by BF16 scales and INT8 zero-points (duplicated to 128-byte DMA alignment). Tiles assigned to each of the 8 AIE compute-core columns are reordered to be contiguous in memory (interleaved column-major), reducing DMA stride from 8-tile-column jumps to sequential reads and extending GEMM support from N≤8K to N≤32K. (2) Mixed-precision microkernels: for GEMM, each AIE compute core unpacks INT4 fields via bit-mask/shift into INT8, converts to BF16, dequantizes with per-group metadata using Eq.(2) (asymmetric) or Eq.(1) (symmetric W8A16), stores BF16 results into a per-core local buffer, and reuses them for all activation slices before advancing to the next weight tile. VLIW scheduling hides local memory latency by overlapping memory ops with compute. For GEMV, the microkernel is widened from 16-output to 64-output elements per iteration by loading 64×8 weight blocks into 64-value BF16 vector registers, reusing each 1×8 activation fragment across 64 rows. (3) GEMV array-level dataflow redesign: the baseline streams weights directly from each shim core to one compute row (8 of 32 cores active). The redesigned path uses memory cores as a distribution layer — each shim streams a bundle of 4 weight tiles into the adjacent memory core, which then forwards distinct tiles to each of the 4 compute rows in that column, activating all 32 cores. The end-to-end framework (C++ with Python bindings) dispatches GEMM-heavy linear layers to the NPU and attention/normalization/FlashAttention-2 to the iGPU sequentially (no concurrent NPU+iGPU pipelining). Key Results: Kernel-level (Ryzen AI 7 350, W4A16, shapes 4K×14K and 14K×4K): NPU peak throughput reaches 12 TOPS vs. 5 TOPS for the 8-CU iGPU (llama.cpp Q4_K); GEMM improvement over full-precision BF16 baseline reaches 121.6%; GEMV improvement over full-precision baseline reaches 281% when combining mixed-precision, optimized microkernel, and 2D dataflow. Pre-tiling alone improves the full-precision GEMM baseline by up to 50%. The Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 (Strix Point) reaches only 9 TOPS NPU peak and shows weaker W8A16 results; the authors attribute this to uncharacterized platform-level streaming bandwidth differences. GEMV in Table 1 shows the CPU (298 GOPs, 4-bit) and iGPU (215 GOPs) outperforming the NPU (206 GOPs) on a 1×4096×14336 shape, confirming the dispatch-overhead bottleneck. End-to-end prefilling (Table 2): on Ryzen AI 7 350, W4A16 NPU achieves up to 2.0× speedup over llama.cpp iGPU at 2048-token prompts for Llama3-8B and ~1.7× for Gemma-2B/Qwen2.5-3B; on Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 (stronger 16-CU iGPU) speedups are modest (1.16–1.40×) and W8A16 NPU is actually slower than iGPU across all models and prompt lengths. Token generation (Figure 8): NPU achieves 15 tok/s vs. iGPU 31–38 tok/s for Gemma-2B, consistently underperforming due to millisecond-range dispatch overhead. Energy: 64.6% reduction on AI 7 350 and 52.8% on HX 370 for Llama3-8B 4096-token prefilling. Prefilling throughput comparison (Table 3, 1024-token context): TileFuse achieves 501.9 tok/s vs. FastFlowLM's 345.9 tok/s on Llama3-8B (1.45×) and 890.4 tok/s vs. ScalingLLM's 550.0 tok/s on Qwen2.5-3B (1.61×). Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. TileFuse is genuinely novel in combining (a) metadata-aware offline interleaved pre-tiling that solves the large-GEMM dimension constraint on XDNA2, (b) fused W4A16/W8A16 microkernels with single-dequantization weight reuse on a spatial NPU array, and (c) a 2-stage memory-core GEMV weight distribution that activates the full AIE array. The individual components (tiled GEMM, mixed-precision quantization, fused kernels) are known; the contribution is their co-design for XDNA2's specific DMA/BD constraints and spatial array topology. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are thorough and well-structured: Figure 10 and Figure 11 isolate pre-tiling, mixed-precision, microkernel widening, and dataflow redesign independently for both GEMM and GEMV, across two platforms. No error bars or variance across runs are reported (single-run timings appear to be used). Source code is publicly released at https://github.com/glassescrab/mlir-aie/tree/feature/update-mix-mmint4-verification, enabling reproduction. The iGPU baseline (llama.cpp as of January 2026) is current and competitive — the authors note it improved 1.5× over 2025 releases. The comparison in Table 3 is thin: only two prior-work data points, on different hardware platforms (Qualcomm vs. AMD), making cross-platform claims hard to verify. No model accuracy or perplexity measurements are reported; the paper implicitly assumes AWQ quantization accuracy is acceptable but does not validate end-to-end output quality. The W8A16 NPU regressing below iGPU on HX 370 is reported honestly and discussed, which adds credibility. Platform performance differences between Krackan Point and Strix Point are unexplained due to lack of public AMD documentation. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built kernel library and runtime framework evaluated by deployment on real hardware. - Practical: Runs on commercially available Ryzen AI 350 and HX 370 laptops with no special modifications beyond installing the MLIR-AIE (IRON) SDK. - Efficient: NPU prefilling consumes 64.6% less energy than the iGPU path on AI 7 350; however, token generation is ~2× slower than the iGPU baseline, making the system unsuitable for latency-sensitive interactive decoding without a hybrid NPU+iGPU dispatch strategy. - Effective: The system demonstrably solves prefilling latency for long-context inference (>512 tokens) on W4A16 models; it does not solve token generation and explicitly leaves that on the iGPU, so end-to-end interactive latency improvement requires context lengths long enough to dominate TTFT over TPOT. - Scalable: Tested on Gemma-2B, Qwen2.5-3B, and Llama3-8B at prompts up to 8192 tokens; larger models (13B+) and longer contexts are untested, and models with MLP hidden dims not divisible by the minimum tile size (e.g., Qwen2.5-3B) require padding that reduces efficiency. The system delivers a software artifact (open-source kernel library + C++/Python runtime) but is tightly coupled to XDNA2's BD-based DMA model and IRON programming flow; porting to other NPU architectures requires non-trivial re-engineering. Strengths: - The interleaved pre-tiling design that simultaneously solves the metadata-delivery problem, the large-stride DMA bottleneck, and the 8K dimension limit is a clean and practically motivated solution; without it the system cannot support real MLP layer shapes. - The 2-stage memory-core GEMV distribution that activates all 32 AIE cores (vs. 8 in the baseline) is a concrete array-level dataflow insight specific to XDNA2's topology, not a generic optimization. - The paper honestly characterizes XDNA2 reconfigurability as a double-edged sword — useful for GEMM, harmful for latency-sensitive GEMV — and quantifies the tradeoff rather than marketing past it. - Releasing source code on a real open-source flow (MLIR-AIE/IRON) is a meaningful contribution to a field where almost all NPU stacks are proprietary black boxes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: (1) token generation underperforms iGPU because dispatch and reconfiguration overhead (milliseconds) exceeds GEMV execution time (hundreds of microseconds); (2) NPU and iGPU execute sequentially rather than in parallel, leaving hybrid concurrency gains unrealized; (3) models with MLP hidden dimensions not aligned to tile size incur padding overhead (noted for Qwen2.5-3B); (4) performance differences between Strix Point and Krackan Point cannot be fully explained from public documentation, limiting mechanistic insight. These are confronted directly and constitute a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The paper does not evaluate model accuracy or perplexity under any of the quantization configurations — it silently inherits AWQ's known quality, but does not validate that the TileFuse dequantization path produces numerically correct outputs relative to GPU-side AWQ inference. A single correctness table would close this. The Table 3 cross-system comparison against FastFlowLM and ScalingLLM conflates different hardware platforms (AMD vs. AMD vs. Qualcomm), different power envelopes, and different quantization schemes (AWQ vs. tile-quantization); the claimed 1.45–1.61× speedups are not apples-to-apples and the authors note this but do not correct for it. The W8A16 NPU being slower than iGPU on the HX 370 — the higher-end platform — is a significant regression that the discussion attributes to uncharacterized platform memory-path behavior; this is an open empirical question that should motivate future profiling. There is no evaluation of multi-batch prefilling (M > 1 at inference time), and no analysis of decode-phase KV cache bandwidth. Finally, the hardware is provided by AMD and the work is supported by the AMD Center of Excellence, which does not invalidate the results but warrants noting for context. Bottom Line: Practitioners building edge LLM inference stacks on AMD Ryzen AI hardware will find TileFuse's pre-tiling design and ablation data directly actionable; researchers in NPU kernel design gain one of the few publicly detailed close-to-metal accounts of spatial-array dataflow for quantized LLMs, making this worth reading despite the token-generation gap remaining unsolved.
Search Results 2606.11357 PDF
#47 — Innovative (69)

Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization: Teacher in Prompts, Not Gradients (40 ▲ )

Byung-Kwan Lee, Ximing Lu, Shizhe Diao, Minki Kang, Saurav Muralidharan, Karan Sapra, Andrew Tao, Pavlo Molchanov, Yejin Choi, Yu-Chiang Frank Wang, Ryo Hachiuma · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ZPPO, a post-training recipe that injects teacher knowledge into reformulated prompts (BCQ and NCQ) rather than the policy gradient, recovering RL learning signal on hard questions where every student rollout fails. - Why it's important: Small VLMs (0.8B–9B) needed for edge deployment are simultaneously hurt by knowledge distillation (brittle generalization) and standard RL (silent discarding of all-wrong questions), leaving a capability gap that ZPPO directly targets. - How it does it: On hard questions, BCQ pairs an anonymized correct teacher response with a wrong student response as shuffled candidates the student must discriminate; NCQ aggregates all the student's wrong rollouts into one prompt to surface shared failure patterns; a prompt replay buffer keeps re-exposing hard questions until mastered. - The good: Distillation degrades LLM/Video generalization by −2.5 to −0.3 pp across scales while ZPPO improves those same held-out families by +6.8 to +2.7 pp, with the 0.8B student gaining +9.3 pp on 16 VLM benchmarks. - The missing: BCQ requires the teacher to succeed on a hard question; when both teacher and student fail, only the weaker NCQ signal applies, creating a capability ceiling directly bounded by teacher strength. - Read it or not: Practitioners training sub-10B VLMs with GRPO-style RL should read this; those using only large models or distillation-only pipelines can skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. This is the most rigorously audited paper in the cluster (Rigor 68, Completeness 72): mechanism audits checking discrimination vs answer-leakage, 10,000-resample cluster bootstraps with all 12 cells excluding zero, ablations at four scales across three benchmark families, and four limitations confronted directly. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (72) are high — the teacher-in-prompt framing (BCQ/NCQ) has no direct precedent, and the super-additive replay×reformulation interaction is a genuine surprise. The distillation-hurts-generalization-while-ZPPO-improves-it result across all scales is strong directional evidence. Resilience (68) is dented by judge circularity (Qwen3.5-27B is both teacher and evaluator on five benchmarks) and single-run training; Impact (58) by no released code/weights. Composite reflects strong novelty plus near-exemplary rigor, held just under the top band by the circularity and reproducibility gaps.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Deployment of LLMs/VLMs on mobile phones, AR/VR glasses, and embodied robots requires models under 10B parameters that generalize broadly. Two dominant training paradigms fail in the small-student regime. Knowledge distillation forces the student to match teacher logit distributions, which concentrates small-capacity students on the teacher's sharpest modes (mode-seeking bias) and causes brittle out-of-distribution generalization beyond the training corpus. Standard RL (GRPO and variants) avoids logit imitation but silently discards questions where every rollout fails—yielding zero group-relative advantage—which are exactly the questions where a teacher could help most. Injecting the teacher's correct rollout directly into the policy gradient (the obvious fix) breaks the on-policy assumption and induces drift because the teacher's response lies far outside the small student's rollout distribution. Key Idea The teacher's response should appear only inside the prompt, never as a gradient target. By reformulating hard questions so the student must generate its own response in the context of teacher and self-generated candidates—rather than imitating teacher logits or receiving teacher tokens in the gradient—every response token remains student-generated and the policy gradient stays on-policy at the response level while still benefiting from teacher knowledge. Methodology ZPPO layers three components onto GRPO augmented with DAPO's clip-higher and token-level loss: (1) BCQ (Binary Candidate-included Question): For each hard question (mean student rollout accuracy $\bar{r}_x < 0.5$), sample one correct teacher rollout and one wrong student rollout, teacher-compress both to ≤512 tokens under a shared compression prompt, anonymize inside identical `<candidate>` tags, randomly shuffle, and append to the question. The student then samples $G_S = 8$ rollouts from this reformulated prompt; every gradient-counted token is student-generated. (2) NCQ (Negative Candidate-included Question): For each hard question, collect all wrong student rollouts from the current group, parse their final answers, teacher-rewrite each rollout, list parsed answers explicitly ("The following answers are all WRONG"), and append as `<candidate>` blocks. This is the first point in the training loop where independently sampled wrong rollouts on the same question converge into a single prompt, turning independent negatives into a shared failure-mode signal. (3) Prompt Replay Buffer: Stores question text only (no responses), admitting hard questions ($\bar{r}_x < 0.5$), graduating them when $\bar{r}_x \geq 0.5$ on plain (non-reformulated) rollouts, and FIFO-evicting at capacity 10K. BCQ and NCQ candidates are re-sampled fresh on every buffer visit. Recipe details: REINFORCE++ two-step advantage estimator with zero-advantage groups excluded from batch normalization (Norm w/o Zero), $I = 4$ gradient iterations per rollout step (vs. GRPO default 16), no KL penalty, asymmetric clip-higher $(\epsilon_{\text{low}}, \epsilon_{\text{high}}) = (0.20, 0.28)$. Training on ZPPO-77K (∼77K multimodal image-question pairs), 64 H100-80GB GPUs for 200 rollout steps. Key Results Students: Qwen3.5 family (0.8B, 2B, 4B, 9B). Teacher: Qwen3.5-27B. Evaluation: 31 benchmarks (16 VLM, 10 LLM, 5 Video). All methods trained on identical data. On 16 VLM benchmarks (training-aligned): ZPPO gains +9.3 / +5.2 / +4.0 / +2.8 pp at 0.8B/2B/4B/9B vs. base. Off/on-policy distillation gains only +0.9/+0.9/+0.7/+0.6 pp. On 10 LLM + 5 Video benchmarks (held-out generalization): Distillation degrades performance by −2.5/−1.8/−0.9/−0.3 pp; ZPPO improves by +6.8/+4.3/+2.7/+2.7 pp. On zero-accuracy hard questions (the BCQ mechanism test): ZPPO graduates 28% (432/1568) vs. GRPO†'s 4% (73/2035) at 2B; on the next-hardest bin, 54% vs. 14%. Component ablation (Tab. 3): BCQ alone or NCQ alone with the buffer each contributes modestly; replay × reformulation is super-additive. ZPPO (full recipe) achieves column-max at every scale. Baselines are current and appropriately strong: off-policy distillation, on-policy distillation, GRPO, GRPO+buffer, Hint (Guide-GRPO style), Prefix (BREAD style). All share identical hyperparameters, data, and training budget. Benchmarks include AIME 2025/2026, GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro, HLE, MathVista, CharXiv, DynaMath—strong and recent. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The "teacher-in-prompt" framing as an alternative to both logit-imitation and teacher-rollout-injection is genuinely novel. BCQ as a forced binary discrimination task at training time and NCQ as collective-negative surfacing have no direct precedents in the RL post-training literature. The super-additive replay × reformulation interaction is a non-obvious empirical finding. The batch normalization Norm w/o Zero analysis is a useful engineering contribution. The underlying RL backbone (GRPO + DAPO + REINFORCE++) is a standard combination of known techniques. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: each of BCQ, NCQ, and the buffer is tested in isolation and combined (Tabs. 3, 12, 13), at all four scales, across all three benchmark families. BCQ/NCQ candidate-match audit (Tabs. 16–17) directly checks whether the proposed mechanism (discrimination vs. trivial answer leakage) actually holds: BCQ accuracy plateaus at 36–69% (not ~100%), and correct rollouts match the teacher candidate 78–91% of the time while wrong rollouts match the student-wrong candidate 77–97% of the time, supporting genuine discrimination rather than surface-tell exploitation. Benchmark-selection robustness is validated via 10,000-resample cluster bootstraps (Tabs. 14–15); all 12 (scale, family) cells for ZPPO − GRPO† exclude zero. Training dynamics (Figs. 22–25) and buffer graduation analyses (Figs. 4, 20–24) are presented. Limitations on rigor: Main results are single runs (no multiple seeds, no run-to-run variance reported). The same judge model (Qwen3.5-27B) serves as both the training teacher and the evaluation judge on five LLM-as-judge benchmarks (CharXiv, MM-Vet, HLE, MultiChallenge, MMVU)—a potential circularity when the teacher's style bleeds into student responses. Code is not yet released. This is a preprint, not peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Training requires 64 H100-80GB GPUs; inference deploys 0.8B–9B student models on standard commercial hardware. - Efficient: ZPPO adds ~12–20% wall-clock overhead over GRPO† (68–110h vs. 61–93h across scales), primarily from BCQ/NCQ rollout phases; teacher generation is overlapped and off the critical path. - Effective: Performance gains extend to 10 held-out LLM and 5 Video benchmarks not in the training corpus, providing strong evidence against training-set overfitting. - Scalable: ZPPO's BCQ contribution diminishes at 4B/9B as BCQ candidates dry up (fewer questions where teacher succeeds but student fails); the system was not tested beyond 9B students or 27B teachers. No ablation at larger student scales is possible from this work. The paper delivers a training recipe and algorithm, not a released software artifact or trained model checkpoint. Strengths - The core mechanism is theoretically well-motivated: BCQ/NCQ recover positive-advantage rollouts on exactly the question class (all-wrong) that GRPO silently discards, which the graduation analysis confirms empirically. - The distillation-hurts-generalization finding is convincingly documented with consistent directional evidence across all scales and three benchmark families—a stronger claim than typical "our method is better" papers. - The super-additivity of replay × reformulation (neither alone is sufficient; together they compound) is an unexpected and interesting structural finding backed by clean ablations at all four scales. - The BCQ/NCQ audit is exemplary scientific practice: the authors check whether their claimed mechanism (genuine discrimination vs. surface leakage/trivial avoidance) is actually operating, and the scale-dependent NCQ failure at 0.8B (match-neg 82.7%) is reported honestly rather than suppressed. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Four limitations are stated explicitly and confronted directly: (1) BCQ is teacher-bounded—questions where both teacher and student fail admit no BCQ candidate, collapsing to NCQ-only; (2) tension with dynamic sampling, which discards all-wrong questions that ZPPO specifically stores; (3) scope is single-turn reasoning accuracy only (no agentic reasoning, robustness, conversational competence, safety); (4) BCQ contribution shrinks with student scale as the teacher–student capability gap narrows. The authors treat the teacher-bounded zone limitation as the primary open problem. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Judge circularity: Qwen3.5-27B is both the training teacher (providing BCQ candidates and reward signals for free-form questions) and the evaluation judge on five benchmarks. If ZPPO students absorb the teacher's lexical or stylistic patterns through BCQ exposure, the judge could score them more favorably. The cluster bootstrap quantifies benchmark-selection robustness but not this bias. - Data construction bias: ZPPO-77K drops examples where the 4B annotator solves them more than half the time, biasing the corpus toward hard examples that benefit BCQ/NCQ. Baselines trained on the same corpus may be somewhat disadvantaged relative to a curriculum-neutral dataset. - Single-run results: All reported numbers are single training runs. At smaller gains (1–2 pp) at larger scales, run-to-run variance could be comparable to the effect size. The cluster bootstrap CIs measure benchmark-selection robustness, not training stochasticity. - Distillation baseline fairness: The distillation baselines use JSD loss with a specific online-teacher setup; other distillation implementations (e.g., SeqKD, GKD with IS correction) are not compared. The "distillation degrades generalization" claim, while well-supported within this paper's setup, may not generalize across all distillation variants. - BCQ at convergence: BCQ accuracy of 36–69% raises an interpretation question—this is partly a function of which hard questions remain at convergence (the hardest ones), not just student discrimination ability. The audit is informative but cannot fully separate discrimination quality from selection effects. Bottom Line A practitioner training sub-10B VLMs with GRPO-style RL should read this; the BCQ/NCQ reformulations address a genuine, reproducible failure mode of existing RL recipes, and the generalization improvements on held-out LLM/Video benchmarks are the most compelling evidence that the method is doing something principled rather than merely overfitting the training distribution.
Recommended 2606.18216 PDF
#48 — Innovative (69)

PI-Hunter: Automated Red-Teaming for Exposing and Localizing Prompt Injections

P He, L Miculicich, V Sharma, A Fox, G Lee, J Tang… · arxiv.org · automated generated environments for agent testing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PI-Hunter, an automated agentic auditing framework that proactively exposes and localizes latent indirect prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agent systems by evolving source-aware test cases through genetic-algorithm-style feedback loops. - Why it's important: LLM agents ingest content from untrusted external sources (emails, web pages, databases) where malicious instructions lie dormant until specific retrieval patterns activate them — developers currently have no systematic way to find these hidden paths before deployment, and existing defenses detect but do not map vulnerabilities. - How it does it: The framework performs a one-time static analysis of the agent's tool surface, seeds one LLM-generated test case per high-risk interface, runs the target agent and audits execution trajectories with a rubric-guided evaluator, mutates test cases and the mutation operators themselves based on failure signals, then transiently patches each discovered injection path to force exploration of previously hidden attack surfaces. - The good: Across five backbone models (Gemini-3.1-pro, Gemini-2.5-pro, GPT-5.4-mini, Claude-4.6-sonnet, DeepSeek) and two benchmarks, PI-Hunter consistently and substantially improves both source recall (e.g., 0.255 → 0.834 under the AgentVigil attack with Gemini-3.1-pro on AgentDojo) and discovery diversity (Source Diversity 0.11 → 0.84 for Gemini-3.1-pro on AgentDojo), including under defenses where the baseline degenerates to zero recall. - The missing: The evaluation compares against a single custom baseline rather than published automated red-teaming systems (GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, SIRAJ), and performance in live production agents with dynamic, unconstrained environments remains entirely untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and security practitioners building or auditing LLM agent systems, as it offers a concrete and well-motivated shift from attack-success measurement to proactive ingestion-path mapping — but expect no code release and calibrate confidence accordingly.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (70) anchor — reframing prompt-injection auditing from attack-success maximization to proactive ingestion-path mapping, plus the patch-and-reexplore co-evolution mechanism and meta-mutation, are sound and non-obvious contributions. Rigor (65) is the limiter: large/consistent improvements with seeding and mutation ablations, but only a single custom baseline (no comparison to GOAT/AutoRedTeamer/SIRAJ), no error bars or multi-seed runs on stochastic LLM systems, conservative exact-match recovery, hand-crafted per-domain operators, no code release, and preprint status. Completeness (70) and Resilience (65) hold up given honest limitations. Weighted: 0.20*70+0.25*65+0.15*75+0.15*70+0.15*65+0.10*68 ≈ 69.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents connected to external tools continuously consume content from untrusted sources — email inboxes, web search results, document stores, databases — that can embed malicious instructions designed to remain dormant until activated by specific user queries, retrieval patterns, or reasoning trajectories. This indirect prompt injection threat differs fundamentally from direct injection through user prompts: the attack surface is not the user's message but the agent's interaction with its environment. Existing defenses (Spotlighting, MELON, PIGuard, SecAlign) address detection and blocking at inference time and assume injections are known. Existing automated red-teaming tools (GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, AgentVigil) optimize for attack success rate — they measure whether an agent can be compromised, not where in its operational reach it is vulnerable. Agent builders therefore lack tools to proactively audit which external sources, interfaces, and interaction patterns introduce exploitable ingestion paths before deployment. Key Idea: The single core insight is that exposing latent prompt injections requires treating the auditing problem as a co-evolutionary search: the auditor generates source-targeted test cases, observes which trajectories produce suspicious behavior, uses rubric-graded diagnostic feedback to mutate both the test cases and the mutation operators themselves, and structurally eliminates discovered paths through transient patching — forcing the search into genuinely unexplored attack surfaces. This patch-and-reexplore mechanism is the distinguishing mechanism: rather than relying on randomness or coverage metrics to drive diversity, it removes each discovered vulnerability from the accessible landscape, making continued exploitation of easy paths impossible and pushing exploration toward sparse, hidden injections. Methodology: PI-Hunter implements a three-stage pipeline operating on a black-box target agent A with tool set I and external sources S. Stage 1 — Static Analysis: A one-time inspection of tool schemas and API interfaces identifies high-risk ingestion channels — specifically interfaces that consume unstructured external content (emails, HTML pages, documents, repository listings) or that can perform privileged actions (send_email, execute_command, modify_files). This produces the seeding surface for Stage 2. Stage 2 — Evolutionary Exploitation Loop (Algorithm 1, T iterations): (a) Source-aware meta-seeding: the LLM-powered Generator creates one seed query per identified high-risk source, designed to embed plausible benign user intent, activate a specific high-risk interface, and elicit verbatim reproduction of retrieved content (quoting, listing, summarizing). (b) Trajectory execution and evaluation: test cases are submitted to the target agent; the trajectory-aware Evaluator audits retrieved contents, tool call sequences, reasoning traces, and outputs against five rubric criteria: intent adherence discrepancy, third-party instruction presence, authority confusion, abnormal interface usage, and sensitive action attempts. The Evaluator returns structured diagnostic feedback, not binary labels. (c) Feedback-driven mutation: the Generator selects and applies mutation operators based on trajectory signals. Seven general-purpose operators are defined (volume_expansion, implicit_assumption, parameter_fuzzing, instruction_hierarchy_override, role_inversion, delimiter_hijacking, encoding_obfuscation), supplemented by domain-specific operators for workspace, banking, travel, Slack, shopping, GitHub, and daily-life scenarios. (d) LLM-guided meta-mutation: a "Hyper-Mutation Engine" prompt refines the mutation operators themselves based on historical effectiveness across iterations. Default configuration: 5 seed queries, 10 iterations. Stage 3 — Verification & Co-evolution (Patcher): Newly discovered injection paths are passed to the LLM-powered Patcher, which applies lightweight transient mitigations (blacklisting injections in the system prompt, restricting vulnerable interfaces, temporarily isolating compromised sources) to reshape the exploration landscape and prevent redundant rediscovery. Objective function: maximize Source Recall and Instruction Recall (precision/recall over compromised source localization and malicious payload recovery) and entropy-based Diversity across source categories and attack consequence categories. Instruction matching uses exact string comparison. Critical design decisions: (1) Source-aware seeding over generic domain prompts — well-justified by the ablation showing Source Recall 0.48 vs. 0.19 (generic); (2) trajectory-level rubric evaluation over binary attack labels — justified conceptually (injections manifest in intermediate reasoning, not only final outputs) but not ablated against simpler evaluators; (3) transient patching for coverage expansion — not ablated independently, leaving its specific contribution to diversity unquantified. Key Results: Tested on AgentDojo (4 domains: banking, Slack, workspace, travel) and AgentDyn (7 domains, 60 user tasks, 560 injection test cases), across ReAct and Planner-Executor architectures, 5 backbone models, 5 attack types (direct, ignore_previous, system_msg, important_inst, agentvigil), and 4 defense settings (None, Spotlighting, MELON, PIGuard). Source Recall improvements (PI-Hunter vs. baseline): Gemini-3.1-pro on AgentDojo under agentvigil: 0.255 → 0.834; under direct attack: 0.248 → 0.881. GPT-5.4-mini on AgentDyn under direct attack: 0.314 → 0.688. Claude-4.6-sonnet on AgentDyn under ignore_previous: 0.356 → 0.889. Diversity improvements: Gemini-3.1-pro, AgentDojo: Source Diversity 0.11 → 0.84; GPT-5.4-mini, AgentDojo: 0.18 → 0.69. Baseline diversity is frequently near zero (0.00–0.15), indicating the baseline repeatedly rediscovers the same injection patterns. Under defenses (Table 3, Gemini-2.5-pro, agentvigil): Under PIGuard (strongest defense), the baseline achieves 0.000 Instruction Recall while PI-Hunter reaches 0.194. Under Spotlight, Source Recall improves from 0.300 to 0.456, Instruction Recall from 0.220 to 0.390. Ablation results (Table 4): Source-aware seeding achieves Source Recall 0.4796 vs. 0.1918 (Generic), 0.3453 (Holistic). Source Precision 0.7794 vs. 0.2338. Table 5: Smart mutation achieves Source Recall 0.4796 vs. 0.3228 (Fixed), 0.3578 (Random). Architecture generalization: Instruction Recall for Planner-Executor agents (baseline 0.22 → 0.67) benefits more than ReAct (0.27 → 0.49), suggesting multi-stage pipelines expose broader attack surfaces. Convergence: Performance saturates around 8–10 iterations (Figure 3). Computational cost: 70–127 queries and 5,759–11,007 tokens per audit run (Table 18), though whether target agent calls are included is unstated. Baseline: a single custom "free interaction" LLM baseline (static analysis → open-ended multi-turn conversation → post-hoc extraction). No comparison to GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, SIRAJ, or SafeSearch as auditing systems. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The auditor-side framing of injection exposure (mapping ingestion paths rather than optimizing attack success) and the specific combination of source-targeted seeding, trajectory-rubric evaluation, and patch-driven exploration diversity are the genuine novelties. The individual components — LLM-powered test case mutation, genetic algorithm exploitation loops, rubric-guided evaluation — are established techniques (AgentVigil uses MCTS mutation for the attack side; rubric-based evaluators are standard); the contribution is their assembly and reorientation toward proactive vulnerability mapping rather than attack maximization. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on seeding strategy and mutation strategy are present and quantitative. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed runs are reported for any table; all numbers are point estimates from stochastic LLM-based systems, and the sensitivity of results to randomness is unknown. No code, model weights, or data are released (no URL mentioned). The exact-match payload recovery metric (explicitly defined in Appendix B) is conservative and will miss semantically equivalent paraphrases, potentially undercounting true recall. The custom baseline is implemented by the authors and not validated against published systems; this is a significant gap, as GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, and SIRAJ could plausibly be adapted as auditing baselines. Domain-specific mutation operators are hand-crafted for each benchmark domain, introducing tuning that may not generalize. This is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2606.12737v1, June 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS): - Practical: PI-Hunter operates as a black-box auditor requiring only API access to the target agent and an LLM for the Generator/Evaluator/Patcher roles — both commercially available. No internal agent access required. - Efficient: Claimed 70–127 queries and ~6,000–11,000 tokens per audit run (Table 18); however, these counts appear to cover only the auditor's own LLM calls, not the target agent's LLM calls, making the true compute cost unclear. No wall-clock timing or dollar cost estimates are provided. - Effective: Improvements are large and consistent, but absolute recall values for PI-Hunter remain below 1.0 in nearly all conditions and fall as low as 0.252 Source Recall under PIGuard on AgentDyn — meaning a substantial fraction of injections remains undiscovered even after 10 iterations. The framework finds more, not all. - Scalable: Tested exclusively on curated benchmark environments. Performance on production agents with hundreds of tools, dynamic content, and real-world variability is explicitly acknowledged as untested. No experiments at scale beyond the benchmarks. No software artifact is released alongside the paper. Strengths: - The ingestion-path framing (source × tool intersection as the unit of vulnerability) is more actionable for practitioners than attack success rate — knowing which external source and which interface introduce risk directly informs remediation. - The meta-mutation mechanism (evolving mutation operators based on accumulated feedback) is a non-obvious and technically sound contribution that operationalizes continual adaptation without manual operator design per deployment. - The patch-and-reexplore co-evolution loop is a structurally clever diversity driver: eliminating discovered paths from the exploration landscape guarantees monotonically increasing coverage, unlike coverage metrics or random restarts. - Evaluation breadth is above average for this literature: five backbone models, two benchmarks, four defense settings, two agent architectures, five attack types, and four injection-scenario configurations, with ablations on the two most critical design choices. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state (1) performance in live, unconstrained real-world environments remains unverified, since production systems present dynamic and uncontrollable variables; and (2) the framework identifies vulnerabilities but proposes no remediation solutions, leaving the gap between discovery and mitigation as future work. The limitations are acknowledged directly and without minimization, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The comparison baseline is a single custom implementation; there is no comparison to published automated red-teaming systems (GOAT, AutoRedTeamer, SIRAJ) as auditing-side competitors, which would be the natural comparison class. (2) No error bars or significance tests are reported for any quantitative result, despite all measurements coming from stochastic LLM systems — some reported improvements may fall within noise. (3) The token cost in Table 18 likely excludes target agent calls, making the cost analysis potentially misleading for practitioners estimating audit budgets. (4) Exact-match instruction recovery will miss paraphrased or reformatted payloads that achieve the same attack effect, creating systematic undercounting of true recall. (5) Domain-specific mutation operators are hand-crafted for each benchmark domain, which constitutes implicit tuning to the evaluation environments and may not generalize to novel deployment domains. (6) No code release makes reproduction and extension impossible, a significant gap for a paper presenting a framework rather than a theoretical result. Bottom Line: PI-Hunter makes a genuine methodological contribution by reframing prompt injection security as proactive ingestion-path mapping and introducing the patch-and-reexplore co-evolution mechanism, but the single-baseline comparison and absence of a code release prevent confident assessment of where it stands relative to the broader red-teaming literature, making it a compelling prototype that warrants follow-up rather than immediate deployment.
Search Results 2606.12737 PDF
#49 — Innovative (69)

FlowBender: Feedback-Aware Training for Self-Correcting Conditional Flows (15 ▲ )

Daniel Gilo, Sven Elflein, Ido Sobol, Or Litany · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces FlowBender, a closed-loop training framework for conditional diffusion and flow-matching models that feeds the model's own alignment error back as a first-class input at each sampling step, training a learned correction policy rather than relying on hand-tuned guidance. - Why it's important: Conditional generative models routinely violate the very constraints that define their task (e.g., a depth-conditioned model producing images whose re-extracted depth disagrees with the input), and the only existing remedy—inference-time guidance—forces a hard trade-off between condition fidelity and sample plausibility. - How it does it: At each denoising step, a first (look-ahead) model pass with zeroed feedback estimates the clean signal x̂₁; the forward operator H computes a task-specific deviation from the target; a second (refinement) pass receives this deviation as an auxiliary input and produces a corrected velocity vector. - The good: FlowBender simultaneously improves both fidelity and plausibility across all tested tasks, a combination that inference-time guidance cannot achieve; orthogonal decomposition shows 80% of the learned correction energy lies perpendicular to the gradient, confirming the policy is genuinely non-linear rather than a learned guidance scale. - The missing: There is no ablation comparing FlowBender against a pure self-conditioning baseline (feeding x̂₁ back without computing the alignment error H(x̂₁)−y), leaving open whether the alignment error signal per se is necessary or whether any look-ahead feedback would suffice. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on controlled generation, image restoration, or inverse problems with flow/diffusion models; the closed-loop formulation is a clean, general idea with strong empirical backing across three distinct task families.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) is the strength: the orthogonal-decomposition analysis (80% of correction energy perpendicular to the gradient) gives genuine mechanistic evidence the policy is non-linear, not a learned guidance scale. Novelty (72) and the simultaneous fidelity+plausibility gains across three task families are credible. Completeness (65) and Resilience (65) are held back by the critical missing self-conditioning baseline, the confounded 3D-texturing data pipeline, and small evaluation sets with no standard benchmarks. Composite ≈ 0.20*72+0.25*68+0.15*75+0.15*65+0.15*65+0.10*68 ≈ 69.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 68
Show full analysis
