Problem & Motivation Conditional diffusion and flow-matching models are trained to sample from p(x|y) for conditioning signals such as depth maps, edge maps, or 3D geometry, but in practice they function as open-loop systems at inference: even when the forward operator H that defines the constraint (e.g., a depth predictor) is available, the model has no mechanism to detect or correct its own drift from the constraint manifold. Standard fine-tuning (e.g., ControlNet) ignores this feedback entirely; inference-time guidance methods (e.g., FlowChef) consult H only at test time through a manually tuned scalar-weighted gradient update, creating a train-test discrepancy and an irresolvable fidelity-plausibility trade-off. The paper targets anyone building conditional image generators, 3D texturing pipelines, or inverse-problem solvers where a differentiable or even black-box measurement operator is available during training. Key Idea The core insight is to treat the model's own alignment error as a first-class conditioning signal. By routing a task-specific deviation signal—derived from H applied to the model's current clean estimate—back into the network as an input during training, the model learns a non-linear correction policy that internalizes how to use feedback to bend its trajectory toward the conditional manifold, rather than having this correction imposed externally through a fixed scalar rule. Methodology FlowBender augments existing conditional flow-matching training (objective L_FM = E[‖v_θ(x_t, t, c) − u_t‖²]) with a two-pass feedback-aware objective L_FA = E[‖v_θ(x_t, t, c, sg[s_t]) − u_t‖²]. In Pass 1 (look-ahead), the network runs with s_t = 0 to produce a velocity v_LA and a clean estimate x̂₁ = x_t + (1−t)v_LA (for OT flow matching). The feedback signal s_t is then computed from H(x̂₁) and the target y; stop-gradient is applied so that no gradient flows through the look-ahead pass or the operator H. In Pass 2 (refinement), the same network receives s_t as an auxiliary channel and produces the corrected velocity v_ref. Three feedback formulations are proposed: (1) First-order: the gradient ∇_{x_t} L(H(x̂₁), y) or the cheaper approximation ∇_{x̂₁} L(H(x̂₁), y) (avoiding the denoiser Jacobian), concatenated to x_t as an extra channel; (2) Zero-order: the raw residual R(H(x̂₁), y) injected via the ControlNet condition path, supporting non-differentiable operators such as JPEG compression; (3) Hybrid: both signals simultaneously. A conditioning dropout rate p_un (tuned to 0.1) ensures the model remains reliable in the unguided regime, which is essential for producing trustworthy look-ahead estimates. A prior-step shortcut uses the cached x̂₁ from the previous step instead of running the full look-ahead pass, reducing inference cost from 2N to N+1 function evaluations with a controllable threshold t_thresh. The framework integrates with existing adapter architectures (ControlNet, LoRA) without architectural changes beyond expanding input channels. Image experiments use Stable Diffusion 3.5 Large + ControlNet trained on Unsplash-25K (20k/5k split) for 6,250 steps on A100s (51–61 GPU hours). 3D texturing experiments fine-tune TRELLIS-2 with LoRA rank 128 on 7,500 Objaverse assets for 25,000 steps on 4× RTX PRO 6000 GPUs (34 hours). Key Results On super-resolution (8× downsampling, Unsplash-25K test): zero-order achieves PSNR 39.25 / LPIPS 0.21 / FID 3.36 vs. Standard FT 34.35 / 0.83 / 3.93; first-order (∇x̂₁) reaches PSNR 44.07 / LPIPS 0.33 / FID 6.45; inference-time guidance (FlowChef) hits PSNR 43.02 but FID 18.96, confirming the fidelity-plausibility trade-off. On JPEG restoration (non-differentiable operator, zero-order only): PSNR 28.86 vs. 26.29 for Standard FT, FID 3.80 vs. 4.35. On depth-to-RGB: first-order (∇x_t) achieves MAE 0.0747 / δ₁.₂₅ 0.8268 / FID 14.57 vs. Standard FT 0.0848 / 0.7882 / 18.21; IT Guidance achieves MAE 0.0866 / FID 223.54. On 3D mesh texturing (Objaverse, ∇x̂₁ variant): M.PSNR 26.39 / MV-LPIPS 0.0098 / FID 6.64 vs. Standard FT 21.91 / 0.0115 / 8.74 and IT Guidance 25.86 / 0.0123 / 9.10. Toys4K results are consistent: 27.26 vs. 23.00 (Standard FT) and 26.82 (IT Guidance). The analysis in §5.4 decomposes the learned correction: cos(Δv, s_t^grad) = 0.42 ± 0.11 with 80% of energy in the orthogonal component, stable across t ∈ [0.1, 0.9]. Doubling sampling steps or adding CFG to open-loop baselines does not close the gap to FlowBender without CFG, confirming the benefit is structural, not computational. Baselines are current and appropriate (ControlNet++, FlowChef). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The closed-loop error-feedback training paradigm applied to conditional diffusion and flow-matching models is the central contribution; the individual components (two-pass inference, stop-gradient on feedback, conditioning dropout) are well-motivated engineering choices rather than independent novelties. The paper draws an explicit and honest lineage from learned iterative refinement in inverse problems and learned optimizers, claiming (plausibly) to be the first to apply this paradigm to conditional generative models. The zero-order variant enabling black-box/non-differentiable operators is a useful extension rather than a separate contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for the key hyperparameters: p_un (Table 3), t_thresh (Figure 6), and t_min for the alignment-loss baseline (Table 5). Standard deviations are reported across all extended tables (Tables 6–11), though FID—a population-level metric—has no confidence interval. The test set for image tasks is 5,000 images, adequate but not large; the 3D texturing test set is 100 + 100 assets, which is small enough that FID estimates carry meaningful variance. A project page is listed (flow-bender.github.io) but explicit code or model-weight releases are not stated in the paper text. One potential methodological concern: the 3D texturing experiment modifies the data preparation pipeline (conditioning images rendered with the TRELLIS-2 PBR renderer rather than Blender) to ensure operator consistency; this means the training distribution differs from the standard TRELLIS-2 setup, making the Standard FT baseline slightly disadvantaged relative to an off-the-shelf ControlNet++ comparison. The paper acknowledges this choice but does not quantify its effect on the baseline. No significance tests are reported beyond standard deviations. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on commercially available NVIDIA A100 / RTX PRO 6000 GPUs; the base model (SD3.5 Large) is memory-intensive but widely accessible; fine-tuning costs 50–60 GPU hours, comparable to standard ControlNet training. - Efficient: Training overhead is 35–60% relative to Standard FT (51–61 vs. 38 GPU hours for image tasks); default inference doubles NFEs (2N), but the prior-step shortcut reduces this to N+1 while maintaining gains over open-loop baselines even at t_thresh = 0. - Effective: Evidence is consistent across four image tasks and two 3D datasets, covering differentiable, neural-network-based, and non-differentiable operators; the simultaneous fidelity and plausibility improvements are the most credible evidence that the method addresses the stated problem rather than a proxy. - Scalable: Evaluated at 1024² pixels for image tasks and on assets up to 8,192 sparse voxels for 3D; no experiments at larger scales; the prior-step shortcut's approximation quality (Figure 6a–b) presumably degrades for trajectories where the feedback signal changes rapidly, but this is not tested beyond the analyzed tasks. The work delivers fine-tuned adapter weights (LoRA/ControlNet) rather than a standalone software library or released checkpoints. Strengths - The central framing—the model is never trained to use its own alignment error—is a crisp, correct diagnosis of a real failure mode shared by two dominant paradigms, and the fix is mechanistically clean. - The zero-order variant genuinely extends the framework to non-differentiable and black-box operators (JPEG, third-party APIs), where all prior gradient-based guidance methods fail; this is validated experimentally rather than just claimed. - The orthogonal decomposition analysis (§5.4) provides genuine mechanistic evidence that the learned correction is not gradient guidance with better hyperparameters—this is the kind of analysis that distinguishes scientific contribution from benchmark chasing. - The prior-step shortcut is well-motivated (backed by the temporal similarity plots in Figure 6a–b) and practically important, reducing inference cost to near-open-loop efficiency while retaining most corrective benefit. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge two limitations: (1) training requires an extra model evaluation per iteration to compute the look-ahead estimate, increasing fine-tuning cost, and (2) performance sometimes further improves when optional CFG is added on top of FlowBender, suggesting the learned policy has not fully internalized the most complex conditioning nuances. Both are presented honestly, with directions for future work (cached prior-step predictions to reduce training cost; more expressive feedback-integration architectures). This is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No ablation against a pure self-conditioning baseline (feeding x̂₁ back without computing H(x̂₁)−y). This is the critical missing experiment: if simply recirculating the prior clean estimate (as in Analog Bits / self-conditioning) already accounts for most of the gain, the role of the alignment error signal is overstated. The paper cites self-conditioning as related work but does not compare against it. - The stop-gradient on the feedback signal is a critical design choice asserted to improve stability, but no ablation with backpropagation through the look-ahead pass is provided, leaving the necessity of this choice unverified. - The 3D texturing baseline comparison is confounded: the data preparation pipeline was modified for FlowBender's operator consistency (different renderer than vanilla TRELLIS-2), meaning Standard FT is not trained on the exact distribution that TRELLIS-2 was designed for. An ablation training Standard FT on the same modified data would clarify how much of the baseline gap is from this mismatch vs. the feedback mechanism. - Evaluation datasets are small (5k test images for image tasks; 100+100 objects for 3D), which limits statistical confidence in FID comparisons and raises questions about whether results generalize beyond the Unsplash photography domain. - The paper does not evaluate on a standard benchmark dataset (e.g., FFHQ for super-resolution, NYU Depth for depth-conditioned generation), making direct comparison with broader literature difficult. Bottom Line A practitioner building constraint-aware conditional generators or inverse-problem solvers with flow/diffusion models gains a concrete, implementable framework with clear empirical advantages over both supervised and guidance-based paradigms—and the mechanistic analysis is unusually thorough for this type of paper, making it worth reading rather than just citing.
Recommended 2606.20404 PDF
#50 — Innovative (69)

Explainable ensemble learning using SHAP for ERP anomaly detection

A Qazi, AAK Jadoon · nature.com · simulation environments for business processes - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a heterogeneous six-model ensemble (Isolation Forest, Local Outlier Factor, One-Class SVM, Elliptic Envelope, Gradient Boosting, Robust Autoencoder) with a three-layer SHAP-based explainability framework for detecting anomalous ERP procurement transactions, evaluated on 209,666 real-world records with a rigorous independent forensic audit ground truth. - Why it's important: ERP fraud evades detection in 85–93% of cases under manual audit sampling regimes, costs organizations a median $145,000 per incident with an 18-month detection lag, and regulatory frameworks (SOX 404, COSO, ISO 27001) mandate interpretable rather than black-box detection — a combination that prior work has not addressed jointly in production. - How it does it: Six models spanning distinct detection geometries (density, boundary, tree, reconstruction) are combined via F-score-weighted voting, with the design justified by Pareto-front analysis over all 63 non-trivial model subsets on three simultaneous criteria (F-score stability, false-positive rate, anomaly coverage), and every flagged transaction explained through three complementary XAI layers targeting different organizational stakeholders. - The good: The ensemble is evaluated on genuine production ERP data with an externally conducted forensic audit as ground truth (Cohen's κ = 0.81 between independent auditors), and the paper honestly reports that the ensemble is statistically significantly lower in peak F-score than its three best constituent models (GB: 0.9321, AE: 0.9254, EE: 0.9182 vs. ensemble: 0.8437), explaining and formally justifying the trade-off rather than concealing it. - The missing: All five generalizability sensitivity scenarios are constructed by perturbing the same single-organization dataset rather than validating on independent external ERP deployments from different organizations or industries, which limits the force of the transferability claims despite being labeled a generalizability analysis. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners deploying anomaly detection in ERP or regulatory-audit contexts who need both a working detection pipeline and audit-defensible explanations; researchers seeking novel ML methodology will find it a careful engineering integration of established techniques applied to an underserved domain.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (built/deployed). Rigor (80) is the standout and the reason this tops the cluster: independently-constructed forensic-audit ground truth (Cohen's κ=0.81), exhaustive Pareto-front justification over all 63 model subsets, 95% CIs, parametric and non-parametric tests, public code on Zenodo, and peer review in Scientific Reports. Completeness (80) and Impact (75) are reinforced by genuine field validation — a 28.2% post-deployment fraud confirmation rate on transactions reviewed by the external firm, not just held-out metrics — and by the intellectual honesty of tabling that the ensemble is significantly worse than its best constituents on peak F-score. Novelty (55) and Non-Obviousness (55) are the limiters: this is a careful engineering integration of established techniques, not an algorithmic advance, and the single-organization evaluation (the 'generalizability' scenarios are perturbations of one dataset) plus approximate SHAP for four of six models keep it below the Highly-Innovative band.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 80 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation ERP systems in manufacturing generate hundreds of thousands of daily procurement, inventory, and financial transactions. Business-process fraud and data anomalies persistently evade detection: the ACFE 2023 report documents a $145,000 average loss per incident and an 18-month median detection lag. The paper's own dataset confirms an 85–93% evasion rate under the organization's pre-existing sampling-based manual audit program. Three structural challenges define this domain: (i) multi-perspective dependencies across control-flow, resource, and attribute dimensions that univariate monitors miss; (ii) a high-dimensional sparse feature space (178 raw attributes); and (iii) severe class imbalance (6.05% anomaly rate) with scarce ground-truth labels that undermines purely supervised approaches. Regulatory audit requirements (SOX 404, COSO, ISO 27001) additionally demand interpretable, traceable flagging decisions that black-box neural network approaches — which achieve F-scores above 0.90 on synthetic benchmarks — cannot provide. The specific gap addressed is the joint integration of multi-geometry ensemble detection with multi-stakeholder explainability in a production ERP context under regulatory constraints. Key Idea The core insight is deliberate Pareto-optimal ensemble design: combining six models that span four detection geometries (density via LOF, boundary via OCSVM, global isolation via IF, distributional via Elliptic Envelope, tree-based supervised via Gradient Boosting, reconstruction via Autoencoder) intentionally sacrifices peak F-score in exchange for cross-validation stability (CV 0.49% vs. 1.4–2.8% for individual models), false-positive rate reduction (19.4%), and detection breadth (5,055 anomalies caught by no single model). The design choice is uniquely justified by exhaustive enumeration of all 63 non-trivial model subsets and confirmed as the unique Pareto-optimal configuration when all three operational objectives and the three-layer XAI requirement are considered jointly. A secondary insight is that three complementary explanation layers — global feature importance (executive-level), surrogate decision rules (compliance-officer level), and SHAP instance attribution (forensic-analyst level) — serve distinct stakeholder audiences from a single detection pipeline. Methodology The dataset consists of 209,666 procurement and inventory transactions (178 raw features: 52 resource identifiers, 41 financial values, 28 status indicators, 57 metadata fields) from a single mid-market multi-plant manufacturing organization over nine months. Ground truth was established through a three-stage independent forensic audit: an external firm produced 18,412 candidate anomalies via rule-based filters; two CIA-credentialed auditors independently adjudicated with Cohen's κ = 0.81; 3,217 disagreements were resolved by a third auditor; yielding 12,682 confirmed anomalies (6.05%). Feature engineering (nested within cross-validation folds, with train/test split preceding all selection): 178 → 48 via Gradient Boosting RFE (iterative bottom-10% removal until CV F-score degrades >1%), → VIF filtering (threshold 5, mean VIF 1.84), → Mann-Whitney U test (p < 0.05), → domain-expert leakage review by a team independent from the labeling auditors. Final 29 features span financial (8), quantity (5), control-flow (6), resource (6), derived (4) categories, retaining 92.3% of discriminative power. Preprocessing (within folds): infinite-value replacement at 99th percentile, median imputation (0.83% missingness), 1st/99th percentile clipping, robust standardization ((x − median)/IQR). Class imbalance is addressed via contamination parameter overcorrection (0.10 vs. observed 0.0605), imbalance-aware metrics (F-score, AUC-PR, recall), stratified CV, and precision–recall threshold optimization (τ = 0.25 for alerting, τ = 0.50 for test metrics). SMOTE was tested and rejected for providing negligible improvement (+0.003 F-score within CV noise) and incompatibility with unsupervised constituent models. Six models with specified hyperparameters: IF (100 trees, max_samples=256, contamination=0.10), LOF (k=20, Euclidean), OCSVM (RBF, ν=0.10), EE (MCD, contamination=0.10), GB (100 stages, lr=0.1, max_depth=5, subsample=0.8), Robust AE (29→64→32→16→32→64→29, BatchNorm, Dropout(0.2), two residual connections, Huber loss δ=1.35, Adam lr=10⁻³, early stopping, 31,446 trainable parameters). Ensemble aggregation: F-score-weighted average (Equation 2), yielding weights wIF=0.1427, wLOF=0.1300, wOCSVM=0.1739, wEE=0.1831, wGB=0.1858, wAE=0.1845. Three critical design decisions: (1) contamination=0.10 above observed 6.05% rate to reduce false-negative risk — justified on boundary-uncertainty grounds but not ablated across the range [0.06, 0.15]; (2) F-score weighting rather than uniform voting — justified by correlation analysis showing the three top models have pairwise error correlations of 0.68–0.74 while lower-performing models add complementary coverage; (3) KernelExplainer (100 background samples) for IF, LOF, OCSVM, and EE — produces approximate rather than exact SHAP values, a computational compromise whose effect on attribution accuracy is not quantified. Statistical validation: 5-fold stratified CV; paired t-test (Shapiro-Wilk normality check on fold differences: W=0.94, p=0.42); Wilcoxon signed-rank non-parametric alternative; Cohen's dz effect size. Generalizability assessed via five controlled perturbation scenarios applied to the held-out test partition with ensemble retrained on corresponding perturbed training partition: low prevalence (2%), high prevalence (15%), financial-module-only (14 of 29 features), distribution shift (±20% mean), cross-industry covariance shuffling. Key Results On the held-out test set (n=41,933, 20% stratified split): F-score 0.8437 [0.8305, 0.8569], Precision 0.8856, Recall 0.8034, AUC-PR 0.8923, AUC-ROC 0.9187. Five-fold CV: F-score 0.8444 ± 0.0041 (CV=0.49%). Individual constituent model F-scores — GB: 0.9321 (CV=1.8%), AE: 0.9254 (CV=2.1%), EE: 0.9182 (CV=1.4%), OCSVM: 0.8723 (CV=1.6%), IF: 0.7154 (CV=2.3%), LOF: 0.6521 (CV=2.8%) — all exceed the ensemble on peak F-score. The ensemble is statistically significantly lower than GB, AE, and EE (paired t-tests, all p<0.001); the gains are cross-validation stability (CV ratio 0.23–0.35) and unique anomaly coverage (+18.2%–+35.8%). The ensemble FP rate (11.44%) is 19.4% lower than the unweighted mean constituent FP rate (14.1%). Pareto-front over all 63 subsets confirms no subset achieves equal or better F-score, lower CV, and higher anomaly coverage simultaneously. Generalizability sensitivity: F-score range [0.794, 0.862] across all five scenarios; largest degradation under financial-module-only (ΔF = −0.049, CV rises to 1.14%). Expert assessment (n=12, single organization, within-subject crossover): classification accuracy 0.682→0.831 (p=0.009, McNemar's), decision time 4.2→2.7 min (p=0.004), confidence 2.9→4.4/5 (p<0.001). Production deployment: 28.2% fraud confirmation rate on 13,682 flagged transactions reviewed post-training by the same external forensic firm; conservative ROI 392% ($3.27M net benefit from $4.1M prevented fraud); Monte Carlo (n=10,000) yields positive ROI in 98.7% of scenarios; break-even confirmation rate 8.4% vs. observed 28.2%. Baselines are internally strong (all 6 constituent models, Pareto-front over 63 subsets) but the external comparison (Table 7) is explicitly labeled indicative due to incompatible dataset types, supervision levels, and granularities. The 150ms per-transaction scoring latency is reported against a 10ms real-time threshold and acknowledged as not meeting real-time requirements. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (engineering integration) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The paper's primary novelty is the joint application of heterogeneous six-model ensemble detection with Pareto-front justification and three-layer multi-stakeholder SHAP-based explainability to production ERP anomaly detection under regulatory constraints. Every individual component — SHAP explanation of autoencoders, surrogate decision trees, OCSVM, IF, ensemble voting — has clear prior-art antecedents that the authors explicitly acknowledge. The NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING contribution is the demonstration of a 28.2% post-deployment fraud confirmation rate and $3.27M net benefit in a real manufacturing deployment, which provides genuine evidence of field utility that synthetic-benchmark papers lack. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and appropriate: leave-one-model-out ablation over all six models, Pareto-front over all 63 non-trivial subsets, hyperparameter sensitivity (F-score variance <8% across model-internal parameters). Error bars: 95% CIs on all test metrics; fold-level standard deviations reported. Statistical tests include both parametric (paired t-test with Shapiro-Wilk normality check) and non-parametric alternatives (Wilcoxon signed-rank), with explicit caveats on effect-size interpretation. Code and synthetic data generation script are publicly released on Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20609426); the original dataset is available on reasonable request (not publicly released). The paper is peer-reviewed (Scientific Reports, accepted June 2026), not a preprint. Methodological concerns: (1) The four-stage feature selection pipeline (RFE → VIF → Mann-Whitney → domain review) is conducted within nested CV folds, which is correct, but the Mann-Whitney location-shift assumption is violated for 5 of 29 features (bimodal or heavy-tailed anomaly distributions), flagged to domain experts but not statistically corrected. (2) KernelExplainer with 100 background samples for four of six constituent models produces approximate SHAP values; this is acknowledged but the consequent accuracy loss on Layer 3 attributions is not quantified. (3) Contamination overcorrection (0.10 vs. 0.0605) is not ablated. (4) The expert-assessment study (n=12) from the same organization as the training data is too small for the scale of the explainability claim. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (built, containerized, production deployed). - Practical: Runs on a GPU workstation (NVIDIA A100, 8-core CPU, 64GB RAM); the A100 is commercially available but high-end, and the paper does not evaluate CPU-only or lower-tier GPU deployment. - Efficient: Per-transaction scoring latency ~150ms (sequential through six models); SHAP computed asynchronously in batch (~49s/day for ~2,613 flagged transactions); 27.30s for 41,933-transaction parallelized fold prediction. LOF dominates prediction cost (12.34s/fold). Memory footprint not reported. The 150ms scoring latency exceeds real-time (<10ms) requirements, limiting use to post-transaction batch audit workflows rather than inline approval-gate deployment. - Effective: The 28.2% post-deployment fraud confirmation rate on transactions scored by the production model and reviewed by the independent external forensic firm (not the training-set auditors) provides genuine evidence that the model detects real fraud rather than benchmark artifacts. - Scalable: Tested at ~200K transactions over nine months from one organization. Behavior at 10×–100× volume (enterprise-scale multi-national ERP) is not tested; quarterly retraining on a sliding 12-month window is described but not evaluated for computational feasibility at larger scale. Delivers: containerized production service (Docker/MLflow), three-layer XAI dashboard, and Zenodo code repository. Stops short of a reusable open-source library. Strengths - The forensic-audit ground truth construction (independent external firm, two CIA-credentialed auditors with Cohen's κ = 0.81, third-auditor tie-breaking, leakage-independence check) is substantially more rigorous than self-labeled or synthetically generated anomaly labels common in this literature. - The Pareto-front analysis over all 63 non-trivial model subsets is a principled, exhaustive justification of the six-model design choice rather than post-hoc assertion; it formally demonstrates why each constituent is necessary and that no subset simultaneously dominates on all three operational objectives. - The paper honestly tables and statistically confirms that the ensemble is significantly worse than its three best constituents on peak F-score (p<0.001 in all three pairwise comparisons), and reports this as a deliberate design trade-off — a degree of intellectual honesty uncommon in ensemble papers. - The post-deployment field validation (28.2% fraud confirmation rate on new transactions, reviewed by the same external forensic firm that established training ground truth) provides genuine evidence of field utility beyond held-out test set metrics. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge four limitations directly and specifically: (1) single legal entity, procurement/inventory modules only — no external dataset validation despite the simulated generalizability scenarios; federated learning and meta-learning identified as future work. (2) Semi-supervised extension with ~1,000–3,000 labeled samples could improve F-scores to 0.92–0.94. (3) ~150ms per-transaction latency exceeds real-time (<10ms) requirements; model parallelization and ONNX optimization proposed. (4) Concept drift and adversarial adaptation under quarterly retraining with KL-divergence drift monitoring are described but formal online drift detection (ADWIN) and adversarial robustness testing remain future work. The expert-assessment external validity caveat is explicitly noted (12 experts from one organization). Authors confront limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The five generalizability sensitivity scenarios are all constructed by perturbing the same single-organization dataset (resampling, feature-set restriction, covariance shuffling) — they are controlled distribution perturbations, not independent external ERP deployments. The paper labels these results as generalizability evidence, but no transaction from a different organization, ERP vendor, or industry sector was used. The distinction between simulated and genuine cross-enterprise validation is noted in passing but understated in the framing of results. - The expert-assessment study (n=12) draws all participants from the compliance function of the same manufacturing organization that provided the training data. These auditors may have informal familiarity with the fraud cases in the dataset, creating a potential familiarity bias that the crossover design does not control for. The n=12 sample also provides very limited power for the McNemar's test. - KernelExplainer with 100 background samples is used for four of six constituent models (IF, LOF, OCSVM, EE); for an explainability-first paper making regulatory compliance claims, the accuracy of Layer 3 attributions from these approximate SHAP estimates versus exact alternatives is never characterized, leaving the forensic-analysis use case partially unvalidated. - The contamination parameter overcorrection (0.10 vs. 0.0605 actual rate, a 65% inflation) systematically biases unsupervised models toward higher recall and lower precision; this hyperparameter is not ablated across the range [0.06, 0.15] despite being the most operationally consequential setting for audit workload. - The ROI Monte Carlo uses uniform distributions (U[2000, 8000] for average prevented loss per flagged transaction) that are internally generated assumptions not anchored to the fraud confirmation rate distribution observed in the actual deployment; the 98.7% positive-ROI claim therefore reflects the generosity of the assumed parameter ranges rather than independent empirical evidence. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying ERP anomaly detection under regulatory audit constraints — where explainability is mandatory and a 28% post-deployment fraud confirmation rate from real transactions constitutes meaningful field validation — will find concrete, honestly characterized implementation guidance here; but the single-organization evaluation scope, approximate SHAP for four of six models, and the very small expert study mean these results should be treated as promising case evidence rather than definitive cross-enterprise benchmarks.
Search Results
#51 — Innovative (69)

OmniVideo-100K: A Dataset for Audio-Visual Reasoning through Structured Scripts and Evidence Chains (23 ▲ )

Xinyue Cai, Chaoyou Fu, Yi-Fan Zhang, Ran He, Caifeng Shan · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OmniVideo-100K, a 100K-sample instruction-tuning dataset for audio-visual QA built via a two-stage automated pipeline — Entity-Anchored Video Scripting and Clue-Guided QA Generation — that produces cross-temporal, cross-modal reasoning questions, along with OmniVideo-Test, a 505-sample human-verified evaluation benchmark. - Why it's important: Existing automated AV-QA pipelines generate separate per-modality captions for short independent clips, producing training data that fails to teach MLLMs genuine cross-modal reasoning, leaving current open-source omni-modal models below 55% accuracy on tasks that require joint audio-visual inference. - How it does it: A global entity list extracted before segmentation enforces referential consistency across segments and anchors speaker diarization; a two-step clue mining phase then forces the LLM to identify multi-segment, cross-modal evidence chains before generating QA pairs, rather than generating questions directly from concatenated captions. - The good: Fine-tuning on OmniVideo-100K yields 20.59% / 17.82% / 13.86% gains on OmniVideo-Test for VITA-1.5-7B / Qwen2.5-Omni-7B / Qwen3-Omni-30B respectively, with consistent cross-benchmark generalization including +12.64% on JointAVBench and +17.23% on Daily-Omni's AV Event Alignment subtask — with no catastrophic forgetting on general video benchmarks. - The missing: OmniVideo-Test is seeded by the same Gemini pipeline used to build the training set; human filtering reduces but does not eliminate the risk that gains partly reflect learning Gemini-style question formats rather than genuine cross-modal reasoning, and the ablation studies use only 20 videos each with no reported significance tests. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers training or fine-tuning omni-modal video LLMs who need principled cross-modal training data; less relevant if your focus is on video-only understanding or model architecture.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (the core artifact is an automated data-synthesis pipeline plus released dataset and checkpoints). Practical Impact (78) leads — OmniVideo-100K, pipeline code, and fine-tuned checkpoints are released and yield consistent, directly usable gains across six benchmarks with no catastrophic forgetting. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (65) are solid: the entity-anchored scripting and clue-first/generate-second decomposition is a genuinely novel composition with the non-obvious insight that QA quality is gated by intermediate-representation quality. Rigor (65) is pulled down by the shared Gemini lineage between train and test (Goodhart risk), small 20-video ablations, no significance tests, and a self-referential Gemini-judges-Gemini loop. Resilience (58) reflects that the headline gains could partly reflect distribution matching rather than learned cross-modal reasoning. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*58 + 0.30*78 = 69.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 70 Resilience 58 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Automated audio-visual QA dataset construction typically follows a "video-caption-QA" paradigm: videos are segmented into short clips, per-modality descriptions are generated independently (visual captions separate from speech transcripts and audio labels), and QA pairs are generated directly from the concatenated text. This creates three compounding problems: (1) separating modalities severs inherent sound-source associations (e.g., a character's speech cannot be tied to their on-screen presence); (2) independent segment captioning causes entity inconsistency, where the same person may be described by different physical descriptors across segments, preventing cross-segment entity tracking; and (3) generating QA directly from long descriptions without forcing temporal spanning produces questions answerable from localized events, failing to require the cross-segment, cross-modal inference that distinguishes true audio-visual understanding. The consequence is instruction-tuning datasets that do not teach models to perform the reasoning that omni-modal benchmarks demand. Key Idea The core trick is to decompose video-to-QA generation into two fully explicit intermediate representations rather than collapsing it into a single pass. First, convert the video into a structured script where a globally extracted entity list (with descriptive unique identifiers per person/object) constrains all segment captions and speaker assignments — forcing narrative coherence as a structural prior rather than relying on the LLM to maintain consistency implicitly. Second, before generating any QA pair, require a separate LLM pass to enumerate multi-segment causal chains (clue chains) that can only be resolved by combining audio from one segment with visuals from another; the QA generator then receives only these pre-verified clues and the relevant segments, not the full script. This clue-first, generation-second decomposition is what produces questions with measured average temporal spans of 144.75s vs. 76.24s for direct generation. Methodology Stage 1 — Entity-Anchored Video Scripting: An MLLM (Gemini-2.5-Pro or Gemini-3-Pro) first processes the entire raw video to extract a Main Entity List — a set of descriptive unique identifiers (e.g., "Brunette Girl") with feature descriptions for all narratively significant people, animals, and objects. Audio is processed separately: speech is transcribed with start/end timestamps and speaker labels assigned by joint audio-visual diarization using the entity list as a prior; non-speech sounds are logged with objective category labels and timestamps. Video is segmented into ~15s primary segments using speech timestamps as hard boundaries (preventing mid-utterance cuts); short adjacent segments are merged. Visual captions are generated per segment analyzing four dimensions (Setting & Environment, Characters & Objects, Actions & Interactions, Cinematography), with the entity list injected as a constraint to enforce consistent naming across all segments. The complete script integrates: video summary, entity list, and timestamped segment entries containing speaker-labeled speech, non-speech sounds, and visual captions. Stage 2 — Clue-Guided QA Generation: For tasks requiring long-term reasoning (Causal Reasoning, Future Prediction, Hypothetical Reasoning), a two-step process applies. In Step 1 (Global Clue Mining), an LLM scans the full script and identifies all causal chains that span at least two non-consecutive segments and require combining audio from one segment with visuals from another — producing structured output including relevant timestamps and a logical description of the cross-modal link. In Step 2 (Locally Focused Generation), a separate LLM call receives only the clue chain, the relevant segment excerpts, and a generation prompt to produce an open-ended QA pair or MCQ; the focused context prevents attention dilution from irrelevant script content. For Alignment and Context Understanding tasks (localized events), the two-step process collapses to a single pass. MCQ distractors are generated with three hard-negative strategies: plausible inference traps, missing-modality answers (correct if only one modality used), and semantic drift (e.g., "arrogance" vs. "confidence"). Validation checks (blind test, simple match test, unimodal trap) gate final output. Dataset composition: 100K training QA pairs from 5,214 videos (avg 103s, ≥480p, English, no hard-coded subtitles, 7 content categories, OE:MCQ = 7:3, 10 tasks distributed evenly). OmniVideo-Test: 505 MCQ from 264 videos; pipeline-generated then human-filtered with explicit criteria for cross-modal dependency, factual accuracy, and answer uniqueness — ~38.14% pipeline pass rate. Fine-tuning: full-parameter, 1 epoch, batch 32, lr 1×10⁻⁵ (5×10⁻⁶ for Qwen3-Omni), using LLaMA-Factory for Qwen-series and official implementation for VITA-1.5. Key Results On OmniVideo-Test, fine-tuning on OmniVideo-100K improves VITA-1.5-7B from 40.99% to 61.58% (+20.59%), Qwen2.5-Omni-7B from 42.77% to 60.59% (+17.82%), and Qwen3-Omni-30B from 49.70% to 63.56% (+13.86%). The Gemini-3.1-Pro ceiling is 83.96%; best unfine-tuned open-source is MiniCPM-o 4.5 at 55.25%. Gains are largest on Alignment tasks (e.g., +31.03% for VITA-1.5 on Alignment, +23.27% for Qwen2.5-Omni). On cross-benchmark evaluation: Qwen2.5-Omni gains +7.43% on Daily-Omni (driven by +17.23% on AV Event Alignment), +6.31% on JointAVBench, +6.15% on FutureOmni; VITA-1.5 gains +12.64% on JointAVBench. On Video-MME-short (general video), fine-tuned models are essentially flat relative to baseline (75.56→76.33 for Qwen2.5-Omni), confirming no catastrophic forgetting. Dataset comparison: fine-tuning Qwen2.5-Omni on AVQA drops Video-MME-short to 68.11 (from 75.56 baseline) and OmniVideo-Test to 6.28%; JavisInst-Und drops most metrics; OmniVideo-100K is the only dataset that produces consistent gains across all benchmarks. Data scaling: average performance across benchmarks peaks at 75K samples (Avg. 54.32%) with slight saturation at 100K (52.98%), attributable partly to Video-MME-v2 and OmniVideoBench fluctuations. A noteworthy failure mode: fine-tuned Qwen3-Omni drops on OmniVideoBench's "sound" category by 4.21%, traced to coarse-grained non-speech sound descriptions in the scripting phase. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (primary), NEW METHOD (data synthesis pipeline). The entity-anchored scripting approach and the explicit two-stage clue-mining-then-generation framework are genuinely novel in the data curation literature, as opposed to prior work that either generates captions independently per modality or performs post-hoc alignment. The individual components (MLLM-based captioning, speaker diarization, structured prompting) are standard; the novelty is in their composition and the insight that QA quality is gated by the intermediate representation quality rather than the QA generation step itself. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover all four major design decisions: entity list presence/absence (entity confusion 23.4% vs. 36.7%; AV mismatch 10% vs. 20%), clue-guided vs. direct generation (avg temporal span 144.75s vs. 76.24s; harder questions confirmed by model accuracy gaps), script-based vs. video-based pipeline (temporal span gap 37.88s overall, 79.65s for 8–14 min videos), and data scaling (10K to 100K). Ablation sample sizes are small (20 videos per experiment, 60–71 QA pairs), and no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests appear anywhere in the paper. LLM-as-judge (Gemini-3-Pro) is used to evaluate ablation QA quality, creating a self-referential evaluation loop since Gemini-3-Pro also generates the data. OmniVideo-Test uses human verification with a documented pass rate (38.14%), and cross-modal dependency of test items is validated by unimodal ablation (Table 3). Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/MiG-NJU/OmniVideo-100K. The pipeline cost (number of Gemini API calls per video, total tokens, dollar cost) is unreported, limiting reproducibility assessment for researchers without equivalent API access. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.14702v2, June 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (the paper primarily evaluates fine-tuned MLLM performance) and SYSTEMS (an automated data generation pipeline is the core artifact). - Practical: Fine-tuned models are 7B–30B parameters, runnable on standard A100/H100 hardware; however, the data generation pipeline requires Gemini-2.5-Pro or Gemini-3-Pro (API access only), which is not freely available and creates a replication barrier. - Efficient: Pipeline compute cost is not quantified; each video undergoes at minimum 6–8 sequential MLLM inference passes (entity extraction, transcription, sound logging, speaker diarization, visual captioning per segment, clue mining, QA generation), implying substantial API cost for 5,214 videos; fine-tuning cost uses 1 epoch at standard hyperparameters and is fully reproducible. - Effective: The evidence for effectiveness is strong: consistent multi-model, multi-benchmark gains with no catastrophic forgetting, and cross-modal dependency validated by unimodal ablation showing performance drops when a single modality is removed from test inputs. - Scalable: Performance saturates around 75K samples; the pipeline scales to any video collection with API access, but quality degradation for very long videos (>14 min), non-English content, and complex non-speech audio (documented -4.21% on sound category) remains unaddressed. The work delivers a dataset (OmniVideo-100K and OmniVideo-Test), pipeline code, and fine-tuned model checkpoints — it is not purely paper-form. Strengths - The two-stage pipeline architecture explicitly operationalizes "cross-modal reasoning" as a structural property of data generation rather than a post-hoc filter; the ablation results provide direct causal evidence that both the entity list (reduces entity confusion by 13.3 percentage points) and the clue-guided step (increases temporal span by 68.51s) independently contribute. - OmniVideo-Test's human verification with a stringent 38.14% pass rate and an explicit criterion rejecting single-modality-answerable questions sets a higher quality bar than most automated AV benchmarks; the unimodal ablation in Table 3 empirically confirms that test items require cross-modal synergy. - Robust cross-benchmark validation across six established benchmarks (Video-MME, Daily-Omni, OmniVideoBench, JointAVBench, FutureOmni, Video-MME-v2) with consistent positive results and explicit dataset comparison (Table 7) showing OmniVideo-100K outperforms both manual (AVQA) and automated (JavisInst-Und) alternatives. - The data scaling study provides actionable guidance (performance peaks at 75K, confirming the pipeline is efficient and not requiring the full 100K to realize most gains). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify three limitations: (1) coarse-grained non-speech sound descriptions cause a 4.21% performance drop on OmniVideoBench's sound category, because general category labels introduce noise into QA generation for complex acoustic events; (2) the fixed ~15s segmentation target lacks adaptivity to content; (3) fine-grained sounds and paralinguistic features (e.g., prosody, hesitation) are not captured without specialized audio models. The authors propose integrating specialized audio models and adaptive segmentation as future work. These are confronted directly and with supporting evidence. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The central Goodhart's Law risk: OmniVideo-Test QA pairs are seeded by Gemini-2.5-Pro/3-Pro — the same model family that generates the training data — and human filtering removes factual errors and single-modality-answerable items but cannot fully remove stylistic bias toward the Gemini generation pattern. An independently constructed evaluation set (fully human-annotated or generated by a different pipeline) would provide cleaner evidence that gains reflect learned capability rather than distribution matching. - Ablation statistical validity: 20 videos per ablation experiment is a small sample for a claim about data pipeline design; Gemini-3-Pro judging QA quality generated by Gemini-3-Pro is self-referential and introduces an unknown systematic bias favoring the proposed pipeline. - Pipeline cost unreported: no API call counts, token counts, or dollar estimates are provided for building OmniVideo-100K, making it impossible for readers to assess feasibility without proprietary API access. - The counterintuitive result that Qwen3-Omni-30B (49.70%) underperforms MiniCPM-o-4.5-9B (55.25%) on OmniVideo-Test (3× the parameters, worse baseline) is not analyzed or explained. - The dataset is English-only; no evaluation of pipeline quality or cross-lingual transfer is discussed. - No analysis of failure cases in the fine-tuned models beyond the sound-category regression and qualitative examples. Bottom Line A practitioner fine-tuning an omni-modal video LLM for cross-modal reasoning tasks gains directly usable, high-quality training data from OmniVideo-100K with documented, consistent gains — but should supplement OmniVideo-Test with independent benchmarks when claiming capability improvements, given the shared generative lineage between train and test sets.
Recommended 2606.14702 PDF
#52 — Innovative (68)

Playful Agentic Robot Learning (35 ▲ )

Junyi Zhang, Jiaxin Ge, Hanjun Yoo, Letian Fu, Zihan Yang, Yaowei Liu, Raj Saravanan, Shaofeng Yin, Justin Yu, Dantong Niu, Zirui Wang, Roei Herzig, Ken Goldberg, Yutong Bai, David M. Chan, Ion Stoica, Angjoo Kanazawa, Jiahui Lei, Haiwen Feng, Trevor Darrell · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RATS (Robotics Agent Teams), a multi-agent Code-as-Policy framework that acquires reusable robot manipulation skills through autonomous self-directed play before any downstream task is specified, then retrieves those skills at test time without fine-tuning the underlying model. - Why it's important: Current agentic robot systems learn skills only reactively — upon receiving explicit instructions — forcing every new task to rediscover manipulation primitives (grasp geometry, articulation directions, object localization) from scratch and leaving substantial compute idle between deployments. - How it does it: RATS selects play tasks using a Goldilocks objective that maximizes the product of object-skill novelty and a competence-frontier term (peaking at ~50% Wilson-bounded success rate), executes them through a Write-Execute-Verify-Diagnose loop, and distills successful trajectories into a reliability-tiered Python code skill library. - The good: A compute-matched ablation (Table 14) shows that spending the same token budget proactively during play (32.3% average LIBERO-PRO success) outperforms giving CaP-Agent0 proportionally more test-time retries (26.0%), demonstrating that play-time distillation is intrinsically more efficient than additional inference-time compute. - The missing: Play is fixed at 50 iterations per environment; the paper never reports what happens as play budget scales — whether skill quality continues to improve, plateaus, or degrades through library bloat — which is essential for practical deployment decisions. - Read it or not: Roboticists and ML researchers building Code-as-Policy systems or studying continual skill acquisition should read this for the compute-matched comparison and cross-environment transfer results; those focused on end-to-end VLA models or RL-based approaches will find limited direct applicability.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (75) are the top contributors: operationalizing curiosity-driven pre-task skill acquisition inside Code-as-Policy agents, with the analytically-grounded Goldilocks criterion and reliability-tiered code library, is a genuinely new synthesis. Rigor (60) is held back by no error bars at 10 trials/task, no released code or skill libraries, non-stable model identifiers, and especially the in-distribution play/test contamination (play and eval share object catalog and task structure). The compute-matched ablation (Table 14) and cross-embodiment RoboSuite transfer (+24pp two-arm from single-arm play) are the strongest, hardest-to-explain-away evidence (Completeness 70, Resilience 65). This mechanism-and-recipe framing resembles the user's positively-rated work.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Code-as-Policy robot agents are task-driven: they write and revise code only after receiving an external instruction, and reusable skills accumulate only as byproducts of solving provided tasks. This means every new task must rediscover manipulation knowledge — grasp geometry, articulation affordances, object localization strategies — from scratch, even when closely related behaviors were successfully executed in prior sessions. Classical intrinsic motivation and curiosity-driven exploration research addressed analogous problems in fixed sensorimotor or goal spaces, but those approaches cannot express exploratory goals in language, execute them as programs, inspect outcomes, or save successful behaviors as named callable functions. The missing piece is a principled mechanism for agentic robot systems to accumulate reusable code-level skills proactively, before any downstream task specification arrives. Key Idea: The core insight is that Code-as-Policy agents can treat play as a practical continual skill-acquisition mechanism: an LLM proposes goals in language, executes them as Python programs, and distills successful trajectories into named, reusable functions that can be retrieved into context at test time without model fine-tuning. The enabling technical move is the Goldilocks task selection criterion — ranking candidate play tasks by τ* = argmax[N(τ)·F(τ)], where novelty N(τ) = (1/|O(τ)×S(τ)|)Σ(1/√(N(o,s)+1)) discounts frequently-practiced object-skill combinations and competence frontier F(τ) = 4r̄(τ)(1−r̄(τ)) peaks at r̄≈0.5 using Wilson lower-bounded success rates — operationalizing the classical principle that agents should practice at the boundary of current competence rather than trivially easy or impossible tasks. Methodology: RATS organizes three collaborating agent teams: Task Proposer Team: At each iteration, an LLM receives the scene context, a compact skill-library summary (names, descriptions, reliability tiers, success statistics), and the last 10 task records, then generates K=5 candidate play tasks. These are ranked by s(τ) = N(τ)·F(τ) + wB·Bretry(τ) − wP·Pfail(τ). An Environment Creator converts the selected task into an executable specification (BDDL for LIBERO, bridge-catalog binding for MolmoSpaces), and an Environment Verifier runs deterministic pre-flight checks before consuming execution budget. Tasks rejected at verification return to the proposer rather than wasting a play iteration. Execution Team: Implements a bounded Write-Execute-Verify-Diagnose loop. A Planner decomposes the task with step-level skill annotations; a Planner Verifier checks visual grounding against the initial observation; a Policy Writer generates executable Python using retrieved skill code; a Quality Checker statically screens for syntax errors, invalid API calls, and unbounded loops; Goal and Per-Step Verifiers evaluate outcomes with both symbolic predicates and visual evidence; a Failure Diagnoser classifies failures (grasp_failure, code_bug, timeout, wrong_affordance, etc.) and routes to code revision, plan refinement, or a SubAgent that practices isolated sub-behaviors from a clean reset state. SubAgent results are available for the current retry but only promoted to the persistent library after contributing to an end-to-end success. Memory-Management Team: Maintains a skill library L with three reliability tiers — experimental (new), verified (≥3 uses, success rate ≥0.5), deprecated (≥10 uses, success rate ≤0.2) — and a failure memory M of raw episodes distilled into WHEN/WRONG/DO lessons. Every K=5 iterations, a Memory Curator merges near-duplicates and removes low-evidence lessons; a Skill Proposer drafts anticipatory helper functions targeting recurring physical bottlenecks. At test time, the library is frozen, task proposal and curation are disabled, and skills are used in either plug-and-play mode (retrieving code into CaP-Agent0's context) or full RATS Execution mode (with verification and retry active). All play uses 50 iterations per environment with gemini-3.1-pro-preview. Token cost analysis uses gpt-5.5. Key Results: - LIBERO-PRO (600 trials: 60 tasks × 10 initializations): RATS raises average success from 23.2% (CaP-Agent0) to 43.8% (+20.6 pp). All VLA baselines are far weaker: OpenVLA 0%, π0 0%, π0.5 12.8%. Gains appear across all six splits (Object/Goal/Spatial × Pos./Task perturbation), with largest gains on object splits (61%, 63%). - MolmoSpaces (400 trials: 40 tasks × 10): Average success improves from 21.0% to 38.0% (+17.0 pp). Close tasks improve most dramatically (36% → 73%); Open tasks improve only modestly (14% → 20%). - Cross-environment transfer to RoboSuite (350 trials): LIBERO-PRO skills plugged into CaP-Agent0 raise average success from 40.3% to 49.1% (+8.9 pp), including +24.0 pp on two-arm lifting despite skills having been practiced on a single-arm robot. - Sim-to-real (80 trials): Real-world average improves from 30.0% to 38.8% (+8.8 pp) with LIBERO skills; additional MolmoSpaces skill real-world experiments (Table 8) show +21.7 pp over 60 trials, though the CaP-Agent0 baseline there is 3.3%. - Ablation (Table 4): Random play under CaP-Agent0 yields 24.7% vs. 23.2% for no play — near-zero gain. Curious Play reaches 32.3%. Combining Curious Play with full RATS Exec yields 44.3%. - Compute-matched comparison (Table 14): CaP-Agent0 with 15-turn retry budget (matched to amortized play cost) reaches 26.0%, vs. 32.3% for CaP-Agent0 with RATS play skills at 10 turns. Baselines are contemporary: CaP-Agent0 is from CaP-X (arXiv:2603.22435, 2026), π0 and π0.5 are peer-reviewed VLA models from RSS 2025 and CoRL 2025 respectively. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is operationalizing curiosity-driven pre-task skill acquisition within Code-as-Policy agents, including the Goldilocks task-selection formula with Wilson-bounded competence estimation, the Write-Execute-Verify-Diagnose play loop with per-step failure localization, and the reliability-tiered code skill library with anticipatory Skill Proposer. Each individual component (intrinsic motivation, Code-as-Policy execution, multi-agent verification) is known; their synthesis for physically grounded, code-level play-time skill acquisition is novel and yields a plug-and-play mechanism that improves other agents without fine-tuning. Rigor & Reproducibility: Meaningful ablations are present: random vs. curious play, CaP-Agent0 vs. RATS Exec, and the compute-matched comparison. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported anywhere; with 10 trials per task, small differences (e.g., +3 pp on MolmoSpaces Open tasks) may be noise. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.19419v1) and no code, trained models, skill libraries, or benchmark splits are released (project page references video only). The system uses gemini-3.1-pro-preview and gpt-5.5 — neither a stable model identifier — which complicates reproducibility. A methodological concern: LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces serve as both play environments and in-domain evaluation benchmarks, so the agent practices tasks from the same distribution it is later tested on; RoboSuite is the only fully held-out evaluation environment. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL — a built system evaluated by task success rates in simulation and physical robot deployment. - Practical: Runs on standard hardware (Franka arm, commercial cloud LLM APIs); no specialized accelerators required beyond what frontier API access provides. - Efficient: A 50-iteration play phase costs approximately 30M tokens (~$150–300 at frontier API pricing); the Failure Diagnoser and Policy Writer together account for 69.3% of play-time token consumption. At test time, RATS adds multiple LLM calls per attempt (planner, verifier, diagnoser) on top of CaP-Agent0's baseline, making it substantially more expensive per task than VLA inference. - Effective: Evidence shows consistent task success improvement, and the compute-matched comparison provides reasonable evidence that gains are not purely attributable to additional compute. However, absolute success rates remain modest (43.8% on LIBERO-PRO, 38.0% on MolmoSpaces), and MolmoSpaces Open tasks improve only from 14% to 20% — suggesting limits in the current system. - Scalable: Only tested at 50 play iterations; no scaling curve is reported. Whether 500 iterations produce proportionally better libraries, or whether skill bloat and lesson noise degrade retrieval quality, is entirely unevaluated. The system delivers an executable framework; no pretrained skill library, dataset, or standalone library artifact is released. Strengths: - The compute-matched ablation (Table 14) is the paper's most intellectually rigorous contribution, directly addressing the confound that RATS might simply be consuming more compute and showing that proactive play distillation beats equivalent reactive retries. - The Goldilocks task selection formula is simple, analytically grounded, and its importance is empirically validated by the ablation showing random play provides near-zero benefit over no play under CaP-Agent0. - Cross-environment transfer to RoboSuite, especially the +24.0 pp gain on two-arm lifting from single-arm LIBERO play, is a strong generalization result with practical implications: the skill library transfers across embodiments without fine-tuning. - The three-tier skill reliability lifecycle (experimental → verified → deprecated) using Wilson lower bounds prevents recently-lucky skills from being over-trusted, addressing a known failure mode of empirical success tracking in small-sample regimes. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: (1) evaluation is primarily simulation-based with limited physical robot validation; (2) play diversity is bounded by simulation environment variety; (3) improper skill retrieval can hurt performance when skills don't fit the downstream task; (4) increased inference costs and heavy VLM verification reliance; (5) primitive-level control APIs limit dexterous manipulation. These are stated plainly and credibly in the Limitations section. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - In-distribution play/test contamination: Using LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces as both play and evaluation environments means play tasks and test tasks share the same object catalog and task structure; the in-domain numbers overstate generalization relative to the cleaner RoboSuite held-out test. - No scaling analysis: The paper fixes play at 50 iterations without reporting whether more iterations monotonically improve performance or plateau, and whether skill library quality degrades through noise accumulation at scale. - Memory Curator effectiveness not ablated: The library grows from 6 to 27 skills over 50 MolmoSpaces iterations; the curator's actual contribution to downstream performance (vs. an uncurated library) is never isolated. - No oracle or upper-bound baseline: No comparison against a system given the ideal skill library from the outset, leaving the headroom of the approach unknown. - Statistical significance absent: With no error bars and 10 trials per task, gains of +3–6 pp on individual splits cannot be distinguished from variance, particularly for MolmoSpaces Open tasks. - LLM dependency not characterized: The system's sensitivity to LLM capability is not ablated; using non-stable model identifiers (gemini-3.1-pro-preview, gpt-5.5) makes reproducibility contingent on commercial API decisions. Bottom Line: Practitioners building Code-as-Policy robot systems will find genuine actionable insights in the compute-matched comparison and cross-environment plug-and-play skill transfer, but should treat the in-domain numbers with caution given the play/test distribution overlap and absence of statistical reporting, and should plan for frontier LLM API costs that substantially exceed VLA inference.
Recommended 2606.19419 PDF
#53 — Innovative (68)

Kairos: A Native World Model Stack for Physical AI (27 ▲ )

Kairos Team, Fei Wang, Shan You, Qiming Zhang, Tao Huang, Zuoyi Fu, Zhisheng Zheng, Yunlong Xi, Feng Lv, Xiaoming Wu, Zeyu Liu, Cong Wan, Pu Li, Ruiqing Yang, Xiaoou Li, Wei Wang, Kangkang Zhu, Yuwei Zhang, Shi Fu, Zheng Zhang, Xiaoning Wu, Xuzeng Fan, Dacheng Tao, Xiaogang Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Kairos, a 4B-parameter world model stack combining a three-stage cross-embodiment data curriculum, a hybrid linear temporal attention architecture (sliding-window + dilated sliding-window + gated linear attention), and deployment-aware quantization and distillation for physical AI. - Why it's important: Existing world models either generate plausible video but lack action grounding, or are far too compute-heavy for real-time closed-loop robot control — Kairos targets both gaps simultaneously in a single system. - How it does it: Kairos replaces full softmax attention for long sequences with a hybrid of local sliding-window attention, mid-range dilated attention, and O(N) gated linear attention that maintains a persistent compressed memory state, backed by formal error-accumulation bounds and distilled to 4B parameters with FP8/INT4 inference. - The good: On 720P 5-second generation, Kairos-4B achieves 43s / 9s latency on 1 GPU / 4 GPUs, versus 2526s / 687s for Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B and 5525s / 1436s for Lingbot-28B — a 28×–85× speedup at 2.3 vs. 156.5 PFlops — while matching or beating those models on WorldModelBench-robot (9.30 vs. 9.26), DreamGen Bench (0.618 avg), and LIBERO-Plus (89.0 average success rate). - The missing: All baseline comparisons reproduce competitor numbers in-house rather than using authors' reported checkpoints; no ablation isolates the contribution of the hybrid attention architecture itself versus the data curriculum, so it is impossible to disentangle the source of the empirical gains. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on embodied AI, video world models, or efficient long-sequence Transformers — Kairos delivers a rare combination of deployment-grade efficiency and top-tier benchmark results; skip it if you need peer-reviewed certainty, since this is a preprint with self-reproduced baselines.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Practical Impact (80) and Novelty (75) drive the score: publicly released weights/code and striking, hardware-grounded efficiency (28x–85x over Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B at comparable quality, runnable on a consumer RTX 5090), plus the formal error-accumulation bounds (Theorems 1–2) for hybrid attention — the most genuinely new intellectual content. Completeness (50) is the clear weak point: the paper acknowledges essentially no limitations and pivots straight to future work, reading as vendor-style reporting. Resilience (55) suffers because no ablation isolates the hybrid attention architecture from the data curriculum, baselines are author-reproduced (some differing from published numbers), theoretical assumptions go unverified, and there is no real-robot evaluation. Treat rankings as indicative pending independent replication.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 50 Resilience 55 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Robotic and physical AI systems require world models that can (1) acquire generalizable physical knowledge from heterogeneous data sources including passive video and robot trajectories, (2) maintain coherent state over long generation horizons (seconds to minutes of video, hundreds of robot steps), and (3) run at low latency on server and consumer hardware in closed-loop control. Prior work partitions into three silos — pixel-level video generators (Cosmos, Wan, LingBot), predictive latent-space models (V-JEPA family), and interactive environment simulators (Genie 3, Dreamer) — each optimizing one of these axes at the expense of the others. Pixel-level generators are slow and expensive; latent predictors lack actionable output; interactive simulators assume fixed environments. No single system trains natively on cross-embodiment data while also targeting real-time inference and joint world-action modeling. Key Idea The core insight is that long-horizon temporal consistency requires architecturally persistent global memory, and that this memory can be made both theoretically justified and computationally cheap by factorizing temporal attention into three ranges: sliding-window attention for local appearance continuity, dilated sliding-window attention for mid-range dynamics, and Gated Linear Attention (O(N) complexity) as a contractive recurrent state that never discards far-past context. The paper proves formally (Theorems 1 and 2) that bounded sliding-window attention produces irreducibly higher prediction risk whenever the optimal predictor depends on supra-window events, while the hybrid factorization limits error accumulation to ξ/(1-ρ) asymptotically, where ρ < 1 is the contraction factor and ξ is the per-step perturbation. This provides theoretical grounding usually absent from engineering-focused systems papers. Methodology Kairos is structured in three interconnected layers: Architecture (Section 2): A Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) Diffusion Transformer whose temporal attention blocks alternate between (a) Sliding Window Attention (SWA) with RoPE for local frames, (b) Dilated Sliding Window Attention (DSWA) with larger receptive field, and (c) Gated Linear Attention (GLA / Gated DeltaNet) maintaining a compressed O(d²) state matrix. Each Hybrid Linear Temporal Block fans through all three paths. The design is unified across world understanding (VLM encoder, Qwen3.5-2B), generation (video diffusion), and prediction (action head), sharing the same backbone weights. Pre-training Curriculum (Section 3): Cross-Embodiment Data Curriculum (CEDC) runs three sequential stages: Stage I trains on "hundreds of millions" of open-world video clips for physical regularities via causal chain-of-thought grounding; Stage II adds "over 100,000 hours" of human-centric behavioral data (egocentric, instructed tasks); Stage III injects robot-specific data (AgiBotWorld-Beta, Droid) with joint video generation and action prediction objectives. A key design choice is that action grounding is native — not a post-hoc fine-tune — motivated by the 23.2-point LIBERO-Plus gain from joint vs. action-prediction-only training. Deployment System Co-Design (Section 5): Timestep distillation compresses multi-step diffusion into fewer function evaluations. Attention layers use FP8 quantization with per-thread INT8/INT4 Q/K and FP8 P·V; the text encoder uses INT4 weight-only compression. A tiled GLA with streaming access avoids OOM on long sequences. These optimizations are targeted specifically at closed-loop control latency, not just offline throughput. Key Results All evaluations are preprint-stage; competitor results marked with * were re-run by the authors rather than taken from original papers. Efficiency (Table 5): At 720P, 5s generation, Kairos-4B uses 2.3 PFlops and 23.5 GB memory, achieving 43s (1 GPU, A800) and 9s (4 GPU). Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B uses 156.5 PFlops and 2526s/687s; Lingbot-28B uses 347.4 PFlops and 5525s/1436s. Against the parameter-comparable Wan2.2-5B (16.6 PFlops, 201s/85s), Kairos delivers ~4.7× speedup at lower compute. WorldModelBench-robot (Table 6): Kairos-4B scores 9.30 total, topping Cosmos3-Nano-16B (9.26) and Lingbot-28B (9.04), with perfect scores on Newton, fluid, and gravity sub-metrics. DreamGen Bench (Table 7): Kairos-4B best overall (0.618 avg score) and best physical adherence (0.538), second in instruction-following (0.698 vs. 0.703 for Wan2.2-14B). PAI-Bench-robot (Table 8): Best among <10B models with domain score 88.59 and overall 82.57, beating Cosmos3-Nano-16B (82.62 — nearly tied), Wan2.2-5B (78.63), and several 14B models. Long-horizon 15s (Table 18): Overall 79.9 vs. Wan2.2-5B 77.8, Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B 76.2, showing smaller degradation from 5s to 15s generation. VideoPhy (Table 17): 45.55 average score vs. Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B 45.16, Wan2.2-5B 38.85. WAM — LIBERO-Plus (Table 12): 89.0 average (best WAM category); joint training (Kairos-joint) reaches 90.8. WAM — RoboTwin 2.0 (Table 11): 96.1 average clean+randomized, first among WAMs (MotuBrain 96.0, SANTS 94.4). Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM / NEW METHOD. The contribution is genuinely novel in the combination of (a) formal theoretical justification for a specific hybrid attention factorization targeting world models, (b) a native three-stage pre-training curriculum that treats robot grounding as Stage III of a continuum rather than fine-tuning, and (c) deployment-aware inference co-design validated on robot benchmarks. Individual ingredients (GLA, SWA, DSWA, diffusion distillation) are drawn from prior work; the integration and the physical AI framing are novel. The theoretical analysis (Theorems 1 and 2) is the most genuinely new intellectual contribution — formal error-accumulation bounds for hybrid attention in a world-model context have not appeared in prior work. Rigor & Reproducibility Code and model weights are publicly released at https://github.com/kairos-agi/kairos-sensenova and https://huggingface.co/kairos-agi. Ablations are present but narrow: human-centric data ablation (+6 LIBERO points, Table 13), joint training ablation (+23.2 points, Table 14), and VLM encoder choice (Table 9). No ablation isolates GLA vs. SWA vs. DSWA — the architectural contribution stands only on the theoretical argument plus end-to-end system comparison. Baselines marked with * are reproduced by the authors' own team, not taken from official checkpoints; given that some competitor numbers differ from published reports, this introduces a potential reproducibility concern. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported for the robot policy benchmarks (LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin), where variance can be high. The theoretical bounds (Theorem 2) require specific assumptions (contractive gated delta update, exact factorization of the Bayes predictor) that are never empirically verified. The paper is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — Kairos is a deployed inference system evaluated on server and consumer hardware. - Practical: Kairos runs on NVIDIA A800 and RTX 5090 (consumer GPU), generating 480P video in 11–12s single-GPU and 3–6s with 4 GPUs, qualifying as practically usable on commercially available hardware. - Efficient: At 2.3 PFlops and 23.5 GB memory for 720P 5s generation, it is 68× fewer PFlops than Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B while using 67% less memory, representing a strong efficiency gain; the RTX 5090 memory footprint (13.9 GB at 480P) is within reach of a $2K–3K consumer GPU. - Effective: Evidence across WorldModelBench, DreamGen, PAI-Bench, LIBERO-Plus, and RoboTwin 2.0 consistently shows Kairos matching or exceeding models with 3×–7× more parameters, which credibly supports the claimed efficiency–capability trade-off; however, all baselines are author-reproduced, and only a subset of results include human evaluation. - Scalable: The paper shows linear latency scaling from 480P 5s to 720P 15s (versus exponential for competitors), and the 15s PAI-Bench results directly test this axis; scaling beyond 15s or to 4K resolution has not been evaluated and remains a claim rather than a demonstration. Kairos delivers a full software artifact: model weights, inference code, and training infrastructure released publicly. Strengths - The theoretical treatment of persistent memory necessity (Theorem 1 + Corollary 1) is the strongest formal justification for hybrid attention in world models published to date, going beyond empirical motivation. - The efficiency gains are empirically striking and backed by specific hardware numbers (PFlops, GB, latency) rather than FLOPs estimates; 28×–85× over Cosmos-Predict2.5-14B at comparable quality is a publishable finding in its own right. - The CEDC curriculum's staged data strategy — treating internet video, human behavior, and robot data as three progressive curriculum stages rather than mixed pooling — is a principled design choice validated by the +23.2 ablation from joint generation+prediction training. - Evaluation coverage is unusually broad: five benchmarks (WorldModelBench, DreamGen, PAI-Bench, LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin 2.0), long-horizon testing at 15s, and human evaluation against anonymized baselines. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge virtually no limitations explicitly. The conclusion pivots immediately to future work (self-evolution, generalist embodied substrate). There is no discussion of failure modes, out-of-distribution generalization bounds, the theoretical gap between the mathematical model and actual training dynamics, or the limited diversity of robot morphologies tested. This is a credibility negative — the absence of limitation discussion reads as vendor-style reporting rather than scientific disclosure. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No architectural ablation: the paper never compares GLA-only vs. SWA-only vs. full hybrid in an end-to-end setting, making it impossible to attribute the performance gains to the architectural choice versus the data curriculum. - Competitor results re-run by the authors: several baseline numbers differ from published reports; no independent verification is offered, and the paper does not disclose which hyperparameters or prompts were used in re-running. - Theoretical assumptions go unverified: Theorem 2 assumes the gated delta update is contractive (ρ < 1) and that the Bayes predictor factorizes over the three temporal scales — neither property is empirically measured. - Action evaluation is narrow: LIBERO-Plus and RoboTwin 2.0 are simulated benchmarks; no real-robot deployment results appear. - Long-context scaling claim is partial: 15s is tested, but the "infinite horizon" claim (arbitrary-length generation via recurrent state passing) is not empirically validated. - Human evaluation used 10 volunteers on a proprietary pool of generated samples, which is a weak statistical basis for the claim that Kairos achieves 65–87% win rates. Bottom Line Kairos is a credible and practically significant system paper — its efficiency numbers are the most compelling argument for reading it, and the theoretical treatment of hybrid attention genuinely advances understanding — but the absence of architectural ablations, self-reproduced baselines, and lack of real-robot validation mean practitioners should treat the benchmark rankings as indicative rather than definitive until independent replication is available.
Recommended 2606.16533 PDF
#54 — Innovative (68)

Geometric Action Model for Robot Policy Learning (88 ▲ )

Jisang Han, Seonghu Jeon, Jaewoo Jung, René Zurbrügg, Honggyu An, Tifanny Portela, Marco Hutter, Marc Pollefeys, Seungryong Kim, Sunghwan Hong · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Jun 2026
