The precision ratio λ_t is replaced by c̄_t (the channel-mean of the routing confidence), which bounds the loss landscape to [0,1] and avoids gradient explosion. Training uses a FIFO buffer (capacity 3000 snapshots) sampled with exponential decay to weight recent data, with cycle training triggered every H steps after the true horizon is observed. Key design decisions: (i) linear adapter is justified as appropriate inductive bias for noisy residuals — well-justified; (ii) substituting c̄_t for the true precision ratio λ_t is justified theoretically by showing it recovers the exact Bayesian posterior mean structure with σ^2_obs=1, σ^2_prior=1/c̄_t — adequately justified; (iii) FIFO buffer with exponential decay rather than uniform replay — empirically validated via ablation. Key Results: Across 40 aggregated (dataset × model) cases, ORCA achieves an average 6.3% MSE reduction over vanilla zero-shot TSFMs. Per-dataset averages range from −0.3% (ETTh2, near-stationary) to −11.5% (Weather). Per-model averages range from −3.8% (Sundial) to −8.4% (TiRex). The five base models tested (Chronos-2, Moirai-2, TiRex, TimesFM-2.5, Sundial) are current-generation, released 2025–2026, and represent the dominant performers on GIFT-Eval and fev-bench — this is a genuine strength over prior works that used first-generation TSFMs. The ablation on adapter inputs is the paper's most informative result: conditioning on base error alone yields −0.7% average (harmful), input alone yields +4.0%, prediction alone yields +1.5%, and both input+prediction yields +6.5%. The structural ablation shows removing the Boltzmann Router drops average improvement from 6.51% to 5.23%, the largest single-component impact. Efficiency: ORCA adds 16.74 ms per step (vs. 121.45 ms for Chronos-2 inference) on ETTh1 with a B200 GPU, satisfying 100 ms latency budgets. Comparison baselines: ELF achieves +0.47% average, δ-Adapter (Ada-Y) achieves +0.79%; white-box baselines adapted to black-box settings all degrade (TAFAS: −1.00%, DSOF: −1.85%, SOLID: −3.61%), confirming that direct white-box-to-black-box transfer fails. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is the "context of errors" framing: the theoretical grounding that optimal black-box residual correction requires conditioning on both input and base-model output, backed by the MSE-conditional-expectation derivation. The Boltzmann Router's connection to DEW with a closed-form regret bound is a secondary technical contribution. The components themselves (linear decomposition adapters, EMA-based routing, Bayesian regularization, FIFO replay) are individually known, but their specific combination and theoretical justification for the black-box TSFM setting is new. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablation studies are thorough and directly test the core hypothesis: five input configurations, five structural ablations, a router variant comparison (hard binary vs. soft Boltzmann), and hyperparameter sensitivity across four parameters (τ, α, buffer size, batch size). Error bars and significance tests are not reported — a reproducibility gap, though results are averaged over three horizons and five base models which provides implicit variance reduction. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/Fifthky/ORCA. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.14222v1), not peer-reviewed. One concern: the "black-box" baselines (TAFAS, SOLID, DSOF) are adapted versions of white-box methods, not natural black-box competitors, so the comparison is somewhat constructed — acknowledged explicitly by the authors. The evaluation uses a single primary metric (MSE) averaged across horizons, and the paper explicitly defers probabilistic metrics (CRPS) to future work, which is a real gap given TSFMs are designed for probabilistic forecasting. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: ORCA's client-side adapter runs on a single GPU with 353.9 MB memory overhead for ETTh1 (7 channels) and 4236.3 MB for Electricity (321 channels), both feasible on commercial hardware; no TSFM weights are needed client-side beyond inference API access. - Efficient: Adapter inference adds 16.74 ms per step on a B200 GPU versus 121.45 ms base model inference (14% overhead); cycle training (2049 ms per cycle amortized over 96 steps) adds ~21 ms per step amortized, remaining within 100 ms latency budgets. - Effective: Evidence is solid for smooth concept drift on standard benchmarks; the paper demonstrates reduction in deterministic MSE but does not evaluate whether improvements hold for probabilistic outputs (quantile loss, calibration), which is the natural evaluation target for TSFMs. - Scalable: Tested up to 321 channels (Electricity dataset); scaling to thousands of channels or very long lookback windows is not evaluated; buffer memory scales linearly with channel count and horizon, which may be limiting for very high-dimensional settings. The work delivers a software artifact (code at GitHub) plus a trained adapter framework usable at inference time. Strengths: - The "learning the context of errors" framing is theoretically grounded: the derivation that MSE minimization targets E[E_t | X_t, Y^base_t] gives a non-trivial justification for using both input and output as context, and the ablation directly validates this at the +6.5% vs. +1.5%/+4.0% level. - Evaluation uses genuinely current TSFMs (Chronos-2, Moirai-2, TiRex, TimesFM-2.5, Sundial released 2025–2026), unlike most prior work that used first-generation models — this meaningfully increases the practical relevance of the results. - The Boltzmann Router provides a safety guarantee: it theoretically prevents the adapted model from performing worse than the base model as c_t → 0 during adapter failure, with O(√T) regret bounding confirmed by a formal DEW equivalence proof. - The choice of linear adapter is explicitly motivated by the noisy-residual inductive bias argument, and the ablation confirms that wrong learning hypotheses cause more damage than wrong structural choices, which is an informative finding for the field. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note two limitations: (1) ORCA is a post-hoc corrector that depends on the base model providing reasonable forecasts — if the base model is wildly wrong, ORCA cannot compensate; (2) evaluation covers standard benchmarks with deterministic metrics, and future work will address abrupt structural shifts and probabilistic metrics. These are stated concisely and honestly, though the scope is narrower than what a reviewer would flag. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The comparison with white-box methods is not an apples-to-apples contest — adapting TAFAS, SOLID, and DSOF to black-box settings necessarily weakens them (stripping their core gradient-based mechanisms), so the result that they underperform ORCA does not establish ORCA's dominance over natively black-box methods at the same capability level. ELF is the only genuine black-box competitor, and ORCA's 6.3% vs. ELF's 0.47% average improvement is the credible headline comparison. The paper evaluates only MSE (point forecasts via median of probabilistic outputs), which is a particularly limited view given TSFMs are probabilistic forecasters — CRPS, winkler score, and calibration metrics are missing entirely. Results are reported only for non-overlapping evaluation windows; rolling/overlapping windows (more realistic for many deployments) are not tested. The benchmark datasets (ETT family, Weather, Traffic, Electricity, Exchange) are standard but long-studied, raising the question of whether ORCA's advantage holds on datasets with genuinely abrupt breaks or irregular sampling. No multi-seed variance or confidence intervals are reported despite the results being on relatively small test sets. Bottom Line: ORCA delivers a practically deployable, theoretically grounded black-box adapter for time series foundation models with solid empirical validation across current base models, making it worth reading for anyone building streaming forecasting systems on top of TSFM APIs — though the absence of probabilistic evaluation metrics and the limited scope of genuine black-box baselines leave the full picture incomplete.
Search Results 2606.14222 PDF
#60 — Innovative (67)

Rethinking DVFS for Mobile LLMs: Unified Energy-Aware Scheduling with CORE

Z Zhang, P Dash, Q Xu, YC Hu, J Li, H Guan · openreview.net · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CORE (Coordinated Optimization of Resource Energy), a unified DVFS governor that jointly sets CPU, GPU, and memory frequencies for mobile LLM inference by replacing three independent OS governors with a single offline-profiled, energy-aware controller. - Why it's important: Mobile LLMs can drain a smartphone battery in under 600 prompts, and the default Android governors make the problem worse than necessary by triggering an antagonistic downward spiral that adds 23–40% latency or 5–17% excess energy versus attainable optima. - How it does it: CORE decouples the three-governor interaction by conducting an offline profiling sweep over the 2808-dimensional CPU×GPU×memory frequency space, reducing it to a two-step greedy search (GPU first, CPU second; memory governor kept at default) that finds the Pareto-optimal frequency pair for each prefill-length bucket. - The good: On real ShareGPT traces, CORE reduces time-per-output-token by 27.8–39.6% across six diverse models without increasing energy per token, while requiring only ~15–31 profiling inferences per model (a 374× reduction from brute-force). - The missing: Evaluation is confined to two nearly identical devices (Pixel 7 / 7 Pro, Tensor G2 SoC, Android 13, llama.cpp + OpenCL), leaving generalizability to other SoC families (Snapdragon, Apple A-series), inference frameworks (Vulkan, NPU-based), and operating conditions entirely undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for systems researchers and mobile-ML engineers who work on on-device LLM deployment, because it provides the first rigorous causal account of why Android's default governors fail for LLM workloads and a deployable fix.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (Novelty 65, Rigor 68, Non-Obv 75, Complete 58, Resilience 62, Impact 72). The empirical discovery of the antagonistic cross-governor downward spiral is non-obvious and well-evidenced (real-time traces, controlled pinning), and the released llama.cpp extension plus 27–40% TPOT gains give solid Practical Impact; MLSys 2026 peer review lifts Rigor above the cluster's preprints. Completeness and Resilience pull the composite down: the single-SoC (Tensor G2) evaluation and lack of error bars limit generalizability, the authors do not acknowledge the single-SoC limitation, and the energy-budget baseline (set equal to the default governor's draw) is an easy bar. Composite: 0.15*65 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*72 = 67.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 58 Resilience 62 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: On-device LLM inference on Android draws from three simultaneously active power-hungry subsystems—CPU, GPU, and DRAM—even for GPU-centric workloads, because the OpenCL runtime executing on the CPU must continuously manage a software command queue (ARM Mali GPUs support at most 2 outstanding hardware queue entries). Android's three governors (EAS for CPU, Quickstep for GPU, interactive for memory) each optimize their own component utilization independently. Because LLM decode is compute-light relative to prefill, decode-phase GPU utilization falls below Quickstep's target range, causing it to reduce the GPU clock; lower GPU frequency reduces CPU utilization (the OpenCL dispatcher sits idle waiting for GPU completions), causing EAS to lower CPU frequency; the lower CPU frequency delays kernel dispatch, reducing GPU utilization further—a feedback loop that drives both clocks to minimum values. No prior work had characterized this cross-component interaction or designed a governor explicitly for LLM workloads. Key Idea: The core insight is that the latency/energy Pareto frontier of mobile LLM inference is structured: varying GPU frequency produces a U-shaped energy-per-token curve with a single minimum, and the CPU frequency sweep given a fixed GPU frequency produces a similar U-shape. This structure means a two-step greedy search—find the GPU frequency that first crosses the energy-budget threshold (starting from the highest), then sweep CPU frequencies at that pinned GPU setting—finds the near-optimal combination in O(|F_GPU| + |F_CPU|) inferences rather than O(|F_GPU| × |F_CPU| × |F_MEM|). Because the memory governor already achieves near-optimal performance when CPU/GPU are fixed, it is left at its default. Methodology: The platform is a rooted Google Pixel 7 / 7 Pro (Tensor G2 SoC, Mali-G710 MP7 GPU) running Android 13, powered through Monsoon high-voltage power monitors at 0.2 ms temporal resolution, with batteries bypassed and screens off. The LLM framework is llama.cpp (tag b2202) with OpenCL/CLBlast GPU backend; six 4-bit quantized models are evaluated: TinyLlama 1.1B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen, Refact, Phi-2, StableLM 2.7B, Llama-2 7B. Governors are studied through a "pin" methodology that locks scaling_min_freq = scaling_max_freq = target, enabling controlled exploration of all 18×12×13 = 2808 CPU×GPU×memory frequency combinations. CORE's offline search covers six prefill-length buckets (32/64/128/256/512 tokens plus a decode bucket) for two optimization goals: G1 (minimize latency given an energy budget equal to Gov's observed draw) and G2 (minimize energy given Gov's latency as target). The two-step greedy search exploits the U-shape structure to terminate early. At runtime, CORE—implemented in ~2K lines of Python—hooks into llama.cpp phase boundaries (prefill start/end, decode start/end) to apply pre-computed frequency settings. Evaluation uses 200 randomly sampled ShareGPT requests (avg. 236 prefill, 139 decode tokens). Critical design decisions: (1) treating frequency config as input-content-agnostic (justified empirically by uniform results across sequence lengths within a prefill-length bucket); (2) leaving the memory governor at default (justified by §5.2 measurement showing near-optimal memory behavior when CPU/GPU are fixed); (3) distributing profiled configs with the app rather than per-user profiling (reasonable given same device-model pair = same optimal config). Key Results: On 200 ShareGPT requests with the energy budget set equal to Gov's draw (Goal G1): TinyLlama TTFT −14.6% (10.8→9.2 s), TPOT −27.8% (212.1→153.1 ms), E2E −23.2%; DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen TTFT −17.7%, TPOT −39.6%, E2E −28.8%; StableLM TTFT −8.5% (25.1→23.0 s), TPOT −38.3% (490.0→302.3 ms), E2E −27.9%—all without increasing total energy. Under Goal G2 (same latency target): TinyLlama saves 7.5% total energy (865.5→800.6 mAh); DS-Qwen saves 15.8% (1288.3→1084.7 mAh); StableLM saves 6.3% (2185.6→2047.9 mAh). The controlled sweep shows that with the same energy budget as Gov, prefill TTFT can be reduced by up to 40.4% and decode TPOT by up to 37.1% for individual models. The antagonistic spiral is visualized in real time: after unpinning the GPU governor from 848 MHz, the system cascades to 151 MHz GPU / 500 MHz CPU within ~700 ms. The memory governor in isolation achieves near-optimal performance (at most 4.8% suboptimal in the tested configurations). Profiling cost: 14–31 inferences per model per goal, finishing in 18–88 minutes depending on model size; this is a one-time offline cost amortized across all users of the same phone–model pair. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The empirical discovery of the antagonistic cross-governor downward spiral in Android DVFS during LLM decode is the paper's primary intellectual contribution—it is a concrete causal mechanism not previously characterized. CORE itself is a NEW SYSTEM that applies standard profiling-based DVFS ideas to a new context; the two-step greedy search exploits the U-shape structure in a way that is simple but not previously applied to multi-component mobile SoC DVFS for LLM inference. The profiling-based approach is not novel per se (prior gaming DVFS work uses similar ideas), but its application to the specific three-way cross-component interaction for LLMs and the derivation of a structured search that reduces from 2808 to ~30 inferences is a genuine contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: The controlled experiments are methodologically careful: each governor is isolated by pinning the other two components, then interaction effects are studied by releasing control. The antagonistic spiral is captured in real-time frequency traces (Fig. 8), not just inferred. Evaluation uses a real-world dataset (ShareGPT). However, no error bars or variance across runs are reported for the headline TTFT/TPOT/energy figures, which is a weakness given that mobile inference can exhibit thermal and scheduler variance. Ablations are implicit in the study design (§5.1–5.4) but no formal ablation of CORE's components is provided. Code is released as a llama.cpp extension (URL not stated in the paper). The paper is peer-reviewed at MLSys 2026, which provides more confidence than a preprint. Methodological concern: all experiments run on a single SoC family (Tensor G2), making it unclear whether the U-shape structure and antagonistic interaction generalize to other mobile SoC designs (Snapdragon, Dimensity, Apple Silicon). The energy budget for Goal G1 is set to exactly Gov's draw, which is an easy bar—comparison against any fixed high-frequency baseline is not provided. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS (a built tool evaluated by deployment on real hardware). - Practical: Runs on commodity Android smartphones (Pixel 7/7 Pro) with root access required for frequency pinning; the user-space prototype needs root, but the authors envision eventual OS-level integration. - Efficient: One-time per-device-per-model profiling cost of 18–88 minutes; zero runtime overhead beyond applying pre-computed frequency settings at phase boundaries. - Effective: Evidence shows 27–40% TPOT reduction and 6–16% energy savings on actual hardware with real prompts, not proxy metrics; the ShareGPT evaluation is more realistic than synthetic fixed-length tests. - Scalable: Only tested on prompts ≤512 prefill / 384 decode tokens due to device memory constraints; behavior at longer contexts (e.g., 4K–8K tokens, increasingly common) is not evaluated and the prefill-length bucket design may require more buckets or retuning. Generalization to other SoC families, other inference frameworks (Vulkan, NPU-based), and multi-user/background-task scenarios is untested. The work delivers a software artifact (llama.cpp extension) that is usable today on rooted Android devices. Strengths: - The antagonistic downward-spiral characterization (§5.4) is the paper's strongest contribution: it provides a causal explanation with controlled experiments, real-time traces, and cross-component utilization measurements that together form a convincing mechanistic account, not just a performance gap. - The measurement infrastructure (Monsoon power monitors, battery bypass, screen-off, sub-millisecond trace resolution) is production-grade and eliminates common confounders that plague mobile energy measurement. - The two-step greedy search reduces the profiling space from 2808 combinations to ~15–31 inferences by exploiting the empirically discovered U-shape structure—a principled engineering reduction rather than a heuristic. - Releasing CORE as a concrete llama.cpp extension makes results reproducible and the approach adoptable without re-implementing the full profiling framework. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that CORE currently operates in user space rather than the OS kernel, and propose OS-level integration as future work. They also mention that CORE could be extended to choose among energy-equivalent frequency configurations based on heat minimization. These are forward-looking statements rather than confessions of failure; the authors do not acknowledge the single-SoC evaluation limitation or the lack of error bars. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Single-SoC evaluation: all experiments use Tensor G2 (Google's proprietary SoC). The antagonistic interaction mechanism depends on specific governor implementations (sched-pixel EAS, Quickstep GPU governor) and Mali GPU shallow-queue architecture; it is not obvious these effects manifest the same way on Snapdragon (with its Adreno GPU and different governor stack) or Apple Silicon, which are far more common LLM deployment targets. - No variance/confidence intervals on headline results: mobile benchmarks are subject to thermal drift and OS scheduler noise; the paper reports single-run means for the ShareGPT trace. - Framework generalizability: results are specific to llama.cpp with OpenCL/CLBlast. NPU-based frameworks (e.g., MLLM-NPU, PowerInfer-2) have different CPU–accelerator coupling patterns and the antagonistic dynamics may not apply. - Comparison baseline is weak: the paper compares CORE only against Android's default governors. A comparison against a naive fixed-frequency policy (e.g., always-max or always-high-GPU) would sharpen understanding of how much of the gain is from the search versus simply overriding the governors at all. - Context-length coverage: capped at 512 prefill / 384 decode tokens; modern LLM applications increasingly use multi-turn contexts of several thousand tokens, where the prefill/decode energy balance shifts substantially. Bottom Line: Systems and mobile ML practitioners working on Android LLM deployment will find the governor-interaction analysis directly actionable and the llama.cpp integration immediately usable, but researchers aiming to generalize or productionize the approach should treat the single-SoC evaluation as a significant open question.
Search Results
#61 — Innovative (67)

DF3DV-1K: A Large-Scale Dataset and Benchmark for Distractor-Free Novel View Synthesis (9 ▲ )

Cheng-You Lu, Yi-Shan Hung, Wei-Ling Chi, Hao-Ping Wang, Charlie Li-Ting Tsai, Yu-Cheng Chang, Yu-Lun Liu, Thomas Do, Chin-Teng Lin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DF3DV-1K, a large-scale real-world dataset of 1,048 scenes — each providing matched clean and cluttered image sets — for benchmarking distractor-free novel view synthesis, together with a comprehensive 10-method benchmark and a fine-tuned diffusion enhancer (DI2FIX). - Why it's important: Existing public datasets in this domain contain only 5–12 scenes and have become too easy for recent methods, making it impossible to discriminate among them or to drive generalizable (non-scene-specific) solutions. - How it does it: Researchers manually captured ~90K images across 1,048 controlled scenes over nine months using consumer cameras, then verified poses via COLMAP and scene quality via instant-ngp, producing paired clean/cluttered image sets per scene. - The good: The benchmark uniquely reveals a consistent publication-timeline-aligned ranking across methods that is not visible on prior benchmarks, validating DF3DV-1K as a genuinely discriminative evaluation surface. - The missing: DI2FIX training and the held-out DF3DV-41 test set both draw from the same DF3DV-1K collection, so it is unclear whether the +0.96 dB PSNR gain reflects genuine generalization or in-distribution advantage; only one out-of-domain test (On-the-go) is included. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone working on distractor-free radiance fields or novel view synthesis in the wild — the dataset fills a real infrastructure gap, the benchmark is the most comprehensive to date, and the DI2FIX pilot is a concrete demonstration of what the scale enables.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (a released dataset/benchmark plus a fine-tuned enhancer). Practical Impact (75) and Completeness (70) anchor the score: a publicly released ~100×-scale matched clean/cluttered dataset that fills a real infrastructure gap, with honest limitations. Rigor (68) is reasonable — DI2FIX ablations, a leave-one-method-out OOD test, and NerfBaselines-standardized evaluation — but no error bars and, critically, the DI2FIX train set and DF3DV-41 test set share the same DF3DV-1K collection, so the +0.96 dB gain may reflect in-distribution advantage rather than generalization. Novelty (62) is in the dataset scale/diversity, not the method (DI2FIX is an engineering fine-tune), and Non-Obviousness (50) is modest since matched-pair capture is sensible rather than surprising.
systems Novelty 62 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 70 Resilience 62 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Distractor-free radiance field methods aim to synthesize photorealistic clean novel views from casually captured images that contain transient visual distractors — people, moving objects, shadows, reflections — collected over a short time span. This is distinct from in-the-wild NeRF (images across seasons) and dynamic NeRF (dense sequential video). The problem is practically important for indoor scanning, outdoor photography, and AR/VR applications where clean GT views are desirable but impractical to capture. Two public datasets dominate the field: RobustNeRF (5 indoor tabletop scenes, 4 distractor types) and On-the-go (12 scenes, 14 distractor types). Both have become insufficiently discriminating — on RobustNeRF, many recent methods exceed 29 dB PSNR, comparable to performance on clean-scene benchmarks. With the number of distractor-free radiance field papers doubling in 2025, no systematic large-scale benchmark exists to identify which methods actually advance robustness, or to enable training of generalizable (not per-scene) models. Key Idea The core idea is to pair every scene with a separate clean capture and a cluttered capture taken under controlled but realistic conditions, enabling ground-truth-based quantitative evaluation of distractor suppression at scale. The key design choice — systematic distractor taxonomy (128 types organized into 17 challenging scenarios in DF3DV-41) — allows failure-mode-specific analysis rather than aggregate performance only. This is a data-contribution paper; the technical ideas in DI2FIX are adapted from DIFIX with a domain-adaptation step rather than a novel method. Methodology Data collection follows a four-stage pipeline: (1) Scene design — themes, distractor types, coverage angle (180°–360°), and image counts are pre-specified; (2) Capture — consumer cameras (iPhone 15 and 11 other smartphone models) at ~12MP under automatic exposure/focus, anti-shake disabled; clean and cluttered images captured either separately (controllable setup) or simultaneously and manually separated (non-controllable); (3) Data curation — two experts per scene remove motion-blur images and verify clean sets are distractor-free, COLMAP estimates poses on combined images, instant-ngp reconstruction validates scene quality (failed scenes either re-parameterized or discarded); (4) Annotation — scene theme, distractor type, scenario, and environment labeled manually. The benchmark evaluates all 10 methods per NerfBaselines to enforce reproducibility. DI2FIX adapts DIFIX (single-step diffusion image enhancer for sparse-view radiance fields) to distractor-free settings by replacing the clean reference view with a same-scene radiance field rendering and fine-tuning on 316,890 training pairs from DF3DV-1K*; pairs with LPIPS > 0.5 are filtered out. Training data scale and degradation-threshold ablations are included. Key Results On DF3DV-1K (1,048 scenes), the top method AsymGS achieves 20.49 dB PSNR / 0.735 SSIM / 0.229 LPIPS vs. the 3DGS baseline of 17.93 / 0.630 / 0.330. The gap between best and baseline is +2.56 dB PSNR, compared to only +4 dB on RobustNeRF where multiple methods cluster near 29 dB. On DF3DV-41, AsymGS achieves 20.35 / 0.712 / 0.247 vs. 18.10 / 0.620 / 0.331 for 3DGS — semantically similar distractors and fluid distractors are identified as the hardest distractor types, nighttime scenes as the hardest scene type. DI2FIX vs. the Vanilla baseline (mean across all radiance field methods) achieves +0.96 dB PSNR and −0.057 LPIPS (21.78 vs. 20.82), while SSIM gains are marginal (+0.006). Out-of-distribution leave-one-method-out test shows stable OOD performance: worst-case −0.1 dB PSNR, −0.005 SSIM, +0.009 LPIPS. Training set size ablation confirms monotonic improvement from 250 to 1K scenes (21.42→21.78 PSNR). Dataset collection required approximately 2.03 L4/L40 GPU years for the full benchmark evaluation. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The dataset itself is the primary contribution — it is genuinely novel in scale (~100× more scenes than prior distractor-free datasets), capture diversity (128 distractor types vs. 4–14), and the matched-pair clean/cluttered design at scale. The benchmark findings (which methods are actually robust, which failure modes remain open) are new empirical knowledge. DI2FIX is an ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION — a fine-tuning of an existing diffusion enhancer, not a methodological invention. Rigor & Reproducibility The paper includes ablations on DI2FIX training: data scale (250/500/750/1K scenes) and LPIPS filtering threshold (0.1–0.9). These are well-controlled and show interpretable trends. A leave-one-method-out OOD test is included for DI2FIX. Method evaluations use NerfBaselines [34] for consistency. The dataset and leaderboard are publicly released at https://johnnylu305.github.io/df3dv1k_web/. No error bars or significance tests are reported for image-quality metrics; however, the dataset's scale (1,048 scenes) partially mitigates this concern. One methodological risk: the DI2FIX training set and DF3DV-41 test set both come from DF3DV-1K, with DF3DV-41 held out from training; the On-the-go dataset serves as the sole out-of-domain transfer test. The benchmark methods are evaluated across all scenes, not a random subset, which is appropriate at this scale. This is a preprint (arXiv:2604.13416v2). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS (a delivered dataset/benchmark infrastructure with an associated trained model). - Practical: DF3DV-1K is publicly released and downloadable; DI2FIX is a plug-and-play post-processing step compatible with any radiance field method on consumer hardware (no special requirements stated beyond what the underlying methods need). - Efficient: DI2FIX is a single-step diffusion model (DIFIX-derived), which is faster than iterative diffusion but the exact inference cost per view is not reported; benchmark evaluation of all 10 methods consumed ~2.03 GPU years in total. - Effective: DI2FIX consistently improves PSNR and LPIPS for every evaluated method, but SSIM gains are marginal; the −0.057 LPIPS gain is perceptually meaningful on distractor artifacts based on qualitative results. - Scalable: DI2FIX is a per-image 2D enhancer, so scaling to larger scenes or higher resolutions is straightforward; the training data scale ablation shows diminishing returns at 1K scenes, suggesting the current 1K-scene dataset near-saturates the model, with a multi-reference architecture identified as a future direction. Strengths - The dataset fills a genuine infrastructure gap: 1,048 scenes with matched clean/cluttered pairs is a ~100× increase over existing public data, and the systematic 17-scenario taxonomy in DF3DV-41 enables failure-mode-specific evaluation rather than aggregate metrics only. - The benchmark reveals a publication-timeline-consistent ranking among methods on DF3DV-1K (recent methods outperform older ones in a consistent ordering), which is not visible on smaller benchmarks — this validates the dataset as a reliable discriminator of genuine progress. - The DI2FIX pilot experiment provides a concrete downstream use case for the dataset, demonstrating scalable training pays off (+0.96 dB PSNR vs. DIFIX without domain fine-tuning which degrades performance), and the OOD generalization test is a credibility-positive inclusion. - Data curation discipline is high: expert manual review per scene, COLMAP + instant-ngp double verification, and 9-month collection over 12 camera types produce a realistic, consumer-camera distribution. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state three limitations: (1) DF3DV-1K is smaller than general novel-view-synthesis datasets (e.g., DL3DV-10K) due to higher per-scene capture and curation costs; (2) slight cloud movement may persist in outdoor clean images despite the short capture window; (3) DI2FIX may fail on severely corrupted input views or exhibit confirmation bias (baking in reconstruction artifacts from the reference radiance field rendering), and a multi-reference framework is suggested as future work. The authors confront these directly and proportionately. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The DI2FIX evaluation mixes On-the-go and DF3DV-41 results into a single averaged number across Tables 4–6, which obscures whether the gain comes primarily from in-distribution DF3DV-41 scenes or from truly out-of-domain On-the-go transfer; per-dataset breakdowns exist in the supplement but are not prominently discussed. - SSIM gains are consistently marginal or unreported as significant across DI2FIX experiments; the paper attributes this to DIFIX not using an SSIM loss, which is technically correct but means one of the three standard metrics provides weak evidence for the claim of consistent improvement. - All 10 benchmarked methods receive fixed default hyperparameters under NerfBaselines; methods that would benefit from scene-specific tuning are evaluated at a disadvantage, and it is unclear whether any method was designed to be robust in its default configuration — this could systematically penalize methods intended for interactive use. - The dataset consists entirely of scenes collected by the authors' institutions (UTS, U. Sydney, NYCU); no diversity analysis by geography, lighting distribution, or sensor brand is reported, leaving open whether the 9-month collection biases toward specific indoor object categories or outdoor weather patterns. Bottom Line Any researcher developing or evaluating distractor-free radiance field methods should treat DF3DV-1K as the new standard evaluation surface — it is the only public benchmark that is both large enough to be statistically meaningful and diverse enough to reveal genuine method differences, and the DI2FIX pilot demonstrates a credible path toward generalizable distractor-free reconstruction.
Recommended 2604.13416 PDF
#62 — Innovative (66)

SwiftCache: Efficient LLM Serving for Multi-turn Conversations with Heterogeneous KV Cache Sharing

J Hu, M Xu, S Wang, C Ma, M Shen, K Ye, L Qu, C Xu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SwiftCache, a multi-model collaborative LLM serving system that shares KV cache across co-located heterogeneous models via NVLink to reduce time-to-first-token latency and extend maximum context length for long multi-turn conversations. - Why it's important: PCIe-based KV cache offloading consumes 73–86% of TTFT at 5k–50k historical tokens, and HBM capacity forces models like LWM-1M-Text to handle only 18K tokens on a 32 GB GPU despite a 1M-token advertised window — both constraints directly degrade user experience in chatbots and AI agents. - How it does it: It exploits idle GPU memory on co-located low-demand worker models and underutilized NVLink bandwidth to store prefix KV cache remotely, while keeping only the active layer's KV in local HBM (layer stream cache) and enabling O(1) elastic cache resizing via a block-major memory layout. - The good: Reduces P99 TTFT by up to 69% vs. SGLang+HiCache and extends maximum context length by up to 3.98× vs. vLLM with chunked prefill, with worker TTFT degradation capped at 9.7%. - The missing: No ablation study separates the contributions of NVLink transfer speed, layer stream cache, and elastic cache — it is impossible to determine which component drives the headline gains. - Read it or not: Worth reading for LLM serving researchers and practitioners running multiple heterogeneous models on a single NVLink-connected server; less relevant if your deployment spans multiple servers, where NVLink does not apply.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Impact (70) and Novelty (75) drive it — a working ~10.4K-line artifact with anonymous code that fills a real gap (heterogeneous cross-model KV sharing over idle NVLink, unhandled by Mooncake/DroidSpeak), plus the independently useful block-major O(1) elastic layout, validated with unusually broad coverage (4 memory tiers, 2 datasets, 3 model families, current baselines). The decisive weakness is the absence of any ablation (Rigor 55): the 69% TTFT gain cannot be attributed to NVLink bandwidth vs. LSC capacity, which materially affects adoption decisions, and the NVLink-only single-server constraint plus no throughput-at-saturation numbers bound Resilience (60). Composite ≈ 0.15*75+0.15*55+0.10*75+0.15*60+0.15*60+0.30*70 = 66.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multi-turn LLM conversations accumulate historical tokens across turns while each new turn adds only a short user prompt. This creates two serving bottlenecks. First, as conversation history grows, KV caches must be offloaded to CPU or SSD, and PCIe transfer becomes the dominant latency — 73.2% of TTFT at 5k tokens on H20 GPUs, rising to 86.2% at 50k tokens, while actual computation remains ~27 ms. Second, HBM limits the maximum achievable context length far below the model's specification: LWM-1M-Text reaches only 18K tokens on a 32 GB V100 and 160K tokens on a 96 GB H20. Chunked prefill, parallelism, and KV compression approaches either require more hardware or sacrifice accuracy. Meanwhile, empirical profiling shows that co-located models serving different workload types (code completion: 0.1% prefix cache hit rate; multi-turn conversation: 90.7%) leave substantial KV cache memory and NVLink bandwidth idle, creating an unexploited opportunity. Key Idea The core insight is that different co-located LLM workloads have sharply heterogeneous KV cache demand, and the high-bandwidth NVLink interconnect between their GPUs is otherwise idle (since standard tensor/pipeline parallelism operates within a single model). SwiftCache maps the prefix cache of a high-demand model into the idle KV cache blocks of low-demand co-located models and streams it back layer-by-layer over NVLink, so the master GPU never needs to touch PCIe or CPU. Separately, since only one transformer layer accesses its KV cache at a time during inference, all other layers' KV can live on remote GPUs, and the master GPU's local HBM can be repurposed to hold a single streaming layer buffer — dramatically increasing the number of tokens that fit. Methodology SwiftCache organizes co-located models into a master (high KV demand) and N workers (low demand). Three mechanisms compose the system: (1) Layer Stream Cache (LSC): The master keeps only the currently executing layer's KV in local HBM. KV for other layers is stored in workers' elastic caches and preloaded asynchronously before attention for that layer fires. Capacity is N_LSC = min(Σ K_i, K_master), where K_i = ⌊C_worker_i / (M_block × L)⌋. Remaining local memory is used as a Regular Cache storing full-layer KV for the highest-hit prefixes with no cross-GPU transfer. (2) Elastic Cache with Block-Major Layout: Workers store KV in shape (n_blocks, n_layers, block_elems) rather than the conventional layer-major (n_layers, n_blocks, block_elems). Resizing requires only appending or releasing the last block with no data movement, reducing complexity from O(n_layers × n_blocks) to O(1). Scaling granularity is determined by the LCM of master and worker block element counts to preserve GPU memory alignment across heterogeneous architectures. (3) Layer-wise Pipelining: KV store after QKV projection is asynchronous. KV load for layer i overlaps with FFN computation of layer i-1. This hides most NVLink transfer latency. Scale-up is triggered per incoming request when existing worker blocks are insufficient; scale-down is triggered periodically using max request length in the past t seconds as a demand estimate. The scheduler uses iteration-level FCFS scheduling. The C++ scheduler and cache manager handle coordination via ZeroMQ; GPU-to-GPU transfer uses torch.distributed. Three critical design decisions: block-major layout (well-justified by O(1) complexity reduction), LCM-based minimum elastic unit (correct but adds heterogeneous-model complexity), and the past-window heuristic for scale-down (simple, but the choice of window t is not ablated). Key Results Testbed: 4× NVIDIA H20 GPUs (96 GB each), NVLink 400 GB/s bidirectional, PCIe 4.0 at 32 GB/s shared. Workloads: ShareGPT (90K multi-turn conversations, sessions up to 602K tokens) and L-Eval (500+ tasks, 2.6K–195K token sequences). Models: Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-14B, Qwen3-32B, LWM-1M-Text. Baselines: vLLM 0.14.0+LMCache 0.3.15, SGLang 0.5.3+HiCache, vLLM with chunked prefill (chunk sizes 8K and 32K). Baselines are current and strong. P99 TTFT: SwiftCache reduces it by ~67–69% vs. SGLang+HiCache and ~54–58% vs. vLLM+LMCache on both datasets, across all three master models. Maximum context length: 1.58× to 3.98× vs. vLLM+chunked prefill (8K, the default), and 1.81× to 6.66× vs. vLLM without chunked prefill, across GPU memory tiers from 24 GB (L4) to 96 GB (H20). Worker interference: TTFT degradation ≤9.7%, TPOT degradation ≤6.5% at all tested request rates and input lengths. KV transfer overhead: <1.4% of total latency for load and <1.1% for store in Qwen3-32B; drops to <0.1% when computation-communication overlap is applied; 99th percentile load time is 11 ms and store time is 6 ms. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The heterogeneous cross-model KV cache sharing via NVLink is genuinely novel: Mooncake shares KV across same-model instances; DroidSpeak requires same-foundation-model architecture; SwiftCache handles architecturally dissimilar models. The block-major layout for O(1) elastic resizing is a clean, independently useful contribution. The layer stream cache (streaming only the active layer) extends prior KV offloading work in a principled way. The combination of these three components into a working end-to-end system distinguishes this from incremental work. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablation study is presented — the paper does not isolate (a) NVLink vs. PCIe transfer speed, (b) layer stream cache context extension, and (c) elastic cache resizing contributions individually. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported for any latency metric. The scale-down heuristic's time window t is mentioned but never ablated. All experiments are run on a single 4-GPU NVLink server; results may not generalize to NVSwitch topologies or GPU-count ratios differing from the 1-master:2-worker setup used. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SwiftCache-97FE (anonymous open-science repository, consistent with double-blind submission). This is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2606.16135v1, June 2026); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS - Practical: Requires co-located heterogeneous models on a single multi-GPU server with NVLink or NVSwitch interconnect; excludes PCIe-only multi-GPU boxes and all cross-server deployments, which represent the majority of production LLM infrastructure. - Efficient: KV transfer overhead drops to <0.1% of total latency with pipelining for Qwen3-32B; worker interference stays ≤9.7% TTFT degradation, making the system's overhead well within acceptable bounds for multi-tenant serving. - Effective: Demonstrates 54–69% P99 TTFT reductions and 1.58–3.98× context length extensions on real multi-turn workloads with current strong baselines — the evidence credibly supports the stated claims for the tested configuration. - Scalable: Explicitly limited to a single multi-GPU server; the paper acknowledges that multi-server NVLink extension requires additional hardware support and is deferred to future work; no experiments test more than 4 GPUs or stress the master under full worker load. The system delivers a working software artifact (~10.4K lines of Python/Triton/C++) supporting Qwen3 and LLaMA2 family models. Strengths - The cross-model KV cache sharing for architecturally heterogeneous models is a genuine capability gap filled: neither Mooncake (same model, different instances) nor DroidSpeak (same foundation model) handles this case, and the NVLink exploitation is well-motivated by concrete bandwidth measurements. - The block-major layout reducing dynamic resize complexity from O(n_layers × n_blocks) to O(1) is a clean, independently deployable insight applicable beyond SwiftCache. - Evaluation covers four GPU memory tiers (24/32/80/96 GB), two real-world datasets, three model families, and three current baselines — unusually broad for a systems paper of this scope. - The worker interference measurement is a responsible design choice that directly addresses the practical concern of co-location, and the ≤9.7% numbers quantify that the sharing is largely transparent. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state that SwiftCache is limited to single-server, multi-GPU environments because cross-server NVLink communication is not possible, and that extending to multi-server deployments would incur significant communication overhead requiring additional hardware — deferred to future work. This is a direct and honest acknowledgment of the system's primary deployment constraint. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The absent ablation study is the most consequential gap: without isolating each component, it is unknown whether 69% TTFT improvement comes primarily from NVLink vs. PCIe speed (a pure bandwidth argument) or from the expanded cache capacity enabled by LSC. This matters for adoption decisions — an operator with PCIe-only hardware cannot know whether partial adoption helps. - The paper does not evaluate a workload scenario where the worker's request rate spikes while the master holds long live sessions — scale-up reclaims blocks from the master, but the paper does not show what happens to in-flight long-context requests when their borrowed cache is partially evicted mid-inference. - No throughput metric (tokens/s or requests/s at saturation) is reported; the layer-wise streaming introduces per-layer synchronization barriers that could reduce decoding throughput under high concurrency, and practitioners need this data. - The deployment model assumes at least one co-located worker model with idle capacity; in practice, operators may run all GPUs near capacity, eliminating the donor pool entirely. The paper does not characterize minimum worker idle fraction required for meaningful gain. - The scale-down heuristic's time window t is not ablated; a poorly chosen t could cause oscillation (frequent scale-up/down cycles) or waste (slow recovery of worker capacity after a brief master quiet period). Bottom Line SwiftCache delivers well-evidenced, practically significant improvements for multi-turn long-context inference on NVLink-connected multi-GPU servers with co-located heterogeneous models, and the block-major O(1) elastic layout is worth adopting broadly; the absence of ablations and the hard NVLink-only single-server constraint prevent this from being a general solution, but practitioners in the target deployment environment should read it.
Recommended 2606.16135 PDF
#63 — Innovative (66)

ITME: Inference Tiered Memory Expansion with Disaggregated CXL-Hybrid Memories

H Jang, Y Min, S Kim, T Ahn, H Kim, Y Joo, H Kim… · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ITME, a disaggregated memory tier that presents CXL-hybrid memory (DRAM-cached NVMe SSDs accessed via RDMA) as a byte-addressable TB-scale extension of the LLM inference memory hierarchy, combined with a pipelined multi-tier DMA prefetching system that exploits the deterministic access patterns of model weights and long-context KV caches. - Why it's important: Agentic and long-context LLM workloads produce KV cache footprints that outgrow both GPU HBM and host DRAM, and existing disaggregated storage solutions (DPU-based JBOF/NVMe-oF) suffer from high cost, read/write I/O contention, and lack of prefetching, making TB-scale inference capacity prohibitively expensive or too slow. - How it does it: ITME co-designs a CXL-hybrid memory device (32 GB DRAM cache over 2-channel PCIe Gen5 NVMe SSDs, exposed as a NUMA node) with a user-level prefetch API and a read-priority I/O scheduler that pipelines SSD→DRAM→RDMA→host→GPU data movement, hiding all three latency tiers behind concurrent GPU computation. - The good: With only a 30 GB host staging buffer, ITME sustains productive inference beyond turn 21 in 35-turn conversations where a 128 GB CPU-offload baseline collapses entirely, delivering up to 35.7% throughput improvement at those extreme footprints. - The missing: The evaluation uses a single A100 GPU, dual 100 Gbps NICs, and at most 256 concurrent conversations; it does not test multi-GPU tensor-parallel serving, InfiniBand-class 400 Gbps interconnects, or the interaction with disaggregated prefill/decode architectures (e.g., DistServe), so the performance picture at production cluster scale is unverified. - Read it or not: Read it if you design LLM serving infrastructure, memory systems, or storage hardware for agentic/long-context workloads; the hardware-software co-design detail and honest characterization of I/O contention are directly actionable, though the limited scale of evaluation warrants caution before extrapolating to production deployments.
Why this rank: Systems profile. Novelty (72) and a strong honest characterization carry it: a co-designed CXL-hybrid memory tier whose read-priority I/O-scheduling insight (write-buffer saturation is the dominant failure mode that collapses NVMe-oF baselines) is concrete and actionable, with unusually quantitative topology/metadata-scaling analysis. Rigor (60) and Resilience (62) limit the composite — single A100 with dual 100Gbps NICs, author-implemented baselines (including a deliberately favorable ideal NVMe-oF), no error bars, no multi-GPU/400Gbps/cluster-scale results, and no tail-latency reporting, so the 35.7% figure is an existence proof rather than a production projection. Composite ≈ 0.15*72+0.15*60+0.10*72+0.15*70+0.15*62+0.30*65 = 66.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 70 Resilience 62 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Large-scale LLM inference faces a compounding capacity crisis. Model weights alone can exceed 300 GB (OPT-175B in FP16: 325 GB); agentic multi-turn workloads additionally accumulate KV cache state that grows with every conversation turn and must persist across turns for prefix reuse. GPU HBM is physically constrained (80 GB on A100), and host DRAM is limited by CPU socket and memory-channel count. Existing disaggregated storage tiers based on DPU/JBOF and NVMe-oF address capacity but introduce three overlapping problems: expensive DPU hardware with complex software stacks, unregulated background SSD writes that starve time-critical reads, and the absence of a prefetching mechanism, all of which cause severe pipeline stalls or cache collapse when the working set exceeds host memory. No prior system bridges the gap between fast-but-finite host DRAM (T2) and cost-efficient-but-slow remote storage (T4) with a low-overhead, byte-addressable, prefetch-capable shared context tier. Key Idea The core insight is that the two largest LLM data types — model weights and long-context prefix KV caches — exhibit deterministic or near-deterministic access sequences (strict layer-by-layer for weights; append-write/sequential-restore for KV caches), which makes them ideal candidates for proactive, latency-hiding prefetching. By mapping these access sequences onto a CXL-hybrid device's internal hardware prefetch engine (NAND→DRAM) and a software-directed multi-tier DMA pipeline (DRAM→RDMA→host staging buffer→GPU), ITME can overlap all storage and network latency with GPU computation, making remote flash-backed memory appear nearly as fast as local DRAM for these specific access patterns. Methodology ITME organizes a four-tier hierarchy: GPU HBM (T1), host DRAM used as a staging buffer (T2), a remote CXL-hybrid memory server (T3.5), and optionally deeper shared storage (T4). The CXL-hybrid device integrates a 4-channel DDR4 32 GB DRAM cache with a 2-channel PCIe Gen5 NVMe SSD array (up to 2 TB), exposed to the OS as a CPU-less NUMA node via the CXL protocol. The hardware controller implements a 16-way set-associative LRU DRAM cache with 4 KB cache lines, managing metadata in 15–16 MB of on-chip SRAM; a three-stage atomic metadata update protocol (read-lock-write-unlock) ensures consistency at the cost of three cycles per update. A user-level prefetch library (chm_prefetch, chm_prefetch_object, chm_prefetch_size) lets the inference framework translate virtual addresses to physical addresses via /proc/pid/pagemap and write hardware prefetch commands to a memory-mapped register set, triggering SSD→DRAM staging with ~1–3 μs software overhead per command. On the software side, ITME is integrated into vLLM (v0.17.0). Two CPU pinned-memory staging buffers decouple GPU execution: an eviction store buffer (GPU→host→CXL writes) and a load ring buffer (CXL→host→GPU reads, maintained 2–3 chunks ahead). KV blocks are aggregated into coarse 512 MB chunks exploiting the natural LRU grouping of per-request blocks. A read-priority I/O scheduler suppresses SSD writes whenever a read slot is demanded, deferring writes to the decode phase's I/O idle windows. Model weights are streamed layer-wise (e.g., 1344 MB per layer for Llama-3.1 70B); prefix KV caches are aggregated into 512 MB sequential reads (32 blocks × 16 MB at block size 128). Critical design decisions: (1) 32 GB DRAM cache (not 64 GB) selected because doubling the cache causes an 86.7% SRAM surge for metadata, confirmed by a metadata capacity analysis; (2) 4-channel DRAM + 2-channel SSD topology selected to balance host-facing (18 GB/s) and SSD-facing (18 GB/s) bandwidth, avoiding the asymmetry that starved the hardware prefetcher in a 2-channel DRAM + 1-channel SSD configuration (10 GB/s vs. 9 GB/s); (3) layer-wise granularity for GPU-side DMA chosen over block-wise because it enables the DMA of layer L+1 to overlap with GPU execution of layer L. Key Results The test platform is two Dell PowerEdge R770 servers (dual Intel Xeon 6730, 256 GB DDR5, NVIDIA A100 80 GB HBM, dual Mellanox ConnectX-6 100 Gbps NICs) with a remote server hosting either an SK Hynix CMM 32 GB + 2× KIOXIA PCIe Gen5 NVMe SSDs (performance potential analysis) or an Intel Agilex 7 FPGA with 32 GB DDR4 + 2× SK Hynix Platinum P51 SSDs (functional feasibility). Benchmarks use Llama-3.1 8B and 70B on ShareGPT (128–256 concurrent conversations, up to 35 turns) and Mooncake (batch 16, 4K input). Key results: (1) At turns 3–5 with 40 GB cumulative KV footprint, ITME achieves 1.81× TTFT speedup vs. recomputation for the 70B model (vs. 3.02× for ideal all-in-GPU 80 GB caching). (2) In the 35-turn stress test, CPU-offload (128 GB host DRAM) collapses at turn 21 (hit rate→0%), while ITME (30 GB staging buffer) sustains inference and achieves up to 35.7% throughput improvement in extended turns. (3) ITME outperforms an ideal local NVMe-oF baseline (same staging buffer, no network overhead), which degrades to recomputation speed from turn 5 due to read/write contention. (4) For weight-only prefetching on Llama-3.1 70B (105 GB weights), ITME with 4 GB DRAM prefetch buffer incurs only 8% throughput degradation vs. host-memory baseline. (5) The FPGA prototype sustains 18 GB/s read / 12 GB/s write on DRAM cache hits, 20–25% below the CMM baseline; cache miss bandwidth degrades below 1 GB/s on the FPGA, which the authors acknowledge as a known limitation under active optimization. The CMM sustains 4–5 GB/s under extreme contention. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The primary contribution is a co-designed hardware-software system: a CXL-hybrid memory device with a novel user-directed prefetch API integrated into an LLM inference framework with read-priority I/O scheduling. The individual components — CXL-hybrid memory, RDMA, NVMe prefetching, tiered KV cache management — are known; the novelty lies in their co-design specifically targeting the deterministic access patterns of LLM inference workloads, the concrete hardware topology analysis (DRAM/SSD channel balancing), and the empirical demonstration that read/write I/O contention is the dominant bottleneck that defeats NVMe-oF without this co-design. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: weight-only prefetching is isolated from KV prefetching (Figure 12); DRAM cache capacity sensitivity is swept from 4 GB to 32 GB (Figure 12b); FPGA vs. CMM bandwidth is compared directly (Figure 14); channel configuration tradeoffs are quantified (Table 3). However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple-seed results are reported — all throughput and TTFT figures appear to be single-run measurements. Code, hardware designs, and model weights are not released; the SK Hynix CMM is referenced as a commercial product rather than an open specification, and the FPGA prototype is proprietary. The comparison baseline (enhanced vLLM with NVMe-oF + weight prefetching) is implemented by the authors and not independently verified; the ideal NVMe-oF baseline deliberately removes network overhead to provide a favorable upper bound, and real-world DPU-based NVMe-oF is not evaluated. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.12556v2); it has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built hardware prototype and software integration evaluated by deployment. - Practical: The CMM evaluation uses production-grade SK Hynix hardware and commodity 100 Gbps NICs on standard Dell servers, so the hardware is commercially available in principle, though the CXL-hybrid device described is a custom SK hynix product not yet broadly available as a standard commodity component. - Efficient: ITME achieves near-host-DRAM weight prefetching throughput with as little as 4 GB DRAM cache (8% degradation), and the CXL-hybrid device sustains ~18 GB/s read bandwidth; the software overhead of the prefetch API is 1–3 μs per command, negligible relative to transfer times. However, the 30 GB host staging buffer still consumes substantial host memory. - Effective: Evidence shows ITME solves the stated problem — sustaining inference across conversation lengths that exhaust host DRAM baselines — but performance degrades under I/O contention (turns 10–20 show declining hit rates and TTFT fluctuations), and KV cache misses are resolved by GPU recomputation rather than retrieval, which may be unacceptable for very long prefixes. - Scalable: Tested on a single A100 with dual 100 Gbps NICs; multi-GPU tensor-parallel serving, 400 Gbps InfiniBand interconnects, and clusters with tens of GPU nodes are not evaluated. The 35-turn / 256-conversation workload is modest compared to production cluster traffic; scaling claims are not empirically tested. The work delivers a software integration (vLLM patch) and a hardware prototype (FPGA + CMM testbed), but neither is publicly released. Strengths - The read-priority I/O scheduling insight — that NVMe SSD write-buffer saturation is the dominant failure mode for disaggregated KV cache tiers, and that this can be mitigated by deferring writes to decode-phase idle windows — is a concrete, actionable finding backed by comparative data (Figure 11 shows NVMe-oF collapse at turn 5 without this mechanism). - The hardware topology analysis (Table 3, metadata SRAM scaling in Table 2) is unusually quantitative for a systems paper, providing actionable design space guidance for choosing DRAM cache size and SSD channel count. - Using two evaluation platforms (CMM for performance potential, FPGA for functional feasibility) acknowledges the gap between prototype and production and allows the authors to bound both dimensions honestly. - The user-directed prefetch API design — translating virtual to physical addresses via pagemap and writing to memory-mapped registers with ~1–3 μs overhead — demonstrates that hardware-level prefetching can be exposed to the inference framework with negligible software cost. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) the FPGA prototype's cache-miss bandwidth degrades below 1 GB/s, far below the theoretical 23.3 GB/s maximum, and is under active optimization; (2) during turns 10–20, ITME's hit rate declines below CPU-offload due to I/O contention within the CXL-hybrid memory, causing TTFT fluctuations; (3) KV cache misses are handled by GPU recomputation rather than retrieval, which they justify on efficiency grounds but acknowledge is not universally applicable; (4) simultaneous weight and KV fetching creates bandwidth contention requiring careful scheduling. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The evaluation is confined to a single A100 GPU with 100 Gbps NICs; production LLM inference typically uses multi-GPU nodes with 400–800 Gbps InfiniBand, where the network-to-storage bandwidth ratio and prefetch scheduling change significantly. The 35.7% throughput claim may not transfer. - All baselines are author-implemented; an independent reproduction of the NVMe-oF baseline or a comparison against a commercially deployed DPU-based JBOF system (e.g., NVIDIA CMX, mentioned in the intro but not evaluated) is absent. - The KV cache miss recomputation fallback is appropriate for short prefixes but becomes expensive for very long contexts (e.g., 100K+ token agents); the paper does not characterize the recomputation cost or the miss rate as a function of prefix length. - The workload (ShareGPT, Mooncake) covers multi-turn chat; it does not cover retrieval-augmented generation, code completion with large repository contexts, or batch inference scenarios where prefix hit patterns differ substantially. - Cost analysis is qualitative ("cost-efficient" vs. DPU); a concrete $/GB or $/throughput comparison against DPU-based alternatives would substantiate the central cost-efficiency claim. - The paper does not evaluate tail latency (P99 TTFT), only average speedup. Given the I/O contention-induced fluctuations visible in Figure 10, tail latency behavior is likely worse than the mean suggests. Bottom Line ITME is a credible, hardware-grounded contribution to the emerging disaggregated LLM inference memory stack that solves a real and acute problem (I/O contention collapsing CPU-offload baselines beyond host DRAM limits), but the single-GPU, dual-100Gbps-NIC evaluation scope and absence of production-scale cluster results mean practitioners building multi-GPU serving clusters should treat the 35.7% throughput figure as an existence proof rather than a deployment projection.
Search Results 2606.12556 PDF
#64 — Innovative (66)

Smaller Models are Natural Explorers for Policy-Level Diversity in GRPO (20 ▲ )

Yiming Ren, Yiran Xu, Zicheng Lin, Chufan Shi, Yukang Chen, Dingdong Wang, Tianhe Wu, Junjie Wang, Yujiu Yang, Yu Qiao, Ruihang Chu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes S2L-PO (Small-to-Large Policy Optimization), a framework that uses a frozen smaller model from the same family as a rollout explorer during GRPO training of a larger model, with progressive annealing from small-model to fully on-policy sampling. - Why it's important: GRPO's gradient quality collapses when rollouts are homogeneous, and the standard fix — raising sampling temperature — creates step-wise noise that breaks long-range logical coherence in multi-step reasoning chains, actively harming gradient signal quality. - How it does it: Smaller distilled models exhibit higher pass@k at large k (policy-level diversity), and because their perturbation is time-invariant across a rollout rather than i.i.d. per token, they produce temporally correlated, strategy-diverse trajectories that yield constructive cross-step gradient accumulation scaling as O(L²) vs. O(L) for token-level noise. - The good: +8.8% on AIME24 and +10.4% on AIME25 using a 1.7B explorer to train an 8B model; gains replicated across Qwen3 and InternLM2.5 families; a controlled knockout experiment (removing diversity from small-model rollouts collapses performance to baseline) cleanly isolates the causal mechanism. - The missing: AIME24/25 have only 30 problems each, and no error bars or multiple seeds are reported, so headline gains of ~8% represent roughly 2–3 extra correct answers and could be within run-to-run variance; additionally, no comparison against other GRPO diversity methods (DRA-GRPO, XRPO, entropy regularization) is provided. - Read it or not: Practitioners training large reasoning models with GRPO who face diversity collapse should read this — the insight is theoretically grounded, implementation effort is near zero, and gains are consistent across two model families; those working on non-verifiable reward settings can deprioritize it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (72) leads: 'smaller models are natural explorers,' the pass@k crossover diagnostic, and the formal O(L²)-vs-O(L) covariance argument for policy-level vs token-level perturbation are non-obvious and substantive (Novelty 68, NEW METHOD + finding). Rigor (66) is solid for a peer-reviewed ICML 2026 paper with released code and a clean causal knockout (equalizing diversity collapses gains to baseline), cross-family replication on Qwen3 and InternLM2.5 — but AIME24/25 have only 30 problems each, no error bars are reported (an ~8.8% gain is 2–3 answers), and there is no comparison to other GRPO diversity methods. Completeness (62) and Resilience (62) reflect those gaps plus an empirically-unverified Hessian-alignment assumption. Strong fit to the user's positively-rated diversity-collapse phenomenology work.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 66 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 62 Resilience 62 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation GRPO computes group-relative advantages from within-group rollout statistics; when all candidates for a prompt are near-identical, advantage signals vanish and learning stagnates. The dominant remedy — raising sampling temperature — injects per-token i.i.d. noise that compounds over long decoding chains: trajectories diverge in prefix exponentially fast (Pr(Mt=1) ≤ (1−p)^t), late tokens are generated under a mixture of divergent prefixes, and long-range cross-token dependence weakens, making gradient contributions across decoding steps cancel rather than reinforce. The result is surface diversity (high token entropy) but behavioral incoherence. Data-engineering alternatives (diverse response curation, intra-group diversity rewards) add task-specific overhead and resist transfer. The gap is a structured, coherent diversity source that does not degrade reasoning trajectory quality. Key Idea Distillation from a large teacher to a smaller student within the same model family induces a time-invariant structured shift in the student's inductive bias — a single perturbation δθ applied at every decoding step rather than fresh noise injected at each step. This means all per-step gradient contributions share a common perturbation component, inducing positive cross-step covariance (lower-bounded by γ − 5B²Pr_std(Mt=0) − O(||δθ||³)) that causes gradient accumulation to scale constructively as O(L²) rather than as a random walk. The empirical signature of this property is the pass@k crossover: smaller models have lower pass@1 but higher pass@k at large k, because they explore diverse solution strategies rather than concentrating on a single mode. Methodology S2L-PO modifies only the rollout generation step of GRPO, leaving the objective, advantage computation, and KL penalty untouched. At each training step, a group of G rollouts is drawn: Gw from a frozen smaller policy πω and Gs = G − Gw from the trainable large policy πθ. Gw is linearly annealed from G to 0 over the first Tmix steps (defaulting to the first half of total training steps, implemented as 8 of 16 logical steps); after Tmix the framework recovers standard on-policy GRPO. The theoretical framework formalizes the distinction via cross-step covariance bounds: Proposition E.2 gives an upper bound for token-level perturbation (|Cov(ft,gs)| ≤ 5Pr(Mt=0), which becomes vacuous as temperature rises), and Proposition E.4 gives a positive lower bound for policy-level perturbation that persists as long as Hessian alignment γ is nonzero. Three critical design decisions: (1) freezing the small model — well-justified for efficiency and stability, enables rollout reuse across runs; (2) progressive vs. abrupt annealing — ablated, progressive strictly better because abrupt switching causes distributional shock; (3) transition length — ablated on Qwen3-8B, longer transitions (8 vs. 5 steps) yield higher ceilings. Training uses DAPO17k (verifiable multi-step reasoning), 16 rollouts per question, 8× NVIDIA L20 GPUs, batch size 1024, max response length 4096 tokens, learning rate 1×10⁻⁶, KL coefficient 1×10⁻³. Key Results Across two model families (Qwen3, InternLM2.5) and four mathematical reasoning benchmarks: Qwen3-8B with 1.7B explorer: AIME24 15.0% → 23.8% (+8.8%), AIME25 12.1% → 22.5% (+10.4%), MATH500 57.3% → 61.5% (+4.2%), OlympiadBench 18.1% → 19.7% (+1.6%). Qwen3-14B with 4B explorer: AIME24 18.0% → 24.4% (+6.4%), MATH500 58.7% → 62.7% (+4.0%). InternLM2.5-7B with 1.8B explorer: AIME24 0.1% → 4.6% (+4.5%), MATH500 18.6% → 22.6% (+4.0%). Out-of-domain CommonsenseQA: Qwen3-8B 63.9% → 67.8% (S2L-PO-4B), showing gains transfer beyond training distribution. Diversity metrics (Self-BLEU, Edit Diversity, Unique Answer Ratio) are strictly monotonic with model size across 1.7B–14B, with 1.7B achieving 21% higher Unique Answer Ratio than 14B (0.576 vs. 0.476). Controlled knockout experiment (Table 4): equalizing small-model diversity to match large-model metrics collapses AIME24 from 23.8% to 14.7% (vs. GRPO baseline 15.0%), directly attributing gains to diversity rather than off-policy mixing. The baseline (standard GRPO) is current and appropriate; the method is not compared against other diversity-enhancing GRPO variants, only against vanilla GRPO and a high-temperature ablation (T=1.5, which regresses during training). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The empirical observation that pass@k crossovers reveal policy-level diversity in distilled model families is a genuine finding (not previously framed this way). The S2L-PO framework is novel in operationalizing this as a training mechanism. The theoretical covariance analysis distinguishing token-level vs. policy-level perturbation quality (O(L) vs. O(L²) gradient accumulation) is a substantive formal contribution. The progressive annealing mechanism is new in this context. Off-policy GRPO mixing is not new (prior work exists), but the specific motivation, theoretical grounding, and annealing design are original contributions rather than a straightforward application of known techniques. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for the three key design decisions (small-only baseline, abrupt vs. progressive switching, transition length), all on Qwen3-8B. The causal knockout experiment (Table 4) is the strongest methodological asset. Formal proofs for the covariance bounds appear in Appendix E with complete lemmas, propositions, and corollaries. However, no error bars or multiple random seeds are reported; AIME24/25 have only 30 problems each, so an 8.8% gain is approximately 2–3 extra correct answers — a meaningful effect size but not robust without variance estimates. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/qishisuren123/S2L-PO. Training data is public (DAPO17k). No comparison against other GRPO diversity methods is included, which is the main empirical gap. The theoretical lower bound (Proposition E.4) requires Hessian alignment condition γ > 0 and small ||δθ||³; these are plausible for same-family distillation but are not verified empirically. Paper is published at ICML 2026 (peer-reviewed). Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Runs on a single 8× NVIDIA L20 node (commodity research hardware); the frozen small model adds negligible overhead beyond storage and inference cost. - Efficient: Rollout compute is reduced because small-model inference is cheaper and rollouts can be reused across multiple large-model training runs; the paper does not quantify the FLOPs reduction numerically, only qualitatively. - Effective: Gains are consistent across two model families and four benchmarks, an OOD benchmark shows generalization, and the knockout experiment confirms diversity as the mechanism rather than off-policy mixing or other confounds. - Scalable: Tested up to 14B target model and 4B explorer on a single node; no experiments at 32B+ scales; behavior with non-verifiable or open-ended rewards is explicitly unvalidated and acknowledged as future work. Software artifact: code released on GitHub. Strengths - The covariance bound analysis (Propositions E.2 and E.4) gives a theoretically principled reason — not just intuition — why policy-level perturbations produce O(L²) constructive gradient accumulation versus O(L) random-walk behavior for token-level noise, which is a non-obvious formal result. - The controlled diversity knockout (Table 4) is methodologically strong: by filtering small-model rollouts to match large-model diversity metrics, it isolates the causal contribution of diversity from other confounds including the off-policy mixing effect itself. - Cross-family validation on both Qwen3 and InternLM2.5 with consistent gains reduces the risk that results are specific to a single model family's distillation procedure. - The method requires zero changes to the GRPO objective, advantage computation, or optimizer — only rollout sampling changes — making it genuinely drop-in compatible with existing implementations. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors state (Appendix F) that evaluation is constrained by computational resources, preventing exhaustive coverage of model families and benchmark categories; S2L-PO has not been validated on tasks with non-verifiable or open-ended rewards; and the capability boundary at broader model scales, task domains, and modalities remains unexplored. The authors acknowledge these directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - AIME24 and AIME25 contain 30 problems each; without error bars or multiple seeds, the headline +8.8% (≈2–3 extra correct answers) cannot be confidently distinguished from run-to-run variance, particularly given that GRPO training curves in Figure 4 show noticeable noise. - No comparison against other GRPO diversity methods (DRA-GRPO, XRPO, entropy regularization, group-aware RL) is included; the competitive landscape relative to the state of the diversity-enhancement literature is entirely uncharted. - The Hessian alignment condition γ > 0 required for the policy-level lower bound (Proposition E.4) is argued to be natural for same-family distilled models but is not empirically measured or verified in any experiment. - Compute reduction claims ("reduced FLOPs," "amortized rollout compute") are purely qualitative; no wall-clock or FLOPs measurements are reported. - The annealing schedule hyperparameter (Tmix = first half of training) is ablated on a single model/benchmark; sensitivity across different model scales, training lengths, or task types is unknown. - The framework requires that same-family distilled models exist and are accessible; for proprietary or custom model families without public distilled variants, the approach cannot be directly applied. Bottom Line A practitioner training reasoning models with GRPO should read this paper: the core insight is theoretically grounded, the implementation overhead is negligible, and gains are consistent and substantial across two model families — even if the evaluation scope is narrow and lacks statistical rigor on the small AIME benchmarks.
Recommended 2605.30789 PDF
#65 — Innovative (66)

DragMesh-2: Physically Plausible Dexterous Hand-Object Interaction with Articulated Objects (61 ▲ )

Tianshan Zhang, Yijia Duan, Yanjun Li, Zeyu Zhang, Hao Tang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces DragMesh-2, a contact-driven reinforcement learning framework and PICA training mechanism that enables a 51-DoF dexterous hand to open articulated objects (drawers, doors) purely through physical hand–handle contact, together with a heuristically generated dataset of 277 reference trajectories across 7 GAPartNet object categories. - Why it's important: Robots deployed in homes and hospitals must manipulate articulated furniture and appliances, but existing RL policies trained under fixed dynamics overfit nominal contact loads and fail when resistance increases, making them brittle for real deployment. - How it does it: PICA augments PPO with contact-maintaining reward terms, multiplicative damping randomization, a Gated Linear Attention (GLA) encoder over a 16-step control-history window, and causal-window auxiliary supervision that forces the temporal encoder to predict observable contact-response signals (object joint displacement, palm–handle distance, detachment risk, tracking stress). - The good: Under ×4 OOD damping, PICA achieves 0.56 deterministic success versus 0.27 for state-only PPO, 0.30 for GRU-PPO, and 0.09 for Transformer-PPO, and a training-length study directly demonstrates that longer training raises nominal (×1) success from 0.90 to 1.00 while collapsing ×4 success from 0.55 to 0.10 — providing clean empirical evidence that nominal success is a misleading checkpoint criterion. - The missing: All quantitative evaluation is purely in simulation; the hardware demonstration is a single qualitative stage video with no success metrics, so sim-to-real transfer is completely uncharacterized. - Read it or not: Worth reading for roboticists working on contact-rich manipulation or dexterous hand RL, particularly for the PICA training protocol and the cautionary finding about saturation collapse; practitioners expecting real-world validation should temper expectations.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Completeness (75) and Non-Obviousness (70) drive the score: the saturation-collapse finding (Table 4 — longer training lifts nominal x1 success to 1.00 while collapsing x4 from 0.55 to 0.10) is a clean, methodologically important insight, and the contact-driven formulation that blocks direct joint actuation is principled. Code and the 277-trajectory dataset are released (Rigor 65), but 20-episode cells give SE~0.10 making several mid-tier comparisons statistically indistinguishable, x4 is always OOD relative to the [1.0,2.0] training interval, and all quantitative results are simulation-only with merely qualitative hardware footage. Honest, direct limitation acknowledgment is a credibility positive.
