Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS — the paper implements and benchmarks algorithms. - Practical: Runs on a standard laptop through a supercomputer cluster via MPI with no special hardware requirement; smallest meaningful tests run on 14 cores. - Efficient: Matrix-free variants store only a list of two integers per node (src→dst pairs sorted high-to-low), achieving near-perfect M/P memory distribution per process; matrix-based variants require sparse CSR storage, approximately 8–10× more memory than matrix-free but still distributed linearly. - Effective: Produces results numerically identical to the exact sequential high-to-low algorithm (relative difference < 10^{-6}); mass is conserved to 10^{-11}; single-process run reduces to exactly one Richardson iteration, matching sequential cost. - Scalable: Demonstrated on 12,288 MPI processes and 1.88×10^9 nodes; iteration count grows empirically with P, but the depression-filling preprocessing step (Barnes et al. 2014 priority-flood algorithm) is sequential and becomes the dominant bottleneck at large P, limiting practical end-to-end scalability; no GPU implementation is provided or discussed. Software artifact: deal.II code gallery contribution (PR pending merge). Strengths - The preconditioner design is the genuine intellectual contribution: the observation that per-process high-to-low numbering makes diagonal blocks triangular, enabling exact local solvers, is a sharp insight that maps the known optimal sequential algorithm directly onto the parallel preconditioner, achieving much faster convergence than the IDA method's ILU approximation. - The I+X reformulation elegantly eliminates redundant computation: by replacing full-matrix products with the cheaper off-diagonal-only R product per iteration, it reduces both memory traffic and arithmetic without changing the mathematical solution. - The three-platform evaluation with explicit discussion of cache-size effects on method choice is more honest and practically useful than single-machine benchmarking; it reveals that the best variant is hardware-dependent and explains why. - Appendix B provides a physically interpretable convergence bound — iteration count proportional to the maximum number of partition-boundary crossings by any stream — grounding the empirical scaling behavior in the problem's graph structure. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The author directly acknowledges that the sub-linear speedup (52× on 128 processes versus ideal 128×) is "disappointing" from a theoretical HPC standpoint. The depression-filling preprocessing step (priority-flood, Barnes et al. 2014) is identified as sequential and a practical bottleneck; Barnes (2016) scales only to ~48 cores with rectangular tiles. A good initial guess (e.g., coarser-DEM solution as a starting iterate) could reduce iteration counts substantially, left as future work. Higher-order preconditioners (I - X + X^2) are suggested but not implemented. The author confronts these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-domain evaluation: all experiments use the Colorado DEM, whose elongated drainage networks (Rocky Mountain rivers crossing E-W partition boundaries) may differ substantially from flat plateaus, braided deltas, or non-rectangular domains — terrains where streams may cross more partition boundaries per unit distance, degrading iteration scaling. - No controlled comparison with IDA: the claimed ~11× speedup over Richardson et al. (2014) compares 2025 hardware to 2014 hardware on different problem instances; re-running IDA on the same cluster on the same problem would be necessary to make this comparison defensible. - Memory footprint not measured: the M/P distribution claim is stated analytically but no actual memory profiling data is reported. - No GPU path: at 10^{11} nodes, GPU cluster implementations may offer competitive or superior throughput; this is not discussed. - The convergence bound (Appendix B) is an intuitive argument, not a formal proof; no spectral radius analysis of the Richardson iteration with the proposed preconditioner is provided, so the convergence rate guarantee is informal. - The "4.0 seconds on 12,288 processes" headline result uses estimates for P=1 extrapolated from P=8 data, not a direct measurement, making the absolute speedup figure partially inferred rather than measured. Bottom Line This paper delivers a practically useful and well-characterized parallel flow routing algorithm that reduces the wall time for billion-point DEMs by orders of magnitude over prior methods; researchers who run large-scale geomorphology or hydrology simulations on HPC clusters should adopt it, but the informal convergence theory and single-domain evaluation leave open questions about robustness across different landscape types.
Search Results 2606.12800 PDF
#76 — Innovative (65)

Text-Vision Co-Instructed Image Editing (12 ▲ )

Chenxi Xie, Yuhui Wu, Qiaosi Yi, Lei Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TV-Edit, a plug-and-play framework that extends instruction-based image editing foundation models with a Content-Aware Spatial Controller to jointly accept textual instructions and sparse point-pair visual prompts, plus a 23K training dataset (TV-Edit-23K) and a 120-sample benchmark (TV-Edit-Bench). - Why it's important: Neither text-only nor drag-only editing methods can simultaneously specify what semantic transformation to apply and where/how far to apply it, leaving users unable to express fine-grained spatially-constrained edits like "open the jaw slightly" without either imprecision or semantic failure. - How it does it: A lightweight control branch encodes sparse source-to-target point pairs, fuses them with image content and text via Content-Aware Spatial Controller MM-DiT blocks, and injects time-modulated residual features into a frozen editing backbone (FLUX.1 Kontext or Qwen-Image-Edit) trained with a Z0-prediction, t²-weighted flow-matching loss biased toward high-noise timesteps via Beta sampling. - The good: TV-Edit-Qwen reduces dense matching distance (MDd) to 0.0462 on TV-Edit-Bench — a 28.7% improvement over the best drag-only baseline (GoodDrag, 0.0648) — while simultaneously raising Prompt Following to 0.93, surpassing the closed-source NanoBananaPro (0.89) and its own Qwen-Image-Edit backbone (0.86). - The missing: TV-Edit-Bench contains only 120 samples drawn partly from the same source video dataset (UltraVideo) used to build TV-Edit-23K, raising a potential train/test overlap concern that the authors do not address; additionally, MLLM-based evaluation uses Qwen-3-VL, which is the same model family as one of the baselines. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building spatially-controlled image editing systems or studying multi-modal instruction fusion in diffusion backbones; practitioners seeking a drop-in spatial controller for FLUX or Qwen-based editing pipelines will find the architecture directly applicable.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (70) is high — formalizing text-vision co-instructed editing as a distinct task plus a new dataset and benchmark is a NEW TASK + NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET contribution. Impact (70) reflects a plug-and-play controller generalizing across two backbones (FLUX, Qwen) with code/weights forthcoming. Rigor (62) is the limiting dimension: well-structured ablations and strong reported metrics, but no error bars/seeds, a 120-sample self-constructed benchmark, a Qwen-family evaluator rating Qwen-based outputs (evaluator circularity), and unaddressed train/test overlap from shared UltraVideo source. Resilience (58) and Completeness (65) are pulled down by these self-evaluation and contamination concerns and the absence of a user study. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*70 = 65.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 58 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Interactive image editing tools require users to express both semantic intent (what transformation) and spatial grounding (where, how much, in what direction). Textual instructions handle semantics well but fail at spatial precision — a prompt like "slightly open the jaw" cannot encode magnitude or exact trajectory. Drag-based editing handles spatial geometry but carries no semantic signal, so identical drag vectors on a face can mean "open the mouth", "deform the jaw", or "tilt the head". Neither modality alone is sufficient for complex edits involving non-rigid motion, trajectory-constrained actions, or magnitude-sensitive transformations. Prior work attempting to bridge modalities (CLIP-Drag, Drag-Flow) either uses coarse global text signals or requires region masks and per-instance optimization. Key Idea: The core insight is that sparse point-pair visual prompts (trajectory vectors) and textual instructions are complementary rather than redundant: text resolves semantic ambiguity in geometry, and geometry resolves spatial ambiguity in text. The key technical move is early fusion — encoding point trajectories together with image content tokens through a ControlNet-like transformer controller, so that the controller learns which semantic entity a trajectory refers to and what action it implies, rather than treating trajectories as geometry-only constraints. The resulting control features are injected into the frozen backbone as residual signals. Methodology: The pipeline has three components. (1) Data construction (TV-Edit-23K): Video frame pairs are extracted from UltraVideo, SAM2, and related datasets. SEA-RAFT computes optical flow to identify significant motion regions; Co-Tracker-V3 extracts dense point trajectories, which are sparsified by flow-magnitude sampling. Qwen-3-VL is prompted with rendered colored points on image pairs to generate motion-aligned text instructions. A closed-loop generate-then-verify filter removes MLLM hallucinations, and optical flow boundary thresholding removes global camera motion. Each raw video pair yields bidirectional editing quadruplets {source image, target image, point trajectories, text instruction}, producing 23K samples. (2) TV-Edit model: Two branches share the same MM-DiT backbone. The main branch (frozen) receives image latents, text tokens, and noisy latent tokens. The control branch encodes source and target point coordinate maps via lightweight convolutional layers into spatial embeddings at 1/8 image resolution, concatenates them channel-wise with image and noisy latents respectively, and feeds them through N=5 MM-DiT blocks (hidden dim halved, ~75% parameter reduction vs. backbone). Each controller block outputs three features scaled by timestep-dependent coefficients (time-modulated injection), providing dynamic control across the denoising trajectory. The training objective is Z0-supervision: L_fm = E[||Ẑ0 − Z0||²], which is mathematically equivalent to v-prediction weighted by t², emphasizing high-noise timesteps where global layout is determined. Timestep sampling further concentrates on high noise via Beta(5,2) → Beta(20,2) annealing for the first 40K of 80K iterations. The trainable parameter set is only the point encoder and controller (~506M for N=5). (3) TV-Edit-Bench: 120 hand-curated evaluation pairs from real videos, video-generated content, and model-synthesized image pairs annotated with paired text/point instructions and ground-truth edited targets. Two sub-tasks test (a) motion magnitude control under fixed semantic intent, and (b) semantic disambiguation under identical visual trajectories. Key Results: On TV-Edit-Bench (120 samples, 10 baselines): TV-Edit-Qwen achieves MDd=0.0462 (best overall; GoodDrag next at 0.0648), DStgt_global=0.9134 (tied best with TV-Edit-Kontext), LPIPS=0.1672 (best), PF=0.93 (tied best with LongCat-Image-Edit), CP=1.00 (tied best with NanoBananaPro). TV-Edit-Kontext achieves MDd=0.0508 with PF=0.86, CP=0.99. On DragBench (205 samples): TV-Edit-Qwen achieves MD=17.31 (best; next best DragLora at 23.77) and IF=0.86, matching most baselines without requiring edit-region masks. Ablations show that content-aware controller alone drops MDd from 0.1355 (sparse-point-only baseline) to 0.0505; time-modulated injectors further improve to 0.0462 with PF rising to 0.93. The paper is a preprint (arXiv 2606.16767v1, June 2026); results are self-reported. Novelty Classification: NEW TASK + NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The formalization of text-vision co-instructed editing as a distinct task is genuinely new. The Content-Aware Spatial Controller's time-modulated injection and the Z0-supervision + Beta-sampling training strategy are novel combinations applied to this task. The ControlNet-style plug-in architecture adapting point-based control to MM-DiT backbones applies known structural patterns (ControlNet, early fusion) but in a setting — multi-modal instruction unification over modern flow-matching editors — that has not been done before. TV-Edit-23K and TV-Edit-Bench are new artifacts with no prior counterparts for this specific task. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and well-structured: architecture components (sparse encoder → +controller → +linear injectors → +time modulation), noise sampling distributions (Beta variants), and controller block count (1, 3, 5, 15 blocks) are each ablated with quantitative metrics. No error bars or multiple random seeds are reported, so variance estimates are absent. Code, data, and model weights are stated to be released ("Data, model, and codes will be released") but no repository URL is provided in the preprint. Three methodological concerns: (a) Evaluator bias — MLLM-based scores (PF, CP) use Qwen-3-VL, a model from the same family as the Qwen-Image-Edit baseline; the evaluator may be biased toward Qwen-style edits. (b) Small benchmark — TV-Edit-Bench has only 120 samples created by the authors using a pipeline that partly overlaps with the training data source (UltraVideo, ref [32], is used in both TV-Edit-23K and TV-Edit-Bench); potential train/test overlap is unaddressed. (c) Self-evaluation — the primary benchmark is the authors' own; no third-party benchmark covering the multi-modal instruction task exists yet to serve as a neutral comparison. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: TV-Edit is trained on 16 NVIDIA A800 GPUs, but inference runs on a single GPU using commercially available FLUX.1 Kontext or Qwen-Image-Edit backbones; hardware requirements are not stated quantitatively for deployment. - Efficient: The control branch adds 506M trainable parameters (N=5) on top of the frozen backbone; inference throughput vs. backbone-only is not measured or reported. - Effective: The method demonstrably improves both geometric accuracy (MDd reduction) and semantic faithfulness (PF) over all baselines on the authors' benchmark; DragBench generalization also shows gains, though the gap on image fidelity (IF=0.86) is marginal vs. baselines that require masks. - Scalable: Scaling behavior beyond the test sets is not evaluated; the 120-sample benchmark is too small to draw conclusions about tail cases, and the paper does not test on images beyond standard resolution or on significantly larger point-set sizes. The paper delivers trained model weights (forthcoming) and code; it does not stop at paper form. Strengths: - The task formulation — treating text and sparse visual prompts as complementary rather than competing modalities — is a coherent and well-motivated problem definition that fills a genuine gap between drag-editing and instruction-editing literature. - Time-modulated injection is a non-obvious design choice with clear ablation support: it raises PF from 0.91 to 0.93 and MDd from 0.0501 to 0.0462, showing that dynamic timestep-sensitive control outperforms static residual injection. - The Z0-prediction + Beta(5,2) sampling training strategy is grounded in the mathematical equivalence to t²-weighted velocity loss, providing a principled justification for spatial-layout prioritization rather than an empirical heuristic. - Generalization across two different backbone architectures (FLUX.1 Kontext and Qwen-Image-Edit) demonstrates that the plug-and-play control branch is not architecture-specific. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors state two limitations: (1) inference speed is bottlenecked by large foundation model backbones, preventing real-time interactive use; (2) the method handles 2D spatial operations well but struggles with complex 3D manipulations such as out-of-plane rotations. These are noted briefly in the conclusion and are accurate scope boundaries. Analyst-Identified Gaps: Several concerns the authors do not address: (1) Train/test contamination: TV-Edit-Bench samples from UltraVideo [32], the same source used for TV-Edit-23K training data — the paper does not specify that test samples are partitioned from training samples, making overlap plausible; (2) Evaluator circularity: Qwen-3-VL (the automatic MLLM evaluator) rates Qwen-Image-Edit-based TV-Edit; this same evaluator was used during data construction (text annotation), which may have biased the annotated semantics toward patterns that Qwen evaluates favorably; (3) No inference latency numbers: the paper claims practical deployability without reporting inference time per image for either backbone variant; (4) Missing mask-free drag baseline comparison: LightningDrag is mask-free and training-based, but the improvement over it on TV-Edit-Bench in geometric accuracy (MDd 0.0689 → 0.0462) is not statistically contextualized; (5) No user study: given that the core claim involves perceptual quality and semantic faithfulness, absence of a human evaluation is a gap, especially since MLLM evaluators are known to be unreliable for fine-grained spatial accuracy. Bottom Line: TV-Edit makes a credible contribution at the intersection of drag-based and instruction-based image editing by delivering a practically pluggable controller with strong quantitative results, but the 120-sample self-constructed benchmark, Qwen-family evaluator, and unaddressed train/test overlap make the numbers less authoritative than they appear — practitioners building on this architecture should treat the gains as directionally valid and await third-party benchmark confirmation.
Recommended 2606.16767 PDF
#77 — Innovative (64)

HexiScale: Facilitating Large Language Model Training over Heterogeneous Hardware

R Yan, Y Jiang, X Nie, F Fu, B Cui, B Yuan · openreview.net · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HEXISCALE, a system for training LLMs on heterogeneous GPU clusters that supports fully asymmetric data-, pipeline-, and tensor model parallelism, paired with a hierarchical graph-partitioning scheduler that finds near-optimal allocation plans in minutes. - Why it's important: Organizations holding mixed GPU fleets (different generations, FLOPS, memory, bandwidth) cannot effectively use existing training systems like Megatron, which require fully symmetric configurations and silently under-utilize faster GPUs to accommodate slower ones. - How it does it: A two-phase iterative scheduler partitions the GPU graph by bandwidth proximity (METIS-style multi-level coarsening + Kernighan-Lin refinement), then searches per-pipeline TP/PP strategies independently via cost-model simulation; asymmetric gradient synchronization is achieved by chunking larger gradients to match the smallest across asymmetric tensor-parallel groups. - The good: On the same heterogeneous hardware, HEXISCALE delivers 1.5×–2.4× higher throughput than the best prior heterogeneous systems (AMP, Espresso, Metis), while achieving an average of 0.83× the throughput of a matched homogeneous Ethernet baseline — near-parity with heterogeneous, cheaper hardware. - The missing: No code is released, the comparison with Cephalo (the closest design competitor) uses the authors' own simulation of Cephalo rather than the closed-source system itself, and the largest real-hardware experiment covers only ~40 GPUs; the 240-GPU and 1024-GPU figures are simulation-only. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems researchers and practitioners who operate heterogeneous GPU clusters — the gains over prior work are large and the methodology is well-justified, but immediate adoption is blocked by the absence of open-source code.
Why this rank: Systems profile (Impact-weighted 30%). Impact (60) is the heaviest term and is capped by the absence of any open-source release despite large, real-hardware gains (1.5–2.4× over AMP/Espresso/Metis, 0.83× of homogeneous parity). Rigor (70) is strong for the venue — peer-reviewed at MLSys 2026, cost model validated to <2.9%, MILP within 0–6%, targeted ablations — but no seeds, training correctness never demonstrated (throughput only), and the Cephalo comparison uses an author-built simulation. Resilience (62): the 240/1024-GPU results are simulation-only and real hardware tops out at ~40 GPUs. The asymmetric chunked gradient-sync mechanism is the non-obvious core (Non-Obviousness 60, Novelty 62). Composite reflects a solid, peer-reviewed systems contribution whose practical impact is constrained by closed code.
systems Novelty 62 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 60 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM training is typically run on homogeneous high-performance GPU fleets, but in practice cloud providers and research institutions hold heterogeneous resources: older GPU generations remain provisioned alongside newer ones (e.g., K80s still available on AWS p2 instances; Nvidia releases new GPU architectures roughly every two years). Existing distributed training frameworks (Megatron-LM, Galvatron, FSDP) require fully symmetric parallel configurations — all tensor model parallel groups must have the same degree, all pipeline stages must carry the same compute load, all GPUs are assumed equivalent. In heterogeneous settings this forces strong GPUs to operate at the throughput of weak GPUs, wastes high-bandwidth intra-machine links by restricting tensor parallelism to the lowest-common-denominator, and results in memory imbalance that triggers OOM on smaller-memory devices. Prior heterogeneous-aware systems (AMP, Espresso, Metis) partially address layer partitioning or data parallelism but leave tensor model parallelism symmetric or employ search algorithms (DFS, DP) that scale poorly and do not explore the full asymmetric TP+PP+DP joint space. Key Idea The core technical insight is that each data-parallel replica (pipeline) can independently adopt its own tensor model parallelism degree, number of transformer layers, and micro-batch size, as long as gradient synchronization across pipelines correctly handles the resulting parameter size mismatch. This is enabled by chunking larger gradients into pieces matching the smallest gradient across all pipelines and performing AllReduce on each chunk subset independently. The scheduling insight is that GPU bandwidth topology is a graph-partitioning problem: group GPUs into pipelines by maximizing intra-group bandwidth (high-bandwidth links serve tensor/pipeline parallelism), then search TP and stage-order strategies within each pipeline independently — a decomposition that reduces an NP-hard joint problem to tractable sub-problems. Methodology HEXISCALE formulates the scheduling problem as minimizing COMM-COST(σ) + COMP-COST(σ) subject to per-device memory constraints MEM-CUMSUM(d) ≤ m_d. Finding the exact optimum is NP-hard; HEXISCALE uses a two-phase heuristic iterated over multiple rounds. Phase 1 constructs a global graph G = (D, E) where GPUs are vertices weighted by peak FLOPS and edges are weighted by pairwise bandwidth; it coarsens G via heavy edge matching (Karypis & Kumar 1998a), partitions the coarsened graph into D_dp parts by minimizing the CUT objective (sum of inter-group bandwidths) with recursive bisection (Karypis & Kumar 1998b), projects back to the original graph, and refines with the Kernighan-Lin algorithm. Phase 2 takes each pipeline's GPU set, further sub-partitions it into intra-group clusters by the same multi-level method, searches each sub-group's TP/PP strategy via a cost model, then orders pipeline stages with a top-τ greedy algorithm over inter-group bandwidth. Iteration enumerates D_dp values, toggles between maximizing and minimizing inter-group bandwidth (trading pipeline vs. data parallel communication costs), and varies the sub-partition count k_i; batch and layer assignments are handled greedily proportional to estimated compute throughput. The cost model covers tensor-parallel communication (latency + BW terms per AllReduce), compute cost (24B_mb·S·H² per layer), data-parallel overhead under DDP/ZeRO-2/ZeRO-3 with overlap constants calibrated by profiling, and pipeline bubble cost under 1F1B. Simulation deviates from real execution by less than 2.9% across all tested configurations. Critical design decisions: (1) chunked gradient synchronization is well-justified and necessary for correctness; (2) the choice of multi-level graph partitioning over ILP or DP is justified by scalability — MILP requires hours at 240 GPUs, the graph algorithm finishes in minutes; (3) the greedy top-τ stage ordering is a pragmatic trade-off that handles bandwidth heterogeneity across inter-machine links without exhaustive enumeration. Key Results Real-hardware experiments train LLaMA-2 (7B, 13B) and LLaMA (30B) on GPU clusters rented from Ucloud, with inter-machine bandwidth of 5.6 Gbps Ethernet. Heterogeneous settings mix 3080Ti (12 GB), 3090 (24 GB), 4090 (24 GB), and A800 (80 GB) GPUs. Homogeneous baselines use two or four 8×A800 PCIe-80G machines with both Ethernet and RDMA. Key results: (i) vs. Megatron/Galvatron/FSDP on the same Ethernet-connected homogeneous hardware, HEXISCALE achieves up to 1.01× and on average 0.83× throughput using heterogeneous hardware with similar theoretical FLOPS — near-parity. (ii) vs. Galvatron on the same heterogeneous clusters, HEXISCALE achieves up to 2.5× and on average 2.1× higher throughput. (iii) vs. AMP/Espresso on both low-bandwidth (5.6 Gbps) and high-bandwidth (InfiniBand, 16 H800+16/24/32 H20) settings for LLaMA-30B, HEXISCALE achieves 2.29×/2.35× (low BW) and 1.73×–1.79× (high BW) higher throughput. (iv) vs. Metis (scheduling algorithm run open-source, deployed on HEXISCALE runtime): 1.5×–1.6× improvement, with Metis's long pipeline configurations incurring excessive bubble time. (v) vs. Cephalo (authors' simulation): 2.57×–3.0× improvement, attributed to Cephalo's reliance on data parallelism degrading under low inter-machine bandwidth. Ablation: removing asymmetric parallel support degrades throughput by up to 23% (LLaMA-30B); removing gradient accumulation degrades by up to 15%. Greedy layer assignment contributes 11.46% gain; greedy batch assignment contributes 9.55%. Algorithm scales to 1024 GPUs in under 7 minutes (50 iterations on 12-core CPU) and produces throughput estimates scaling from 1.6 to 6.9 PFLOPS at 128–1024 GPUs. MILP comparison (Appendix E) shows HEXISCALE within 0–6% of optimal throughput across all tested configurations. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The asymmetric parallelism runtime (supporting heterogeneous TP degrees across pipeline replicas with correct gradient synchronization via chunking) and the hierarchical graph-partitioning scheduler are jointly novel. The individual algorithmic building blocks — METIS-style coarsening, recursive bisection, Kernighan-Lin refinement, greedy search — are classical HPC graph-partitioning techniques applied to a new domain; the novelty lies in their composition and in the asymmetric gradient synchronization mechanism, which has no direct counterpart in prior heterogeneous training systems. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and targeted: asymmetric parallelism and gradient accumulation are each removed independently, and greedy batch/layer assignment is compared against uniform distribution. The cost model is rigorously validated (Table 2: <2.9% deviation across 11 real-hardware configurations; Table 3: memory estimates within ~3% of observed; Table 4: communication latency estimates within ~1 ms). Algorithm near-optimality is validated against MILP (Table 6) with HEXISCALE matching MILP within 0–6% while running orders of magnitude faster. The scheduling algorithm is compared against random graph partitioning over 20 runs. No error bars or multiple seeds are reported for the end-to-end throughput experiments. No code, trained weights, or data are publicly released (no URL or repository cited in the paper). The Cephalo comparison is a known methodological concern: Cephalo is closed-source, so the authors simulate its design themselves — the comparison is plausible but cannot be independently reproduced. The paper is peer-reviewed (MLSys 2026). Deployability (4-property check) SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs on standard NVIDIA GPU hardware available from commercial cloud providers (3080Ti through H800); requires PyTorch, NCCL, FlashAttention-2/3, and custom FSDP hooks — all accessible. - Efficient: The scheduling overhead is sub-7-minutes for 1024 GPUs versus hours for Metis/MILP; the asymmetric design reduces pipeline bubbles and over-communication vs. symmetric baselines, as confirmed by latency breakdowns in Figure 7. - Effective: The system demonstrably solves the stated problem — near-homogeneous throughput on heterogeneous hardware — with 1.5×–2.4× gains over prior heterogeneous systems on real clusters, not just proxies. - Scalable: Real experiments reach ~40 GPUs across 3 machines (heterogeneous settings); the 240-GPU and 1024-GPU cases are simulation-only and have not been validated on physical hardware; only NVIDIA GPUs with NCCL are supported, which limits generalization to other hardware ecosystems. The system delivers a working training framework but is not open-sourced, limiting deployment to those who can re-implement from the paper description. Strengths - The asymmetric gradient synchronization scheme (chunking larger gradients to match the smallest tensor-parallel shard size across pipelines) is a technically necessary and non-obvious contribution that makes the entire asymmetric design correct — prior systems avoided this complication by enforcing symmetry. - Leveraging multi-level graph partitioning for GPU topology-aware pipeline grouping is well-motivated and principled: HEM coarsening naturally clusters GPUs with high intra-machine bandwidth, and KL refinement corrects balance violations after projection, yielding near-MILP results at polynomial cost. - The evaluation breadth is strong for an MLSys paper: five heterogeneous baselines (AMP, Espresso, Metis, Galvatron, Cephalo), three model scales, both low and high inter-machine bandwidth, and a MILP upper-bound comparison. - The cost model's <2.9% accuracy across diverse GPU combinations and network conditions is a credible engineering contribution that grounds the simulation-based design decisions in measured reality. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note that only NVIDIA GPUs are supported (NCCL dependency). They acknowledge that homogeneous RDMA baselines significantly outperform HEXISCALE on Ethernet-connected heterogeneous clusters and attribute this gap to network speed, not the system design, validating that RDMA would close the gap through simulation. They explicitly state that Cephalo is closed-source and that their comparison uses an author-implemented simulation. These are direct acknowledgments rather than minimizations, which is creditable. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No open-source release is mentioned anywhere in the paper; without code, the community cannot reproduce results, integrate HEXISCALE with existing training stacks, or build on the system — a significant gap for a systems paper. - The largest real-hardware experiment involves approximately 40 GPUs; the 240-GPU cluster results are simulated. The cost model does not capture all real-system effects at scale (e.g., NCCL collective tree construction at hundreds of nodes, network congestion, thermal throttling). - Training correctness is never demonstrated: the paper reports throughput (PFLOPS) exclusively. Whether asymmetric parallelism with chunked gradient synchronization produces numerically identical model weights to homogeneous training (or introduces systematic bias from unequal gradient averaging) is not verified. - All heterogeneous settings use NVIDIA hardware with PCIe or NVLink; AMD ROCm, Intel Gaudi, or cross-vendor training environments are entirely out of scope and cannot be inferred from the results. - The cost model assumes 1F1B pipeline scheduling; newer schedules (zero-bubble parallelism, interleaved 1F1B) are not modeled, limiting applicability to practitioners using those schedules. - The Cephalo comparison, while the authors are transparent about it, uses an author-implemented simulation of Cephalo's design — the 2.57×–3.0× advantage over Cephalo cannot be treated with the same confidence as comparisons run on real hardware. Bottom Line A practitioner running LLM training on mixed GPU fleets will find HEXISCALE directly relevant — the gains over Metis and AMP/Espresso are large and backed by real-hardware experiments — but the absence of open-source code and the limitation to NVIDIA-only environments significantly constrain immediate utility.
Citations
#78 — Innovative (64)

Interpreting Genomic Language Models using Sparse Autoencoders

AA Nair, J Joo, J Lee, L Takemaru, Y Huang… · openreview.net · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a systematic framework for applying sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to genomic language models (gLMs), presenting a 55-concept human genomic annotation benchmark and a Tanimoto/Node2Vec graph algorithm for organizing learned features into a visualizable atlas. - Why it's important: Genomic language models like Evo2 achieve strong performance on mutation effect prediction and regulatory element identification, but their opacity limits adoption in biomedical research where interpretable, validatable predictions are required for disease applications. - How it does it: SAEs are trained post-hoc on Evo2 embeddings extracted from specific layers, then their per-nucleotide activation signals are evaluated against curated annotation sets using three complementary metrics (domain-recall F1, sparse probing AUPRC, sparse event enrichment); feature relationships are then organized via Tanimoto similarity on activation patterns fed into Node2Vec graph embeddings. - The good: SAE features from intermediate layers outperform raw Evo2 embeddings on 42/55 (76%) genomic concepts, with 26 concepts reaching modified F1 > 0.7, and the mixed-species SAE surprisingly outperforms the human-only SAE on exon detection despite fewer human training sequences. - The missing: The paper never evaluates whether improved SAE interpretability translates to better performance on any downstream biomedical task, leaving the practical payoff of this framework undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on mechanistic interpretability of biological sequence models or seeking standardized benchmarks for gLM evaluation; practitioners who need actionable tools for genomic analysis should temper expectations given the lack of downstream task validation.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. The reusable 55-concept benchmark and the well-controlled evolutionary-proximity finding (mixed-species SAE beating human-only) give meaningful Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (65), with 5-seed averaging and a strict chr22 holdout supporting Rigor (65). The composite is pulled down by Completeness (60) and Impact (55): the paper never demonstrates that improved SAE interpretability helps any downstream biomedical task, evaluates on a single atypical chromosome, confounds the NT-v3 comparison with a 7B-vs-8M scale mismatch, and releases only partial code with no SAE weights — leaving practical payoff hypothetical.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 60 Resilience 60 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Genomic language models trained on multi-species DNA corpora achieve competitive performance on tasks like transcription factor binding site prediction and variant effect scoring, yet their internal representations remain opaque. Interpreting what biological concepts these models encode is non-trivial because genomic sequences are orders of magnitude longer than natural language sentences and ground-truth functional annotations cover only a fraction of the genome, often with cell-type or developmental-stage specificity. Clinicians and biologists cannot readily trust or validate gLM predictions without understanding the features driving them, which blocks adoption in disease-relevant workflows. The superposition problem — where individual neurons respond to multiple unrelated inputs — complicates direct inspection of model internals. Key Idea: The core insight is that sparse autoencoders, which have already been shown to disentangle polysemantic neurons in vision and NLP models (and, in concurrent work, protein language models), can be applied post-hoc to gLM embeddings to recover monosemantic features that align with known biological annotations. The non-obvious extension is that the choice of SAE training data — particularly the evolutionary proximity of training genomes to the target (human) genome and the nucleotide context length used to generate embeddings — substantially determines which biological features the SAE recovers, requiring genome-domain-specific adaptation beyond what NLP SAE practice prescribes. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. First, embeddings are extracted from six layers of Evo2 (7B parameters, Hyena convolutional architecture, 1M-token context) by passing 10kb genomic sequences and collecting per-nucleotide hidden states. Second, a BatchTopK SAE is trained on these embeddings: encoder dimension 4096, dictionary size 16384 (4× expansion), top-k sparsity with k=64, AdamW optimizer, 5 epochs on 2 H100 GPUs. The SAE loss minimizes reconstruction error while enforcing that at most k features activate per token. Third, test sequences from chromosome 22 (held out) are passed through the trained SAE to generate feature activation signals, which are compared against 55 curated genomic annotation categories using three metrics: (i) a modified F1 combining nucleotide-level precision with domain-level recall, binarized at threshold τ=0; (ii) univariate sparse probing AUPRC using logistic regression on single SAE features ranked by mean activation difference; and (iii) a Gaussian-windowed enrichment score for sparse point events such as transcription start sites. For the feature atlas, a 500kb representative sequence activates all live features; pairwise Tanimoto coefficients on downsampled activation signals form a weighted graph; node2vec produces 8-dimensional embeddings subsequently reduced to 2D via UMAP. Critical design decisions: single-feature probing (not multi-feature) is justified by the monosemanticity ideal but is pessimistic about feature splitting/absorption phenomena the SAE may exhibit; the Tanimoto coefficient is motivated by known good behavior on sparse non-negative vectors; chromosome 22 isolation prevents annotation leakage but means all evaluation is on a single atypical chromosome. Key Results: On Evo2, SAE features outperform raw embeddings in sparse probing AUPRC for 42/55 concepts (76%), with median gain +0.084 for SAE-winning concepts vs. median loss of only −0.031 where embeddings win. Standout per-concept AUPRCs: repeats Retroposon 0.925 (SAE) vs. 0.796 (embeddings), rRNA 0.896 vs. 0.551, repeats RNA 0.842 vs. 0.478, repeats SINE 0.770 vs. 0.542, mane introns 0.776 vs. 0.665. Embeddings retain leads on CpG islands (0.700 vs. 0.444), phastCons conserved elements (0.729 vs. 0.628), and mane CDS (0.761 vs. 0.591). Intermediate layers (12, 19, 26) consistently yield the best SAE performance; early (layer 1) and final (layer 30) layers favor embeddings. On Nucleotide Transformer v3 (8M parameters), SAEs win on 30/55 (55%) concepts with similar layer-depth dependence. The evolutionary proximity experiment shows performance on exon detection increasing monotonically from E. coli to human training data (fold-change gains of 2–5×), with the mixed-species SAE achieving slightly higher AUPRC than human-only despite containing fewer human sequences. Context length from 50bp to 1000bp raises mean top F1 from ~0.4 to ~0.8. Hyperparameter grid search (dictionary expansion ×2–16, sparsity k=8–64) shows no universal optimum: promoters are sensitive to hyperparameter choice while SINE repeats are robust. The Tanimoto/Node2Vec atlas achieves higher kNN precision than SAE decoder weights at all resolution levels (k=10,25,50): introns 0.663 vs. 0.469, SINE repeats 0.735 vs. 0.595, Retroposon 0.340 vs. 0.211. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The benchmark (55 human genomic concepts spanning gene structure, regulatory elements, repeat classes, and evolutionary conservation with three complementary metrics) is a genuine contribution that fills an infrastructure gap. The empirical finding that SAE interpretability is sensitive to training genome evolutionary distance and context length is non-obvious and domain-specific. The Tanimoto/Node2Vec atlas method is a targeted adaptation of established graph-embedding tools to the feature organization problem. The SAE-for-gLMs concept is a direct transfer from InterPLM (Simon & Zou, 2025, Nature Methods) for protein LMs; the genomic domain introduces additional challenges (sequence length, annotation sparsity) but the conceptual framework is not original. Rigor & Reproducibility: Results are averaged over 5 random seeds for all main experiments except hyperparameter tuning, which is a sound practice. No error bars are shown for the evolutionary proximity or context length experiments. Chromosome 22 is used as a strict held-out set, preventing annotation leakage. Code is deposited in an anonymous repository flagged as incomplete: "The repository will be updated with complete scripts and datasets in an accepted version." No model weights for the trained SAEs are released. The evaluation strategy of reporting the single best feature per annotation is optimistic — in a real application, the user would not know which feature to select without the annotation as a guide. The NT v3 comparison uses an 8M-parameter model against Evo2's 7B-parameter model, making cross-model conclusions about SAE suitability confounded by model scale and architecture. This is a preprint under double-blind review at ICML 2026; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Extracting Evo2 7B embeddings requires NVIDIA H100-class hardware; SAE training on those embeddings required 2 H100 GPUs, placing it out of reach for typical academic labs without HPC access. - Efficient: SAE training is a one-time post-hoc cost; inference (feature activation extraction for a new sequence) adds one forward pass through the SAE dictionary on top of the gLM embedding cost, which is negligible. The atlas algorithm scales as O(F_live × L) for smoothing and O(F_live log F_live) for ANN, tractable for the dictionary sizes tested. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrates that SAE features align with known annotations, but whether this translates to better variant prioritization, regulatory element discovery, or any downstream clinical task is not evaluated — the paper stops at feature–annotation correspondence. - Scalable: The method is tested only on a single chromosome (chr22) evaluation set and SAEs with 16K features; behavior at genome-wide scale, larger dictionaries, or on Evo2's full 1M-token context window is not tested. The authors note computational cost scaling as a limitation. The work delivers code (partially, for reproducibility) and a benchmark dataset; no trained model weights or production-ready tool is released. Strengths: - The 55-concept benchmark with three complementary metrics is a reusable infrastructure contribution that the field has lacked for gLM evaluation; the choice of chromosome 22 as a holdout is principled and clean. - The evolutionary proximity experiment is well-controlled (equal total base pairs, same SAE architecture) and yields an actionable insight: mixed-species training outperforms human-only training, a counter-intuitive finding with practical implications for SAE training protocols. - Reporting results averaged over 5 random seeds and including per-concept, per-layer AUPRC matrices (Figure 9, Appendix E) provides enough raw data for readers to form their own conclusions beyond the headline numbers. - The Tanimoto/Node2Vec atlas, while methodologically incremental, is meaningfully better than the decoder-weight baseline (kNN precision gains of 41–61% at k=10), and the choice of Tanimoto over cosine similarity for sparse non-negative data is technically motivated. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly identify two limitations: (1) genomic annotations used for evaluation are incomplete and context-dependent — cell-type, developmental stage, and environmental variation could cause a feature to underperform against a bulk annotation for reasons unrelated to the model; (2) computational cost of SAE training and feature atlas construction scales poorly with very large dictionary dimensions. These are honest and direct. The authors do not acknowledge the single-chromosome evaluation scope, the incompleteness of the released code, or the scale mismatch between Evo2 7B and NT v3 8M. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No downstream task evaluation: the entire paper measures feature–annotation correlation but never asks whether better interpretable features lead to better performance on mutation effect prediction, disease variant prioritization, or any applied task. This is the most significant missing piece — without it, the utility of SAE interpretability for gLMs remains hypothetical. - Single-chromosome evaluation: chromosome 22 is one of the smallest autosomes with known compositional biases (high gene density, immunoglobulin loci). Evaluation on even two or three chromosomes would substantially strengthen generalization claims. - The NT v3 comparison uses an 8M-parameter model vs. Evo2's 7B-parameter model. The observed 55% vs. 76% SAE win rate could reflect model scale rather than architecture or training data differences. A fair comparison would require matched model scales. - The best-feature selection strategy requires the annotation as a supervisory signal, making the evaluation circular in the sense that a practitioner with unknown annotations cannot use this to discover new features without a target concept to rank against. - No baseline for untrained or randomly initialized gLM SAEs to establish how much of the feature–annotation correspondence is due to the gLM pre-training versus the SAE compression itself. - Hyperparameter sensitivity (Section 7.3) is reported but no principled guidance for hyperparameter selection in the absence of a labeled validation set is provided. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on gLM interpretability or genomic foundation model analysis will find the annotation benchmark, evolutionary-proximity experiments, and Tanimoto/Node2Vec atlas worth reading, but should note that the paper establishes correlation between SAE features and known annotations without demonstrating that this interpretability enables any practical downstream improvement.
Search Results
#79 — Innovative (64)

From Content to Knowledge: Lightning Fast Long-Video Understanding with Neural Knowledge Representations

Y Guan, X Li, Z Guo, X Zhang, X Peng, C Yuan, Y Lu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Neural Knowledge Representation (NKR), a paradigm that compresses a long video's semantic content offline into a per-video LoRA adapter, enabling query-time inference with zero video-token overhead by merging the adapter into a frozen VLM. - Why it's important: Both dominant long-video approaches fail at interactive use: tokenization VLMs hit context limits forcing aggressive frame subsampling, while agentic RAG systems require 2–4 minutes per query — neither supports low-latency multi-turn interaction over hour-length video. - How it does it: Agentic Knowledge Distillation (AKD) uses GPT-4.1 to generate tri-level multi-scale video descriptions and a ReAct agent (o3) to synthesize ~1000 complex QA pairs per video offline, then trains a LoRA adapter (rank 256 on Qwen3-14B) with a mixed NTP+SFT loss; at inference only the merged adapter is needed. - The good: Inference runs at 0.33s/query (100–270× faster than competing methods on the same hardware) with zero memory overhead, and accuracy drops only 0.3% from videos under vs. over one hour — versus 6.7% for GPT-4.1 and 2.9% for VideoRAG — demonstrating genuine duration invariance. - The missing: The encoding cost (2–3 hours of GPT-4.1/o3 API calls plus 2 hours of 4×A100 training per 1-hour video) is never amortized per query, and AdaReTaKe-7B outperforms NKR on overall LVBench accuracy (51.2% vs. 48.8%) while also being faster at inference — a comparison the paper does not explain. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers on efficient long-video inference and the offline/online compute trade-off; the duration-invariance result is the strongest contribution and will likely spawn follow-on work.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) anchors the score — applying implicit-neural-representation framing to semantic video knowledge (per-video LoRA) is a genuinely new paradigm with no direct precedent. Rigor (62) is helped by clean data-component and backbone ablations, the in-context vs NKR control isolating representation format, and ICML 2026 peer review, but no error bars and unreleased code temper it. The duration-invariance result (0.3% drop) is the well-evidenced highlight (Non-Obviousness 68). Resilience (58) is the soft spot: AdaReTaKe-7B beats NKR on aggregate LVBench at a smaller size and faster effective inference, and the paper does not explain this. Impact (58) is gated by heavy encoding cost and the textual bottleneck.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 68 Resilience 58 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Hour-scale video understanding faces a fundamental tension between accuracy and latency. Tokenization-based VLMs (GPT-4o capped at 60 frames, Qwen2.5-VL at 768 frames) must aggressively subsample long videos, discarding content critical for temporal reasoning and entity tracking. Agentic RAG systems (VideoRAG, Deep Video Discovery) pre-build external databases and run multi-round tool-calling loops, achieving better understanding but at 90–180 seconds per query — prohibitive for interactive use. Both paradigms treat the video as an external data source that must be re-processed at runtime, so inference cost scales with video duration. The gap is that no existing method achieves both persistent video knowledge and constant-time inference for repeated queries. Key Idea: Represent each video as an implicit neural field — a small LoRA adapter — trained to internalize the video's semantic content, analogous to how NeRF compresses a 3D scene into network weights. Once trained offline, the adapter is merged into a frozen VLM backbone; queries are processed as plain text with no video tokens, decoupling inference cost from video duration entirely. Methodology: The pipeline has two phases. In the offline AKD phase, a video is segmented into multi-scale clips (log-linear from 5s to full length). Stage 1 uses GPT-4.1 to generate tri-level descriptions (low: visual details, mid: actions/events, high: narrative summary) plus entity registries per clip, with each text rephrased 5× by GPT-4.1-mini, yielding a description dataset Ddesc (~30–40k entries for a 1-hour video). Stage 2 uses o4-mini for 10 clip-level QA pairs per clip, and a ReAct agent (o3) with four tools (Global Browse, Clip Search, Temporal Search, Frame Inspect) to generate ~1000 complex video-level QA pairs across six LVBench categories (Dqa, ~15–20k pairs). The NKR is then a LoRA adapter (rank 256, alpha 512) on all attention layers of Qwen3-14B, trained with a combined loss LAKD = LNTP + LSFT at a 2:8 ratio over 5 epochs on 4×A100-40G GPUs (~2 hours). Three critical design decisions: (1) text-only Ddesc because VLMs cannot emit visual tokens as supervision — acknowledged as a bottleneck; (2) the mixed NTP+SFT loss, where NTP drives content memorization and SFT drives query-aware reasoning — ablation shows both are necessary; (3) LoRA implementation preserves the base VLM, enabling per-video adapter swapping. At inference: load adapter → merge into frozen VLM → process text query only. Key Results: On LVBench (103 videos, 117 hours, 1549 multiple-choice questions), NKR (Qwen3-14B, r=256) achieves 48.8% overall accuracy at 0.33s/query with 0GB runtime memory overhead. Competing tokenization approaches: VideoLLaMA3-7B (45.3%, 64.5s, 1.2GB), Qwen2.5-VL-7B (43.8%, 30.0s, 2.5GB), Qwen2.5-VL-32B (47.6%, 45.6s, 11.3GB), AdaReTaKe-7B (51.2%, 45.0s, 0.9GB). Agentic approaches: VideoRAG (49.2%, 90.0s, 0.5GB external DB), Deep Video Discovery-o3 (74.2%, 180.0s). Duration robustness: NKR drops 0.3% (<1hr 48.9% vs. >1hr 48.6%); GPT-4.1 drops 6.7%, VideoRAG drops 2.9%. On LongVideoBench-Long (564 questions, 188 videos, 900–3600s), NKR achieves 52.9% vs. AdaReTaKe-7B 55.5% and GPT-4o 60.9%. For image NKR on a 38-image MME-RealWorld subset (526 QA pairs), NKR reaches 35.4% vs. 32.1% for the Qwen2.5-VL-7B base and 19.6% for a text-in-context baseline using the same source descriptions — a controlled result demonstrating the representation format advantage. Ablation on data components (Table 5): dense description alone gives 14.3%; description + clip QA gives 45.0%; description + video-level QA gives 38.8%; all three gives 48.8%. Backbone capacity matters more than LoRA rank: 8B→14B yields +9.3%, r64→r256 yields +4.6%. Novelty Classification: NEW PARADIGM, NEW METHOD. Applying implicit neural representation to semantic video knowledge — rather than to signal reconstruction (as in NeRF/INR for video pixels) — is a genuinely new framing. The AKD pipeline assembles known components (LoRA fine-tuning, agentic QA generation with ReAct, knowledge distillation) in a novel configuration targeting per-content knowledge compression. The specific goal of decoupling inference cost from video duration via per-video adapter training has no direct precedent in the literature. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations on data components (Table 5) and backbone/rank (Table 4) are present and cleanly isolate key variables. The KNN retrieval analysis (Fig. S4) addresses the memorization hypothesis by showing test questions are semantically related but not matched by training pairs. The in-context baseline (Table S11) directly isolates representation format from information content. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported. Code is not released (not mentioned). Reproducibility depends on access to GPT-4.1, o3, and o4-mini APIs, making replication non-trivial. The LongVideoBench evaluation covers only the Long subset (564/6678 questions), not the full benchmark. The paper is published at ICML 2026 (peer-reviewed). Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: Inference runs on a single A100-80G GPU at 0.33s/query, commercially accessible; however, encoding requires 4×A100-40G GPUs for ~4–5 hours of total processing (data generation + training) per 1-hour video, plus substantial GPT-4.1 and o3 API spend. - Efficient: At inference, overhead is essentially zero beyond the base VLM (LoRA merged in). Encoding cost is front-loaded and amortized across queries — economical only when the same video receives many queries; for single-query or low-query scenarios, tokenization approaches are far cheaper. - Effective: NKR achieves 48.8% on LVBench — competitive with 7B–32B tokenization models and VideoRAG, but 25.4 percentage points below Deep Video Discovery (74.2%). The textual-bottleneck limitation means fine-grained visual content (scoreboards, dense text on screen, non-verbal cues) may be systematically underrepresented. - Scalable: Inference cost is duration-invariant by design, confirmed by the 0.3% performance drop across video lengths; encoding cost scales with video duration (2h encoding per 1h video), but this is offline. Stress-scale testing (multi-hour videos, very large video libraries) is not reported. No software artifact released. Strengths: - Duration-invariant inference is the headline result: a 0.3% accuracy drop from <1hr to >1hr videos versus 3–7% for all competing methods is a well-supported and non-trivial finding that directly validates the core design premise. - The inference efficiency advantage (100–270× faster than tokenization and RAG methods, zero memory overhead) is independently verifiable from the reported hardware setup and not merely claimed. - The in-context vs. NKR ablation (Table S11) provides direct evidence that the LoRA representation learns a qualitatively different, more generalizable knowledge structure than in-context retrieval — +15 to +37 points on reasoning tasks using identical source information. - The fully automated AKD pipeline eliminates human annotation, making the approach applicable to arbitrary video content without domain-specific labeling effort. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly identify: (1) per-video upfront optimization, making cost non-amortizable across videos; (2) the textual bottleneck in Ddesc — VLMs cannot output visual tokens as supervision, so fine-grained visual details resisting textualization may be lost; (3) adding visual tokens as input during training increased cost but yielded no improvement. They frame the gap with Deep Video Discovery as a "different design philosophy" targeting interactive versus offline scenarios. The limitations are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - AdaReTaKe-7B outperforms NKR overall (51.2% vs. 48.8%) at comparable speed (45.0s vs. 0.33s effective inference) with lower memory (0.9GB), and is also a 7B vs. 14B model — this is the strongest direct competitor, yet the paper does not analyze why NKR with a larger model underperforms it on aggregate. - The encoding cost (GPT-4.1 + o3 API calls plus GPU training) is not quantified in monetary terms or broken down per query at realistic query volumes; the claim of high "amortized efficiency" is not backed by an explicit amortization analysis. - LongVideoBench evaluation covers only 564 of 6678 questions (the Long subset); full benchmark results are absent, making cross-benchmark comparison incomplete. - The paper does not study variance in NKR quality across video types or topics — videos with dense visual information (sports statistics, technical lectures, medical imaging) may compress poorly through the textual channel, but this is not probed. - The 25-point accuracy gap versus Deep Video Discovery is framed as a scenario difference, but a Pareto frontier analysis showing NKR on the accuracy/latency curve would more honestly characterize where the method sits. Bottom Line: For researchers in multimodal video understanding, the NKR paper delivers a real and well-evidenced contribution — duration-invariant inference and sub-second query latency — but the substantial encoding cost and 25-point accuracy deficit versus the best agentic system limit immediate practical adoption to the specific regime of many repeated queries over pre-indexed videos.
Search Results 2606.11913 PDF
#80 — Innovative (64)

Beyond the Current Observation: Evaluating Multimodal Large Language Models in Controllable Non-Markov Games (38 ▲ )

Shengyuan Ding, Xilin Wei, Xinyu Fang, Haodong Duan, Dahua Lin, Jiaqi Wang, Yuhang Zang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 18 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces RNG-Bench (Reconstructive Non-Markov Games), a benchmark suite of two closed-loop games — Matching Pairs and 3D Maze — designed to isolate multimodal LLMs' ability to reconstruct hidden state from interaction history and act on it, distinct from post-hoc recall. - Why it's important: As MLLMs are deployed in embodied and agentic settings, closed-loop tasks that require acting on observations no longer in view are ubiquitous, yet no existing benchmark isolates this remember-to-act capability cleanly from rule understanding, planning, and perception confounds. - How it does it: Both games run as POMDPs under a unified evaluation harness with three controlled difficulty axes (grid/maze size, visual pattern, observation modality); a Memory Gap metric — ratio of performance deficit closed by injecting the true hidden state — disentangles forgetting from suboptimal decision-making. - The good: The finding that stripping the model's own action-feedback text collapses Matching Pairs Score% by ~75% across both GPT-5.4 and Qwen3.5-397B — even though board images in principle encode the same information — is a crisp, quantified demonstration that MLLMs cannot self-infer belief state from visual diffs alone. - The missing: The benchmark covers only two game environments and five models; the Memory Gap oracle provides full hidden state rather than a realistic partial aid, so it measures an upper bound on recoverable performance rather than a decomposition of causal failure modes. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on MLLM agents, embodied AI, or long-context evaluation — the benchmark cleanly operationalizes a failure mode that prior suites obscure, and the diagnostic axes give actionable handles for identifying bottlenecks.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. This paper resembles the user's positive calibration set — controlled, mechanism-isolating design. Completeness (70) and Rigor (62) lead: the difficulty axes (scale/pattern/modality) hold rules fixed so any drop is attributable, ablations are thorough (10 board sizes, 6 themes, history windows, external-memory and action-feedback interventions), results are averaged over 4–5 seeds, and the Memory-Gap metric is explicitly qualified as a diagnostic rather than a causal decomposition — a notably honest move. Non-Obviousness (68): the action-feedback ablation (75% Score collapse despite board images being in principle sufficient) is a crisp, surprising finding. Novelty (62) is a NEW BENCHMARK + finding. Impact (52) is limited to a benchmark artifact, and no significance tests are reported.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 52
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multimodal foundation models deployed as closed-loop policies — in embodied navigation, multi-turn tool use, or interactive games — must frequently act on information that appeared earlier in an episode but is no longer in the current observation. Existing benchmarks fail to isolate this regime cleanly. Fully observed game benchmarks (chess, Go) require only current-state reasoning. Agent suites that include hidden information (AgentBench, BALROG, GameWorld) entangle hidden-state tracking with exploration, rule discovery, action formatting, and perception, so a failure cannot be attributed to memory. Long-context and episodic memory benchmarks (LongBench, EMemBench) test remember-to-answer — a model reads a trajectory and answers once — where a recall error has no effect on subsequent inputs. The gap is remember-to-act under closed-loop feedback: each recall error reshapes the next observation and can compound through the episode. No existing benchmark controls for this while also scaling difficulty and permitting localized diagnostic attribution. Key Idea The benchmark enforces hidden-state reconstruction as the sole explanatory variable by holding rule complexity constant (rules given in-prompt), enforcing strict action parsing (eliminating output-format confounds), and varying difficulty along independent axes (grid size, visual pattern, observation modality). The Memory Gap metric then separates forgetting from decision quality by comparing each model's score against an oracle condition that injects the true hidden state at every step: MemGap(m) = (1 − S(m)/S*(m)) × 100%. A large gap implicates memory; a small gap implicates planning or perception given correct state. Methodology Both games are formalized as POMDPs (S, O, A, T, Z, R). Matching Pairs places R×C card pairs face-down on a grid; each turn the model flips two cards, matched pairs are removed, mismatched pairs are flipped back. The hidden state is the set of identity-location bindings for previously revealed but currently hidden cards. Optimal play requires exploiting known pairs and avoiding redundant re-flips. 3D Maze has the agent navigate from start to goal receiving only an egocentric first-person rendering; the hidden state is global topology, visited cells, current position and orientation. Mazes are generated with a loop rate of 0.15, making wall-following unreliable. Models are evaluated as raw history-based policies π(a_t | h_t) with no external belief module unless explicitly intervened. Three controlled axes: (1) scale — board/maze size from 4×4 to 12×14 (Matching Pairs) and 5×5 to 15×15 (3D Maze); (2) visual pattern — six card themes (ASCII, Poker, Noise, Textures, Abstract, Similar-Colors) and 2D/3D renderings; (3) observation modality — symbolic text, 2D image patch, 3D first-person view. Critical design decisions: (a) closing the action-feedback channel as an ablation to measure whether models can infer belief state from visual diffs alone (they cannot); (b) a head-to-head duel protocol where two models alternate on the same board, controlling for card-layout variance; (c) the oracle intervention supplies the full hidden state mid-episode. Evaluation uses VLMEvalKit as the harness with identical prompts across models. SFT on Qwen3.5-9B uses 32K trajectories from optimal-policy rollouts and filtered model rollouts, masking loss on observation tokens; training sizes are strictly smaller than evaluation sizes. Key Results On Matching Pairs (10×10 image-noise, 50 pairs), GPT-5.4 matches 62.3% of pairs at 8.01 responses per match; Gemini-3.1-Pro reaches 50.0% at 10.00 responses; Qwen3.5-397B reaches 25.3% at 19.74 responses. Parse failures and invalid actions remain below 5% across all models, ruling out output-format confounds. In the duel protocol (16 games each), Gemini-3.1-Pro wins all 16 matchups (Elo 1803); GPT-5.4 wins 50% (Elo 1492). On 3D Maze (13×13, mean optimal path 60 steps), Gemini-3.1-Pro leads with 50.0% SR and 49.7% GS; GPT-5.4 reaches 20.0% SR/30.5% GS despite leading on Matching Pairs, a rank reversal indicating the tasks probe distinct hidden-state demands. Qwen3.5-397B reaches 0.0% SR on 3D Maze. Scale sweep with Qwen3.5-397B: Matching Pairs Score% drops from 90.6% (4×4) to 0.7% (12×12); 3D Maze GS peaks at 7×7 (66.7%) and falls to 19.7% at 15×15. Modality ablation: both Qwen3.5-397B and Kimi-K2.5 score 100% under text but 38.3%/43.3% under image-noise on Matching Pairs; 3D Maze text-symbolic GS is 70.9%/60.0% versus 23.7%/39.7% for 3D scene. Action-feedback ablation: removing the model's own action history collapses GPT-5.4 Score% from 69.6% to 15.0% on 8×10 (−78.4%) and from 62.3% to 15.3% on 10×10 (−75.4%); Qwen3.5-397B drops 69–75% from a lower base. External memory (memory map for Matching Pairs; minimap for 3D Maze) roughly doubles Matching Pairs Score% (Qwen: 38.3→78.7, MemGap 51.3; Kimi: 43.3→80.3, MemGap 46.1) but yields smaller gains on 3D Maze (Qwen: 23.8→40.2 GS; Kimi: 24.6→35.6 GS), indicating that 3D Maze failure involves planning beyond memory access. SFT on Qwen3.5-9B: the rmix32k recipe (26K optimal + 6K filtered rollouts) lifts Matching Pairs from 0.0 to 29.5 and achieves the only non-zero maze SR (10.0%) on held-out sizes; the model's transfer to EMeMBench improves +5.2 and group-mean across four memory/spatial benchmarks improves +3.4, while general multimodal capability shifts by only +0.5. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark design is genuinely novel in combining controllable POMDP difficulty axes, the remember-to-act closed-loop structure, and a principled oracle-based Memory Gap metric within a single suite. The core empirical finding — that visual recognition rather than context length is the primary bottleneck, quantified by the modality sweep and the action-feedback ablation — is a non-obvious and actionable result. The SFT training recipe is a competent application of known supervised fine-tuning ideas to a novel data source; the transfer results are positive but modest. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough: scale sweep across 10 board sizes and 6 maze sizes, six visual themes, three observation modalities, external-memory intervention, action-feedback removal, history-window restriction (3/5/10 turns vs. full), ask-output prompting, and color-tagged walls. The duel protocol with swapped player orders controls for first-mover bias. The Memory Gap metric is explicitly stated to be a practical diagnostic under a specific oracle interface rather than a causal decomposition — a notably honest qualification. Error bars are not reported; maze results are averaged over five seeds and Matching Pairs over four seeds, but no significance tests are given. Model evaluation uses VLMEvalKit for prompt and metric consistency. Code and data are not explicitly stated to be released in the paper, though the benchmark is described as releasable. The SFT study trains only one base model family (Qwen3.5-9B) for one epoch with a single seed, explicitly described as a feasibility demonstration. This preprint (arXiv:2606.19338v1, June 2026) has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — all claims rest on experiments run by the authors across multiple frontier MLLMs and a fine-tuned 9B model. - Practical: The benchmark itself runs on commercially available hardware; SFT uses 8 GPUs with gradient accumulation and 28K-token context windows, feasible with standard GPU clusters but not laptops. - Efficient: Hardest configurations require ~128K tokens and 350 images per episode, making evaluation expensive for image modalities — a meaningful cost barrier for large-scale sweeps or future model comparisons. - Effective: The Memory Gap metric, scale sweeps, and modality ablations collectively demonstrate that the benchmark cleanly isolates the stated bottleneck (belief-state maintenance) rather than measuring rule comprehension or output formatting. - Scalable: The benchmark's difficulty axes are designed to scale arbitrarily with grid/maze size; however, the largest models evaluated (GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro) are closed-source, and replication depends on API availability. SFT scalability beyond Qwen3.5-9B is not tested. The paper delivers a benchmark artifact and SFT recipe, not a library or deployed tool. Strengths - The remember-to-act vs. remember-to-answer distinction is cleanly operationalized and fills a genuine gap in existing benchmark taxonomy — the closed-loop feedback loop where a recall error reshapes the next observation is not captured by any prior suite reviewed. - The three-axis difficulty control (scale, visual pattern, modality) with rules held fixed is methodologically sound: any performance drop is attributable to the axis rather than a confound, and empirical results validate the axes are genuinely independent sources of variance. - The action-feedback ablation (Score% collapses 75% when action history is removed even though board images are in principle sufficient) is a sharp, surprising empirical finding with concrete implications for MLLM architecture: models do not extract belief state from visual diffs. - The duel protocol that controls for board randomness by having both models play the same layout with swapped turn order is a thoughtful design that yields a complementary robustness ranking and reveals Gemini's advantage at exploiting opponent-revealed information. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) only two game environments are included; broader coverage of game genres, model families, and visual styles is left to future work; (2) the Memory Gap metric is a practical diagnostic under the specific oracle interface rather than a standalone causal decomposition, since hidden-state tracking is observed through the model's perceptual interface; (3) the fine-tuning study is a feasibility demonstration on a single base model family. These limitations are confronted directly and honestly, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The oracle condition injects the complete true hidden state rather than a realistic partial aid, which means the Memory Gap measures an upper bound on what better memory would yield, not the performance attributable to imperfect but realistic memory systems. The paper acknowledges this is not a causal decomposition, but the boundary condition is not empirically probed (e.g., injecting only k most recent hidden states). - All five evaluated frontier models are from a narrow set of commercial API providers (OpenAI, Google, ByteDance, Moonshot, Alibaba); no open-weight models at frontier scale (e.g., Llama-4, Mistral) are included, limiting the ability to draw architectural conclusions. - The SFT study trains on only one random seed and evaluates a single model family (Qwen3.5-9B), making it impossible to distinguish whether the +3.4 transfer improvement reflects the training regime, the base model architecture, or variance. - The 3D Maze modal ranking (Gemini leads, Qwen3.5-397B at 0%) diverges substantially from Matching Pairs rankings, but the paper attributes this qualitatively to "distinct hidden-state demands" without quantifying how much of the rank reversal stems from visual perception differences versus spatial belief maintenance differences — an ablation that would substantially strengthen the argument. - Benchmark contamination risk is not addressed: frontier models may have been trained on Concentration/Memory-card game traces or maze-navigation data; the paper does not control for or assess pre-training data overlap. Bottom Line A practitioner working on long-horizon MLLM agents or benchmarking gains a clean, actionable diagnostic tool that none of the existing suites provide — the remember-to-act failure mode is real, quantified, and the benchmark's controlled axes make it the right instrument for isolating it, though the narrow model and environment coverage means headline numbers should be treated as a snapshot rather than a definitive ranking.
Recommended 2606.19338 PDF
#81 — Innovative (64)

VibeThinker-3B: Exploring the Frontier of Verifiable Reasoning in Small Language Models (63 ▲ )

Sen Xu, Shixi Liu, Wei Wang, Jixin Min, Yingwei Dai, Zhibin Yin, Yirong Chen, Xin Zhou, Junlin Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces VibeThinker-3B, a 3-billion-parameter dense language model trained via a multi-stage post-training pipeline (curriculum SFT → multi-domain RL → offline self-distillation → instruction RL) that achieves frontier-level performance on competition mathematics and coding benchmarks against models orders of magnitude larger. - Why it's important: If verifiable reasoning can be compressed into a 3B parameter footprint without sacrificing quality, it dramatically reduces deployment cost and democratizes access to high-capability reasoning, challenging the assumption that frontier reasoning requires hundred-billion-parameter scale. - How it does it: The core mechanism is MaxEnt-Guided Policy Optimization (MGPO), which reweights RL training signal toward prompts where the model's current correctness rate is near 0.5 (maximum entropy), combined with Diversity-Exploring Distillation in SFT that merges domain-specialist checkpoints rather than training a single multi-task model. - The good: On recent LeetCode weekly/biweekly contests (Apr–May 2026), VibeThinker-3B passes 123/128 first-attempt Python submissions (96.1%), surpassing GPT-5.2, Kimi K2.5 (1T), and Claude Opus 4.6 on the same execution-verified OOD evaluation. - The missing: No ablation study isolates the contribution of individual pipeline stages (MGPO vs. Long2Short RL vs. offline self-distillation vs. Instruct RL), making it impossible to determine which components are load-bearing versus incidental. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML practitioners building small reasoning models or studying RL-based post-training pipelines; the concrete pipeline description and multi-benchmark OOD results are more valuable than the somewhat speculative Parametric Compression-Coverage Hypothesis framing.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Practical Impact (80) is the engine — a released 3B dense model that runs on a single consumer GPU yet matches frontier 100B-1T models on competition math, with an execution-verified OOD LeetCode result (96.1% on fresh contests) that is the most credible generalization evidence in the cluster. Rigor (50) is the major drag: the paper presents NO ablations, so none of the six pipeline innovations (MGPO, Long2Short, CLR, etc.) has isolated evidence, and baseline numbers are lifted from heterogeneous leaderboards rather than reproduced. Novelty (72) is real but combinatorial, and the code-specialized base model partly confounds the coding gains. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*50 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*80 = 64.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 62 Resilience 62 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Frontier-level complex reasoning — competition mathematics, olympiad-level proofs, algorithmic coding — is widely believed to require hundreds of billions of parameters, concentrating capability in models that are expensive to deploy and inaccessible to most practitioners. Prior small-model reasoning work (including the authors' own VibeThinker-1.5B) demonstrated feasibility of coherent reasoning traces at small scale but did not push into first-tier performance territory. The gap this work attempts to close is whether a strictly 3B dense model, given sufficiently optimized post-training, can match the reasoning performance of 100B–1T models on verifiable tasks — not as a general-purpose substitute, but as a specialized reasoning engine. Key Idea: Verifiable reasoning (search, constraint satisfaction, multi-step composition) is fundamentally different from knowledge retrieval in its parameter demands: it is compressible into a reusable reasoning core rather than requiring broad coverage of open-domain facts. The operational manifestation of this insight is MGPO, which focuses RL gradient updates on the capability boundary (problems where pass rate ≈ 0.5) via a maximum-entropy weighting scheme, combined with a curriculum SFT that builds diversity before RL sharpens it — the "Spectrum-to-Signal" principle. The key trick is that RL training is most efficient when neither over-easy nor under-hard problems dominate the batch; MGPO operationalizes this directly in the loss weighting. Methodology: The pipeline proceeds in five sequential stages, all applied on top of Qwen2.5-Coder-3B as the base. Stage 1 — Curriculum SFT: Two sub-stages.
