Bottom Line: For researchers working on dLLM post-training, this is a must-read: it delivers a principled, computationally efficient alternative to RLVR with strong empirical support on an 8B model, though the single-architecture scope and single-seed evaluation mean results should be treated as promising rather than definitive.
Recommended 2606.18195 PDF
#93 — Innovative (61)

Skip a Layer or Loop It? Learning Program-of-Layers in LLMs (15 ▲ )

Ziyue Li, Yang Li, Tianyi Zhou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces POLAR (Program-of-Layers), a lightweight 2.1M-parameter prediction network that learns to generate input-specific execution programs for frozen LLMs by dynamically skipping or repeating contiguous layer segments at inference time, improving accuracy while reducing or matching compute cost. - Why it's important: Fixed-depth LLM inference wastes compute on easy inputs and may under-compute on hard ones, leaving latent reasoning capacity untapped; standard dynamic-depth methods either degrade accuracy (skip-only) or require architectural changes (recurrence-based). - How it does it: MCTS first characterizes the structure of valid alternative execution programs offline; a cross-attention predictor trained on MCTS-collected supervision then predicts segment-level skip/keep/repeat decisions in a single forward pass before the frozen LLM executes. - The good: Combining skip and repeat operations jointly yields +46.8 pp (LLaMA-3.2-3B, DM-1) to +66.1 pp (Qwen2.5-3B, DM-1) accuracy gains via MCTS oracle, and POLAR's learned predictor achieves up to +20.8 pp pass@5 over base sampling while simultaneously reducing end-to-end latency to 0.83×. - The missing: All evaluation uses direct prompting without chain-of-thought (50-token output limit), a non-standard regime for math benchmarks; whether gains persist under standard CoT is not tested, and the MCTS data-collection cost is never disclosed. - Read it or not: Researchers working on adaptive inference, test-time compute scaling, or efficient LLMs should read this — the MCTS diagnostic findings on program structure are independently valuable — but practitioners should withhold deployment confidence until CoT and larger-scale (70B+) evaluations appear.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) are high: the unified skip+repeat program-of-layers over a frozen model, the single-shot cross-attention predictor, and the MCTS finding that 75.5% of correctly-solved inputs admit shorter valid programs are genuinely new and counterintuitive. But Rigor (58) and Resilience (52) pull the composite down sharply: evaluation uses direct prompting with a 50-token limit and no chain-of-thought — a non-standard regime that suppresses all baselines (several to 0.0%, suggesting adaptation problems) and is never validated as representative — the MCTS data-collection cost is undisclosed, the 600M-parameter embedding encoder is omitted from the '2.1M' footprint, and there are no error bars. Peer-reviewed (ICML 2026) with released code are credits. Composite reflects novel ideas whose headline numbers rest on a questionable evaluation regime.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 52 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Standard LLM inference applies a fixed sequence of all D transformer layers to every input regardless of difficulty, applying identical computation to a trivial arithmetic problem and a multi-step proof. This uniform execution over-computes on easy inputs and may under-compute on hard ones, failing to exploit the latent diversity of computation that pretrained layers can support. Prior dynamic-depth methods address this only partially: layer-skipping methods reduce depth but frequently degrade accuracy; recurrence methods (looped transformers, Universal Transformers) demonstrate the value of layer reuse but require training from scratch or architectural redesign; and no prior work unifies both operations over a frozen model without per-input search at inference time. ML engineers and researchers who deploy 3–8B instruction-tuned LLMs for mathematical or general reasoning face the specific problem that test-time scaling (more sampling) shows diminishing returns, while model compression trades accuracy for efficiency — POLAR targets the gap between these two strategies. Key Idea The core insight is that pretrained transformer layers can be treated as a reusable library of fixed functions, and inference as the execution of an input-specific program over that library — a program that can skip layers entirely or revisit them. MCTS empirically confirms this: better programs (shorter, or more accurate) almost always exist for every input. The practical trick is to amortize this insight by learning a single-shot predictor that maps an input directly to its execution program, replacing per-input search with a cheap cross-attention operation and beam search over a constrained segment-level decision space. Methodology The pipeline has two phases. In the offline phase, MCTS with a UCB-style tree policy (balancing exploitation, exploration, and a length-regularization penalty) searches over variable-length programs defined as sequences of layer indices, applying skip or repeat operations on contiguous blocks of up to 4 layers with at most 1 repetition per block. Binary reward is correctness on a given input. MCTS is used purely as a diagnostic and data-collection tool, not at inference time. In the learned predictor phase, the POLAR prediction network encodes input text via frozen Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B, projects to working dimension d, then performs multi-head cross-attention using learnable layer-index embeddings as queries and token representations as keys/values. A cross-layer transformer encoder models dependencies across depth, and two linear heads produce logits for (i) binary segment boundary positions and (ii) per-segment operation labels {skip, keep, repeat}. Training objective: BCE for segmentation + masked cross-entropy for operations, with shorter valid programs down-weighted to favor efficiency. At inference, segment boundaries are decoded by thresholding; beam search over segment-level operation choices produces a ranked set of k candidate programs. Three critical design decisions: segment length cap Kmax=4 (empirically justified — 54.5% of valid segments are single-layer); single-repeat only (>75% of MCTS segments require at most 1 recurrence); and use of a separate embedding model as input encoder rather than the base LLM's representations (enabling domain transfer). Key Results MCTS oracle (Skip&Loop combined) achieves +46.8 pp over base on LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct DM-1 (37.9%→84.7%), +66.1 pp on Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct DM-1 (25.4%→87.4%), and +52.8 pp on Qwen3-8B DM-1, establishing a strong ceiling. The learned POLAR predictor achieves pass@5 gains of +20.8/+4.8/+13.2/+7.6/+10.2 pp over Base (sampling) on LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct DM-1 to DM-5. Prior dynamic-depth baselines perform poorly: ShortGPT, MindSkip, and FlexiDepth achieve 0.0% on multiple model/difficulty combinations, while DR.LLM (the closest competitor) consistently trails POLAR, particularly at larger k. OOD results using Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B-Chat show POLAR at 63.8 vs. base 59.1 on ASDiv, 46.7 vs. 41.7 on MAWPS, with improvements across all 13 MMLU-Pro subject areas. Inference overhead is 3.05 ms total (0.8% of a forward pass), and end-to-end latency is 0.83× base on DM-1 and 0.95× on DM-5 for Qwen1.5-MoE (24 layers). The predictor contains ~2.1M parameters (0.01–0.06% of base LLM). A notable structural finding: 75.5% of inputs already solved correctly admit shorter valid programs; 36.2% of initially-wrong inputs are correctable by shorter programs. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The unified program-of-layers formulation (joint skip+repeat over packed contiguous segments in a frozen model) is genuinely novel relative to prior work, which treats skip and repeat as separate operations requiring separate architectures. The MCTS-based empirical characterization of valid program space — particularly that most valid programs are shorter than standard forward pass and dominated by contiguous single-layer segments with at most one recurrence — constitutes a new empirical finding about LLM layer structure. The single-shot learned predictor with cross-attention over layer-index queries is a new architectural component. Rigor & Reproducibility Code is publicly available at https://github.com/tianyi-lab/PoLar. The paper is peer-reviewed (ICML 2026). Ablations in Table 1 cleanly isolate the contribution of skip-only, loop-only, and combined operations across 4 models and 5 difficulty levels — a thorough component study. No error bars or multi-seed results are reported for the POLAR predictor results; only single runs at each k value. The direct prompting regime (no chain-of-thought, 50-token output limit) is a significant methodological choice that suppresses all baselines from their typical performance levels and is not validated as representative of real-world deployment. The MCTS data collection cost — which must be run once per model before training the predictor — is never disclosed, representing a hidden cost. In-distribution experiments train and test within the same DART-Math difficulty level, which does not test cross-difficulty transfer. The comparison with FlexiDepth and MindSkip shows near-zero accuracy on some models (e.g., MindSkip 0.0% on Qwen3-8B), raising questions about whether those baselines are properly adapted. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: All four evaluated models (3B–8B, including a MoE) run on commercially available hardware, and the 2.1M-parameter predictor adds negligible resource requirements. - Efficient: Predictor overhead is 3.05 ms (0.8% of one forward pass), and end-to-end latency is reduced by 5–17% on easier inputs; the Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B encoder used as input backbone adds ~600M parameters not counted in the 2.1M figure. - Effective: POLAR improves pass@k accuracy on both in-distribution DART-Math and OOD benchmarks including diverse MMLU-Pro subjects, but exclusively under direct prompting without chain-of-thought, which is a non-standard and potentially unrepresentative evaluation regime. - Scalable: Tested on models with up to 8B parameters and 24–36 layers; no evaluation on 70B+ models; the MCTS supervision collection phase has not been characterized for scalability to deeper models or larger program spaces. Delivers a software artifact (GitHub repo with code). Strengths - The two-phase design is tight: MCTS findings directly motivate every key design constraint (Kmax=4, single-repeat), making the predictor architecture derivable from first principles rather than arbitrary hyperparameter choices. - The empirical finding that 75.5% of correctly-solved inputs admit shorter valid programs is counterintuitive and meaningfully challenges the assumption that all layers contribute to every prediction. - Skip+repeat jointly outperforms either operation alone by a large margin (e.g., 84.7% vs. 54.9% loop-only vs. 45.7% skip-only for LLaMA DM-1), empirically validating the unified program space as strictly richer than either prior line of work. - OOD generalization from DART-Math to diverse MMLU-Pro subject areas (humanities, social sciences, natural sciences) despite training only on math suggests the predictor learns transferable compute-routing heuristics. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly limit evaluation to mathematical reasoning and call for future work evaluating robustness, calibration, interpretability, and safety before applying POLAR to high-stakes domains. They note that the input-dependent execution paths warrant future interpretability analysis. The impact statement acknowledges that improved LLM efficiency could amplify both beneficial and harmful uses. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The direct prompting regime (no CoT, 50-token limit) is not the standard way math benchmarks are evaluated; POLAR's gains under standard CoT prompting are untested and could be smaller or larger. - The MCTS data-collection cost is unreported — this is a prerequisite for training the predictor on any new model, and its scaling behavior with model size, vocabulary, or problem domain is unknown. - The pass@k comparison between POLAR (beam search over execution programs) and Base (stochastic decoding) conflates two different sources of diversity; total compute per candidate is not equal, making the improvement attribution partially ambiguous. - The Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B encoder used as the input representation backbone is a significant unlisted dependency (600M+ parameters); the ~2.1M predictor figure understates the total added footprint. - Several baselines (MindSkip, FlexiDepth, ShortGPT) show near-zero accuracy on some models, suggesting potential implementation or adaptation issues rather than true method capability — this weakens the competitive comparison. - No evaluation on models larger than 8B parameters; it is unstated whether the predictor's cross-layer encoder, with D positional slots, scales gracefully to 80+ layers. Bottom Line A researcher working on adaptive LLM inference will find concrete, well-supported findings here — particularly the MCTS characterization of valid program structure and the surprisingly large gains from combining skip and repeat — but should treat the accuracy numbers with caution until validated under standard chain-of-thought evaluation and at larger model scales.
Recommended 2606.06574 PDF
#94 — Innovative (61)

JanusMesh: Fast and Zero-Shot 3D Visual Illusion Generation via Cross-Space Denoising (16 ▲ )

Siang-Ling Zhang, Huai-Hsun Cheng, Tsung-Ju Yang, Yu-Lun Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces JanusMesh, a training-free two-stage framework that generates a single textured 3D mesh embodying two (or three) entirely different semantic identities viewable only from specific camera angles, completing in 3–5 minutes on a single GPU. - Why it's important: Existing SDS-based 3D illusion methods require ~40 minutes per object and suffer from color over-saturation, while naive geometry stitching produces visible seams and semantic leakage — JanusMesh eliminates both failure modes without requiring any training or per-shape optimization. - How it does it: At each rectified-flow denoising step, two semantic branches are decoded from latent to voxel space, rotation-aligned via CLIP-guided orientation search, fused by averaging their Signed Distance Fields, then re-encoded — a cross-space decode-blend-encode loop that enforces coherent geometry while preserving per-viewpoint semantics. - The good: The method achieves 84% GPT-4.1-mini accuracy on semantic recognizability (vs. 76% for Direct Concatenation and 70% for Shape from Semantics) across 50 prompt pairs, with near-unity Impact Factor (0.994) indicating seamless geometry at the fusion boundary, all in the same 3–5 minute wall-clock time as the naive stitching baseline. - The missing: The orientation search samples only at 90° intervals (28 candidates), and no experiment tests whether finer angular resolution (e.g., 45°) actually improves results — a straightforward ablation the authors defer to future work. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in 3D generative modeling, computational illusions, or synchronized diffusion denoising; practitioners wanting a fast, plug-and-play illusion generator will find the implementation details sufficient to reproduce the approach.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (70) and Novelty (68) are the high points — the cross-space decode-blend-encode loop inside a pretrained 3D rectified-flow process is a clean, non-obvious move, and the method handles the controllability-vs-realism trade-off well. Rigor (55) and Resilience (55) are the limiters: author-curated 60-object vocabulary and only 50 pairs, a 2-way GPT-4.1-mini metric (50% chance baseline) using the same model family, no code release, no failure-rate analysis, and omitted directly-relevant baselines (Illusion3D, LookingGlass). Composite ≈ 0.20*68+0.25*55+0.15*70+0.15*60+0.15*55+0.10*58 ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Creating a single 3D mesh that presents semantically distinct objects depending on the viewpoint — a 3D visual illusion — is commercially relevant for interactive art, entertainment, and design, but technically difficult because the geometry must simultaneously satisfy two conflicting shape priors. Prior optimization-based approaches (SDS/DreamFusion-style) achieve this but require ~40 minutes per object and produce over-saturated textures due to pathologies in the SDS loss. Simple alternatives — independently generating two objects and stitching them at the midplane — expose unnatural seams at the junction view and allow each object's geometry to "leak" into the opposing viewpoint. The gap in prior work is the absence of a fast, training-free method that fuses two 3D shapes into a single geometrically coherent mesh without per-shape optimization. Key Idea The central trick is performing geometry fusion inside a pre-trained 3D generative model's denoising loop rather than before or after it: at every rectified-flow denoising step, the two semantic latents are decoded into voxel space, their Signed Distance Fields are averaged element-wise, the blended voxel is binarized with a threshold, then re-encoded back to latent space for the next denoising step. This cross-space decode-blend-encode loop co-guides both branches toward a jointly realizable geometry instead of fusing two independently finalized shapes. Methodology Stage 1 (Geometry): Starting from shared noise z_t, two independent rectified-flow branches condition on prompts y₁ and y₂ respectively, each computing a clean-latent estimate x^i_{1|t} = z_t + u_θ(z_t; t, y_i)(1 - t). At each of 25 denoising steps, both estimates are decoded by TRELLIS's Sparse Structure Decoder into voxel grids v₁, v₂. Before blending, CLIP-guided Orientation Search selects the relative rotation of object 2: 4 candidate views of v̂₁ are scored by CLIP text-image similarity to y₁, and 28 3D rotation combinations of v̂₂ are scored by CLIP image-image similarity to the best view of v̂₁, selecting the angle that maximizes silhouette alignment. After rotating v₂ by θ₂, the paper computes SDF_blend = (SDF(v₁) + SDF(v₂)) / 2 and binarizes with threshold τ = 0.8 to obtain a merged voxel x̂_{1|t}. This merged voxel is re-encoded via the Sparse Structure Encoder and injected back into both denoising branches for the next step. Classifier-Free Guidance (scale ω = 7.5) is applied only within t ∈ [0.5, 0.95] (Interval CFG) to avoid over-saturation. Optional Noise Guidance can inject a spatial prior before denoising: Noise Blending initializes z_init = α·Encoder(v_guide) + (1-α)·z_noise (α = 0.3), while Space Control skips the first t₀ = 10 steps and restarts from the encoded guidance latent at that point. Stage 2 (Texture): Because the fused mesh geometry is unnatural, TRELLIS's own texture stage fails. Stage 2 instead uses a depth-conditioned ControlNet (Stable Diffusion) to predict textures from viewpoints θ₁ and θ₂, un-projects each clean image estimate onto the mesh surface, and aggregates contributions via cosine-weighted blending based on surface normals over 30 denoising steps. Key design decisions: (1) SDF averaging rather than binary occupancy averaging — well-justified, as SDF's continuous distance field provides geometric continuity absent in binary OR/AND; (2) separating geometry and texture stages — well-justified, since TRELLIS's appearance module cannot interpret fused unnatural geometry; (3) coarse 90° angle sampling for orientation search — not deeply justified, and 45° sampling is deferred to future work. Key Results All experiments ran on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090. Over 50 randomly sampled prompt pairs from a 60-object vocabulary (16 birds, 19 mammals, 5 reptiles/aquatic, 9 plants, 11 man-made artifacts): JanusMesh achieves GPT-4.1-mini accuracy of 84% (Direct Concatenation: 76%, Shape from Semantics: 70%, TRELLIS: 60%, DreamBeast: 65%). CLIP similarity: 28.170 (Direct Concatenation artificially inflates to 29.030 by preserving per-view appearance trivially). View-conditional CLIP contrast (lower = less leakage): 19.26 vs. 19.72/20.38 for the two primary baselines. FID: 185.555 (vs. 194.136 for Shape from Semantics; TRELLIS achieves lowest FID at 174.130 but fails the illusion task). Object Detection average count at junction: 0.86 (vs. 2.1 for Direct Concatenation), multi-object rate: 18% (vs. 56%). Impact Factor (boundary smoothness, ideal = 1.0): 0.994 (vs. 1.129 for Direct Concatenation). Runtime: 3–5 minutes vs. ~40 minutes for Shape from Semantics and ~3–4 hours for DreamBeast. User study (50 participants): 78.5% rated results as clearly recognizable; 71% preferred JanusMesh; 91% preferred CLIP-guided orientation over fixed 0°/180°. Ablation on blending strategy confirms SDF averaging outperforms Union, Blur Average, Minkowski, and Polar Coord strategies qualitatively. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.20563v1, June 2026) — these are unreviewed results. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. The genuine novelty is the cross-space decode-blend-encode loop applied within a pre-trained 3D rectified-flow denoising process, which differs meaningfully from prior synchronized diffusion work (Visual Anagrams, SyncTweedies) that operates in 2D pixel/latent space. The SDF averaging for voxel fusion, CLIP-guided adaptive orientation search, and view-conditioned texturing as a separate stage are combinations of known techniques (CLIP scoring, SDF geometry, ControlNet) but assembled in a configuration not previously applied to this task. The extension to three-object illusions is a natural additive contribution rather than a new idea. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present for all four major components (blending strategy, noise guidance, texture synthesis, orientation search), which is a meaningful strength. However, error bars and confidence intervals are absent from Table 2; no statistical significance tests are reported. The evaluation dataset is author-curated (60 prompts drawn partly from the Shape from Semantics baseline's own prompt set), and testing covers only 50 random pairs — a modest scale. The GPT-4.1-mini accuracy is a binary 2-way forced choice (50% chance baseline), which inflates apparent accuracy; absolute recognizability is not independently anchored. FID/KID are computed against 1,000 reference renders of 50 Objaverse objects, a small reference set that may not reflect domain distribution well. No code or trained model weights are released at the time of the preprint (project page exists at siang1105.github.io/JanusMesh.github.io/ but the paper does not state code availability). DreamBeast's ~3–4 hour runtime makes fair comparison difficult, but it is evaluated. The user study (50 participants) does not specify recruitment, compensation, or attention-check methodology. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (method evaluated with controlled experiments and user study). - Practical: Runs on a single consumer NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU without training or specialized infrastructure, making it practically deployable for researchers and artists with access to modern consumer hardware. - Efficient: 3–5 minutes end-to-end vs. ~40 minutes for the SDS baseline and ~3–4 hours for DreamBeast; the primary overhead is TRELLIS inference plus 25 decode-blend-encode cycles per denoising step, which adds modest latency over standard TRELLIS generation. - Effective: The evidence shows genuine improvement in semantic recognizability (84% GPT accuracy) and geometric coherence (Impact Factor 0.994, multi-object rate 18%) over both stitching and optimization baselines, though on a relatively small and domain-limited evaluation set. - Scalable: The paper tests two- and three-object illusions but does not evaluate scaling beyond three objects; three-object cases already require mandatory Space Control Guidance with stronger constraints (t₀ = 20/25 steps), suggesting geometric conflicts grow with the number of semantics and the approach may not generalize gracefully beyond three. The work delivers a pipeline (mesh output) but no released software artifact; reproducibility depends on TRELLIS's publicly available weights and Stable Diffusion ControlNet. Strengths - The cross-space decode-blend-encode loop is a technically clean and practically effective way to co-guide two denoising branches toward a shared geometry without requiring model retraining or SDS optimization. - The novel evaluation metric suite — particularly View-Conditional CLIP Contrast (penalizing cross-view leakage) and the Impact Factor (curvature discontinuity at the fusion seam) — addresses a genuine measurement gap and provides more diagnostic signal than CLIP similarity alone. - The identification and explicit handling of CLIP's limitation (Direct Concatenation inflates CLIP scores by preserving per-view appearance trivially) shows methodological awareness that prevents Goodhart's Law from masking baseline failures. - The three-object extension scales the method without architectural changes, demonstrating compositionality of the dual-branch denoising design. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) JanusMesh inherits TRELLIS's failure cases for specific object categories (pigs, bats), pointing users to supplementary material for failure examples — this is a candid acknowledgment. (2) CLIP-guided Orientation Search fails for three-object illusions because three averaged silhouettes produce ambiguous shapes, so three-object angles are currently fixed at 0°/120°/240°, with automated alignment left as future work. (3) CLIP misclassifications can yield suboptimal angles for already geometrically compatible pairs, and the paper recommends adaptive search only for challenging pairs. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The orientation search samples only at 90° intervals (28 candidates). The paper asserts that denser sampling (e.g., 45°) "could further improve alignment precision" but provides no ablation quantifying this effect — a simple and important missing experiment given that CLIP orientation is a primary claimed contribution. - The 60-object vocabulary and 50 evaluation pairs are author-curated; no held-out test set from an independent source is used, raising questions about whether prompt selection favors the method. - The GPT-4.1-mini accuracy metric uses the same model family (GPT-4.1-mini) that may have biases toward visually coherent renders; an independent human perceptual accuracy experiment would be more credible. - The paper does not evaluate failure rate: how often does JanusMesh produce a mesh where neither target semantic is recognizable? Only successes are shown in qualitative figures. - The view-conditioned texture uses a hard 180° angular split for texture assignment (0°–90° and 270°–90° for one semantic). No evaluation examines whether this hard cutoff produces visible artifacts at oblique angles beyond the two target viewpoints. - No comparison is made to Illusion3D [18] or LookingGlass [5], which are directly relevant 3D illusion methods cited in related work but excluded from quantitative baselines. Bottom Line A practitioner building 3D visual illusion tools gains a concrete, fast, training-free pipeline from this work — the cross-space SDF blending insight is the right technical move and the evaluation is sufficient to establish superiority over the primary alternatives, even if the evaluation scale and missing ablations leave room for a follow-up to tighten the claims.
Recommended 2606.20563 PDF
#95 — Innovative (61)

Stable Multivariate Functional Time Series Prediction for Major Geomagnetic Indices

Y Yu, S Zou, T Pulkkinen, Y Chen · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces OLFTSA, an overlapping functional time series analysis framework that jointly forecasts five geomagnetic indices (Kp, Dst, SYM-H, SME, SMR) at 6–24 hour horizons by combining overlapping rolling-window segmentation, per-variable FPCA with varimax rotation, VARX modeling in score space, and conformal prediction intervals. - Why it's important: Accurate multi-hour geomagnetic forecasting is critical for protecting power grids, satellites, and communications from solar storm damage, yet prior data-driven methods are largely limited to 1–3 hour horizons and treat each index independently. - How it does it: Raw index streams are segmented into overlapping 48–72 hour functional patches (overlap ratio γ up to 0.875), compressed via per-variable FPCA, jointly forecast via VARX in the low-dimensional score space, and uncertainty-quantified with Mahalanobis-type conformal prediction that provably achieves O(D^{-1/3}) conditional coverage deviation under strong-mixing dependent errors. - The good: OLFTSA beats LSTM and CHRONOS on all five indices at both horizons — Dst RMSE 8.41 nT vs. 13.33 (LSTM) and 10.10 (CHRONOS) at 6 hours — with empirical conformal coverage of 0.9236 for Dst at the nominal 95% level. - The missing: No operational space weather baselines (e.g., persistence, climatology, NOAA physics-based models) are included, making it impossible to judge whether the improvement translates to operational forecasting value. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on functional data analysis or space weather machine learning and want a principled, code-released framework for joint index forecasting with calibrated uncertainty; skip it if you need evidence against operational baselines before adoption.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (70) is the highest dimension — a NEW METHOD (overlapping-window FTS + Mahalanobis conformal under dependent errors) backed by a genuine asymptotic coverage theorem (O(D^-1/3)) extending Xu et al. 2024, a real theoretical contribution beyond the application. Rigor (60) is dragged down by the most serious gap: no operational/physics-based or persistence baselines, so improvements over LSTM/CHRONOS (the latter self-admittedly handicapped) don't establish operational value; a single train/test split, no CIs, and an unprincipled τ also weigh. Non-Obviousness (55) is modest since overlapping rolling windows is the simplest possible modification to standard FTS. Code+data on Zenodo and honest limitation acknowledgment support Completeness/Impact (62/60). Composite: 0.20*70+0.25*60+0.15*55+0.15*62+0.15*55+0.10*60 = 61.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Geomagnetic activity driven by solar wind and interplanetary magnetic field variations is monitored through indices (Kp, Dst, SYM-H, SME, SMR) that characterize distinct aspects of the magnetosphere-ionosphere system. Disruptions in these indices signal risks to power grid stability, satellite electronics, and GPS navigation. The forecasting challenge is severe: these indices exhibit heterogeneous cadences (3-hourly Kp to 1-minute SYM-H/SME/SMR), dynamic ranges spanning orders of magnitude (Kp: 0–9; SME: 0–6000 nT), abrupt storm-time transitions, and non-negligible cross-index correlations. Existing data-driven approaches treat prediction as a single-output regression problem, producing forecasts for one index at a time with skill typically limited to 1–3 hours ahead. Non-overlapping functional time series methods artificially fragment temporal continuity at segment boundaries, losing transition dynamics and reducing effective sample size — a particular problem for storm events that span multiple windows. The gap is a joint multivariate forecasting framework that respects functional structure across correlated heterogeneous series and delivers calibrated uncertainty bounds beyond 3 hours. Key Idea The central insight is that conventional non-overlapping FTS segmentation discards information at window boundaries: a storm onset straddling two non-overlapping patches is split, degrading both FPCA basis estimation and cross-patch dynamics. Replacing fixed non-overlapping partitions with overlapping rolling windows of configurable overlap ratio γ ∈ [0, 1) preserves boundary information, increases the effective number of functional observations by a factor of 1/(1-γ), and produces smoother FPCA covariance estimates. This same overlapping structure also tightens conformal prediction intervals, because the calibration score distribution is estimated from a denser and more temporally coherent set of residuals. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. (1) Patch construction: all 11 series (5 geomagnetic responses + 6 solar wind predictors) are resampled to a common 1-hour grid (or 5-min for SYM-H/SME/SMR), then segmented into overlapping patches of length 48 or 72 hours via rolling stride of (1-γ)·n hours. (2) FPCA: each variable's patches are smoothed via penalized B-splines; per-variable FPCA via the Karhunen-Loève expansion retains K_p components explaining 99% of variance. The stacked score matrix across all 11 variables is then orthogonally rotated by varimax for parsimony. (3) VARX forecasting: the stacked score time series {ξ_{P∪Q,d}} is modeled with a vector autoregressive model with exogenous inputs, using only the most recent lag (h=1), justified by rapid empirical autocorrelation decay. Forecasted score vectors are back-projected onto eigenfunctions to produce functional predictions on the original observation grid. (4) Conformal prediction: calibration residuals from all training patches form a Mahalanobis nonconformity score using a thresholded (entrywise, parameter τ) empirical covariance to ensure positive-definiteness. The sequential conformal quantile regression of Xu et al. (2024) handles temporal dependence and produces ellipsoidal prediction regions. Theorem 1 establishes a formal asymptotic coverage bound under five stated assumptions (stationarity, Lipschitz CDF, prediction error bounds, covariance regularity, weak dependence with moment conditions). Critical design decisions: overlapping windows (ablated empirically across γ); varimax rotation (justified by parsimony, not ablated for accuracy); single-lag VARX (motivated by autocorrelation plots, not ablated for storm-time performance). Key Results At 6-hour horizons (Table 1), OLFTSA achieves RMSE/R of 0.836/0.767 (Kp), 8.41/0.907 (Dst), 8.89/0.901 (SYM-H), 8.93/0.886 (SMR), 162.9/0.653 (SME), outperforming LSTM (1.012/0.726, 13.33/0.831, 12.55/0.847, 13.90/0.829, 222.0/0.587) and CHRONOS-base (0.948/0.693, 10.10/0.863, 10.71/0.854, 10.53/0.840, 180.8/0.577) on all metrics across all five indices. At 24-hour horizons (Table B.4), the gap narrows but OLFTSA still leads: Kp R = 0.590 vs. 0.463 (LSTM) vs. 0.491 (CHRONOS); Dst RMSE 13.09 vs. 15.97 vs. 15.06. SME at 24 hours is severely degraded (R = 0.42 for OLFTSA vs. 0.28 for LSTM), consistent with substorm physics limiting predictability at this horizon. Conformal coverage is close to nominal: Dst 6h ECP = 0.924, 24h ECP = 0.892 (nominal 0.95). FSSA-Bootstrap in simulation yields severely undercovering intervals (ECP 0.38–0.59 at nominal 0.95). OLFTSA is faster than CHRONOS (Kp: 0.93 vs. 1.61 sec; SYM-H: 15.44 vs. 20.67 sec per step). The LSTM baseline is a 2-layer encoder-decoder with 10% dropout; CHRONOS is the smallest base variant without multivariate extension or covariate incorporation — both are deliberately conservative choices. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuinely novel contribution is the overlapping-window FTS framework combined with Mahalanobis conformal prediction under dependent errors, along with the formal coverage theorem (Theorem 1). The application of FPCA, VAR, and conformal prediction individually is well-established; the combination for multivariate geomagnetic index joint forecasting with exogenous solar wind inputs is new. The overlap design as a mechanism for improving both point forecasts and conformal coverage in FTS is a methodological contribution beyond the application domain. Rigor & Reproducibility Code and data are publicly released on Zenodo (doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20274712). Simulations use 10 replications across 12 configurations (2 values of D̃ × 3 values of n × 4 values of γ); ablation on γ directly demonstrates the causal effect of overlapping. Theorem 1 is proved formally in the supplementary (Appendix A) with supporting Lemmas A.1–A.3, which appear complete and correctly structured. Real-data results use a single train/test split (1995–2019 train, 2020–2025 test), with no cross-validation or confidence intervals on test metrics — a common limitation in space weather forecasting but a gap nonetheless. The thresholding parameter τ for covariance estimation lacks a principled selection rule or sensitivity analysis. The LSTM is not hyperparameter-tuned against a validation set, and the CHRONOS comparison is self-admittedly handicapped (smallest model, no multivariate extension). The paper is a preprint on arXiv (arXiv:2606.14417v1, June 2026); results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — methods, forecasting experiments, and uncertainty quantification on real geomagnetic data. - Practical: Runs on standard CPU hardware with per-step prediction times of 0.9–15.4 seconds depending on index cadence; no GPU required, making operational deployment feasible at NOAA-class computing environments. - Efficient: Computationally lighter than CHRONOS for high-resolution indices (SYM-H: 15.44 sec vs. 20.67 sec) and far lighter than FSSA-Bootstrap (seconds vs. 12–23 minutes per run in simulation); VARX fit scales with total retained PC dimensions across 11 variables, which grows with window length. - Effective: Evidence for improvement over LSTM and CHRONOS is consistent across all five indices at both 6- and 24-hour horizons on a 5-year out-of-sample test set spanning solar cycle 25; visual storm tracking (Figure 4) demonstrates appropriate storm morphology capture. Whether it improves over operational physics-based models is not tested. - Scalable: Training uses all data from 1995 through the current date, so the VARX model grows with data volume; no stress-scale experiment is reported. For longer windows (72h vs. 48h), more functional PCs are retained, increasing VARX dimensionality and prediction cost — acknowledged by the authors. The work delivers a code-and-data artifact on Zenodo, placing it above paper-form-only. Strengths - The overlapping rolling-window design is the simplest possible modification to standard FTS methodology and yields consistent, monotonic improvement in both RMSE/MAE and conformal coverage tightness as γ increases — demonstrated across all simulation configurations. - Theorem 1 provides a non-trivial asymptotic coverage guarantee under strong mixing (not i.i.d.), extending the Xu et al. (2024) result to handle covariance estimation error from thresholded estimators; the proof machinery is complete and uses established results from Chen et al. (2013). - The real-data train/test split respects temporal ordering across two distinct solar cycles, and the test set includes the extreme May 2024 geomagnetic storm — a meaningful stress test that is difficult to game. - Public code and data release on Zenodo substantially lowers the barrier for independent validation and extension by the space weather community. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note: (1) SME at 24-hour horizons has fundamentally limited predictability due to substorm life cycles of minutes, so R = 0.42 is expected to be near the predictability ceiling; (2) longer windows increase VARX dimensionality and computational cost; (3) the CHRONOS comparison uses only the smallest variant without multivariate extension, which the authors frame as a conservative choice that highlights the upside of larger foundation models. These are acknowledged directly in Sections 4 and 5, which reflects credibly on the authors' self-assessment. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The absence of operational and physics-based baselines (NOAA Dst nowcast, persistence forecast, climatological mean, Tsyganenko empirical models) is the most serious omission. Without them, it is not possible to assess whether OLFTSA closes any gap relevant to space weather operations, where the competitive baselines are not LSTM or CHRONOS. - CHRONOS-2 (Ansari et al. 2025), which supports universal/multivariate forecasting and is explicitly cited in the reference list, was not used as a baseline, despite being a fairer comparison for a method that jointly models multiple series with exogenous covariates. - Distribution shift between solar cycle 24 (training, a historically weak cycle) and cycle 25 (test, notably more active, including the May 2024 extreme event) is not analyzed; if OLFTSA's functional basis is tuned to cycle 24 dynamics, its advantage may partly reflect favorable storm morphology in the test window rather than generalization. - The thresholding parameter τ for covariance estimation in the conformal procedure is a consequential tuning choice with no selection rule, sensitivity analysis, or default reported. - Varimax rotation on the stacked score matrix is not ablated against unrotated FPCA or other rotation strategies — it remains unclear whether it improves or hurts forecasting accuracy versus interpretability. - The VARX model assumes linear dynamics in score space; known nonlinearities in solar wind–magnetosphere coupling (e.g., Bz southward saturation, substorm triggering thresholds) are not addressed and could limit performance during extreme events. Bottom Line For functional data analysts and space weather ML researchers, OLFTSA is a methodologically rigorous and fully reproducible step forward in joint geomagnetic index forecasting — but operational space weather practitioners should treat the claimed improvements as preliminary until comparisons against physics-based and persistence baselines are added.
Citations 2606.14417 PDF
#96 — Innovative (61)

CloudCons: A Comprehensive End-to-End Benchmark for Cloud Resource Consolidation

X Zhang, L Shen, M Chen, Z Li, H Li, H Fu, J Sun… · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CloudCons, an end-to-end benchmark that evaluates time series forecasting models not just on prediction error but on their downstream utility in cloud resource consolidation decisions, using multi-cloud workload datasets from Huawei, Azure, and Google Borg. - Why it's important: Cloud data centers operate at only 15–20% average CPU utilization due to over-provisioning, and the emerging forecast-then-optimize paradigm for consolidation remains unvalidated at the decision level — existing benchmarks stop at prediction error and cannot verify whether foundation models' forecasting gains translate into operational value. - How it does it: It couples a forecasting stage (statistical, deep learning, and zero-shot foundation models) with a resource optimization stage (FFD, BFD, ACO, Gurobi solving a dynamic bin-packing formulation), then measures five dimensions: prediction error, resource efficiency, load balance, service reliability, and uncertainty quantification at the physical-machine level via Monte Carlo aggregation. - The good: The paper demonstrates empirically, across four real-world datasets and four optimizers, that superior MASE/CRPS does not guarantee better violation rates, including a controlled case study showing Moirai 2 and Sundial underestimate aggregated demand peaks despite lower point-forecast error, while DeepAR with higher error averts the same contention event. - The missing: The benchmark fixes a static VM-to-PM assignment throughout the forecast horizon and excludes reinforcement learning; this simplification removes migration dynamics that are central to real consolidation pipelines, limiting external validity for production schedulers. - Read it or not: Cloud infrastructure engineers and AIOps researchers evaluating foundation models for scheduling should read this; pure forecasting researchers can skip it unless they care about decision-oriented metrics.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (70) is the standout: the demonstrated forecasting-to-decision misalignment — better MASE/CRPS does not yield lower violation rates, with a controlled DeepAR-averts-contention case — is a counter-intuitive, practically important finding prior work only asserted. Novelty (65) and Impact (68) reflect a released multi-cloud benchmark with actionable quantile-calibration thresholds. Rigor (57) and Completeness/Resilience (55/54) pull the composite down: only 25 scenarios per dataset after 10% downsampling, no error bars or significance tests, ≤575 VMs vs. production millions, a static VM-assignment simplification, and zero-shot-vs-full-shot protocols that conflate training exposure with architecture. Composite: 0.20*65+0.25*57+0.15*70+0.15*55+0.15*54+0.10*68 = 61.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 57 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 54 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Cloud data centers globally maintain CPU utilization of only 15–20% because operators over-provision to protect SLAs. The forecast-then-optimize paradigm — predict future VM resource demand, then pack VMs onto fewer physical machines — promises to close this gap. Emerging time series foundation models (Moirai, Chronos, TimesFM, etc.) have shown strong zero-shot forecasting on diverse domains, but no prior benchmark asks whether their forecasting advantage translates into better consolidation outcomes. Existing cloud benchmarks (BOOM, CloudOps, Toner et al.) either evaluate on a single cloud platform with homogeneous workloads, omit foundation models, or stop at prediction error metrics without connecting to downstream decisions. The gap is practically significant: a model that achieves low MASE may systematically underestimate demand peaks, causing the optimizer to over-pack VMs and trigger SLA violations. Key Idea: The central insight is that prediction error metrics (MASE, CRPS) are misaligned with consolidation objectives: a model can minimize average forecast deviation while systematically underestimating the tail peaks that drive capacity violations. CloudCons operationalizes this misalignment by constructing a simulation environment that converts probabilistic forecasts into bin-packing decisions and then measures actual violation rates and utilization on held-out ground-truth demand — making the gap between accuracy rank and decision rank directly observable. A secondary insight is that predictive quantile selection (using the q-th quantile of the forecast distribution as the demand estimate) acts as a continuous lever between resource efficiency and service reliability, enabling practitioners to tune operating points without changing the forecasting model. Methodology: The benchmark pipeline has two stages. Stage I (Demand Forecasting): given historical CPU usage sequences with look-back windows (512/96/36 timesteps for 5-min/30-min/1-hour granularities), models generate probabilistic forecasts over horizons of 48–336 steps. Statistical models (AutoARIMA, AutoETS, AutoTheta) fit per-window; deep learning models (DLinear, PatchTST, DeepAR, TFT) are trained from scratch per dataset with Optuna hyperparameter search (15 trials); foundation models (Moirai 2, Chronos 2, TimesFM 2.5, Sundial, TOTO, FlowState-9.1M, Kairos 50M) are evaluated zero-shot. Stage II (Resource Optimization): predicted demands feed a dynamic bin-packing MILP where binary variables assign VMs to PMs, the objective minimizes active PM count, and the capacity constraint must hold at every future timestep in the horizon. Solvers include FFD, BFD (greedy heuristics), ACO (metaheuristic), and Gurobi (exact). Decision quality is measured by Util, PAR, VR, VS averaged across optimization methods. Uncertainty is assessed by Monte Carlo aggregation of per-VM predictive distributions to PM level, then evaluated by PICP, MPIW, and Winkler Score at 95% confidence. Critical design decisions: (1) averaging consolidation metrics across all four optimizers to avoid algorithm-specific bias — justified because individual optimizers have strongly different risk profiles; (2) using a sliding window (stride=1) for simulation versus non-overlapping rolling for forecasting evaluation — this doubles the number of consolidation scenarios to 25 per dataset; (3) fixing the VM assignment statically throughout the forecast horizon to minimize migration overhead — a simplification that is declared but not validated. Key Results: On prediction error, foundation models dominate in complex workloads: Chronos 2 achieves MASE/CRPS of 0.752/0.642 on Huawei2025 and 0.690/0.364 on Azure2019, outperforming all statistical and deep learning baselines in zero-shot mode. On the structured Borg2019-e dataset (strong 24-hour seasonality), PatchTST (0.870/0.371) approaches the leading foundation model (TimesFM 2.5: 0.846/0.333). On decision utility, the misalignment finding is the paper's headline: across all four datasets and optimizers, foundation models show no consistent advantage in VR/VS at comparable Util levels. On Borg2019-e, where all models converge to Util=0.637, AutoETS achieves VR=0.038 and DeepAR VR=0.042, while Moirai 2 reaches VR=0.083 and Chronos 2 VR=0.106 — worse reliability despite better MASE. On Azure2019, statistical models (AutoARIMA VR=0.110) substantially outperform the foundation model TFT (VR=0.474) in reliability at similar utilization, illustrating that deep learning models trained full-shot can underestimate bursty peaks. Fine-tuning foundation models improves MASE/CRPS but does not consistently improve VR: TOTO fine-tuned on Azure2019 sees MASE drop from 0.706 to 0.660 but VR worsens from 0.122 to 0.150. Quantile sensitivity analysis shows that VR < 0.01 reliability thresholds first appear at α ≥ 0.8, median forecasting (α=0.5) is insufficient in volatile environments, and the transition zone α∈[0.4,0.6] exhibits the highest VR sensitivity. Datasets were downsampled to 68–575 VMs for simulation; results are averaged over 25 test scenarios per dataset. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark construction (multi-cloud datasets + end-to-end evaluation pipeline) is the primary artifact; the forecasting-to-decision misalignment finding is an empirical contribution. The individual components — bin-packing formulation, time series models, evaluation metrics — are all standard; the novelty is their integration into a decision-centric evaluation harness and the systematic quantile sensitivity analysis that bridges probabilistic forecasting to operational policy. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code and datasets are released at https://github.com/dmwyd/CloudCons. Deep learning models undergo Optuna hyperparameter search (15 trials per model-dataset pair). The paper reports no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests on consolidation metrics, despite using only 25 test scenarios per dataset — with 14 models and 7 metrics, many comparisons may not be statistically robust. The simulation is restricted to hourly granularity and a 14-day window, with 10% downsampling on large datasets. Foundation models are evaluated zero-shot while deep learning models are full-shot trained — this is a realistic deployment comparison but means the comparison is not model-architecture-controlled (training data advantage conflated with architecture). The fine-tuning experiments in Appendix B.2 are supplementary and cover only 3 foundation models. No ablation isolates the effect of Monte Carlo sample count K on uncertainty metrics. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems-level benchmark with simulation experiments). - Practical: The simulation runs on standard hardware using SimPy; Gurobi requires a commercial license for large-scale exact solving, but FFD/BFD are trivially deployable. - Efficient: The benchmark is computationally tractable at the tested scale (68–575 VMs, 25 scenarios), but scales quadratically with VM count for Gurobi and must be rerun from scratch for each forecasting model; no runtime figures are reported. - Effective: The benchmark reliably surfaces the forecasting-to-decision misalignment across four datasets and four optimizers, and the quantile sensitivity analysis yields actionable thresholds (α∈[0.4,0.6] for cost-sensitive, α≥0.8 for mission-critical). - Scalable: The simulation is downsampled to ≤575 VMs; production data centers operate millions of VMs. The paper does not test or claim scalability beyond the evaluated range, and the Gurobi exact solver is explicitly noted to have exponential time complexity. The work delivers a software artifact (benchmark suite with datasets, simulation code, and evaluation framework). Strengths: - The end-to-end simulation pipeline that converts probabilistic forecasts into bin-packing decisions and measures real violation rates is a genuine methodological contribution over forecasting-only benchmarks; it surfaces a concrete and practically important misalignment that prior work asserted but did not demonstrate at this scale. - The multi-cloud dataset construction covering four qualitatively distinct workload regimes (heterogeneous diurnal, pulse-like bursty, high-frequency noisy, synchronized periodic) strengthens external validity compared to single-platform benchmarks. - The quantile sensitivity analysis yields specific, actionable operational guidelines (α=0.8+ for VR<0.01 threshold) rather than qualitative recommendations, making it directly usable by practitioners configuring production schedulers. - The inclusion of a fine-tuning experiment (Appendix B.2) that shows fine-tuning improves MASE but can worsen VR is a strong additional piece of evidence for the core claim, not merely exploratory. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The paper excludes reinforcement learning methods to maintain focus on the decoupled forecast-then-optimize paradigm; the static VM assignment assumption throughout the forecast horizon minimizes migration overhead but does not capture dynamic reassignment. The paper treats these as scope boundaries rather than confronting them as validity threats. No other limitations are explicitly acknowledged — in particular, the small simulation scale (≤575 VMs), absence of statistical significance tests, and the conflation of zero-shot vs. full-shot training protocols are not discussed. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The comparison pits zero-shot foundation models against full-shot deep learning baselines — while this reflects a realistic deployment scenario (no retraining budget), it means differences in decision utility could stem from training data exposure rather than model architecture, and the paper does not disentangle these. The static assignment constraint (VM-to-PM placement fixed for the entire forecast horizon) is a strong simplification: real consolidation involves rolling decisions with migration costs, and models that underestimate peaks at H=24h may still perform well at H=1h rolling replanning. The simulation uses only 25 test scenarios per dataset after downsampling to 10% of traces — this is a very narrow evaluation window and the absence of variance/confidence bounds on VR is a methodological gap. The paper is a preprint submitted to what appears to be an ACM venue (the header reads "Conference'17" — a template artifact — suggesting it has not yet been through peer review). The paper does not report wall-clock runtimes for the simulation or the Gurobi solver, making computational cost comparisons between optimization methods implicit rather than measured. Bottom Line: Cloud infrastructure engineers and researchers building AIOps scheduling systems will find the decision-misalignment finding and quantile calibration guidelines immediately actionable; the benchmark itself is a useful artifact, though its small simulation scale and lack of statistical significance testing mean findings should be treated as directional rather than definitive.
Citations 2606.13513 PDF
#97 — Innovative (61)

GameCraft-Bench: Can Agents Build Playable Games End-to-End in a Real Game Engine? (36 ▲ )

Tongxu Luo, Rongsheng Wang, Jiaxi Bi, Chenming Xu, Zhengyang Tang, Jianlong Chen, Juhao Liang, Ke Ji, Shuqi Guo, Yuhao Du, Fan Bu, Wenyu Du, Xiaotong Zhang, Kyle Li, Shaobo Wang, Linfeng Zhang, Yuxuan Liu, Xin Lai, Chenxin Li, Yiduo Guo, Zhexin Zhang, Xinyuan Wang, Tianyi Bai, Ziniu Li, Benyou Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GameCraft-Bench, a Godot 4-based benchmark of 140 tasks across 15 game families that evaluates whether coding agents can generate complete, playable games end-to-end from natural-language specifications, judged through replayed interaction traces and multimodal rubric scoring. - Why it's important: Existing game-generation benchmarks either target web games (not a real engine), test only localized edits within existing projects, or assess games via static inspection rather than observed gameplay — none simultaneously enforce engine-native development, a complete launchable artifact, and interaction-based evaluation. - How it does it: Each task packages a game specification, a Godot runtime environment, and a hidden rubric; an agent produces a full Godot project plus replayable JSON interaction traces; a verifier launches the project, replays the traces, samples frames at 2 FPS, and scores the resulting video evidence against the rubric using GPT-5.5 as a multimodal judge. - The good: The benchmark is internally validated — every task's rubric is checked against an oracle (minimal working Godot sketch) authored by the annotator, ensuring specifications are engine-implementable and rubric items are observable from replayed gameplay rather than subjective or untestable. - The missing: The scoring formula weights (0.15M + 0.35D + 0.15V + 0.35A) are asserted without ablation; the same GPT-5.5 model is both the evaluation judge and one of the evaluated agent backbones, creating a potential scoring advantage not controlled for; and human calibration covers only 3 of 15 game families from a single agent. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on coding-agent evaluation or game-generation systems — it provides a concrete instantiation of interaction-grounded evaluation methodology applicable beyond games; practitioners looking for a benchmark to train or fine-tune agents on will find 140 tasks limiting.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (a built benchmark infrastructure). The composite is pulled down by Rigor (58) and Resilience (55): GPT-5.5 serves as both the multimodal judge and one of the evaluated agent backbones (an unacknowledged confound), the scoring weights (0.15/0.35/0.15/0.35) receive no ablation, human calibration covers only 3 of 15 families from a single agent, and agent-submitted traces are a gaming vector. Offsetting strengths are the oracle-validated task authoring and the non-obvious replay-trace evaluation design (Novelty 63, Non-Obviousness 62), plus a publicly released harness (Impact 65). At 140 tasks with 4–5 per small family, family-level findings are high-variance.
systems Novelty 63 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Coding agents are increasingly capable of autonomous software development, but game generation demands more than correct code: a game must launch in an engine, respond to player input, maintain consistent visual state, and deliver a coherent interactive experience. Prior game-generation benchmarks fail to capture this fully. OpenGame-Bench targets web games and uses static page-level judgment. GameDevBench uses Godot but only evaluates localized edits to existing projects via deterministic unit tests, not end-to-end construction. WebGameBench (concurrent) evaluates browser games through interaction but operates outside engine-native development and relies on evaluator-side exploration to discover gameplay. The open question is whether coding agents can transform a natural-language game specification into a complete, self-contained, engine-native game artifact whose correctness is verified through actual player–game interaction. Key Idea: The central insight is that end-to-end game generation evaluation must constrain three spaces simultaneously: the development space (where the agent builds — a real engine, not a browser sandbox), the output space (what it delivers — a complete launchable project, not isolated scripts), and the evaluation space (how it is judged — observed behavior under replayed player input, not static source inspection). The operationalization trick that makes interaction-based evaluation tractable without autonomous game exploration is requiring the agent itself to submit replayable JSON traces alongside the game project; the verifier replays these fixed traces rather than having to discover how each game should be played, keeping evaluation deterministic and comparable across diverse open-ended tasks. Methodology: Each task is defined as τ = (s, E, ρ): a natural-language game specification s, a Godot 4 runtime environment E = (R, W, A, C) (runtime, workspace, shared assets, submission contract), and a hidden rubric ρ. The agent observes (s, E) but not ρ, and must produce (G, Π): a complete Godot project G and up to 10 replayable interaction traces Π. The five-stage pipeline is: (1) Task Packaging — task authored with aligned public specification and hidden rubric decomposed into Core Mechanics (M), Content Depth (D), Functional Visuals (V), Art & Presentation (A) items, validated by annotator oracle; (2) Agent Generation — agent iterates with Godot tools for up to 7200 seconds; (3) Build Gate — if the project fails headless launch or no trace can be parsed, BUILD=0 and final score=0; (4) Replay — each trace replayed in a fresh 1280×720 Godot instance, recorded at 30 FPS, frames sampled at 2 FPS; (5) Scoring — GPT-5.5 as multimodal judge scores each demo against rubric items (0–1 scale); per-item aggregation uses max for scenario-specific requirements and mean for persistent properties; final score = BUILD × (0.15M + 0.35D + 0.15V + 0.35A). Content Depth and Art & Presentation are weighted 0.35 each (higher than Mechanics and Visuals at 0.15 each) to penalize minimal-mechanic prototypes. The benchmark uses Godot 4.6.2 on Ubuntu 22.04 with Xvfb for headless display. Rubric items are capped at 24 per task. The annotation process pairs each public specification with a hidden rubric and an oracle solution to verify internal consistency. Key Results: Seven frontier agent configurations were evaluated across all 140 tasks. Claude Code with Opus-4.7 (high) achieves 41.46% overall (55.34% mechanics / 39.48% depth / 42.78% visuals / 36.86% art), the highest result. Codex with GPT-5.5 (high) reaches 39.49%, Kimi Code with Kimi-K2.6 reaches 30.65%, MiMo-V2.5-Pro scores 24.10%, GLM-5.1 scores 18.29%, MiniMax-M2.7 scores 10.95%, and DeepSeek-V4-Pro collapses to 2.15% due to systematic failure to submit valid traces (build pass rate 25.6%). Most strong agents achieve build pass rates of 93–100% and valid trace rates of 96–100%, demonstrating that the low overall scores reflect rubric failures, not build failures. Core Mechanics scores are consistently 10–15 pp higher than Content Depth and Art & Presentation scores across all agents, indicating agents more often implement recognizable local mechanics but fail to expand them into complete, visually coherent experiences. Family-level variation is high: Opus-4.7 scores 57.30% on Horror but only 33.78% on Card Game. Judge stability on fixed evidence is very high: std dev ≤ 0.005 across 10 repeated GPT-5.5 runs. Human calibration on three families (Card Game, Idle, Racing) shows the judge is 3.32 pp more permissive on average, with the largest gap in Idle (+8.76 pp). Tool usage volume is uncorrelated with task quality (r = +0.016 for MiMo-V2.5-Pro). Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark is the first to jointly satisfy all three desiderata (engine-native Godot development, complete artifact delivery, and interaction-grounded evaluation via agent-submitted replay traces) for end-to-end game generation. The replay-trace mechanism for avoiding autonomous game-exploration during evaluation is a non-obvious design choice. The empirical finding that mechanics and presentation capabilities are partially factorized (Art & Presentation correlates with Functional Visuals at r = 0.11 for Kimi-K2.6) is a genuine observation rather than assumed. The rest — rubric-guided multimodal judging, MDA-framework-grounded evaluation categories, headless Godot execution — are competent applications of existing ideas to a new evaluation setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: Every task includes an oracle Godot solution authored by the annotator to verify specification implementability and rubric observability, which is a creditable quality-control step absent from most benchmarks. Judge stability is formally measured (10-run repeat with fixed evidence; std dev < 0.005, well below inter-agent performance gaps). Human calibration is preliminary and narrow: three game families, one agent (Kimi-K2.6), no inter-annotator agreement reported, and the evaluation subset is not designed to estimate agreement. The scoring-weight choice (0.15/0.35/0.15/0.35) is stated as a design decision but receives no ablation. Code and data are stated to be available at the project website. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.17861v1, June 2026); results have not undergone peer review. A notable confound: GPT-5.5 is both the multimodal judge for all agents and one of the evaluated agent backbones — whether GPT-5.5's judge scores its own game outputs at the same calibration as other agents' outputs is not tested. Deployability (4-property check): SYSTEMS profile — GameCraft-Bench is a built benchmark infrastructure evaluated by deployment. - Practical: Runs on standard Ubuntu 22.04 with Godot 4.6.2, Xvfb, xdotool, and ffmpeg; no GPU required for the benchmark itself; the agent models are frontier API services, not locally deployed. - Efficient: Each task allows agents up to 7200 seconds and verifiers up to 1800 seconds; running 7 agents × 140 tasks involves substantial compute; costs are not reported in the paper. - Effective: The build gate, replay infrastructure, rubric framework, and oracle-validated annotations together produce a benchmark that differentiates agents substantially (41.46% vs. 2.15%) and is stable under repeated evaluation, suggesting it measures something real about end-to-end game generation capability. - Scalable: The benchmark is limited to 140 tasks and 15 families (some with as few as 4–5 tasks); whether family-level conclusions hold at larger task counts is not tested; multi-engine extension is explicitly deferred to future work. The paper delivers a complete software artifact (benchmark harness, task suite, evaluation pipeline) available publicly. Strengths: - The oracle-validation step — requiring annotators to implement a working Godot sketch before finalizing each task's specification and rubric — is a principled and unusual quality control measure that guards against untestable or underspecified tasks. - The replay-trace design cleanly decouples evaluation from autonomous game exploration: agents submit fixed interaction evidence, making judging deterministic and game-type-agnostic without requiring a general game-playing agent as verifier. - The decomposition finding (mechanics/content/visuals are moderately coupled, art is weakly coupled) provides a diagnostic signal not available from a single aggregate score, and it holds consistently across two different agents. - The diagnostic analysis of tool-usage patterns (MiMo-V2.5-Pro's bash-heavy, write-first pattern with r = +0.016 score correlation) provides actionable evidence about agent failure modes beyond raw leaderboard numbers. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state that the benchmark is restricted to 2D Godot games and does not cover Unity, Unreal, 3D games, multiplayer, large-scale physics, or long-form production workflows. They acknowledge that audio-dependent game qualities (rhythm, horror, sports) are evaluated only through visual proxies. They note that the multimodal judge may be affected by model bias, API drift, or visual understanding failures. They explicitly state the benchmark does not measure subjective fun, only specification adherence. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The GPT-5.5 model is simultaneously the evaluation judge and an evaluated agent backbone; if GPT-5.5 generates games whose visual style or structure is more legible to GPT-5.5 than to a neutral judge, its reported scores may be inflated relative to competitors. This confound is not acknowledged. - The scoring weights (0.15/0.35/0.15/0.35) are unjustified empirically; an ablation showing how ranking order changes under alternative weights would establish whether conclusions are robust to this choice. - Human calibration covers only Kimi-K2.6 submissions from 3 families; inter-annotator agreement on the rubric items themselves is not reported, leaving open whether different human raters would agree on the rubric scores. - Agent-submitted traces introduce a potential gaming vector: an agent can craft traces that avoid areas of the game where it knows failures exist, showing only the most favorable gameplay evidence. The paper does not analyze whether agents strategically under-exercise parts of their games. - With 4–5 tasks in the smallest families (Idle, Racing, Sports, Card, Horror, Rhythm), family-level scores have very high variance; the finding that Opus-4.7 scores 57.30% on Horror (5 tasks) should not be treated as robust. Bottom Line: Practitioners working on coding-agent evaluation or game-generation systems will find the benchmark design — particularly the oracle-validated task authoring and replay-trace evaluation protocol — worth studying as a methodology transferable to other interactive software generation settings, even if the benchmark's 140-task scope and Godot-only coverage limit its immediate use as a training signal.
Recommended 2606.17861 PDF
#98 — Innovative (61)

HarnessX: A Composable, Adaptive, and Evolvable Agent Harness Foundry (33 ▲ )

Tingyang Chen, Shuo Lu, Kang Zhao, Weicheng Meng, Hanlin Teng, Tianhao Li, Chao Li, Xule Liu, Jian Liang, Zhizhong Zhang, Yuan Xie, Heng Qu, Kun Shao, Jian Luan · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces HarnessX, a runtime foundry that formalizes the agent harness as a typed, composable first-class object and evolves it automatically from execution traces using a four-stage multi-agent engine (AEGIS), with an optional co-evolution loop that simultaneously updates the task model via cross-harness GRPO. - Why it's important: Current agent harnesses are hand-crafted, architecturally entangled, and static — every model or task change requires bespoke re-engineering, and execution data never feeds back into systematic improvement, leaving substantial performance on the table especially for weaker models. - How it does it: It maps harness adaptation onto a Markov Decision Process over symbolic artifacts (the "operational mirror"), then defends against the resulting RL pathologies (reward hacking, catastrophic forgetting, under-exploration) via a Digester→Planner→Evolver→Critic pipeline gated by a deterministic seesaw constraint that rejects any edit regressing a previously solved task. - The good: On ALFWorld, Qwen3.5-9B rises from 53.0% to 97.0% (+44.0%) across 9 evolution rounds, and the variant-isolation ablation on GAIA lifts GPT-5.4 from stagnation (∆=0.0) to 87.4% (+13.6%, non-degrading), empirically validating the catastrophic-forgetting prediction. - The missing: All reported gains — including the headline +14.5% average — are measured on the same fixed task set used for evolution; no held-out generalization is evaluated, so the numbers carry Goodhart's Law risk and cannot be distinguished from overfitting to the adaptation set. - Read it or not: Read it if you build or study agent scaffolding infrastructure; the MDP formalization, nine-dimensional taxonomy, and AEGIS pathology analysis provide a more principled design vocabulary than most prior harness work, but treat the numeric claims with appropriate skepticism pending held-out evaluation.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Novelty (75) and Non-Obviousness (75) are high — the 'operational mirror' formalizing harness adaptation as a symbolic MDP, with pathology defenses (seesaw constraint, variant isolation, cross-harness GRPO) derived from RL theory, is a more principled abstraction than prior harness work, and Completeness (65) reflects unusually honest self-assessment (e.g., admitting AEGIS gives no accuracy gain over a single-agent evolver). Resilience (48) and Rigor (50) are the drag: the paper explicitly reports ALL gains on the same task set used for evolution, uses PEAK rather than final accuracy (GAIA Global peaks 73.8% then collapses to 49.5%), releases no code, and depends on a closed Opus meta-agent — the headline +14.5% cannot be distinguished from overfitting. Weighted: 0.15*75 + 0.15*50 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*48 + 0.30*60 = 61.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 65 Resilience 48 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Agent performance depends not only on the underlying model but critically on the surrounding runtime harness — the prompts, tool definitions, memory policies, and control flow that mediate how a model observes, reasons, and acts. Current harnesses suffer three structural deficits: they are hand-engineered and static, lacking any experience-driven update mechanism; they entangle concerns (prompt templates, tool wrappers, retry logic, memory) so that a change to one component silently breaks others; and harness engineering and model training are decoupled, so trajectory data accumulated while improving the harness is discarded rather than converted into model training signal. The gap is acute for weaker or smaller models, which exhibit more behavioral gaps addressable at the harness level, and for heterogeneous task distributions where a single harness configuration cannot simultaneously serve conflicting subtask requirements. Key Idea: The central insight is that harness adaptation structurally mirrors reinforcement learning in a symbolic space: harness configurations are states, typed code-level edits are actions, and execution traces plus verifier scores constitute feedback. This "operational mirror" is not merely an analogy — it converts RL pathologies (reward hacking, catastrophic forgetting, under-exploration) into concrete design requirements and motivates specific architectural defenses. Because harness components are typed and substitutable, edits are scoped and isolated, enabling variant-level seesaw constraints that prevent local gains from producing global regressions. A reader who grasps this MDP correspondence can reconstruct the entire AEGIS pipeline from first principles. Methodology: HarnessX has three layers. Composition: a harness is formalized as H = (M, C), where M is a model configuration and C = (P, S) is a hook-indexed pipeline of typed processors plus shared slot resources. Processors attach to eight lifecycle hooks (task_start, step_start, before_model, after_model, before_tool, after_tool, step_end, task_end) and satisfy a type contract (input event type = output event type per hook), enforced at runtime. A nine-dimensional taxonomy spans model selection, context assembly, memory management, tool ecosystem, execution environment, evaluation/reward, control/safety, observability, and training bridge. Adaptation (AEGIS): a four-stage pipeline driven by a single meta-agent (Opus 4.6 throughout) runs iteratively: the Digester compresses raw traces (∼10M tokens per GAIA round) into structured per-task summaries; the Planner constructs an adaptation landscape spanning both incremental and structural directions; the Evolver produces typed builder edits with change manifests and smoke tests; and the Critic, together with a deterministic gate, applies a seesaw constraint (no regression on previously solved tasks) before shipping. Stages are selectively invoked based on continuation conditions; only the Critic and gate are unconditional. A variant-isolation extension maintains up to K harness variants and routes tasks to the best-matching variant, scoping the seesaw constraint per-variant to prevent cross-cluster forgetting. Co-evolution: trajectories enter a shared FIFO replay buffer; AEGIS performs a non-parametric harness edit while cross-harness GRPO takes a parametric model update, grouping trajectories by task identity across all harness versions (Gx = {τ ∈ B | task(τ) = x}) to compute group-relative advantages (Âᵢ = (rᵢ − μ(Gx))/(σ(Gx) + ε)). Behavior log-probabilities are cached at buffer insertion to enable off-policy training without additional rollouts. Key design decisions: (1) the seesaw constraint is deterministic rather than LLM-judged — justified by the need for provable safety properties; (2) Digester compression before downstream LLM stages — justified by the ∼10M-to-10K compression ratio; (3) cross-harness grouping by task identity rather than action space — justified by the need to handle incompatible tool schemas across harness versions. Key Results: Across 15 model–benchmark configurations, AEGIS improves 14 of 15, averaging +14.5% absolute pass@2 (peak, on adaptation set). ALFWorld gains: Sonnet 4.6 83.6%→94.8% (+11.2%), GPT-5.4 76.9%→97.8% (+20.9%), Qwen3.5-9B 53.0%→97.0% (+44.0%). WebShop: +13.0% to +18.0%. SWE-bench Verified: +10.9% to +18.2%. GAIA: Sonnet 4.6 +9.7%, GPT-5.4 stagnates at 0.0% under Global (single harness), but +13.6% (87.4%) under Ensemble variant isolation. τ³-Bench: +1.1% to +14.5% (Qwen3.5 near-ceiling at 93.5%). Baselines are a handcrafted static harness and a CC SDK single-agent evolver sharing the same infrastructure; the CC SDK comparison (GAIA, GPT-5.4, 15 rounds) shows AEGIS achieves 87.4% vs. CC SDK 86.4% — a 1.0% gap within one standard error (∼3.3% at n=103) — meaning the four-stage architecture provides efficiency (+12% fewer tokens) and auditability but not measurable accuracy gain at this model capability level. Co-evolution adds +4.7% on average over harness-only on GAIA (37.4%→41.7%) and WebShop (49.0%→54.0%) using Qwen3.5-9B. The inverse-scaling finding — that weaker models benefit most — is consistent across all benchmarks. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formalization of the harness as a typed first-class MDP object and the operational mirror framework are genuinely novel as a unified abstraction; prior work (DSPy, OPRO, EvoPrompt, AFlow) optimizes subsets of harness components without a unifying symbolic-MDP grounding. The seesaw constraint with variant isolation, cross-harness GRPO, and the deterministic decoupling of LLM judgment from shipping decisions are new engineering contributions.
