The empirical finding that harness evolution gains are inversely proportional to baseline model strength — and that a single Opus-class meta-agent can evolve effective harnesses across three heterogeneous model families — is a substantive empirical contribution. What is standard: the individual RL components (GRPO, importance-sampling replay buffers) and most LLM tooling building blocks. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: the Global vs. Ensemble strategy comparison (Table 5) directly validates the catastrophic-forgetting prediction; the AEGIS vs. CC SDK comparison (Table 6) isolates architecture from infrastructure; the co-evolution experiment (Figure 5) isolates the joint-optimization gain. However, several methodological concerns are material. First, the paper explicitly states "all reported gains are measured on the same task set used for evolution" and reports peak accuracy — introducing both Goodhart's Law risk and selection bias (the peak–final gap on GAIA Global is −24.3%, and the SWE-bench GPT-5.4 peak degrades 12.7% by R5). Second, SWE-bench uses only 55 tasks (each flip is ∼1.8% of aggregate score) and τ³-Bench covers only 3 domains, limiting statistical power. Third, pass@2 masks sub-threshold regression accumulation — a structural limitation the paper acknowledges but does not resolve. Fourth, the meta-agent is always Opus 4.6 (closed-source), making replication contingent on API access; open-weight meta-agents are explicitly listed as untested. Fifth, the codebase is not yet released ("will be open-sourced in a future release"), and trajectory files are referenced but not included. Sixth, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed results are reported for the main Table 4 outcomes; only the GAIA binomial 95% CI (±8.5%) is mentioned, to rule out noise in the −24.3% Global collapse. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built tool, infrastructure, and runtime, evaluated by deployment across benchmarks). - Practical: The harness composition layer and AEGIS runtime require only standard cloud compute; the meta-agent requires a capable frontier model (Opus 4.6 used throughout), which is commercially accessible but expensive. Co-evolution requires 8×H100 GPUs for model training. - Efficient: Meta-agent token budgets range from 43.4M (ALFWorld, 7 rounds) to 175M tokens per run, plus all task-agent rollout tokens. These are non-trivial costs; the paper estimates GAIA upfront investment amortizes over ∼1,300 invocations (∼83K tokens saved per task). ALFWorld per-task cost rises ∼60% post-evolution, so cost-benefit is benchmark-dependent. - Effective: Evidence that the method addresses real failures is strong for ALFWorld and GAIA (case studies, lever effectiveness heatmaps, falsifiable manifests). However, all effectiveness claims are measured on the adaptation set, so they conflate genuine generalization with harness overfitting. - Scalable: Tested on task sets of 55–134 tasks and up to 15 evolution rounds. No held-out test, no deployment at larger task distribution. The paper explicitly warns that variant isolation "may not extend to longer horizons (where variants may over-specialize) or to task distributions whose inter-task dependencies prevent clean variant separation." The system delivers a runtime framework and evolution engine; model weights are not released, and the codebase is promised for future release. Strengths: - The operational mirror formalization — treating harness configurations as MDP states, typed edits as actions, and deriving pathology defenses from RL theory — is a conceptually coherent and practically useful contribution that prior harness-evolution work lacked. - The seesaw constraint plus deterministic gating cleanly decouples LLM judgment (which may fail) from shipping decisions (which are provably non-regressive per-task under pass@2), a principled safety design. - The AEGIS vs. CC SDK ablation is an unusually honest self-assessment: the authors directly report that their four-stage architecture provides no measurable accuracy gain over a single-agent evolver, only efficiency and interpretability benefits. This calibrates expectations honestly. - The failure analysis (Section 6.6) with three concrete case studies demonstrating reward hacking, catastrophic forgetting, and under-exploration — each matching a predicted pathology — strengthens the operational mirror framework beyond analogy. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: - No held-out evaluation: all gains are on the adaptation task set, with explicit acknowledgment of selection bias and potential overfitting. - Discrete action spaces only: not tested on continuous-action domains. - Closed-source meta-agent: open-weight alternatives untested. - Joint control assumption: co-evolution requires shared access to harness evolution and model training, often separated across teams. - Benchmark coverage: SWE-bench uses a 55-task subsample; τ³-Bench covers only three domains; inverse-scaling conclusions may not generalize. - Sub-threshold coupling is a structural gap in per-edit gating, evidenced by the τ³-Bench Telecom −14.0% regression that accumulated undetected across 5 rounds. The paper confronts these limitations directly and at length (Section 7.7), which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The headline average of +14.5% reports PEAK accuracy across rounds, not final accuracy. The Global strategy's GAIA GPT-5.4 result shows peak 73.8% but final 49.5% (−24.3%); the headline tables use peak for all configurations, so the aggregate +14.5% overstates durable improvement. The paper does not report a peak-averaged gain for the Ensemble strategy across all benchmarks. - The CC SDK baseline is a single LLM session per round — not a multi-round optimizer with the same trace history as AEGIS. A fairer comparison would give CC SDK full access to the cross-round trace store, not just the current round. The 1.0% gap might shrink or reverse with this correction. - The inverse-scaling claim is computed across different models on the same benchmarks, confounding model capability with benchmark starting points (Qwen3.5-9B starts at 53% on ALFWorld vs. 83.6% for Sonnet 4.6 — the gap is partly ceiling effect). - The co-evolution experiment uses only Qwen3.5-9B on two benchmarks (GAIA and WebShop) and the training hyperparameters (no KL penalty, 5 training steps per round) are unusual enough to warrant more thorough ablation; the +4.7% gain may not transfer to stronger base models or other tasks. - The seesaw constraint uses pass@2 binary signals, which the paper shows are insufficient to detect sub-threshold coupling. The authors note this but offer no mitigation beyond variant isolation, which itself depends on clean cluster separation. Bottom Line: HarnessX makes a genuine contribution by formalizing harness adaptation as a principled MDP problem and providing a working system with honest self-assessment of its limitations, but the headline gains should be treated as upper bounds — they are peak accuracy on training tasks with no held-out test — making the paper essential reading for agent infrastructure researchers while its numerical claims await held-out validation.
Recommended 2606.14249 PDF
#99 — Innovative (60)

Perceive, Interact, Reason: Building Tool-Augmented Visual Agents for Spatial Reasoning

C Li, M Lu, Y Wu, L Zhu · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PERIA (PERception-Interaction-reason Agent), a tool-augmented visual agent that combines an 18-tool sandbox with a two-stage training recipe — GPT-5-synthesized SFT trajectories followed by OR-GIGPO reinforcement learning — to teach VLMs to actively acquire and compose spatial evidence across map reasoning, visual probing, and vision reconstruction tasks. - Why it's important: Open-source VLMs given raw tool access frequently perform worse than without tools (Qwen3 series degrades by up to 17.6%), meaning spatial reasoning bottlenecks cannot be solved by tool access alone; they require a trained policy for tool selection, invocation, and feedback integration. - How it does it: The core mechanism is Observation-Relaxed Group-in-Group Policy Optimization (OR-GIGPO), which replaces exact anchor-state matching in GIGPO with SequenceMatcher-based similarity grouping (δ=0.9) to handle non-deterministic visual tool outputs, enabling meaningful step-level credit assignment across multi-turn trajectories. - The good: PERIA-8B outperforms comparably sized tool-augmented baselines by 7.0%–14.8% across 13 benchmarks and matches Qwen3-235B and GPT-5 in overall average accuracy despite a ~30× parameter disadvantage. - The missing: Backbone validation is limited to ≤8B due to computational constraints; OOD gains average only 4.4%, with several individual tasks showing near-zero improvement, leaving generalization claims undersubstantiated. - Read it or not: Researchers building agentic visual systems or studying tool-use RL should read it — the motivating failure analysis (Section 3) and OR-GIGPO algorithm are substantive contributions; practitioners seeking a production-ready spatial reasoning system should note the absence of latency benchmarks, released weights, or large-scale validation.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (58) is supported by well-structured ablations (RL-algorithm, scale, SFT-vs-RL, tool-family) and a quantitative 300-case failure taxonomy, but undercut by single-run numbers, in-distribution Goodhart risk, and a conflated GPT-5 distillation/baseline relationship. Non-Obviousness (65) is earned by OR-GIGPO's targeted fix for non-deterministic tool observations (9.3%→62.3% valid step-advantage). Novelty (62) covers a NEW SYSTEM + method. Impact (55) is capped by unreleased weights, absent latency data, and weak/heterogeneous OOD gains (+4.4%). The motivating tool-use-policy-gap analysis is a standalone strength, but the distillation confound limits resilience.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Spatial reasoning tasks — subway-map route tracing, path drawing on maps, visual probing in cluttered scenes, multi-view 3D reasoning — require models to build coherent spatial state that links entities across image regions and supports topology-dependent inference. These tasks cannot be solved by reading a local text span or detecting isolated objects, unlike chart understanding or mathematical QA where mature expert tools suffice. Existing tool-augmented VLMs use limited tool spaces or domain-specific training data that fails to transfer to broader spatial domains. The critical empirical observation motivating the work: when Qwen3-VL-Thinking models (2B–235B) are given direct access to 18 perception and interaction tools, they degrade on average by 3.2%–17.6% compared to no-tool baselines. Analysis of 300 failure cases where GPT-5 succeeds but Qwen3 fails reveals that 59%–72% of errors are tool-call omissions (models skip needed tools) and 25%–61% are tool-induced errors (models are misled by tool output during reasoning), not formatting failures. The gap is a policy-learning problem, not a tool availability problem. Key Idea The single core insight is that visual tool observations are non-deterministic — semantically equivalent tool calls (e.g., two OCR calls on the same map region) may return slightly different text orderings or localization descriptions — making exact anchor-state matching, as used in GIGPO and similar RL methods, too restrictive to form meaningful step-level comparison groups. OR-GIGPO replaces exact hash matching with relaxed grouping: image-returning tools use exact matching (deterministic outputs), while text-returning tool agents additionally merge groups whose SequenceMatcher similarity exceeds δ=0.9. This increases the fraction of steps with valid step-level advantage signal from 9.3% (original GIGPO) to 62.3% (OR-GIGPO), enabling fine-grained credit assignment across multi-step tool-use trajectories. Methodology Three-stage construction pipeline: Stage 1 — Tool Sandbox: 18 tools split into two families. Visual perception tools (12) extract structured evidence: text_ocr, text_spotting, map_text_ocr, formula_ocr, table_ocr (all powered by PaddleOCR-VL-1.5 at 0.9B), grounding_dino (GroundingDINO base, box/text thresholds 0.25, NMS 0.8), auto_segment/bbox_segment/text_segment/exemplar_segment/concept_count/presence_check (SAM3.1, score threshold 0.25, ≤20 proposals). Visual interaction tools (6) perform deterministic image manipulation returning modified images: image_crop, image_label, draw_line, draw_path, bounding_box, image_highlight. All coordinates are normalized in a 1000×1000 space. Stage 2 — SFT Trajectory Synthesis: GPT-5 generates tool-use trajectories via explore-and-exploit sampling (nsample=3 per round, max T=11 turns). Round 1 forces a tool call; rounds 2–T-1 allow tool call or answer; rejected answers trigger forced self-reflection with a different tool. Correct trajectories are validated against task-specific criteria (rule-based + LLM-judge fallback for QA; NDTW for MapTrace). Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Thinking then diversifies only intermediate reasoning text while keeping tool calls, parameters, observations, and answers fixed. Total SFT data: 10,661 samples (SFT), 8,652 samples (RL). Stage 3 — OR-GIGPO RL Training: POMDP formulation with composite trajectory-level reward R(τ) = R_rep + R_format + R_correct. R_rep applies turn-normalized penalties for character/word/span repetition (−3.0 to −1.0). R_format gives +1.0 for valid tagged trajectories, 0.0 for truncated, −1.0 otherwise. R_correct is binary (with GPT-5-Mini judge fallback) or continuous NDTW-based for MapTrace. Episode-level advantage A_E normalizes trajectory rewards within same-input rollout groups. Step-level advantage A_S normalizes discounted returns (γ=0.99) within observation-relaxed anchor groups. Final advantage A_t = A_E(τ_i) + ω·A_S(a_t), ω=1.0. Optimized with PPO-style clipped objective (ε_clip=0.2), no explicit KL penalty. Training: lr=1e-6, batch 64, group N=4, 3 epochs. Key design decisions: (1) Observation-relaxed matching — well-justified by the 9.3%→62.3% step advantage ratio improvement. (2) SFT before RL — well-justified by w/o SFT ablation (overall 33.3% vs. 44.4% for full model). (3) Spatial-specific tool design vs. generic tools — justified by prior-tool-set ablation (47.6% vs. 54.1%). Key Results Evaluated across 13 benchmarks from 8 datasets, PERIA-8B achieves 54.1% in-distribution average (+10.0% over Qwen3-8B backbone at 44.1%) and 36.1% OOD average (+4.4% over 31.7% backbone). Against same-scale baselines: +7.0%–14.8% over VTool-R1-7B (30.2%), R1-OneVision-7B (29.6%), Mini-o3-7B (37.1%), InternVL3.5-8B (36.6%). Overall 44.4% average approaches GPT-5 (46.8%) and Qwen3-235B (44.3%). Largest gains on in-distribution tasks: ReasonMap +14.8% (12.3%→27.1%), VisualProbe Hard +17.7% (26.6%→44.3%), VisualProbe Medium +9.9%. OOD gains are modest: Cube 3-View +4.7%, V* +4.7%, BabyVision +2.5%, Ball Tracking +1.0%. MapTrace at stricter NDTW threshold 0.3: PERIA-8B achieves 44.1%, best among all models including GPT-5 (41.8%). Consistent gains at 2B (35.9%→39.3%) and 4B (41.2%→48.8%). RL algorithm comparison: OR-GIGPO outperforms GRPO by 2.4%, GIGPO by 2.8%, DAPO by 11.1% on in-distribution average. Baselines include current frontier models (GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro/Flash, Qwen3-VL-235B) and current open-source tool-augmented systems — strong and recent. All tests on standard benchmark scale (thousands of examples); no stress-scale evaluation. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The PERIA system (tool sandbox + training recipe) is a new artifact. OR-GIGPO is a novel modification of GIGPO with a technically motivated improvement (observation-relaxed grouping) backed by ablations. Genuinely novel: the relaxed observation matching for visual RL, the spatial-specific tool design (draw_path, map_text_ocr), and the empirical characterization of the tool-use policy gap. Standard combination of existing ideas: the ReAct-style perceive-act-observe loop, PPO-family policy optimization, SFT-then-RL training sequence, and trajectory diversification are all known; PERIA applies them to the new target of spatial visual tool use. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and well-structured: RL algorithm comparison (GRPO/GIGPO/DAPO/OR-GIGPO), model scale (2B/4B/8B), SFT vs. RL vs. both, and inference-time tool family removal. The failure mode analysis on 300 cases provides quantitative grounding for the main motivation. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed results are reported anywhere; all numbers are single-run. No public code, weights, or model repository is mentioned. Methodological concerns: (1) SFT trajectories are generated by GPT-5, which is also a primary baseline — the PERIA vs. GPT-5 comparison partially conflates knowledge distillation with architectural improvement, not disentangled. (2) In-distribution evaluation uses the same benchmark families (MapTrace, ReasonMap, Visual Probing) as training data sources, with only train/test splits separating them — modest Goodhart's Law risk. (3) OOD tasks show heterogeneous gains, and training data provenance (Mini-o3-Coldstart-Dataset, DeepEyes) is not checked for overlap with OOD benchmarks. (4) The LLM judge used in evaluation is GPT-5-Mini, introducing potential measurement noise that is unquantified. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.12830v1); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: PERIA-8B runs on commercially available GPU hardware (vLLM inference); the tool sandbox requires three concurrently loaded auxiliary models (PaddleOCR-VL-1.5 at 0.9B, GroundingDINO base, SAM3.1), all publicly available. Practical for research deployment with sufficient GPU memory. - Efficient: Inference cost is substantially higher than single-pass VLM inference: up to 11 tool-call turns, each invoking one or more auxiliary models. Per-query latency and total memory footprint are not reported, making cost comparison to baselines impossible. - Effective: Evidence shows PERIA solves spatial reasoning tasks better than comparable baselines across 13 diverse benchmarks. The in-distribution/OOD gap (10.0% vs. 4.4%) indicates the method partially specializes to its training domains. - Scalable: Tested only at 2B, 4B, 8B backbone scales; the paper explicitly defers larger-backbone validation to future work. Performance at 30B+ scales is unknown; no extrapolation evidence is provided. The work produces a trained model artifact (PERIA-8B) and a replicable training recipe but does not release code, weights, or the synthesized trajectory dataset. Strengths - The motivating empirical study in Section 3 is a standalone contribution: it cleanly quantifies that tool access without tool-use training hurts open-source VLMs (−17.6% for Qwen3-2B, −8.1% for Qwen3-30B), with a typed failure taxonomy (tool-call omission, tool-induced error) on 300 analyzed cases, grounding the problem in evidence rather than assumption. - OR-GIGPO solves a concrete technical problem (visual tool observations are non-deterministic, breaking exact anchor matching) with a targeted fix (SequenceMatcher similarity grouping) that dramatically changes the fraction of steps receiving step-level gradient signal from 9.3% to 62.3%, with performance consequences shown by direct comparison to GIGPO. - The tool design is spatially motivated rather than generic: draw_path (route tracing), map_text_ocr (noise-filtered map text extraction), and text_spotting (spatially-grounded OCR) are purpose-built for the domain, and the ablation showing generic prior tools reach only 47.6% vs. 54.1% for the full sandbox validates the design choices empirically. - The SFT trajectory synthesis procedure is carefully engineered: forced exploration on round 1, self-reflection triggers after rejected answers, thinking-style diversification while preserving tool behavior, and task-specific validation — producing a training pipeline that avoids common pitfalls of reward hacking or reasoning collapse. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: (1) main experiments are limited to 8B backbone due to computational constraints; larger-scale validation and study of trajectory data and tool-diversity scaling are deferred; (2) inference is capped at 10 interaction turns, which may be insufficient for longer-horizon spatial tasks requiring stronger memory and exploration. They also identify future targets: 3D reasoning, maze navigation, Sokoban-like planning, embodied tasks, and larger trajectory datasets. These are stated forthrightly and reflect genuine scope boundaries rather than minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The SFT trajectories are generated by GPT-5, the same model used as a primary baseline, creating an implicit knowledge distillation advantage. The w/o SFT ablation (33.3% vs. 44.4%) cannot distinguish whether gains come from the PERIA training recipe or from learning to mimic GPT-5's tool-use strategy — the paper does not ablate with trajectories from a weaker source model. - OOD gains are weak and heterogeneous: Ball Tracking (+1.0%), BabyVision (+2.5%), Paper Folding (+4.7% but from a low 11.0% base). Several OOD improvements are within the noise of a single-run evaluation. The paper's OOD claim would be more credible with significance testing or multiple-run variance estimates. - Inference latency is entirely absent from the paper. PERIA runs up to 11 turns with 3 auxiliary models per query; wall-clock comparison to single-pass GPT-5 or Qwen3-235B is not reported. For a practical system, cost-efficiency tradeoffs are essential and cannot be inferred from accuracy alone. - The training data includes Mini-o3-Coldstart-Dataset and DeepEyes data, whose content overlap with OOD evaluation benchmarks (V*, BabyVision, ball tracking) is not documented, creating unquantified contamination risk. - The paper does not evaluate on any real-world spatial deployment (e.g., navigation, robotics), restricting claims of practical impact to structured benchmark performance. Bottom Line A practitioner building visual agents for spatial understanding will extract concrete, reusable value from this paper — particularly the tool-use policy gap analysis, OR-GIGPO algorithm, and spatial tool design — but should not interpret the 8B-scale benchmark results as evidence of production-readiness given the absent latency data, unreleased artifacts, and weak OOD generalization.
Search Results 2606.12830 PDF
#100 — Innovative (60)

S-Agent: Spatial Tool-Use Elicits Reasoning for Spatial Intelligence (27 ▲ )

Yalun Dai, Hao Li, Shulin Tian, Runmao Yao, Yuhao Dong, Fangzhou Hong, Zhaoxi Chen, Fangfu Liu, Baoliang Tian, Dingwen Zhang, Tao Wang, Kim-Hui Yap, Ziwei Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces S-AGENT, a spatial tool-use agentic framework that reformulates video and multi-view spatial reasoning as iterative spatio-temporal evidence accumulation, using a three-level hierarchy of spatial tools and a dual memory system rather than single-shot VLM inference. - Why it's important: VLMs deployed on spatial tasks (robotics, AR/VR, autonomous driving) fail because they make stateless, frame-level predictions that cannot maintain object identity across viewpoints, integrate metric measurements over time, or resolve partial occlusions through evidence from complementary views. - How it does it: A VLM semantic planner iteratively requests evidence from hierarchical tools (2D detection/grounding → metric 3D lifting via Depth-Anything-3 → task-specific expert aggregation for counting, measurement, orientation, and relative position), while a Scene Memory consolidates persistent spatial facts and an Agent Memory records reasoning history to prevent redundant or contradictory tool calls. - The good: Trajectory distillation from S-AGENT into Qwen3-VL-8B (S-AGENT-8B) achieves 41.6% on MMSI-Bench versus 31.1% for the unaugmented base model (+10.5% absolute), while matching GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3 Pro despite being a compact open-weight model. - The missing: Inference latency and per-question compute cost of the multi-round, multi-tool pipeline are never reported, making it impossible to assess the practical cost of the accuracy gains versus a single VLM forward pass. - Read it or not: Researchers and engineers building spatial reasoning agents over video or multi-view inputs should read this — the dual-memory design and expert-mediated 3D evidence hierarchy are validated, concrete advances; those focused on single-image or text-only tasks can skip it.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (58) rests on well-designed Table-5 ablations and the non-obvious finding that raw Level-2 3D evidence adds only +0.8pp while expert-mediated Level-3 adds +7.7pp (Non-Obviousness 65), plus an honest tool-hurts-without-training negative result; but single-run numbers, a single-planner/single-benchmark ablation, and a narrow 1.2pp MMSI margin without significance testing cap it. Novelty (62) covers a NEW SYSTEM + dataset. Impact (58) is held back by entirely unreported inference cost (likely 5–20× a single pass) and a closed-source GPT-5.4 teacher dependency that blocks reproduction of the distillation path.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation VLMs are trained on passive 2D image-text corpora and produce answers from isolated visual inputs in a single forward pass. This creates a fundamental semantic-to-geometric gap: questions that require integrating evidence across time (e.g., counting objects that appear in different frames, measuring absolute distances, inferring relative positions from multiple viewpoints) cannot be reliably answered from any single frame. Prior tool-augmented agents (VADAR, SpaceTools, GCA) added geometric tools but still operated on static or isolated observations, failing to maintain persistent object state or accumulate spatial evidence across frames. The missing ingredient identified by this paper is not stronger per-frame perception but a stateful reasoning mechanism that continuously integrates evidence from a partial, evolving stream of 2D observations into a coherent 3D scene understanding. Key Idea The core insight is to separate semantic planning (what evidence is needed, which tool to call) from spatial evidence acquisition (the actual geometric computation), casting the VLM strictly as an orchestrator while delegating perception and 3D reasoning to specialized tools. Crucially, both the evolving scene state (entity registry, geometry, spatial facts) and the reasoning trajectory (tool calls, observations, failures, conclusions) are maintained in distinct persistent memories that condition every subsequent tool request — so later reasoning steps build on accumulated evidence rather than re-processing frames from scratch. Methodology S-AGENT formalizes spatial reasoning as an iterative loop: at step t, a tool-calling VLM planner π_θ maps (question q, frame sequence F, scene memory S_t, agent memory H_t) → evidence request r_t; a spatial tool executes r_t and returns observation o_t; memories update as S_{t+1} = Merge(S_t, e_t), H_{t+1} = Append(H_t, c_t), where e_t is reusable scene content and c_t is process context. Three tool levels: (1) Level 1 — 2D visual evidence acquisition: keyframe selection (TStarKeyframeSearchTool), open-vocabulary detection (GroundingDINO), two-stage VLM+detector multi-frame grounding (vlm_ground_objects), and image-level depth estimation. (2) Level 2 — 2D→3D geometric lifting: metric_depth3d_tool built on Depth-Anything-3, producing metric depth, 3D coordinates, camera poses, and BEV projections. (3) Level 3 — spatial knowledge aggregation: five specialized experts — Metric Measurement (closest-point/center-to-center/object-size from sampled 3D points), Counting (NMS-deduped multi-frame detection aggregation), Visual Orientation (appearance-based facing direction), Relative Position (3D coordinate comparison under query-specific reference frame), and Object-Centric View (labeled viewpoint reasoning). For training distillation: a frozen teacher S-AGENT (GPT-5.4 planner) generates trajectories over 100K questions from SenseNova-SI-800K (disjoint from all evaluation benchmarks). Trajectories are filtered by final-answer correctness (MCQ: exact option match; numeric: mean relative accuracy ≥ 0.6; text: normalized string match), then decomposed into three supervision types: final-answer, turn-level planner calls, and expert/tool-specific sub-samples — yielding 292,391 SFT samples (S-300K). Qwen3-VL-8B is fine-tuned on S-300K with LLaMAFactory, 8× B200 GPUs, max seq 8192, lr 5×10⁻⁵, cosine decay, 1 epoch. Three design decisions are validated by ablation: expert mediation of raw 3D data (Level 3 experts provide +7.7 pp on ViewSpatial versus raw Level 2 alone); dual memory separation (Scene +1.5 pp, Agent +0.9 pp); and not using tool-call presence as a hard SFT filter (allowing the model to learn when tools are beneficial). Key Results - MMSI-Bench (zero-shot): S-AGENT achieves 46.4%, beating Gemini 3 Pro (45.2%) by 1.2% and GPT-5.4 (41.9%) by 4.5%. All open-source spatial models lag substantially (best: SN-SI-1.1-Qwen3VL-8B at 38.1%). Largest gains on camera motion (46.0%), object motion (48.7%), and multi-step reasoning (44.4%), consistent with the design emphasis on temporal evidence accumulation. - ViewSpatial-Bench (zero-shot): S-AGENT achieves 60.0%, outperforming GPT-5.4 (45.6%) by 14.4% and Gemini 3 Pro (50.4%) by 9.6%. Dominant on C-OVO (55.5%) and P-RD (81.1%). Best open-weight spatial model is VST-3B-SFT at 52.9%. - ReVSI (zero-shot): S-AGENT achieves 58.8, ranking second overall (Gemini 3 Pro: 60.9). Best open-source model by a margin; beats all spatially specialized models (best competing: VLM3R-7B at 50.1). Strongest on relative direction (66.4%) and route planning (66.1%). - VSI-SUPER VSR: S-AGENT achieves 77.2% at 240-minute videos versus 40.0% for the strongest baseline (Cambrian-S-7B-LFP), +37.2 pp — attributed to frame-selection tooling. Fails to outperform Cambrian-S-7B-LFP on VSC; reliability of VSI-SUPER questioned in the literature and flagged by the authors. - S-AGENT-8B (SFT): 41.6% MMSI, 46.8% ViewSpatial, 52.8% ReVSI — competitive with GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3 Pro. Counter-intuitive finding: equipping the untuned Qwen3-VL-8B with S-AGENT zero-shot reduces MMSI performance (30.7% vs. 31.1% baseline), confirming that a weak planner cannot handle tool-selection decisions and noisy observations without trajectory training. - Baselines are current and strong: GPT-5.4, Gemini 3 Pro, Grok-4, Qwen3 series, InternVL3.5 series. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The specific combination of hierarchical spatial tools with dual stateful memory for continuous multi-view video reasoning is novel. The distillation pipeline (S-300K) constitutes a new training dataset and methodology. Genuinely novel versus known ideas: the three-level tool hierarchy with expert mediation of raw 3D signals, the dual-memory separation, and the empirical finding that raw 3D evidence is detrimental without expert filtering — these go beyond the existing agentic spatial reasoning paradigm (ReAct, VADAR, SpaceTools). Standard components include GroundingDINO, Depth-Anything-3, VGGT, and SFT on synthetic trajectories, which are established practices. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations in Table 5 are well-designed, isolating each evidence level and each memory component on ViewSpatial-Bench. No error bars or significance tests are reported — all numbers are single-run evaluations on standardized benchmarks. The training data split is explicitly verified as disjoint from all evaluation benchmarks. Filtering criteria are detailed (Appendix C). Code is not confirmed as released; only a project page (Ropedia/S-Agent) is mentioned. The teacher model for S-300K generation is GPT-5.4, a closed-source API — full reproduction requires access to this or an equivalent closed model. Tools (GroundingDINO, Depth-Anything-3) are publicly available. The ablation uses a single planner (GPT-5.4) on a single benchmark (ViewSpatial); whether component contributions generalize across planner types or benchmarks is untested. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2606.20515); results are not peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL profile) - Practical: Core tools (GroundingDINO, Depth-Anything-3) run on commercially available hardware; S-AGENT-8B inference can run on a single high-end GPU. Zero-shot setting with GPT-5.4/Gemini planners requires API access. - Efficient: Inference cost is not reported. Each question involves 3–4 reasoning rounds, each potentially invoking detection, depth estimation, and expert computation — latency versus single VLM inference is likely 5–20× higher and is not characterized. - Effective: Evidence across four benchmarks with clean train/test separation and strong baselines confirms the method solves the stated problem, not just a proxy. - Scalable: 240-minute video results on VSI-SUPER demonstrate temporal scaling, but scene complexity scaling (more objects, denser scenes, longer object tracks) is not evaluated. The memory architecture could scale poorly with very long videos if Scene Memory grows large. This is a claim, not a demonstrated result. The work delivers a trained model (S-AGENT-8B) and a trajectory dataset (S-300K); code and model weights are not confirmed released. Strengths - The dual-memory design (Scene vs. Agent memory) is principled and well-motivated: it separately handles what is known about the scene and how that knowledge was acquired, preventing both duplicate evidence accumulation and redundant tool calls — more sophisticated than naive context concatenation used in prior agents. - The ablation result that raw Level-2 3D evidence adds only 0.8 pp while expert-mediated Level-3 aggregation adds 7.7 pp is an important and non-obvious empirical finding with direct architectural implications for future system design. - The trajectory decomposition strategy (final-answer + turn-level + expert-specific supervision from a single rollout) is a compact and efficient way to extract multi-granularity training signal, enabling an 8B model to close most of the gap to frontier proprietary models. - The finding that tool use hurts without trajectory training (Qwen3-VL-8B + S-AGENT = 30.7% < 31.1% baseline) is an honest and practically important negative result that warns against naive application of agentic frameworks to small models. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note VSI-SUPER's contested reliability and caution against over-interpreting VSC results (citing [22]). They acknowledge that applying S-AGENT to the base 8B model without trajectory training does not consistently help and sometimes hurts. No inference latency or computational cost discussion appears in either the main paper or appendices. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No inference cost characterization: Multi-round tool invocation makes S-AGENT substantially more expensive per query than single-pass VLM inference. Without latency numbers, the cost-accuracy tradeoff cannot be evaluated for deployment decisions. - Closed-source teacher dependency: S-300K is generated by GPT-5.4 — practitioners without API access cannot reproduce the distillation pipeline. Whether open-source teachers (e.g., Qwen3-VL-72B) produce comparable SFT data is not studied. - Single-planner ablation: All component ablations use GPT-5.4 on ViewSpatial-Bench. Whether the contribution ordering (L1 < L2 << L3, scene memory > agent memory) holds for weaker planners or on MMSI/ReVSI is untested. - Narrow margin on MMSI: The 1.2 pp gain over Gemini 3 Pro is the headline zero-shot result; no significance testing makes it unclear if this is reliable. - Failure mode analysis is shallow: The qualitative section shows only successes; systematic analysis of when S-AGENT fails (e.g., detection failures, depth errors cascading through the pipeline, memory corruption from false positives) is absent. Bottom Line A practitioner building spatial reasoning pipelines over video or multi-view scenes will find concrete, validated ideas here — the expert-mediated evidence hierarchy and dual-memory architecture represent a meaningful advance over existing agents — but should factor in the unmeasured inference cost and the closed-source teacher dependency before committing to the distillation path.
Recommended 2606.20515 PDF
#101 — Innovative (60)

BadWorld: Adversarial Attacks on World Models (14 ▲ )

Linghui Shen, Mingyue Cui, Xingyi Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BADWORLD, a label-free adversarial attack framework that corrupts autoregressive visual world models by perturbing flow-matching velocity predictions without access to ground-truth future videos or knowledge of future user controls. - Why it's important: Visual world models are being deployed in safety-critical applications (robotics, autonomous driving, interactive simulation), yet their robustness to imperceptible input perturbations has never been systematically characterized—a gap that could allow silent rollout degradation in deployed systems. - How it does it: The attack suppresses predicted velocity norms at early denoising timesteps (where the latent approximates pure noise, bypassing the need for ground-truth frames), and uses CMA-ES-driven bi-level optimization to discover control trajectories that resist the perturbation, forging control-agnostic adversarial images. - The good: Velocity-Min at η=0.05 induces a 74.8% increase in MEt3R geometric inconsistency on Astra and drops imaging quality from 0.690 to 0.513, while random noise with matched statistics produces essentially no measurable effect—demonstrating that structured velocity disruption, not just noise, drives rollout collapse. - The missing: The sole comparison baseline is random noise; no existing I2V adversarial method (I2VGuard, Anti-I2V, Immune2V) is adapted for comparison, making it impossible to gauge how much of the attack strength comes from the design choices rather than simply optimizing any objective via PGD. - Read it or not: Researchers working on VWM safety, adversarial robustness of generative video models, or privacy protection for interactive content should read this; practitioners need defenses the paper does not provide.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (72) anchor the composite: a genuinely new label-free velocity attack on autoregressive visual world models, with the counter-intuitive and well-evidenced finding that suppressing velocity magnitude is more destructive than amplifying it (errors fail to converge to the manifold and compound through the autoregressive window). Ablations on the early-timestep trick and history proxy are clean. Rigor (55) and Resilience (55) are bounded by the central weakness: the only baseline is random noise — no existing I2V adversarial method (I2VGuard, Anti-I2V) is adapted — so attack strength is poorly contextualized; custom-only datasets at 100-image scale, no error bars, no wall-clock/compute figures, and no released artifact (Impact 45) further limit it. This is a preprint. Composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*45 = 60.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Visual world models (VWMs) synthesize action-conditioned future video rollouts from a single context image and are increasingly used in autonomous driving simulation, embodied robotics, and interactive gaming. As these models develop more coherent temporal dynamics, a fundamental question arises: are their learned dynamics stable under imperceptible input perturbations? Existing adversarial attacks on generative models target text-to-image personalization, image-to-image editing, or standard I2V pipelines—all of which assume either fixed generation conditions or reference output videos. Autoregressive VWMs present two unique obstacles that prior work cannot handle: the attacker has no ground-truth future video at attack time (no reference loss is computable), and the model's rollout depends on future user-controlled action sequences that are unknown and variable (an attack optimized for one trajectory may fail under a different one). Key Idea In flow-matching-based autoregressive generation, the velocity field v̂ specifies the direction and magnitude of each latent-state update during denoising. Suppressing this magnitude at early denoising timesteps (where z_t ≈ ε because t≈1, eliminating dependence on the unknown future chunk z_n) causes incomplete denoising—the latent never reaches the data manifold—and these local errors compound through the autoregressive history window to collapse subsequent chunks. This yields a self-supervised adversarial objective that needs no ground-truth future frames, no action annotations, and operates entirely within the model's own predicted dynamics. Methodology BADWORLD has two components. The first is the label-free velocity attack. Four adversarial objectives are defined over the predicted velocity v̂: Velocity-Max (-‖v̂‖²₂, forces over-denoising), Velocity-Min (‖v̂‖²₂, suppresses denoising), Drift-Max (-‖v̂ - v_ref‖²₂, pushes away from static reference), and Drift-Min (‖v̂ - v_ref‖²₂, collapses toward static reference), where v_ref = ε - x_ctx is derived from the context image. Two approximations make these label-free: (i) restricting queries to timesteps t ∈ [950, 1000] where z_t ≈ ε, bypassing the unknown future chunk; and (ii) replacing true autoregressive history z_{<n} with a repeat of the encoded adversarial context E(x+δ). PGD with ℓ∞ projection is used for inner optimization. The second component is trajectory-adaptive bi-level optimization formulated as min_{‖δ‖∞≤η} max_{τ∈T} F(τ;δ). The outer maximization uses CMA-ES (derivative-free) to discover hard control trajectories that currently resist the perturbation, capturing temporal correlations in camera motion (coordinated pan and zoom) that independent random sampling misses. The inner minimization updates δ via PGD against trajectories sampled from the evolving hard pool, which is maintained separately per autoregressive chunk. Critical design decisions: (1) early-timestep restriction is load-bearing—removing it collapses MEt3R from 0.264 to 0.167 (well-justified by ablation); (2) context-repeat history proxy matches near-identical performance to full self-rollout at far lower cost (MEt3R 0.264 vs. 0.271 for self-rollout, which runs every 50 steps at 4 trajectories—not a fully fair comparison); (3) CMA-ES over random search is motivated by temporal correlation structure but not ablated against simpler black-box optimizers. Key Results On Astra (100 landscape images from SpatialVID, 832×480, 3 trajectories per image, η=0.05): Velocity-Min reduces aesthetic quality from 0.501 to 0.405 (-19%), imaging quality from 0.690 to 0.513 (-26%), and background consistency from 0.915 to 0.845; MEt3R (geometric inconsistency) increases from 0.151 to 0.264 (+74.8%). Random noise with matched statistics: MEt3R 0.150 (negligible). On Matrix-Game 2.0 (100 GTA gameplay frames, 640×352): Velocity-Min reduces I2V Subject from 0.897 to 0.708 (-21%) and MEt3R from 0.098 to 0.204 (+108%). Bi-level optimization adds minimal improvement on the full benchmark (MEt3R 0.265 vs. 0.264 for Velocity-Min alone) but shows clearer benefit on the 10 hardest samples (aesthetic quality 0.425 vs. 0.440). Preprocessing robustness: light Gaussian noise (σ=0.5) leaves the attack largely intact (MEt3R 0.255), but JPEG-75 reduces it substantially (MEt3R 0.164). Black-box transfer: Matrix-Game→Astra degrades background consistency from 0.878 to 0.846 (moderate); Astra→Matrix-Game shows near-negligible degradation, attributed to resolution mismatch destroying pixel-level perturbations. All experiments run on A800 80GB GPU; no wall-clock runtime figures are reported. Tested at a scale of 100 images per model—not at stress scale. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The formalization of image-space adversarial attacks on autoregressive VWMs addresses a genuine gap—prior work targets condition-level attacks (physical perturbations in driving scenes) or search-level attacks (automated attack search for world agents), not pixel-level context-image perturbations on interactive VWMs. The velocity-level self-supervised loss with early-timestep approximation is a novel attack formulation. The finding that suppressing velocity magnitude is more destructive than amplifying it—because weakened updates prevent convergence to stable manifolds and errors compound through the autoregressive history—is a non-obvious mechanistic insight. What is standard: PGD optimization, CMA-ES, flow-matching formulations, and the privacy-protection framing borrowed from T2I/I2I adversarial literature. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: timestep selection, history proxy, and attack budget are each ablated with clear directional evidence. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported anywhere; the experimental scale (100 images × 3 trajectories) is modest. The project website (https://linghuiishen.github.io/BadWorld/) is listed but no explicit code or model release is confirmed in the paper text. Methodological concerns: (1) the only baseline is random noise—existing I2V adversarial methods are not adapted, leaving attack strength poorly contextualized; (2) evaluation datasets are entirely custom-built with no standard benchmark, making cross-paper comparison impossible; (3) Velocity-Min is selected as the strongest objective and the bi-level study is conducted with Velocity-Min already near ceiling for most samples, structurally limiting the room for bi-level to show improvement; (4) the GTA dataset images are stylized via Flux Kontext to standardize object appearance, which may not represent natural distribution shift in real deployments. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.16519v1); results are not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Requires an A800 80GB GPU for attack optimization; no consumer-hardware path is described or estimated. - Efficient: 300 PGD steps per image for the base attack; bi-level adds CMA-ES trajectory mining on top. No wall-clock time per image or FLOPs are reported—a significant omission for a paper claiming practical relevance. - Effective: Velocity-Min demonstrably collapses rollouts on two architecturally distinct models (flow-matching with continuous and discrete control), confirmed both quantitatively (VBench, MEt3R) and qualitatively (structural collapse, grey artifacts, near-static frames). Effectiveness against higher-capacity or closed-source models is not tested. - Scalable: Tested on 100-image datasets; no analysis of scaling to larger image sets, longer rollouts, or more complex scene types. Transferability across model architectures is limited and resolution-dependent. The paper delivers empirical findings and an algorithmic procedure; no software artifact, library, or trained model is released. Strengths - The problem decomposition into two challenges (missing future supervision, unknown future controls) and the direct technical responses to each is architecturally clean; the method design is internally coherent rather than assembled from unrelated techniques. - The early-denoising approximation (z_t ≈ ε at t≈1) is a genuinely clever way to eliminate dependence on unknown future chunks, and the ablation confirms it is the single most load-bearing component of the design. - The velocity scaling experiment in the appendix (varying s in v̂' = s·v̂) provides mechanism understanding for why Velocity-Min outperforms Velocity-Max, elevating the paper beyond a purely empirical contribution. - CMA-ES is an appropriate algorithm for trajectory mining over the temporally correlated control space—the covariance update captures coherent camera motions (e.g., continuous pan + forward zoom) that independent random sampling structurally misses. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) asymmetric black-box transferability, with Astra→Matrix-Game failing due to resolution mismatch disrupting pixel-level perturbations; (2) moderate overall bi-level improvement because Velocity-Min already achieves near-ceiling degradation on most samples; and (3) JPEG compression and TV denoising partially neutralize the attack. These are confronted directly and honestly, which is credible. The authors do not claim strong black-box universality. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The random-noise baseline is trivially weak. At minimum, I2VGuard or Anti-I2V should be adapted to the autoregressive setting to calibrate how much of BADWORLD's effectiveness comes from the velocity-level objective versus simply running any optimized PGD attack. - No compute cost is reported. Wall-clock time per image (especially for the bi-level variant) is essential information for assessing whether this is a practical attack or a research prototype. - Only two models are tested, and both use flow-matching autoregressive denoising. Generality claims are not supported by testing on diffusion-based AR VWMs, memory-based architectures (WorldMem), or token-prediction-based world models (Oasis, MAGI-1). - No defense is evaluated beyond image preprocessing. Whether adversarial training, randomized smoothing, or VWM-specific robustness strategies can close these vulnerabilities is entirely open, limiting the paper's actionability for practitioners. - The context-repeat vs. self-rollout ablation is not fully controlled: self-rollout runs every 50 steps with 4 trajectories rather than at every step, so the comparison understates the potential benefit of true history. Bottom Line BADWORLD is a focused and technically coherent first attack on autoregressive VWMs—the velocity-level self-supervised formulation and early-timestep trick are the paper's intellectual core—and any researcher building or deploying world-model-based simulators should read it; however, the absence of non-trivial baselines and runtime data makes the attack's practical strength and efficiency harder to assess than it should be.
Recommended 2606.16519 PDF
#102 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Moebius: 0.2B Lightweight Image Inpainting Framework with 10B-Level Performance (96 ▲ )

Kangsheng Duan, Ziyang Xu, Wenyu Liu, Xiaohu Ruan, Xiaoxin Chen, Xinggang Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 19 Jun 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Moebius, a 0.22B-parameter latent diffusion inpainting specialist built on a novel Local-λ Mix Interaction (LλMI) block and adaptive multi-granularity latent distillation that matches or exceeds the benchmark scores of 10–12B-parameter industrial models at 2% of the parameter count. - Why it's important: Deploying 10B-parameter inpainting models (FLUX.1-Fill-Dev, SD3.5) on edge or latency-sensitive devices is impractical, yet naive architectural compression of smaller models causes catastrophic quality collapse (FID degradation from 32.75 to >43), blocking the path to efficient specialist deployment. - How it does it: Both self-attention and cross-attention are replaced by fixed-size linear matrix summaries (λ = softmax(K)^T V), eliminating quadratic attention maps while preserving semantic context interaction; combined with multi-granularity distillation performed entirely in the latent space to recover compressed representational capacity. - The good: On Places2 (Small), Moebius achieves 0.92 FID / 0.091 LPIPS, surpassing FLUX.1-Fill-Dev (0.94 / 0.099) and SD3.5 Large-Inpainting (3.02 / 0.105) at >15× lower total inference latency (0.52s vs. 8.05s), supported by a 15-configuration ablation table that systematically validates each component. - The missing: Moebius is fine-tuned per benchmark while its primary competitors (FLUX.1-Fill-Dev, SD3.5) are zero-shot generalists, and the distillation teacher is PixelHacker (862M) — not a 10B model — so performance parity with industrial models reflects benchmark specialization as much as architectural efficiency. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners working on efficient diffusion deployment and researchers studying linear attention mechanisms for image generation, but approach the top-line efficiency-vs-generalist comparisons with awareness of the fine-tuned-specialist-vs-zero-shot-generalist confound.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (58) is undercut by the central confound the analyst flags: Moebius is fine-tuned per benchmark while its 10B competitors are zero-shot generalists, and the distillation teacher is only 862M — so 'rivals 10B' reflects benchmark specialization, not architectural parity. Novelty (60, new method + engineering optimization; Interactive-λ is the genuine novel piece, Local-λ is close to LambdaNetworks) and weak Resilience (50) cap the score, despite a strong 15-config ablation and real efficiency gains. The n=22 underpowered user study and unconfirmed code release also weigh on it. Composite ≈ 0.20*60+0.25*58+0.15*58+0.15*62+0.15*50+0.10*70 ≈ 59.
empirical Novelty 60 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 62 Resilience 50 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Image inpainting — reconstructing masked regions with semantically coherent content — has been pushed to high quality by 10B-parameter industrial models (FLUX.1-Fill-Dev at 11.9B, SD3.5 Large-Inpainting at 8B), but these are unusable in latency-sensitive or resource-constrained settings (edge devices, mobile, real-time pipelines). The next-best academic option, PixelHacker (862M), uses Gated Linear Attention (GLA) to reduce cost, but still exceeds edge deployment budgets. The critical gap: naive substitution of DWConv and linear attention into existing diffusion backbones causes FID to collapse from ~33 to >43, because (a) lightweight operators have reduced representational capacity for the semantic reasoning inpainting demands, and (b) GLA cannot be mathematically formulated to perform cross-attention, blocking the integration of global semantic priors (LCG embeddings) needed for high-quality restoration. Key Idea The key insight is that both intra-image self-attention and cross-attention with semantic priors can be unified under a single fixed-size linear matrix abstraction: instead of computing quadratic O(N²) attention maps, summarize the key-value context into a compact C×C matrix λ = softmax(K)^T V, then compute output as Qλ in O(NC) time. Local-λ applies this to spatial self-aggregation with an additional positional convolution term; Interactive-λ extends it to cross-attention with LCG embeddings by projecting the semantic prior into the same fixed-size matrix format. This breaks the architectural impasse of GLA and enables holistic compression of both attention types without per-head quadratic cost. Methodology Moebius is a latent diffusion U-Net trained with PixelHacker (862M) as teacher in a student-teacher distillation framework. The denoising network is restructured as follows: LλMI block: For input X_in, the forward pass is: X₁ = Local-λ(LN(X_in)) + X_in, where Local-λ computes λ_c = softmax(K)^T V and λ_p = Conv3D_pos(V), output Y = Qλ_c + Qλ_p; X₂ = Interactive-λ(LN(X₁), E_LCG) + X₁, where Interactive-λ projects LCG embeddings to K, V and computes λ_c = softmax(K^i)^T V^i, λ_p = E_pos V^i, output Y^i = Q^i λ_c^i + Q^i λ_p^i; X_out = Mix-FFN(LN(X₂)) + X₂ (depthwise-augmented MLP replacing dense FFN). Spatial blocks use Depthwise Residual (DWConv) modules. Multi-granularity distillation: Four losses operating strictly in latent space: (1) Coarse L2 alignment at 16×16 bottleneck (L_C_KD); (2) Fine L2 alignment at 64×64 final output (L_F_KD); (3) Task noise prediction loss (L_task); (4) Latent perceptual loss via E-LatentLPIPS (L_perceptual). Adaptive gradient-norm-based weights dynamically balance contributions: W_F_KD = ||G(L_task, θ_F)||² / ||G(L_F_KD, θ_F)||², and similarly for perceptual and cross-granularity terms. This eliminates manual hyperparameter tuning of loss scales. Critical design decisions: (a) Fully latent-space distillation avoids expensive VAE decoding during training — justified both for memory efficiency and because the student lacks the decoder-level parameters; (b) Adaptive gradient-norm balancing is well-justified empirically (Tab. 5: FID degrades from 26.43 to 74.20 using only coarse loss); (c) Using PixelHacker rather than a 10B model as teacher is pragmatic but caps the knowledge ceiling. Training: 16 NVIDIA L40S GPUs, batch size 768, 138K iterations BF16, Muon optimizer, lr 2e-4 with decay. Per-benchmark fine-tuning on RTX 3090s. 512×512 input (Places2, CelebA-HQ) and 256×256 (FFHQ). Key Results On Places2 (Small), Moebius: FID 0.92, LPIPS 0.091 — beats FLUX.1-Fill-Dev (0.94/0.099) and SD3.5 (3.02/0.105), though below teacher PixelHacker (0.82/0.088). On CelebA-HQ (512): FID 5.39, LPIPS 0.122 — dramatically beats FLUX.1-Fill-Dev (10.13/0.141) and SD3.5 (11.80/0.134), below PixelHacker (4.75/0.115). On FFHQ (256): FID 8.15, LPIPS 0.231 — beats FLUX.1-Fill-Dev (11.19/0.268). On Places2 (Test): FID 9.48, LPIPS 0.207 — worse than both FLUX.1-Fill-Dev (8.02/0.279) and PixelHacker (8.59/0.203). Efficiency: 0.226B params, 0.154 TFLOPs, 26.01 ms/step, 0.52s total for 20 steps vs FLUX.1-Fill-Dev's 11.9B, 9.927 TFLOPs, 161ms/step, 8.05s total. User study (22 participants, 50 cases/scenario, forced choice): Moebius 31.76% vs. PixelHacker 32.18%, FLUX.1-Fill-Dev 23.70%, SD3.5 12.36%. OOD (LVIS): Moebius FID 17.81 vs. PixelHacker 13.84, FLUX.1-Fill-Dev 14.52 — weaker zero-shot natural scene generalization. Baselines are current and strong for the academic track; the comparison against 10B industrial generalists is the headline but is complicated by the fine-tuning asymmetry. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION. The Interactive-λ formulation for cross-attention via fixed-size linear matrices is genuinely novel — it solves a specific architectural constraint (GLA's inability to handle cross-attention) with a principled linear extension. The Local-λ formulation is closely related to LambdaNetworks and SANA linear attention, with the dual positional + semantic aggregation being a modest extension. The multi-granularity latent distillation is a competent combination of existing KD techniques (feature alignment, task loss, latent perceptual loss) with an adaptive balancing mechanism borrowed from GAN loss weighting — this is engineering optimization rather than new methodology. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation table (Tab. 2) with 15 architectural configurations is unusually thorough and systematically proves the necessity of each component. Distillation objective ablation (Tab. 5) validates each loss. CFG scale ablation and OOD evaluation are provided in supplementary. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported. The user study has only 22 participants — underpowered for statistical confidence; no inter-rater agreement metrics. Code and weights are not explicitly stated as released at submission; a project page (https://hustvl.github.io/Moebius) is referenced. This is a preprint (arXiv:2606.19195v1, June 17, 2026), not peer-reviewed. One methodological concern: each Moebius model is fine-tuned on the exact benchmark it is evaluated on (Places2, CelebA-HQ, FFHQ), while FLUX.1-Fill-Dev and SD3.5 are evaluated zero-shot — the paper labels this as standard practice in the community, which is accurate, but the comparison is not like-for-like. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS — a trained inpainting model with full architecture specification and inference evaluation. - Practical: Runs fine-tuning on NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPUs and inference at batch size 1 on a single L40S; the parameter count and FLOPs are firmly within commercial hardware reach. - Efficient: 0.154 TFLOPs and 26.01 ms/step — 2.2× lower FLOPs than PixelHacker (0.338 TFLOPs) and 64× lower than FLUX.1-Fill-Dev (9.927 TFLOPs); total inference 15× faster than FLUX counting required sampling steps. - Effective: Convincingly solves the stated problem on portrait benchmarks (CelebA-HQ, FFHQ), where it substantially surpasses 10B generalists; less convincing on Places2 (Test) where FID (9.48) exceeds FLUX.1-Fill-Dev (8.02). - Scalable: Only tested at 512×512 and 256×256; no experiments or claims about higher resolutions (1024×1024+), which are increasingly the relevant operating point for practical image editing. The approach relies on fixed-size latent grids, so scaling behavior is uninvestigated. Delivers a trained model (weights accessible via project page) and a reusable architectural building block (LλMI). Does not deliver a software library or CLI. Strengths - The 15-configuration ablation table (Tab. 2) is unusually systematic — it proves the necessity of the LλMI combination by exhaustively showing that substituting individual lightweight operators in isolation fails, and only the holistic design succeeds, directly refuting the naive compression hypothesis. - Interactive-λ genuinely resolves a real architectural conflict: GLA has no cross-attention formulation, so any architecture combining GLA self-attention with LCG priors was stuck. The fixed-size matrix reformulation is clean and extensible. - Performing all distillation — including perceptual alignment — inside the latent space is a meaningful training-time efficiency gain; E-LatentLPIPS avoids VAE decode overhead, making the perceptual objective feasible in a lightweight training budget. - The adaptive gradient-norm balancing eliminates a major practical obstacle (manual tuning of heterogeneous loss scales), and its necessity is empirically demonstrated through the ablation rather than assumed. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly show failure cases (Fig. 11): Moebius produces less plausible textures in tiny background regions with severely limited contextual information, where the 862M teacher is noticeably better. They frame this as an acceptable efficiency-performance trade-off. They also acknowledge that Moebius is a task-specific specialist and note slightly weaker OOD performance on natural scenes (LVIS FID 17.81 vs. FLUX 14.52). The authors confront these limitations directly, which increases credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The fine-tuned-specialist-vs-zero-shot-generalist asymmetry is the single largest interpretive issue: Moebius is trained on Places2 train set and evaluated on Places2 test set; FLUX.1-Fill-Dev has never seen this data distribution in the same way. The headline "rivals 10B models" is true numerically on most benchmarks but requires this context to interpret correctly. - The teacher ceiling is PixelHacker (862M), not a 10B model. Moebius's performance is bounded above by its teacher, not by FLUX.1-Fill-Dev — the 10B comparison reflects benchmark fine-tuning, not 10B knowledge transfer. - No text conditioning: Moebius uses LCG (category embeddings from unmasked image context), not text prompts. FLUX.1-Fill-Dev supports both text-guided and context-based inpainting. For practical deployment this is a meaningful capability gap, unaddressed. - The user study (n=22) is substantially underpowered — no p-values, no confidence intervals, no correction for multiple comparisons across four conditions. - Only 512×512 maximum resolution tested; modern practical inpainting targets 1024×1024 and above — whether the linear attention mechanisms remain superior at higher resolutions is untested. - Places2 (Test) FID: Moebius scores 9.48 vs. FLUX.1-Fill-Dev 8.02 — the paper does not explicitly discuss this; the focus shifts to the Places2 (Small) subprotocol where Moebius wins. Bottom Line Moebius is a well-executed, rigorously ablated lightweight inpainting system that delivers genuine deployment-relevant efficiency gains (>15× faster, 2% parameters) with competitive benchmark quality, and practitioners targeting latency-sensitive or edge inpainting should read it — but the headline claim of "10B-level performance" should be understood as performance parity on academic benchmarks under fine-tuned-specialist conditions, not a zero-shot generalist capability match.
Recommended 2606.19195 PDF
#103 — Moderately Innovative (59)

Navigating the Safety-Fidelity Trade-off: Massive-Variate Time Series Forecasting for Power Systems via Probabilistic Scenarios

K Xu, A Wang, X Dai · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PowerPhase, a probabilistic forecasting benchmark covering six transmission grids with up to 36,964 jointly forecasted channels generated by AC power-flow simulation, together with PowerForge, a scenario-based ordered-quantile forecaster with type-specific decoding heads and a causal cross-type bridge. - Why it's important: Power grid operators need probabilistic forecasts that respect voltage constraints for reserve scheduling and contingency screening, yet existing benchmarks cap at ~2,000 channels and carry no constraint-aware metrics, leaving the transmission-scale probabilistic regime entirely unevaluated. - How it does it: PowerForge encodes residuals relative to a per-channel attention-weighted diurnal anchor, mixes cross-channel information via K=64 shared global tokens at O(CK) cost, and conditions V,θ predictions on predicted P,Q through a causal cross-attention bridge that mirrors AC power-flow causality. - The good: The benchmark empirically surfaces a safety-fidelity trade-off — TimePrism achieves CRPS 0.0096 at 2383-bus but CVaR0.1 of 3.50, while PowerForge achieves CRPS 0.0042 and CVaR0.1 0.0004 — demonstrating that standard distributional metrics are insufficient for constraint-bound decisions. - The missing: The entire evaluation is on synthetic data generated from pandapower simulators with Gaussian noise; safety metrics cover only voltage-band [0.95, 1.05] p.u. violations and not full AC feasibility (line limits, generator reactive limits, angle consistency); no code or data release is mentioned. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML researchers working on large-scale multivariate probabilistic forecasting or power systems ML, particularly for the benchmark and the documented safety-fidelity trade-off; skip if real-data validation or production deployment is the primary concern.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (72) is the highest in the cluster — a transmission-scale benchmark (up to 36,964 jointly forecast channels, an order of magnitude beyond prior ~2,000) with AC-power-flow-typed trajectories and constraint-aware metrics, plus the well-ablated anchor-delta architecture (+84% CRPS when removed). Practical Impact (44) is the decisive drag: the work is paper-form only with no code, data-generation pipeline, or trained models released, and the entire evaluation is synthetic pandapower output with Gaussian noise. Resilience (50) and Rigor (52) are weakened by synthetic-only validation, a mild Goodhart concern (architecture designed for the benchmark then evaluated on it), a materially weakened TACTiS-2 baseline, single-seed ablations, and four baselines excluded at the largest scale. Composite: 0.20*72+0.25*52+0.15*70+0.15*62+0.15*50+0.10*44 = 59.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 62 Resilience 50 Impact 44
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Power system operators require short-term probabilistic forecasts of grid state — active power P, reactive power Q, voltage magnitude V, and voltage angle θ at each bus — to support reserve scheduling, contingency screening, and short-term market clearing. As renewable penetration increases grid stochasticity, probabilistic forecasts have become operationally routine. Two independent gaps block progress. First, standard ML benchmarks (ETT, Electricity, Traffic, Wiki) top out at ~2,000 jointly forecasted channels and carry no feasibility model, so there is no standard way to measure whether a forecast respects the AC power-flow equations governing the system. Second, power-system tools such as pandapower expose network models but provide neither standardized forecasting splits nor probabilistic metrics. A transmission grid with N buses produces a 4N-channel multivariate signal, placing even a modestly sized system (500 buses → 2,000 channels) at the upper limit of existing benchmarks and a large system (9,241 buses → 36,964 channels) an order of magnitude beyond them. The operational consequence is concrete: a model that achieves low CRPS while systematically underestimating voltage excursions can be more dangerous than one with slightly worse distributional fit but reliable constraint satisfaction. Key Idea The central architectural insight is the anchor-delta residual parameterization: subtract a per-channel attention-weighted reference constructed from the seven most recent daily segments before encoding, predict residuals, then add the anchor back. This removes the dominant diurnal component — which is nearly identical across all power-grid channels — leaving the encoder and decoder to model only deviations in the narrow physical band around 1.0 p.u. The ablation validates this as the single most important component: removing it raises CRPS by 84% and Distortion by more than 2×. The second structural idea is sub-quadratic cross-channel interaction via K=64 learnable global tokens that alternate read and write attention with all C channels at O(CK) cost, motivated by the observation that system-wide grid behavior mixes a small number of latent factors (aggregate load, renewable generation) into thousands of channels. Methodology PowerForge is an ordered-quantile scenario forecaster. The pipeline has five stages. (1) Anchor-delta: the last S=7 daily segments are extracted; per-channel attention weights are computed using Pearson correlation between each segment's tail and the most recent Q=8 input steps; a bias-correction term absorbs slow drift. The residual input x − Z_ref is passed to the encoder. (2) Channel encoder: a multi-scale temporal backbone with 1-D convolutions at kernel sizes {5, 25, 97} (1.25-hour, 6.25-hour, 1-day receptive fields at 15-minute resolution) is applied independently per channel, concatenated, and projected to hidden dimension H=128. Node embeddings (d=8), variable-type embeddings (d=2), and averaged calendar features are added. A low-rank global token mixer with K=64 tokens alternates read (channels attend to tokens) and write (tokens attend back to channels) steps, sharing projection weights across the two read steps; total cost is O(CK). (3) Type-aware decoder: M=16 learnable scenario embeddings are injected additively, and separate per-type heads emit residuals — P and Q heads use an unbounded linear projection with a softplus-scaled Gaussian stochastic regularizer; V head uses tanh-gated output scaled by a learnable Δ_V initialized at 0.05; θ head uses tanh-gated output with fixed Δ_θ=π and angular wrapping via atan2. (4) Causal cross-type bridge: V and θ hidden states attend to projected P,Q prediction tokens through scaled dot-product attention, encoding the known AC power-flow direction P,Q → V,θ as an inductive bias. (5) Training objective: scenarios are pointwise sorted along the M axis and supervised by a pinball loss at assigned quantile levels τ_m on a learnable Beta(α,β) grid; four learnable per-type loss coefficients balance gradient magnitudes; physics regularizers penalize voltage residual magnitude (smooth-L1) and angle temporal discontinuities (wrapped difference); auxiliary width and jitter regularizers constrain the quantile fan. PowerPhase generates ground-truth trajectories by running AC power flow (Newton-Raphson with Iwamoto fallback) through six pandapower test case networks (500 to 9,241 buses) driven by German TSO load, solar, and wind signals at 15-minute resolution over 2015-2016, yielding 70,176 time steps. Five daily-shape load archetypes (industrial, residential, PV-correlated, wind-correlated, EV-correlated) are assigned per bus with spatially correlated regional noise. The metric suite adds constraint-aware scores to CRPS: Safety_mBrier (scenario-level Brier score on voltage-band violations), NECV (expected constraint violation magnitude), and CVaR0.1 (worst-decile constraint violation magnitude). Key Results PowerForge achieves the best average rank across all four metrics on every grid. On the five main grids with eight baselines and three seeds: at 500-bus, CRPS 0.0030 (vs. TACTiS-2 0.0057; TimeMCL 0.0054); Safety_mBrier and CVaR0.1 both 0. At 2383-bus, CRPS 0.0042 (vs. TACTiS-2 0.0039, within one standard deviation); Safety_mBrier 0.0010 vs. next-best 0.0032 (TempFlow/Trans-TempFlow); CVaR0.1 0.0004 vs. next-best 0.0005. At 3120-bus, strict best on all metrics: CRPS 0.0038, Distortion 0.0076 vs. next-best 0.0162 (TimeMCL, >2× better). At PEGASE 9241 (36,964 channels, four-baseline comparison): CRPS 0.0077 vs. TimePrism 0.0112; CVaR0.1 0.0023 vs. Trans-TempFlow 0.0029. The safety-fidelity trade-off is the most concrete finding: TimePrism achieves competitive CRPS at multiple scales but CVaR0.1 reaches 3.50 (2383-bus) and 5.42 (3120-bus), indicating its winner-takes-all objective generates low-probability hypotheses that systematically violate voltage bounds. DeepAR fails on both axes (worst CRPS on every grid, Safety_mBrier never below 0.54). No single baseline holds a stable position across both scales and axes. Baselines include recent methods from ICML 2025 (TimeMCL) and ICLR 2025 (TimePrism); TACTiS-2 is from 2023. Note: this is a preprint; results are not yet peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (PowerPhase) + NEW METHOD (PowerForge) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (safety-fidelity trade-off). The benchmark is genuinely novel in combining transmission-scale channel counts, AC-power-flow-simulated physically typed trajectories, and constraint-aware metrics — no prior benchmark covers all three. The anchor-delta residual parameterization is a well-motivated and empirically validated contribution. The low-rank global mixer is a known bottleneck-attention pattern (Linformer, Perceiver) applied to time-series channels; the ordered-quantile loss is an established extension of quantile regression; physics regularization via soft penalties is established in physics-informed neural networks. The primary novelty is the domain-specific combination and the scale demonstration. Rigor & Reproducibility Main experiments report mean±std over three random seeds on ten rolling-origin test windows per network — adequate for seed variance but the effective test set is small (10 windows × 96 steps = 960 forecast steps per network). The ablation study uses a single seed on PEGASE 1354, which is weaker than the main evaluation protocol; safety metric ablation in Table 8 is also single-seed. TACTiS-2 is trained on its first stage only due to compute constraints, which likely understates its performance at scale. At PEGASE 9241 (the hardest grid), four of eight baselines (DeepAR, TimeGrad, TACTiS-2, TempFlow) are excluded from comparison without formal justification beyond noting TempFlow tracks Trans-TempFlow on smaller grids. No code, data generation scripts, or trained models are announced for release in the paper. The benchmark is fully self-generated by the authors; evaluation on independently sourced real PMU data is absent. A concern regarding Goodhart's Law applies mildly: PowerForge's architecture and hyperparameters were designed specifically for PowerPhase, then evaluated on it, though the benchmark's construction from established pandapower networks and public TSO data provides partial independence. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: All experiments run on a single NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell (96 GB); the largest network (36,964 channels) requires batch size 8, so commodity GPU deployment at this scale is not demonstrated. - Efficient: O(CK) cross-channel interaction with K=64 scales linearly in channel count; M=16 scenarios are produced in a single forward pass rather than per-step sampling; inference cost is stated to be tractable but wall-clock inference time is not reported. - Effective: The model achieves best rank on both distributional accuracy and voltage-band safety across all six grids on synthetic data; no real-data validation is presented, so effectiveness on actual grid operation is unverified. - Scalable: Demonstrated up to 36,964 channels on a single GPU; behavior at 10× scale (hundreds of thousands of channels or real interconnected systems) is not tested or claimed. The work delivers a paper-form artifact; no software library or trained model is released. Strengths - PowerPhase is the first publicly described benchmark combining transmission-scale channel counts (up to 36,964), AC-power-flow-simulated physically typed variables, temporal trajectory structure, and constraint-aware metrics; it fills a documented gap between ML and power-systems communities. - The anchor-delta residual parameterization is not only architecturally motivated but empirically dominant: its ablation (+84% CRPS, +108% Distortion) provides unusually strong evidence that residual decomposition around a diurnal reference is the correct inductive bias for grid signals. - The safety-fidelity trade-off is quantified concretely across eight baselines and six grids, demonstrating that CRPS-optimal models (TimePrism, TimeMCL) can be dangerous in constrained settings — this is an actionable empirical finding for practitioners choosing forecasting models. - The causal P,Q→V,θ cross-type bridge encodes the known AC power-flow direction as a soft architectural prior and is ablated (+22% CRPS without it), providing partial evidence that physics-structured architectures outperform unconstrained alternatives. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront three limitations: (1) safety metrics cover only voltage-band [0.95, 1.05] p.u. violations and do not certify line limits, generator reactive limits, power-balance residuals, or angle consistency; (2) the benchmark is synthetic, driven by national-level load/renewable traces with archetypal bus profiles and Gaussian perturbations, so safety-fidelity trends may reflect simulator artifacts; (3) PowerForge encodes variable type and P,Q→V,θ dependency but not explicit grid topology or admittance structure. These are acknowledged as future work rather than minimized.
